text: The SS *William G. Mather* was a Great Lakes freighter launched in 1925 as the flagship of the Cleveland-Cliffs Iron Company. Named after company president William Gwinn Mather, it served until 1980 and was notable for wartime supply missions, early radar use, and pioneering automated boiler systems. After restoration, it became a museum ship in Cleveland, now operated by the Great Lakes Science Center.

Stonestown Galleria station is a light rail station on the Muni Metro M Ocean View line in San Francisco, California. Located at 19th Avenue and Winston Drive near Stonestown Galleria, it opened in 1925 and was rebuilt in 1993 with an accessible island platform. The station serves as a key transit hub with connections to multiple Muni bus routes and SamTrans.

Ticagrelor, sold under the brand name Brilinta among others, is a medication used for the prevention of stroke, heart attack and other events in people with acute coronary syndrome, meaning problems with blood supply in the coronary arteries. It acts as a platelet aggregation inhibitor by antagonising the P2Y12 receptor. The drug is produced by AstraZeneca.

The most common side effects include dyspnea (difficulty breathing), bleeding and raised uric acid level in the blood.

It was approved for medical use in the European Union in December 2010, and in the United States in July 2011. In 2020, it was the 247th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 1 million prescriptions.

Paterson station is a New Jersey Transit rail station in Paterson, New Jersey, serving the Main Line. Located near downtown Paterson, it features a high-level island platform and connects to multiple bus routes. Originally opened in 1832, it was rebuilt several times, most recently in 2001.

Waiau is a small rural town in the Hurunui District of Canterbury, New Zealand, with a population of 255 (2018 census). Located on State Highway 70, it was once the terminus of the Waiau Branch railway. The town was heavily affected by the 2016 Kaikoura earthquake, suffering significant damage to buildings, including the historic Waiau Lodge Hotel, which later burned down in 2021. Waiau has a primary school and a community swimming pool.

Powers Auditorium is a historic theater and concert hall in Youngstown, Ohio. Originally opened in 1931 as the Warner Theatre, it was designed by Rapp & Rapp and operated as part of the Warner Brothers cinema chain. After closing in 1968, it was saved from demolition by a donation from the Powers family and reopened in 1969 as a performance venue. Renovated for acoustics and expanded with modern facilities, it now seats 2,303 and hosts events such as Youngstown Symphony Orchestra performances. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places, it retains its Art Deco design while incorporating additions like the Beecher Flad Pavilion and Ford Recital Hall.

Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was an American-born Upper Canadian farmer, land speculator, magistrate, militia captain and surveyor. Jones trained as a surveyor in New York City, and fled as a United Empire Loyalist to Upper Canada. In Upper Canada, he worked as a crown surveyor in the Nassau District, where he quickly rose to the position of Deputy Surveyor General, the highest position in a district of Upper Canada. He occupied that position from 1789 informally, and 1791 formally, until his retirement in 1799. During that time he laid down many of the township boundaries in the Niagara Peninsula and on the north shore of Lake Ontario. He led various teams that cut many of the first sideroads and concession roads into these areas, facilitating their settlement by European and American immigrants. Jones also surveyed the routes for Dundas Street and Yonge Street, and supervised their construction. After his retirement, Jones farmed first in Saltfleet Township, later moving to Brantford and finally an estate outside Paris named Cold Springs, where he died in 1836.

Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is the debut novella of the Indian author Bankim Chandra Chatterjee. The English-language novella, claimed by many to be the very first Indian novella, was published by a lesser-known periodical named Indian Field edited by Kishori Chand Mitra. The novella abruptly ended for unknown reasons, and later grew more obscure, until it was rediscovered and republished.

The Audubon Aquarium of the Americas is a public aquarium in New Orleans, Louisiana, specializing in aquatic life from North and South America. Opened in 1990, it houses around 10,000 animals representing 530 species. The facility was severely impacted by Hurricane Katrina in 2005, losing most of its marine life due to power failures. After reopening in 2006, it expanded exhibits, including Caribbean species, jellyfish displays, and a Gulf of Mexico tank replicating an oil rig ecosystem. It is accredited by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums (AZA).

A civil parish is a country subdivision, forming the lowest unit of local government in England. There are 264 civil parishes in the ceremonial county of Cambridgeshire, most of the county being parished; Cambridge is completely unparished; Fenland, East Cambridgeshire, South Cambridgeshire and Huntingdonshire are entirely parished. At the 2001 census, there were 497,820 people living in the parishes, accounting for 70.2 per cent of the county's population.

"I Am Real" is a 2011 dance-pop and EDM single by Simon van Kempen, an Australian hotelier and reality television personality known for appearing on *The Real Housewives of New York City* alongside his wife, Alex McCord. Released on April 21, 2011, through JSM Music, the song was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, with production by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. Van Kempen described the track as a tongue-in-cheek commentary on reality TV, featuring lyrics addressing his critics and experiences on the show, including the infamous phrase *"Thug in a cocktail dress."*  

The song received largely negative reviews, with critics comparing it unfavorably to other *Real Housewives*-related music. Despite low sales—reportedly around 1,000 digital copies in the U.S.—it gained attention through performances on *Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen* and at New York’s Fashion Night Out. A remix, *"Thug in a Club,"* was released on May 12, 2011.

An avulsion injury occurs when a body structure is forcibly torn away, either partially or completely. Common types include skin avulsions (ranging from minor flaps to severe degloving), ear avulsions (often from bites or trauma), nail avulsions (where the nail detaches from the nail bed), and brachial plexus avulsions (nerve damage from trauma or birth complications). Treatment varies by severity, including suturing, grafts, or surgical reattachment. Surgical avulsions may also be performed to treat conditions like varicose veins or ingrown nails.

Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 – April 27, 1961) was an American educator and recreation advocate. He worked in New York City schools, led the Playground Association of America, and directed wartime recreation programs during World War I. He received the Boy Scouts of America's Silver Buffalo Award in 1926.

Madonna and Child Kissing is an oil-on-panel painting by Quentin Matsys (c. 1525–1530) depicting the Virgin Mary embracing the Christ Child. It features symbolic elements like grapes and an apple and is held at the Mauritshuis in The Hague.

The Irving–Williams series refers to the relative stabilities of complexes formed by transition metals. In 1953 Harry Irving and Robert Williams observed that the stability of complexes formed by divalent first-row transition metal ions generally increase across the period to a maximum stability at copper: Mn(II) < Fe(II) < Co(II) < Ni(II) < Cu(II) > Zn(II).

Specifically, the Irving–Williams series refers to the exchange of aqua (H2O) ligands for any other ligand (L) within a metal complex. In other words, the Irving–Williams series is almost exclusively independent of the nature of the incoming ligand, L.

The main application of the series is to empirically suggest an order of stability within first row transition metal complexes (where the transition metal is in oxidation state II).

Another application of the Irving–Williams series is to use it as a correlation "ruler" in comparing the first stability constant for replacement of water in the aqueous ion by a ligand.

"I Am Real" is the debut single by Australian hotelier Simon van Kempen. It was released on 21 April 2011, through JSM Music, during his wife Alex McCord's tenure on the American reality television series The Real Housewives of New York City. The song was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, while production was handled by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. During its development, van Kempen and McCord met with the team of writers to ensure that the song's lyrics would represent them accurately. A dance-pop and EDM track, "I Am Real" contains tongue-in-cheek lyrics that address van Kempen's critics. One lyric references a comment McCord used to describe fellow cast member Luann de Lesseps.

"I Am Real" received mostly unfavourable reviews from music critics, who dismissed van Kempen as a television personality who tried launching a singing career. It also received comparisons to "Money Can't Buy You Class" (2010) by de Lesseps. The song was unsuccessful, selling just 1,000 copies in the United States, as of September 2011. van Kempen has since performed "I Am Real" on Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen and at Manhattan's annual "Fashion Night Out" event in 2011.

Baasskap (IPA-af) (also spelled baaskap), literally "boss-ship" or "boss-hood", is an Afrikaans term that was used during apartheid to describe the social, political and economic domination of South Africa by its minority white population generally and by Afrikaners in particular. The term is sometimes translated to the English-language term "white supremacy" and functioned either as a description or an endorsement of white minority rule in South Africa.
Al-Majd (Arabic: خربة المجد) is a Palestinian village in the Hebron Governorate, with a population of 1,925 (2007). It has Byzantine-era remains and was historically known as Khirbet Medjed.

Gold Hill, also known as Goldhill, Gold Mine, or Gold Ridge, is an unincorporated community north-centrally located in Lee County, Alabama, United States, just a few hundred feet south of the Chambers County line. It is part of the Columbus, Georgia-Alabama Metropolitan Area. Today, Gold Hill lies mostly in the corporate limits of Auburn.

The tie is a typographical symbol used in various contexts, including Cyrillic transliteration, Greek script, musical notation, and phonetic alphabets. In the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA), it indicates double articulation or affricates (e.g., [t͡ʃ]). The undertie (‿) represents syllable linking, while the ligature tie (͡) connects phonetic symbols. Unicode encodes these as U+203F (‿) and U+0361 (͡), respectively. The tie also appears in the Uralic Phonetic Alphabet and other linguistic notations.

*In the Night Room* (2004) is a horror novel by Peter Straub. It follows author Timothy Underhill, who receives disturbing emails from deceased acquaintances, while children's writer Willy Patrick believes her dead daughter is being held captive. Their stories intertwine as they confront supernatural horrors. Critics praised its metafictional elements and emotional depth, though some found its protagonist occasionally unappealing. A sequel to *Lost Boy, Lost Girl*, the novel explores trauma, grief, and the blurred lines between fiction and reality. It received positive reviews, with *The Washington Post* calling it a "powerful foray into the dark fantastic."
Estelle Desanges (born 8 March 1977) is a French former pornographic actress, known for her Hot d'Or awards and later work in media and activism.

Andrew Keary (born 1987) is an Irish former hurler who played as a left wing-forward for the Galway senior team from 2006 to 2008. He previously captained Galway to an All-Ireland Minor Hurling Championship title in 2005.
Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995), is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

The Mississippi Wing Civil Air Patrol is the state-level branch of the Civil Air Patrol in Mississippi. With over 500 members, it supports emergency services, cadet programs, and aerospace education. The wing conducts search and rescue missions, disaster relief, and provides training for youth and the public.

Michael Meacher (4 November 1939 – 21 October 2015) was a British Labour politician who served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Oldham West from 1970 to 2015. A left-wing figure, he held ministerial roles under Harold Wilson and James Callaghan, and later became Minister of State for the Environment (1997–2003) under Tony Blair. He was a vocal critic of Blair's policies, including the Iraq War, and briefly sought the Labour leadership in 2007 before backing John McDonnell. Meacher was also known for his environmental activism and advocacy for 9/11 truth campaigns.

The April 19 University Movement (Spanish: *Movimiento Universitario 19 de Abril*) was a Nicaraguan student-led protest movement formed on April 25, 2018, in response to government repression during the 2018–2020 protests. Based at the Polytechnic University of Nicaragua (UPOLI), the group demanded the release of political prisoners, accountability for police violence, and justice for those killed in the crackdown. They also called for the resignation of high-ranking officials, including National Police chief Francisco Díaz. The movement held President Daniel Ortega and Vice President Rosario Murillo responsible for human rights violations and sought mediation by Nicaragua’s Catholic bishops. One of its leaders, Amaya Coppens, was later recognized as an International Woman of Courage by the U.S. State Department. The protests resulted in hundreds of deaths, according to human rights groups.

Barry Took (19 June 1928 – 31 March 2002) was an English writer, television presenter and comedian. His decade-and-a-half writing partnership with Marty Feldman led to the television series Bootsie and Snudge, the radio comedy Round the Horne and other projects.

He is also remembered in the UK for presenting Points of View, a BBC Television programme featuring viewers'letters on the BBC's output, and the BBC Radio 4 programme The News Quiz.

Carroll Ashmore Campbell Jr. (July 24, 1940 – December 7, 2005), was an American Republican Party politician who served as the 112th governor of South Carolina from 1987 to 1995. Prior to this, he served as a member of the South Carolina Senate representing the 2nd district from 1977 to 1978 and as a congressman representing South Carolina's 4th district from 1979 to 1987.

American International School of Abuja (AISA) is a coeducational international day school in Abuja, Nigeria serving grades Pre-School through Grade 12.

The school year runs from August to June, with 180 student contact days. At the end of the 2015–2016 school year, the student population was 470 spanning from over 40 countries.

SMS Frankfurt was a light cruiser of the Wiesbaden class built by the German Kaiserliche Marine (Imperial Navy). She had one sister ship, SMS Wiesbaden; the ships were very similar to the previous Karlsruhe-class cruisers. The ship was laid down in 1913, launched in March 1915, and completed by August 1915. Armed with eight 15 cm SK L/45 guns, Frankfurt had a top speed of 27.5 knots (50.9 km/h; 31.6 mph) and displaced 6,601 t (6,497 long tons; 7,276 short tons) at full load.

Frankfurt saw extensive action with the High Seas Fleet during World War I. She served primarily in the North Sea, and participated in the Bombardment of Yarmouth and Lowestoft and the battles of Jutland and Second Heligoland. At Jutland, she was lightly damaged by a British cruiser and her crew suffered minor casualties. At the end of the war, she was interned with the bulk of the German fleet in Scapa Flow. When the fleet was scuttled in June 1919, Frankfurt was one of the few ships that were not successfully sunk. She was ceded to the US Navy as a war prize and ultimately expended as a bomb target in tests conducted by the US Navy and Army Air Force in July 1921.
François Picquet (IPA-fr; 4 December 1708 – 15 July 1781) was a French Sulpician priest who emigrated to Montreal, Canada, in 1734.

Planet Earth is a British nature documentary franchise produced by the BBC, beginning with *The Blue Planet* (2001). It includes television series like *Planet Earth* (2006) and *Planet Earth II* (2016), companion films such as *Earth* (2007), and related books and soundtracks. Known for its high-definition cinematography and global reach, the franchise explores Earth's diverse ecosystems and conservation challenges.

Bubble sort, sometimes referred to as sinking sort, is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. These passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. The algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements "bubble" up to the top of the list.

This simple algorithm performs poorly in real world use and is used primarily as an educational tool. More efficient algorithms such as quicksort, timsort, or merge sort are used by the sorting libraries built into popular programming languages such as Python and Java.

Idol Puerto Rico (season 1) was the debut season of the Puerto Rican singing competition, airing on WAPA-TV from August 7 to November 21, 2011. Hosted by Jaime Augusto Mayol, the judging panel included Ricardo Montaner, Jerry Rivera, Erika Ender, and Topy Mamery. The winner was Christian Pagán, with Japhet Albert as runner-up. Auditions were held in Ponce and San Juan, narrowing contestants down to 24 semifinalists, then 12 finalists. The finals took place at the Centro de Bellas Artes de Caguas.
Air Commodore Patrick Desmond Callaghan (16 July 1926 – 6 April 1992) was a one-star air officer in the Pakistan Air Force who is credited for his pioneering work in Flight safety in Pakistan.

SBI Cards & Payment Services Ltd., previously known as SBI Cards & Payment Services Private Limited, is a pure play credit card company and payment solutions provider in India. SBI Card was launched in October 1998 by State Bank of India, India's largest bank, and GE Capital. In December 2017, State Bank of India and The Carlyle Group acquired GE Capital's stake in the company. SBI Card is headquartered in Gurugram, Haryana/Delhi NCR and have branches in over 100 cities across India. The company is the only publicly listed pure-play credit card issuer in India.

Henry I of Cyprus, nicknamed the Fat (Lang-fr; 3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253 at Nicosia) was King of Cyprus from 1218 to 1253. He was the son of Hugh I of Cyprus and Alice of Champagne. When his father Hugh I died on January 10, 1218, the 8-month-old Henry became king. His mother was the official regent, but handed off the actual governing to her uncle, Philip of Ibelin. When Philip died, the effective regency passed to his brother, John of Ibelin, the Old Lord of Beirut.

Dinkar D. Patil (Marathi: दिनकर द. पाटील) (1915–2005) was a prominent Marathi film director, scriptwriter, and dialogue writer during the Golden Era (1950–1990) of the Marathi Film Industry. He directed, wrote scripts and dialogue for more than 60 Marathi films. He also directed two Hindi Films-Mandir and Gharbar. He wrote his famous autobiography titled as Patlache Por.

Phenoxyethanol is an organic compound (C₈H₁₀O₂) used as a preservative in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and vaccines, as well as a solvent and antiseptic. It exhibits germicidal properties and is effective against bacteria and fungi. Its use is regulated in some regions due to potential allergenic effects.

Harrisburg is a rural small town in Lewis County, New York, United States. The population was 437 at the 2010 census. The town is in the northwestern part of the county, northwest of Lowville, the county seat.

Nikaho (にかほ市, *Nikaho-shi*) is a city in southwestern Akita Prefecture, Japan, bordered by the Sea of Japan and Yamagata Prefecture. As of December 2019, it had a population of 24,291. Established in 2005 through a merger of three towns, its economy relies on fishing, agriculture, and electronics manufacturing. The city includes part of Chōkai Quasi-National Park, home to Mount Chōkai, a popular hiking destination.

Richard Gordon Guindon (December 2, 1935 – February 27, 2022) was an American cartoonist best known for his gag panel Guindon. Guindon's cartoons have appeared in the Minneapolis Tribune, The Realist, and the Detroit Free Press.

The 1945 College Football All-America Team consists of players recognized as the best in college football that year. The NCAA acknowledges 10 official All-American teams to determine consensus selections. Additionally, due to racial segregation, African-American players from historically black colleges and universities (HBCUs) were excluded from major All-America honors. However, *The Pittsburgh Courier* selected its own All-America team to highlight standout Black players. This article lists the consensus All-Americans and the HBCU selections for the 1945 season.

Ronald Welch (1909–1982) was a Welsh author and educator, best known for his Carey Family series of historical novels for children. A major in the Welch Regiment during World War II, he later became headmaster of Okehampton Grammar School in Devon. His final work, *The Road to Waterloo*, was published posthumously in 2018.

*The Time* is a 2017–2018 C-pop album by Chinese singer Xu Weizhou, featuring rock and EDM influences. Released in four quarters, it includes hit singles like *"Leave Me Alone"* and *"Orange Sky"*, achieving platinum sales shortly after release.
Rémire Island, or Eagle Island, is an island in Seychelles, lying in the Outer Islands of Seychelles, with a distance of 245 km south of Victoria, Seychelles.

*The Love of Hypnosis* (Chinese: 南烟斋笔录; also known as *Records of the Southern Mist House*) is a 2018 Chinese fantasy period drama starring Liu Yifei and Jing Boran. Based on a manhua of the same name, it follows a woman with a unique ability to resolve emotional conflicts while navigating her own romantic struggles. The series was filmed at Hengdian World Studios and marked Liu Yifei's return to television after 12 years.

Henry A. A. Ugboma (born 15 November 1966) is a Nigerian professor of feto-maternal medicine and the Chief Medical Director of the University of Port Harcourt Teaching Hospital (UPTH). A graduate of the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, he specialized in obstetrics and gynecology, becoming a Fellow of the West African College of Surgeons (FWACS) and the International College of Surgeons (FICS). He joined the University of Port Harcourt as a lecturer in 2003 and rose to full professorship in 2013.  

Appointed UPTH's Chief Medical Director in 2017 (and re-appointed in 2021), Ugboma modernized the hospital with new equipment and facilities. He has held leadership roles in medical associations, government committees, and academic boards. Married to radiologist Dr. Enighe Ugboma, he has five children and is active in sports, politics, and religious activities. Ugboma has also contributed to medical research with numerous publications.
Software was a German electronic duo active from 1984 to 1999, originally composed of Peter Mergener [de] and Michael Weisser [de]. The duo formerly used the name Mergener & Weisser.

Phenoxyethanol is the organic compound with the formula C6H5OC2H4OH. It is a colorless oily liquid. It can be classified as a glycol ether and a phenol ether. It is a common preservative in vaccine formulations.

Franciszek Piłsudski (1713-1791) Piłsudski coat of arms, was a Lithuanian nobleman, Colonel and Commander in the Samogitian division of the Grand Ducal Lithuanian army, Deputy cup-bearer, member of the Sejm of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth and recipient of the Order of Saint Stanislaus (1779) and the Great Cross of Divine Providence.
Live in Boston (a.k.a. Boston Live and Jumping at Shadows) is a live album by British blues-rock band Fleetwood Mac that was first released in 1985.

An offer in compromise (OIC) is a program by the U.S. Internal Revenue Service (IRS) that allows taxpayers to settle their tax debts for less than the full amount owed. To qualify, taxpayers must meet one of three conditions: doubt as to liability (disputing the tax owed), doubt as to collectibility (inability to pay the full amount), or effective tax administration (economic hardship or equity considerations). The IRS evaluates offers based on income, expenses, and asset equity. Applicants may need to submit partial payments upfront, though low-income taxpayers may qualify for exemptions. The program halts levies during processing but does not remove existing liens until full payment. The IRS warns against misleading claims by tax settlement companies.

The COVID-19 pandemic in Tunisia is part of the ongoing pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2). The disease was confirmed to have reached Tunisia on 2 March 2020.

Quick couplers (also called quick hitches) are used with construction machines to allow the rapid change of buckets and attachments on the machine. They remove the need to use hammers to manually drive out and insert the mounting pins for attachments. They also bring with them additional safety risks that must be overcome by careful design and manufacture, and proper use.

Anne Ebbs (née Sinnott born 1940 or 1941) is a retired Irish Paralympic table tennis medallist. Outside of her table tennis career, Ebbs founded the Paralympic Council of Ireland in 1987 and was the organization's secretary general until 2008. Ebbs was awarded the Paralympic Order in 2010.

HMS *Sir Isaac Brock* was an incomplete British sloop-of-war built during the War of 1812 at York (now Toronto). Intended to counter American naval forces, she was burned on the stocks during the Battle of York in April 1813 to prevent capture.
Autumn Exhibition of Leningrad artists of 1978 (Lang-ru) opened in Exhibition Halls of the Leningrad Union of Soviet Artists and became one of the largest art exhibitions of 1978 in the USSR.

Conrad Albert Lau (February 8, 1921 – April 18, 1964) was a Trinidadian-American aeronautical engineer who worked at Chance Vought and contributed to aircraft such as the F8U Crusader and A-7 Corsair II. He also co-authored an early Lunar Orbit Rendezvous study for NASA's Apollo program.

Beautiful Creatures is the debut studio album by American hard rock band Beautiful Creatures. It was produced by Sean Beavan at Bay 7 Studios in Valley Village, Los Angeles, California, and was released on August 16, 2001, through Warner Bros. Records. The album peaked at number 29 on the Billboard Top Heatseekers, while the single "Wasted" peaked at number 37 on the Mainstream Rock Tracks.

Following the release of the album, Beautiful Creatures went on to promote the album, performing at Ozzfest 2001, from June 8—August 12, 2001, the Rolling Rock Tour as well performing in Japan. The album received mixed reviews.

Philip Brandon Martin (born October 31, 1978), better known as Bishop Lamont, is an American rapper from Carson, California. He was signed to Dr. Dre's Aftermath Entertainment record label and released one project under the label, eventually leaving in 2010.

*Shonan Maru No. 7* was a Japanese whaling ship requisitioned by the Imperial Japanese Navy in 1941 as an auxiliary submarine chaser. It was sunk in 1944, though sources differ on the exact circumstances.
Stockland Bristol (formerly Stockland Gaunts) is a village and civil parish in the Sedgemoor district of Somerset, between Bridgwater and the Steart Peninsula.
The 62nd Ohio Infantry Regiment was a Union Army unit active from 1861 to 1865, fighting in battles such as Fort Wagner and the Siege of Petersburg.

Formula One 99 is a racing video game developed by Studio 33 and published by Psygnosis for the PlayStation and Microsoft Windows in 1999. It is part of the *Formula One* series and features drivers such as Michael Schumacher, Mika Häkkinen, and Jean Alesi on its cover. The game includes both single-player and multiplayer modes.  

The PlayStation version received generally favorable reviews, with critics praising its improved gameplay, sense of speed, and customization options. *Official UK PlayStation Magazine* called it a return to form for the series, while *GameSpot* and *IGN* highlighted its strong controls and replay value. In Japan, *Famitsu* rated it 28 out of 40. The game was later ported to Microsoft Windows in late 1999.

Unconscious communication refers to the transmission of information—such as thoughts, emotions, or intentions—without conscious awareness. It operates through processes within the unconscious mind, a concept popularized by Sigmund Freud, who believed it housed repressed instincts and emotions that influence behavior. Unconscious communication can occur intrapersonally (within an individual) through dreams, internal monologues, or automatic thought processes, shaping perceptions and decisions. It also manifests interpersonally (between individuals) through unintentional cues like facial expressions, body language, tone of voice, and speech patterns, which others instinctively interpret. Studies suggest that people often unconsciously mimic emotional expressions during social interactions. While Freud emphasized the role of unconscious projections, modern research explores how these automatic signals influence relationships and social dynamics.

Arbignieu (IPA-fr) is a former commune in the Ain department in the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region of eastern France. On 1 January 2016, it was merged into the new commune Arboys en Bugey.

The inhabitants of the commune are known as Arbignolans or Arbignolanes

Leigh Diffey (born 3 March 1971) is an Australian American auto racing commentator. His career began calling motorcycle races in his home country before moving to the United Kingdom to cover other forms of motorsport. Diffey then moved to the United States to join Speed Channel, while simultaneously working for Network Ten in Australia. Since 2013, he has served as a play-by-play announcer and studio host with NBC Sports in the United States.

Bristol is a city in the State of Tennessee. Located in Sullivan County, its population was 26,702 at the 2010 census. It is the twin city of Bristol, Virginia, which lies directly across the state line between Tennessee and Virginia. The boundary between the two cities is also the state line, which runs along State Street in their common downtown district. Bristol is a principal city of the Kingsport−Bristol−Bristol, TN-VA metropolitan statistical area, which is a component of the Johnson City−Kingsport−Bristol, TN-VA combined statistical area − commonly known as the "Tri-Cities" region.

Bristol is probably best known for being the site of some of the first commercial recordings of country music, showcasing Jimmie Rodgers and the Carter Family, and later a favorite venue of mountain musician Uncle Charlie Osborne. The U.S. Congress recognized Bristol as the "Birthplace of Country Music" in 1998, and the Birthplace of Country Music Museum is located in Bristol. It also is the birthplace of Tennessee Ernie Ford.

Bristol is also the site of Bristol Motor Speedway, a NASCAR short track that is one of the best-known motorsports facilities in the country.

The world's fifth-largest coal producer, Contura Energy, is based in Bristol.

Tulane University Law School is the law school of Tulane University. It is located on Tulane's Uptown campus in New Orleans, Louisiana. Established in 1847, it is the 12th oldest law school in the United States.

In addition to the usual common law and federal subjects, Tulane offers electives in the civil law, giving students the opportunity to pursue comparative education of the world's two major legal systems (Louisiana is the only state to have a civil law system, rather than common law). Students are permitted to survey a broad range of subject areas or to concentrate in one or more.

Tulane Law School's environmental law and sports law programs are considered among the strongest nationwide, and its maritime law program is among the most well-regarded in the world. For more than 20 years, the school has hosted the Tulane Corporate Law Institute, a preeminent mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and corporate law forum.

Gene DeWeese (January 31, 1934 – March 19, 2012) was an American writer best known for his science fiction, fantasy, and media tie-in novels. Under various pseudonyms, he wrote over 40 books, including works in *Star Trek*, *Lost in Space*, and *Dinotopia*, as well as young adult fiction like *The Adventures of a Two-Minute Werewolf*. He also contributed to fanzines and worked as a technical writer before becoming a full-time author.

The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (Moldavian ASSR; 1924–1940) was an autonomous republic within the Ukrainian SSR, established by the Soviet Union to promote its claim over Bessarabia and serve as a base for communist influence in Romania. Its capital was initially in Balta before moving to Tiraspol in 1929, while Chișinău (then under Romanian control) was declared its official but "temporarily occupied" capital.  

The republic had a mixed population, with Moldavians constituting less than a third of its inhabitants. Soviet authorities promoted a distinct Moldavian identity, including a Cyrillic-based Moldavian language to distance it from Romanian. The region underwent rapid industrialization, collectivization, and famine in the 1930s.  

Following the Soviet occupation of Bessarabia in 1940, the western part of the Moldavian ASSR was merged with Bessarabia to form the Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic, while the remaining territory was reabsorbed into Ukraine. The republic's legacy persists in modern debates over Moldovan identity and Transnistria's continued use of Cyrillic script.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English football coach and former professional player.

Lee played as a defender from 1988 until 2004, notably played in the Premier League for Chelsea and in the Australian top flight for Parramatta Power. He also played in the Football League for Reading, Plymouth Argyle, Portsmouth, Sheffield United, Bristol Rovers, Crystal Palace, Colchester United and Exeter City. He then went on to play in Non-league for Havant & Waterlooville, Forest Green Rovers, Weston-super-Mare, Yate Town and Mangotsfield United. He was capped ten times by England U21.

After retiring became a scout for Bristol City and later assistant manager of Northampton Town where he briefly managed in a caretaker role.

Ohio State Route 159 (SR 159) is a 30.91-mile (49.74 km) north–south state highway in Ohio, running from Chillicothe to near Lancaster. Established in 1923, it was extended in 1927 and fully paved by 1938. The route was realigned in 1971–1972 to its current termini. It passes through Ross, Pickaway, and Fairfield counties.

Matching logic is a formal logic mainly used to reason about the correctness of computer programs. Its operators use pattern matching to operate on the power set of states, rather than the set of states. It was created by Grigore Roșu and is used in the K Framework.

Hamish McLachlan (born 28 August 1975) is an Australian sports broadcaster for the Seven Network. He is best known as the host of *AFL Game Day* and as a commentator for Australian rules football matches. McLachlan has also hosted major sporting events, including the Australian Open, the Olympics, and the Commonwealth Games. Before his media career, he worked in sports management, including roles at the 2000 Olympics and the AFL Hall of Fame. His brother, Gillon McLachlan, is the CEO of the AFL.

In Hindustani music (North Indian classical music), a gharānā is a system of social organisation in the Indian subcontinent, linking musicians or dancers by lineage or apprenticeship, and more importantly by adherence to a particular musical style.

The word gharana comes from the Hindi word 'ghar' which is derived from the Sanskrit word Griha, which means 'house'. It typically refers to the place where the musical ideology originated; for example, some of the gharanas well known for singing khyals are: Dilli(Delhi), Agra, Gwalior, Indore, Atrauli-Jaipur, Kirana and Patiala. Four famous kathak gharanas are: Lucknow, Atrauli-Jaipur, Benares and Raigarh.

Blackwell Farm is a historic estate in Surrey, England, dating to the 17th century. Once owned by railway engineer William F. Faviell and later by industrialist John C. Dennis, it housed wartime evacuees and saw a 1952 Cold War-era jet crash. Now owned by the University of Surrey, its proposed redevelopment into housing has sparked local controversy.

Lead Section for *Jumping Flash! 2*  

*Jumping Flash! 2* is a 1996 first-person platformer developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation. A sequel to *Jumping Flash!* (1995), the game follows Robbit, a robotic rabbit, as he battles the villainous Captain Kabuki and Baron Aloha to save the planet Little Muu. Retaining the original’s core mechanics, players navigate 3D environments using triple jumps while shooting enemies and collecting power-ups. New features include expanded levels, underwater segments, rideable objects, and Performance Medals awarded for completing challenges.  

Critics praised its improved graphics, larger worlds, and creative additions but noted its short length and low difficulty. The game was re-released on PlayStation Network in 2009. While some reviewers considered it more of an expansion than a full sequel, it was regarded as a worthy follow-up that refined the original’s formula. *Electronic Gaming Monthly* ranked it among the "100 Best Games of All Time" in 1997.

*Fear* is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991, by Columbia Records. Produced by Gavin MacKillop, it was recorded in January 1991 at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada. The album features the singles *"All I Want"*, *"Walk on the Ocean"*, and *"Hold Her Down"*, which helped establish the band's mainstream success. Two outtakes from the sessions, *"Good Intentions"* and *"All in All"*, were later included on the rarities compilation *In Light Syrup* (1995). *Fear* marked the beginning of the band's collaboration with MacKillop, who also produced their subsequent albums.

The Cóndores UNAM (English: UNAM Condors), was one of the three American football teams that represented the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM) from 1970 to 1997. The Cóndores competed in the ONEFA and won 10 National Championships, including four in a row from 1983 to 1986.
Nothing's Real is the debut studio album by English singer Shura, released on 8 July 2016 by Polydor Records. The album was met with positive reviews from music critics.

The Yale Glee Club is a mixed-voice choir of Yale University, founded in 1861. Comprising 70–90 undergraduate and graduate students, it performs a diverse repertoire ranging from classical works to contemporary compositions. Originally an all-male ensemble, it became coeducational in 1970 following Yale’s admission of women. The group is led by the Marshall Bartholomew Professor of Choral Conducting, currently Jeffrey Douma, and has produced notable alumni, including composers Charles Ives and Cole Porter. The Glee Club tours annually, fosters new music through competitions, and collaborates with local high schools. It maintains traditions such as the "Blue Book" of Yale songs while expanding its classical and modern choral works.

The BB 1800 were motor luggage vans of SNCF. These were the so-called "first-series" Z 5000 locomotives ordered by the Chemins de fer de l'Ouest. They were put into service, with running numbers Z 5001 to Z 5010, on 12 April 1900, on the line from Paris-Invalides to Issy-Plaine, which was extended to Meudon on 1 July 1901 then to Versailles RG on 31 May 1902.

Bishop Lamont (born Philip Brandon Martin; October 31, 1978) is an American rapper and songwriter from Inglewood, California. Signed to Dr. Dre's Aftermath Entertainment in 2005, he gained recognition for his mixtapes and appearances on soundtracks for video games and films.

Ronald Welch (14 December 1909 – 5 February 1982) was the pseudonym of Welsh writer Ronald Oliver Felton TD, who wrote in English. He is best known for children's historical fiction. He won the 1956 Carnegie Medal from the Library Association for the year's best children's book by a British author, for Knight Crusader, the first in his so-called Carey Family series of novels.

The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was the 22nd season of Big 12 Conference football, taking place during the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The season began with non-conference play on Saturday, September 2, 2017, when all 10 of the league's teams played their first non-conference opponents. Big 12 Conference play began on Saturday, September 23, 2017.

The 2017 season was the sixth for the Big 12 since the 2010–13 Big 12 Conference realignment brought the Big 12 membership to its current form. The conference had ten members: Baylor, Iowa State, Kansas, Kansas State, Oklahoma, Oklahoma State, TCU, Texas, Texas Tech and West Virginia. The Big 12 is a Power Five conference under the College Football Playoff format, along with the ACC, the Big Ten, the Pac-12 and the SEC.

As a ten-team league, the Big 12 will played a nine-game, round-robin conference schedule. Each member will played three non-conference games–one of was required to be against another Power Five conference foe. The regular season was followed by a conference championship game played between the regular season champion and regular season runner-up. The 2017 Big 12 Championship Game was held Saturday, December 2, 2017, at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.

The Holy Science is a 1894 book by Sri Yukteswar Giri, comparing Hindu scriptures with the Christian Bible, particularly the Book of Revelation. It presents an alternative Yuga Cycle theory, proposing that Earth is in Dwapara Yuga (not Kali Yuga) due to the sun's binary motion. The book divides knowledge into four stages, aligning with ascending spiritual ages.

The mercy rule, also known as the run rule, slaughter rule, or continuous clock rule, is a regulation in sports that ends a game early if one team has an insurmountable lead over the other. The rule varies by sport and level of competition but is commonly used to prevent lopsided contests from continuing unnecessarily.  

In American football, many high school leagues implement a continuous clock when a team leads by a certain margin (often 35+ points), stopping only for scores or timeouts. Some states allow games to end early if the deficit is extreme. College football permits shortening quarters by mutual agreement but does not allow a running clock.  

In baseball and softball, games may end early if a team leads by 10+ runs after a set number of innings (e.g., 7+ innings in international play, 5+ in high school). Basketball often uses a continuous clock in high school games with large leads (e.g., 30+ points).  

Other sports with mercy rules include soccer (ending at a 10-goal lead), boxing (stopping if a fighter trails by 20+ points), wrestling (ending on a 15-point lead), and goalball (ending at a 10-goal difference). The rule ensures sportsmanship and efficient game management.

Zheng Zhi (Chinese: 郑智; pinyin: Zhèng Zhì; born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager who played most of his career for Chinese Super League club Guangzhou F.C., becoming their captain and serving also as their caretaker manager in two stints. After starting his career as a defender, Zheng was later moved into a central midfield role by then head coach Zhu Guanghu at Shenzhen Jianlibao and experienced immediate success there by winning the 2004 league title with the club. A move to Shandong Luneng Taishan saw a prolific goal scoring period in his career and he soon became the captain of the Chinese national team, which then led to moves to Charlton Athletic and Celtic. He moved back to China in 2010 and joined Guangzhou Evergrande, making over 300 appearances so far as captain while helping the club win all major trophies a Chinese club could compete for, including Chinese Super League for a record 8 times and AFC Champions League twice.
Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is a British pop singer and television presenter. At the age of 17, she was briefly a member of the girl group that would go on to become the Spice Girls.

Acoustic torpedo  

An acoustic torpedo is a self-guided underwater missile that uses sonar to detect and home in on a target, typically a ship or submarine. These torpedoes employ passive sonar to detect noise from the target (such as engine or propeller sounds) or active sonar to reflect pulses off the target. Early models had fixed sensor patterns, while modern versions use computer-controlled systems for greater precision.  

First deployed in World War II, the German G7es (T5) torpedo proved highly effective against Allied ships, forcing the development of countermeasures like the Foxer noisemaker, which created distracting cavitation noise to lure torpedoes away. Allied tactics, such as the "Step-Aside" maneuver, also reduced their effectiveness. Postwar advancements led to improved decoy systems like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie and AN/SLQ-61 Lightweight Tow.  

Captured German technology, including from U-505 and U-250, provided valuable insights into acoustic torpedo design. Despite initial setbacks, countermeasures significantly reduced their wartime success rate, with only about 77 out of 700 German torpedoes hitting their targets. Today, acoustic torpedoes remain a key component of naval warfare, with ongoing improvements in guidance and evasion tactics.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English cardinal and Archbishop of Canterbury (1207–1228). A key figure in the dispute between King John and Pope Innocent III, he was exiled until John's submission in 1213. Langton later helped draft Magna Carta (1215) and promoted church reforms. A scholar and theologian, he is traditionally credited with dividing the Bible into modern chapters. He died in Sussex and was buried at Canterbury Cathedral.

The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is the point over which the aircraft would balance. Its position is calculated after supporting the aircraft on at least two sets of weighing scales or load cells and noting the weight shown on each set of scales or load cells. The center of gravity affects the stability of the aircraft. To ensure the aircraft is safe to fly, the center of gravity must fall within specified limits established by the aircraft manufacturer.

William Methwold (sometimes spelled Methold), (baptised 1590 in South Pickenham, near Swaffham, Norfolk; died 5 March 1653, Kensington), was an English merchant and colonial administrator in India. He is credited with identifying the site of Bombay as a strategic port.

A fictional character in the novel Midnight's Children by author Salman Rushdie bears the same name and is described as a direct descendant. (See List of Midnight's Children characters). He himself appears as a character in 1637: The Peacock Throne by Eric Flint and Griffin Barber.

Rifle bedding is a process of providing a rigid and consistent foundation for a rifle’s operational components, by creating a stable and close-fitting bearing surface between the gun’s functional parts (i.e. the receiver housing the barrelled action) and its support part (i.e. the stock) that do not deform with pressure or shift under the shear stress of the recoil from firing. This is for the goal of accurizing the rifle and (to a lesser extent) prolonging the life of the stock. The bedding process is often an aftermarket improvement.

Paul Luker (born 8 July 1951) is a retired British Royal Air Force officer who served as Commander of Joint Helicopter Command and later as Deputy Commanding General of the US-led coalition in Afghanistan.

The Silent Sejm (Polish: *Sejm Niemy*) was a session of the Polish-Lithuanian parliament (*Sejm*) held on 1 February 1717 in Warsaw. Under pressure from Tsar Peter the Great of Russia, the assembly imposed strict limits on the power of King Augustus II and the Polish nobility (*szlachta*). The Sejm, lasting only six hours, banned the king from maintaining a standing army and restricted political reforms. It marked the beginning of Russian dominance over the Commonwealth, weakening its sovereignty and setting the stage for later partitions.

Asia Aluminum is the largest aluminum extrusion group in Asia, with an annual designed capacity of 350,000 metric tons. It employed, directly and indirectly, in excess of 10,000 people, predominantly in the Asia Aluminum Industrial City which housed its primary manufacturing facilities, located in the Zhaoqing region of the People's Republic of China. Asia Aluminum made its debut on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in 1998.

Rifle bedding is a process used to improve firearm accuracy and stock durability by creating a precise fit between the rifle's action and its stock. Common methods include glass bedding, which fills gaps with epoxy, and pillar bedding, which uses metal cylinders to stabilize the action. Proper bedding reduces stress on the action, prevents shifting during recoil, and can extend the stock's lifespan. Techniques vary, including free-floating barrels and pressure pads to optimize barrel harmonics. However, improper bedding can permanently damage the rifle if the action becomes locked to the stock or epoxy seeps into critical components.

*Soul Power* is the debut studio album by American musician Curtis Harding, released on May 6, 2014, by Burger Records. Blending soul, disco, indie rock, and gospel influences, the album features Harding's versatile vocals and genre-spanning songwriting. It received praise for its retro-inspired sound and cohesive production.

The Gao Empire (c. 9th century–1430) was a West African kingdom centered in Gao, located along the Niger River in present-day Mali. Emerging as a regional power by the 9th century, Gao became a key trade and manufacturing hub, particularly known for its carnelian bead production. Initially influenced by African traditional religion, the empire gradually adopted Islam, with its rulers converting by the late 11th century, possibly under Almoravid influence.  

Gao consisted of two main settlements: Gao Ancien and Gao-Saney, the latter being an important archaeological site. The empire's history is primarily documented through Arabic geographers and later chronicles like the *Tarikh al-Sudan* and *Tarikh al-Fattash*. By the late 13th century, Gao was absorbed into the Mali Empire, though it later regained independence under the Sunni dynasty in the 15th century, leading to the rise of the Songhai Empire. The Tomb of Askia, built in 1495, remains a testament to Gao's significance under Songhai rule.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (1874–1965) was an American biblical scholar and theologian, best known for developing the *Concordant Literal New Testament* and promoting a unique dispensationalist theology. A former member of the Plymouth Brethren, he rejected traditional doctrines like eternal hell and the Trinity, advocating instead for a systematic, word-based approach to Scripture. He founded the *Concordant Publishing Concern* and the journal *Unsearchable Riches*.

The Slutsk uprising (November 27 – December 31, 1920) was an armed rebellion in Belarus against Soviet rule during the Russian Civil War. Led by local Belarusian nationalists, the uprising sought to defend the independence of the Belarusian Democratic Republic following the Soviet-Polish War. Despite initial successes, the poorly equipped Belarusian forces were defeated by the Red Army. The event remains a symbol of resistance in Belarusian nationalist memory, though it is not officially recognized by the Belarusian government.
Charles Parker (1809–1898) was a New Zealand politician who represented Motueka in Parliament and the Nelson Provincial Council.

Soul Power is the debut studio album by American singer Curtis Harding, released May 6, 2014, by Burger Records. It encompasses a wide breadth of genres, though it is primarily inspired by soul music and his mother's gospel singing. The album received generally favorable reviews from music critics.

KNBN (channel 21) is a television station in Rapid City, South Dakota, United States, affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Owned by Jim Simpson's Rapid Broadcasting, the station maintains studios on South Plaza Drive in Rapid City, and its transmitter is located on Cowboy Hill west of downtown.

KNBN can also be seen on low-power digital translators KWBH-LD (channel 27) in Rapid City; K22AD-D (channel 22) in Gillette, Wyoming; and K35MW-D (channel 35) in Lead, South Dakota (also serving the Black Hills region).

The Northampton Red Sox were a minor league baseball team based in Cape Charles, Virginia, which lies within Northampton County, Virginia, in 1927 and 1928. The Northampton teams played as exclusively as members of the Class D level Eastern Shore League.

As of the 2010 U.S. Census there were 11,813 ethnic Koreans in Harris County, Texas, in the Houston area, making up 4.2% of the county's Asian population. In 2015 Haejin E. Koh, author of "Korean Americans in Houston: Building Bridges across Cultures and Generations," wrote in regards to the census figure that "community leaders believe the number is twice as large."

NBA Street Vol. 2 is a 2003 arcade-style basketball video game developed by NuFX and EA Canada, and published by EA Sports BIG. A sequel to *NBA Street*, it features 3-on-3 street basketball with NBA teams and legends, including three versions of Michael Jordan. Players compete in modes such as *Be a Legend*, *NBA Challenge*, and *Pick Up Game*, using trick moves and "Gamebreaker" abilities to gain advantages. The game includes a hip-hop soundtrack with artists like Nelly and Nate Dogg. It received critical acclaim, with praise for its gameplay, presentation, and multiplayer experience.

"The Crocodile" is the fourth episode of the second season of *Once Upon a Time*. It explores the backstory of Rumplestiltskin (Robert Carlyle) and his feud with Captain Hook (Colin O'Donoghue), revealing how Hook lost his hand and earned his name. In Storybrooke, Belle (Emilie de Ravin) confronts Mr. Gold about his secrets while reopening the town library. Meanwhile, in the Enchanted Forest, Hook allies with Cora (Barbara Hershey) to reach Storybrooke. The episode received positive reviews for its action and character development.
Microsoft DNS is the name given to the implementation of domain name system services provided in Microsoft Windows operating systems.

Kris Fisher is a fictional character from the British soap opera *Hollyoaks*, portrayed by Gerard McCarthy from 2006 to 2010. Introduced as a flamboyant and rebellious student, Kris became known for his bold fashion, sharp wit, and involvement in key storylines, including a groundbreaking HIV awareness plot and a bisexual love triangle. His complex relationship with his brother, Malachy (Glen Wallace), and his unapologetic personality made him a fan favorite. McCarthy's portrayal earned praise, with Kris becoming one of the show's most distinctive characters before his departure in 2010.

The Cello Concerto is a three-movement orchestral work composed by Esa-Pekka Salonen in 2017. Scored for solo cello and large orchestra with live electronics, the concerto explores cosmic imagery, contrasting lyrical passages with rhythmic intensity. Salonen described its structure as evolving from "chaos to line," with influences from his earlier solo cello work *knock, breathe, shine*. Premiered by Yo-Yo Ma and the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, the concerto received praise for its inventive orchestration and technical demands.

The 2000 Save Mart/Kragen 350 was the 16th race of the 2000 NASCAR Winston Cup Series, held on June 25, 2000, at Sears Point Raceway in Sonoma, California. The 112-lap race was contested on the 1.99-mile (3.20 km) modified road course, featuring the Chute bypass instead of the full layout. Rusty Wallace won the pole position, while Jeff Gordon led the most laps (43) and secured the victory. The race was broadcast on ESPN, with commentary by Bob Jenkins, Ned Jarrett, and Benny Parsons. Three drivers—Rick Mast, Geoff Bodine, and R. K. Smith—failed to qualify for the event.

Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is an English-language novella by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay. Set in rural Bengal, it follows Matangini, a young woman trapped in an abusive marriage to Rajmohan, who becomes entangled in a plot involving theft, betrayal, and forbidden love. The story explores themes of female agency, social constraints, and moral dilemmas in 19th-century India. It is considered one of the earliest Indian novels written in English.

Hugh Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician and newspaper editor. A member of the Country Party, he served in the Western Australian Legislative Assembly (1943–1949) and later represented Moore in the Australian House of Representatives (1949–1958, 1961–1963). He was awarded an OBE in 1967.

Trapping the queen is a tactical motif which occurs in both amateur and master games. The tactic is similar to a mating net, whose target is the defender's king, rather than his queen. When the opponent's queen is successfully trapped, it usually results in his immediate resignation.
Eddy L. Harris (born January 26, 1956) is an American writer of creative nonfiction and a filmmaker.

Gretta Kehoe-Quigley (Irish: *Mairéad Uí Choigligh*) is a former camogie player from Wexford, Ireland. A centre back, she won a Leinster senior championship medal in 1974 and an All-Ireland title in 1975 with her sisters.
Ciudad del Plata is a city in San José Department, Uruguay, part of Montevideo's metropolitan area.

2011 MD is a small near-Earth asteroid discovered on June 22, 2011, by the LINEAR survey. Measuring approximately 6 meters (20 ft) in diameter, it is a porous rubble pile with a density similar to water. The asteroid made a close approach to Earth in 2011, altering its orbit. It was later considered a candidate for NASA's Asteroid Redirect Mission.

The Sabre Wildcat is a two-seat, weight-shift control ultralight aircraft with a cable-braced high wing and pusher engine. Designed for affordability, it features an open cockpit, tricycle landing gear, and a Dacron-covered wing.

Longwood House is a historic residence on Saint Helena where Napoleon lived in exile from 1815 until his death in 1821. Originally a farmhouse, it was later transferred to France in 1858. Now a museum, it is maintained by the French government as a memorial to Napoleon.

Franciszek Piłsudski (1713–1791) was a Polish-Lithuanian nobleman, military officer, and politician. A colonel in the Samogitian division of the Grand Ducal Lithuanian Army, he served as *podczaszy* (deputy cup-bearer) and was a member of the Sejm. He expanded his family's estates, including Pajūralis and Šilalė, and was awarded the Order of Saint Stanislaus.

The hôtel de la Marine (also known as the hôtel du Garde-Meuble) is an historic building located on place de la Concorde in Paris, to the east of rue Royale. It was designed and built between 1757 and 1774 by the architect Ange-Jacques Gabriel, on the newly created square first called Place Louis XV. The identical building to its west, constructed at the same time, now houses the hôtel de Crillon and the Automobile Club of France.

The Hôtel de la Marine was originally the home of the royal Garde-Mobile, the office managing the furnishing of all royal properties. Following the French Revolution it became the Ministry of the French Navy, which occupied it until 2015. It was entirely renovated between 2015 and 2021. It now displays the restored 18th century apartments of Marc-Antoine Thierry de Ville-d'Avray, the King's Intendant of the Garde-Meuble, as well the salons and chambers later used by the French Navy. A separate part displays the Al Thani Collection presenting international and inter-cultural works of art from the collection of Sheik Hamad bin Abdullah Al Thani.

The New Philharmonic Society was a London-based concert society active from 1852 to 1879. Founded as an alternative to the Philharmonic Society, it featured prominent conductors like Hector Berlioz and performed major orchestral works. Its concerts were later succeeded by Wilhelm Ganz's orchestral series.

Société Belge des Transports par Air SA, known by its short form Sobelair, was a Belgian charter airline from that operated from 1946 to 2004. It was headquartered in Brussels (later in Zaventem) and operated mostly non-scheduled passenger and cargo flights out of Brussels Airport.

*Mélite* is a 1629 comedy by Pierre Corneille, reportedly inspired by real events. The play follows Éraste, who, jealous that his beloved Mélite loves his friend Tircis, forges letters to deceive them. The scheme causes heartbreak until Éraste repents, confesses, and reconciles with the couple, later marrying Tircis' sister. The plot hinges on misunderstandings and false letters, a common theme in early French comedy.

Mansfield is a borough located in east-central Tioga County, Pennsylvania, United States, in the Tioga River valley. It is situated at the intersection of U.S. Route 6 and U.S. Business Route 15, about 36 miles (58 km) southwest of Elmira, New York.
Doon Valley is a ward in East Ayrshire, Scotland, covering the southernmost part of the council area, including Dalmellington, Patna, and Drongan. Established in 2007, it elects three councillors.

The Ladies of the House is a 2014 American horror film directed by John Stuart Wildman. The film follows three men who, after a violent altercation with a stripper, are hunted by her cannibalistic roommates. Starring Farah White, Melodie Sisk, Brina Palencia, and Belladonna, it premiered at the Dallas International Film Festival. Critics praised its blend of grindhouse aesthetics with dark humor, with positive reviews from *Dread Central* and *Bloody Disgusting*.

HMS Sir Isaac Brock was a warship which was destroyed before being completed at York, Upper Canada during the War of 1812. The ship was named after the famed hero of the war, Major General Sir Isaac Brock.
"Space Under Sun" (2004) is the second studio album by American R&B singer Ari Gold, blending pop, dance-pop, and soul.

The 1952 Mysore State Legislative Assembly election was held on 26 March 1952 to elect all 99 members of the legislature. The Indian National Congress (INC) won a majority, and K. Hanumanthaiah became Chief Minister. Voter turnout was 50.38%.

Marcus Bontempelli (born 24 November 1995) is a professional Australian rules footballer playing for the Western Bulldogs in the Australian Football League (AFL). He has served as Western Bulldogs captain since the 2020 season, and was previously the vice-captain from 2018 to 2019.

Bontempelli was drafted with the fourth selection in the 2013 AFL draft. He made his AFL debut in the 2014 AFL season. He received a nomination for the 2014 AFL Rising Star award in round 13 of the 2014 season, placing second in the overall count at the conclusion of the season. Since then, he has been an AFL Premiership player, three-time All-Australian, three-time Charles Sutton Medallist, and a recipient of the AFLCA Champion Player of the Year Award. He has polled a total of 97 career Brownlow Medal votes.

John William Gleeson (14 March 1938 – 7 October 2016) was an Australian cricketer who played in 29 Test matches from 1967 to 1972. He is best known for his unique bowling style, which according to Cricket Australia CEO James Sutherland "bamboozled batsmen" and could "regularly dumbfound the best batsmen in any team".

Charles Percy Abbott (born 10 June 1939) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Hawthorn in the Victorian Football League (VFL), a polo player, and the recipient of the Australian Sports Medal.

Professor Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan academic surgeon, and a consultant in GastroIntestinal Surgery. Currently, he is the chairman and senior professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya Medical School, Sri Lanka. He is a founder professor of the Department of Surgery and previously, he held the position of the head of Department of Surgery from 1998 to 2003. His academic degrees are MBBS (Peradeniya); MD (Birmingham); MS (Colombo); FRCS (Glasgow). In 2014, he was elected as the president elect for The College of Surgeons of Sri Lanka.
Giorgio Zur (1930–2019) was a German archbishop and Vatican diplomat who served as Apostolic Nuncio to several countries, including Russia and Austria.

Helmut Rahn (16 August 1929 – 14 August 2003) was a German footballer who played as a wing forward. Nicknamed *"Der Boss"*, he was a key player in West Germany's 1954 FIFA World Cup victory, scoring the winning goal in the final against Hungary—known as *The Miracle of Bern*. He spent most of his club career at Rot-Weiss Essen, winning the 1955 German championship. Rahn later became a car dealer in Essen, where a statue honors his legacy.

Randy Kaplan (b. Randall Leigh Kaplan) is an American songwriter, playwright, poet, and performer. He gained renown for his “not-JUST-for-kids” music in the mid-aughts, first in Brooklyn, New York, and later nationwide. Dedicated members of his fan base call themselves FORKs (Fans of Randy Kaplan). He was signed with myKaZoo Music for his CD dedicated to Country Blues and Ragtime music (Mr. Diddie Wah Diddie), which Universal Music released.

Randy has released six not-JUST-for-kids CDs (with his seventh, Shake It and Break It, released on May 10, 2019). Previous "kids" records include Five Cent Piece (2006), Loquat Rooftop (2008), The Kids Are All Id (2010), Mr. Diddie Wah Diddie (2012), Jam on Rye (2014), and Trippin'Round the Mitten (2017). Previous not-REALLY-for-kids records include Boyish Hips (1997), Reborn As Bees (1999), Lake Champions (1999), Miraculous Dissolving Cures (2001), Perfect Gentleman (2004), Ancient Ruins (2008), Durango (2008), and Songs For Old Lovers (2011). Randy has also released several digital singles, including "Don't Fill Up On Chips,""Nagasaki,""On the Phone on the Toilet,"and"Hugs for My Family, High Fives for My Friends."

Randy's songs have been included on many compilations, including those for Putumayo World Music, Fast Folk, and Park Slope Parents.

CNN Pipeline was an English language video news service providing both live and on-demand video to subscribers' computers via broadband Internet connections. It was part of the CNN group of news services. The service was subscription-based, and did not contain advertising like other CNN stations. The bandwidth, storage and streaming servers was provided by AOL, which is also owned by Time Warner. Each of the feeds broadcast in the 16:9 aspect ratio, however the resolution of the broadcast prohibited it from being considered a high definition channel, and in many cases not even SDTV.

Grange Barn, Coggeshall is a 13th-century timber-framed barn in Essex, England, and one of the oldest surviving in Europe. Originally part of Coggeshall Abbey, it remained in agricultural use until 1960. After falling into disrepair, it was restored in the 1980s and awarded a Europa Nostra prize. Now a Grade I listed building, it is managed by the National Trust and open to the public, featuring historical displays and event spaces.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21) was a Roman aristocrat and governor of Syria (AD 6–12). He conducted a census in Judea, sparking a revolt by Judas of Galilee. Earlier, he served as consul (12 BC) and led military campaigns in Galatia and Cilicia. Quirinius is mentioned in the Gospel of Luke in connection with Jesus' birth, though this conflicts with other historical accounts.

Robert Prosky (December 13, 1930 – December 8, 2008) was an American actor known for his roles in film, television, and theater. A prolific character actor, he appeared in movies such as *Christine*, *The Natural*, *Mrs. Doubtfire*, and *Dead Man Walking*, and was a regular on TV shows like *Hill Street Blues* and *Veronica's Closet*. On Broadway, he earned acclaim for his performance in *Glengarry Glen Ross*. A longtime fixture at Washington, D.C.'s Arena Stage, he performed in over 100 productions there. He was nominated for two Tony Awards and won multiple theater honors.
Matheus Ribeiro (born 23 October 1993) is a Brazilian footballer who plays as a right back for Vasco da Gama.

Methionine aminopeptidase 2 is an enzyme that in humans is encoded by the METAP2 gene.

Methionine aminopeptidase 2, a member of the dimetallohydrolase family, is a cytosolic metalloenzyme that catalyzes the hydrolytic removal of N-terminal methionine residues from nascent proteins.

MetAP2 is found in all organisms and is especially important because of its critical role in tissue repair and protein degradation. Furthermore, MetAP2 is of particular interest because the enzyme plays a key role in angiogenesis, the growth of new blood vessels, which is necessary for the progression of diseases including solid tumor cancers and rheumatoid arthritis. MetAP2 is also the target of two groups of anti-angiogenic natural products, ovalicin and fumagillin, and their analogs such as beloranib.

The 1950 World Snooker Championship was a professional snooker tournament held from 12 December 1949 to 18 March 1950 in Blackpool, England. The final took place at the Tower Circus, where Walter Donaldson of Scotland defeated England's Fred Davis 51–46 to claim the title. The qualifying rounds were held in Battersea, London, with Kingsley Kennerley emerging as the top qualifier. The event was organized by the Billiards Association and Control Council.
Mohammed Ilyas Cherchar (born January 18, 1992) is an Algerian football player who plays for RC Arbaâ in the Algerian Ligue Professionnelle 1.
Duško Tarbuk (Serbian Cyrillic: Душко Тарбук; born 21 August 1972) is a politician in Serbia. He has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2016 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.

The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident occurred in Okinawa, Japan, during the U.S. occupation after the Battle of Okinawa. Three African-American Marines, accused of repeatedly raping village women, were ambushed and killed by villagers with the help of Japanese soldiers. Their bodies were hidden in a cave and remained undiscovered until 1997. The case was not criminally investigated, and the incident has been cited by critics of the U.S. military presence in Okinawa as an example of postwar tensions.

Scott Alan Mendes (born July 4, 1969) is an American former professional bull rider and 1997 PRCA World Champion. A co-founder of the Professional Bull Riders (PBR), he competed in multiple world finals across organizations. After retiring, he became a Christian minister, founding Western Harvest Ministries and working in Christian media, including film production and motivational speaking. He also created the mobile game *Rodeo Judge*.
Israel Freedman (1878–1934) was a Romanian-born Yiddish journalist, playwright, and socialist activist in New York.

Anna Magdalena Appel, (later known as Freifrau Anna Magdalena von Hochstädten), (Darmstadt, 8 March 1846-Wiesbaden, 19 December 1917) was a German ballet dancer, morganatic wife to Louis III, Grand Duke of Hesse.

Satellite in the Sky (1956) is a British science fiction film directed by Paul Dickson. It follows the crew of the *Stardust*, the first manned spaceship, who discover their mission secretly involves testing a nuclear bomb in space. When a stowaway journalist (Lois Maxwell) complicates matters, the crew must defuse the bomb after it magnetically attaches to their ship. The film, notable for its early use of CinemaScope and WarnerColor, received mixed reviews for its simplistic portrayal of space travel.

On July 9, 2014, a mass shooting occurred in a home located in northern Harris County, Texas, near the Spring census-designated place, a suburban area of the Greater Houston area, leaving six family members dead, four of them children, and a lone survivor. 34-year old Ronald Lee Haskell, was apprehended after a standoff that lasted several hours. Haskell was related to the victims via his ex-wife.

Mothers against decapentaplegic homolog 7 or SMAD7 is a protein that in humans is encoded by the SMAD7 gene.

SMAD7 is a protein that, as its name describes, is a homolog of the Drosophila gene: "Mothers against decapentaplegic". It belongs to the SMAD family of proteins, which belong to the TGFβ superfamily of ligands. Like many other TGFβ family members, SMAD7 is involved in cell signalling. It is a TGFβ type 1 receptor antagonist. It blocks TGFβ1 and activin associating with the receptor, blocking access to SMAD2. It is an inhibitory SMAD (I-SMAD) and is enhanced by SMURF2.

Smad7 enhances muscle differentiation.

Frederik Wilhelm Stabell (25 May 1763 – 2 June 1836) was a Norwegian military officer and politician. He distinguished himself at the Battle of Trangen and served in the Norwegian Constituent Assembly in 1814. He rose to the rank of general and was awarded the Order of the Dannebrog.

Barbil is a city in the Keonjhar district of Odisha, India, known for its iron and manganese ore industries. Located at an elevation of 477 meters (1,565 ft), it is surrounded by hills and has a population of around 66,540 (2011 census). The city serves as a major industrial hub with steel plants, pellet factories, and mines. Barbil is well-connected by rail and road, with links to cities like Bhubaneswar, Kolkata, and Rourkela. Odia is the official language, though tribal languages like Ho and Santali are also spoken. The region faces environmental challenges due to mining-related dust pollution.

Banjara Hills is an upscale neighborhood in Hyderabad, Telangana, India, known for its luxury hotels, shopping malls, and fine dining. Developed in the early 20th century by Nawab Mehdi Nawaz Jung, it was named after the Banjara community, the area's original inhabitants. The neighborhood features prominent landmarks such as the Taj Krishna hotel, GVK One mall, and Jalagam Vengal Rao Park. It is also home to educational institutions like Muffakham Jah College of Engineering and Technology and cultural spaces such as Lamakaan. Banjara Hills is well-connected by road and public transport, including TSRTC buses and the nearby Khairtabad MMTS station. Adjacent localities include Jubilee Hills, Panjagutta, and Somajiguda.

Dale Van Sickel (November 29, 1907 – January 25, 1977) was an American football player and Hollywood stuntman. A two-time All-American end at the University of Florida (1928–1929), he was the school's first College Football Hall of Fame inductee (1975). After coaching briefly at Florida, he moved to Hollywood, performing stunts in over 280 films and TV shows, including *The Searchers* and *Spartacus*. He co-founded the Stuntmen's Association of Motion Pictures.

400th Missile Squadron  

The 400th Missile Squadron is a former United States Air Force unit that served as both a bomber squadron in World War II and later as an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) squadron. Activated in 1942 as the 400th Bombardment Squadron, it flew B-24 Liberators in the Southwest Pacific Theater, earning three Distinguished Unit Citations for actions in Papua, New Guinea, and Wewak. After the war, it was inactivated in 1946.  

Reactivated in 1964 as part of the 90th Strategic Missile Wing, the squadron operated LGM-30 Minuteman I and III missiles before transitioning to the LGM-118 Peacekeeper in 1986—the only USAF squadron to deploy the Peacekeeper operationally. It was awarded the General Samuel C. Phillips Award in 1999 as the top missile squadron in Air Force Space Command. The squadron was inactivated in 2005 following the retirement of the Peacekeeper under the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START).

Severn FM is an internet radio station based in Gloucester, UK, owned and operated by Ringtone.net Ltd since 2011. Broadcasting 24/7, it features a mix of talk, music, and sports programming, including shows like *The Severn Bore Breakfast Show*, *The Encyclopedia of Rock*, and live coverage of Gloucester City AFC matches. Originally licensed to South Gloucester CIC (2011–2013), the station moved to Hardwicke, Gloucestershire, after the CIC's liquidation. Severn FM briefly transmitted via FM during a 2011 restricted service license (RSL), reaching areas beyond Gloucester. It streams globally online at two bitrates (128Kbps and 64Kbps) and has expanded its reach, with some shows simulcast on Spanish FM stations.

B.A. Van Sise is an American photographer known for his exhibitions *Children of Grass* (2016), *Eyewitness* (2017), and *One Second* (2019–2020). His work has been featured at the Smithsonian National Portrait Gallery and the Museum of Jewish Heritage. He is also the author of the award-winning poetry book *Children of Grass* (2019).
Richard Jata (born September 21, 1985) is an American former professional soccer midfielder.

Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (born 8 September 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter, best known for hosting the talk show *De Wereld Draait Door* (2005–2020). He has also presented *Holland Sport* and co-created the *Popquiz*.
Michael N. Delagi (April 22, 1879 – October 7, 1957) was an American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.

WiQuest Communications was an American computer hardware company founded in 2003 in Allen, Texas. It specialized in Wireless USB technology, holding an 85% market share in 2008 before ceasing operations on October 31, 2008, due to financial and technical challenges. Its Bangalore division was acquired by Staccato Communications.
Doug Chapman is a stunt coordinator, stunt performer and actor, a member of Stunts Canada, who works in film and television.

William Husel (born November 11, 1975) is an American former physician who was acquitted in 2022 of murder charges related to prescribing high doses of fentanyl to critically ill patients. A graduate of Ohio University College of Osteopathic Medicine, he worked as an intensive-care doctor at Mount Carmel West Hospital in Ohio. From 2015 to 2018, he was accused of hastening the deaths of 34 patients with excessive fentanyl doses, leading to his dismissal and a high-profile trial. A jury found him not guilty on all counts. His medical license was later revoked.

Guylian is a Belgian chocolate company founded in 1958 in Sint-Niklaas by Guy Foubert, who named the brand after himself and his wife, Lilian. Known for its signature seashell- and seahorse-shaped pralines, Guylian uses a traditional hazelnut praline recipe and sustainably sourced cocoa.
