The company expanded internationally, opening chocolate cafés in cities such as Antwerp, Sydney, and Dubai. In 2008, it was acquired by South Korea’s Lotte Confectionery for €105 million.  

Guylian has received numerous awards, including the Superior Taste Award and Belgium’s highest export honor. It holds a Guinness World Record for the largest chocolate Easter egg sculpture (2005). The company is also recognized for its sustainability efforts, including partnerships with Project Seahorse for marine conservation and the Cocoa Horizons Foundation to support ethical cocoa farming. Guylian continues production in Belgium, maintaining its reputation as a premium chocolate brand.

Samira Ćosović (Serbian Cyrillic: Самира Ћосовић) is a Serbian politician and teacher. A member of the Sandžak Democratic Party (SDPS), she has served in the Prijepolje municipal assembly since 2008 and was elected to the National Assembly in 2020. She later became deputy mayor of Prijepolje in 2021, resigning from parliament. Previously, she worked as a Serbian language teacher in Brodarevo.

Charlotte Deane (born 6 May 1975) is a British structural biologist and bioinformatician, known for her work on protein structure prediction, particularly antibodies. She is a professor at the University of Oxford and former head of its Department of Statistics.

The Autumn Exhibition of Leningrad Artists (1978) was a major Soviet art exhibition featuring nearly 1,100 works by over 650 artists, including prominent figures of the Leningrad School. Held at the Leningrad Union of Soviet Artists, it showcased paintings, sculptures, and graphic art.
Dominic Michaelis (1938 – 22 December 2015) was an Anglo-French architect, inventor, and solar energy advocate.

Oscar Braynon, II (born February 1, 1977) is an American Democratic politician from Miami Gardens, Florida. He served in the Florida House of Representatives from 2008 to 2011 and then in the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing parts of southern Broward and northern Miami-Dade County.
Christina Elliot is a retired British Air Vice-Marshal who served in the Royal Air Force from 1985 to 2020.

The Button car plan, also known as the Button plan, was the informal name given to the Motor Industry Development Plan, an Australian federal (Labor) government initiative intended to rationalise the Australian motor vehicle industry and transition it to lower levels of protection. It took its name from Senator John Button, the then federal Minister for Commerce, Trade and Industry.
Breandán Ó hEithir (1930–1990) was an Irish writer, journalist, and broadcaster known for his works in both Irish and English.

Nomads R.F.C. are a Hong Kong rugby union club, who play in G4S League Divisions 2,5 and 7 and are based in the Tai Hang Tung area in New Kowloon. Between 2008/09 the club has won over 6 different league and 7's trophies.

Duško Knežević (Montenegrin Cyrillic: Душко Кнежевић) is the founder and president of the board of directors of Atlas Group Corporation (AGC), a financial group based in Montenegro consisting of companies with interests in banks in Serbia, Cyprus, Russia and Great Britain.

In addition to his business interests, he is the founder and president of the board of two business-oriented universities, in Montenegro and in Serbia. In April 2010, Knežević became a member of Clinton Global Initiative.

Knežević founded the Atlas Foundation in 2010. The mission of the Foundation is to support the creation of new societal values and economic empowerment of citizens through enhancement of business environment and corporate social responsible practices. Improving regional cooperation is one of the key goals of the Atlas Foundation. Knežević organised the Inaugural Conference “Balkan Networking for Social Empowerment of South-Eastern Europe” that was held in Budva in May 2011, under the auspices of the Government of Montenegro and Atlas Foundation, and in partnership with Clinton Global Initiative. The main objective of this event was to address pressing challenges facing South-Eastern Europe through developing cross-border and new models of cross-sector cooperation.

Kentucky Route 8 (KY 8) is a 114.202-mile (183.790 km) state highway in northern Kentucky, divided into two segments. The longer western segment (78.819 mi or 126.847 km) runs parallel to the Ohio River from near Francisville to Maysville, passing through Covington, Newport, and Augusta. It intersects major routes including I-71/I-75, US 25/US 42/US 127, and US 27. A landslide near Fort Thomas forced a reroute, with the original alignment redesignated as KY 445 and KY 6335. The eastern segment (35.383 mi or 56.943 km) stretches from Concord to South Portsmouth, originally part of KY 10 before being absorbed into KY 8 in the late 1990s. The highway serves Boone, Kenton, Campbell, Pendleton, Bracken, Mason, Lewis, and Greenup counties.

Donia Jessop (born 1970) is an American politician and activist serving as the mayor of Hildale, Utah, since 2018. A former member of the Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints (FLDS), she became the first female and first non-FLDS-endorsed mayor of Hildale. Her administration has focused on economic development, infrastructure, and tourism. In 2020, *USA Today* named her one of Utah's most influential women.

Banjara Hills is an urban commercial centre and one of the most affluent neighbourhoods in Hyderabad, Telangana, India. This is an upmarket locality close to Jubilee Hills. This area was a hilly forest and was least inhabited in the past. Only few royal members of the Nizam's dynasty lived here, which was a hunting ground for them. Even with its history and status, this area now has completely been transformed to an urban commercial centre consisting of an array of high-end hotels, restaurants, night clubs and office buildings of global corporations. Banjara Hills is segregated by its road numbers, with each road having its own importance: the numbers start from 1 and end at 14.

Banjara Hills is considered the most expensive zip code in India according to Economic Times magazine and along with Jubilee Hills, is the most prestigious borough/city in the greater Hyderabad area to live in. Economic Times estimated that properties in Banjara Hills were worth "a whopping Rs 96,000 crore", an equivalent to US$20.7 billion (As of 8 September 2011).

The much neglected Banjara Lake is also located here.

The Black Country derby is the local derby between English football teams West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, eleven miles (18 km) apart in the Black Country of the West Midlands.

The most successful side in the Black Country derby is West Bromwich Albion, losing just three of the past 23 matches between the two sides. Wolves are currently in the Premier League, and West Brom in the EFL Championship following their relegation in 2021.

Koro is known as a Tibeto-Burman language belonging to the Sino-Tibetan language family, even though it has resemblances to Tani farther to the east. It has been argued that Koro is actually part of the Greater Siangic family, independent from but influenced by the Sino-Tibetan family. Koro is spoken by about 1,500 people in the Koro-Aka tribe who are found in the East Kameng District of Arunachal Pradesh in northeast India. Few speakers are under 20 years old. The majority of Koro speakers live in bilingual households in which one or more members speak Ako or another indigenous language rather than Koro. The Koro-Aka tribe lives among the Aka (Hruso) tribe. However, the Koro-Aka people speak a very distantly related language from the remaining Aka tribe who speak Hruso-Aka. Researchers hypothesize Koro may have originated from a group of people enslaved and brought to the area. Since there are so few people who speak Koro, it is considered an endangered language.

The Young Plan (Chinese: 楊慕琦計劃) was a proposed constitutional reform for Hong Kong introduced in 1946 by Governor Sir Mark Young after the colony's liberation from Japanese occupation. Aimed at fostering local loyalty amid growing Chinese nationalist influence, the plan sought to establish a partially elected Municipal Council with financial autonomy and expanded powers over urban services, education, and welfare. It also proposed modest reforms to the Legislative Council, increasing unofficial representation.  

The plan faced strong opposition from British business elites, colonial officials, and conservative legislators, including Governor Alexander Grantham, who succeeded Young in 1947. Critics argued the reforms were impractical and risked politicizing Hong Kong. After prolonged delays, the plan was formally abandoned in 1952, with Britain citing Cold War tensions and the Korean War as reasons to avoid major changes. Instead, limited reforms were introduced, such as adding elected seats to the Urban Council. The Young Plan marked the last significant attempt at democratic reform in Hong Kong until the 1980s.
Martyrs Bus Service is a bus operator in Melbourne, Australia. It operates one route under contract to Public Transport Victoria. It is a subsidiary of the Dineen Group.

George Washington (bust by Houdon) is a public artwork that is a limited edition copy of an original work by French neoclassical sculptor Jean Antoine Houdon that is on display inside the Indiana Statehouse, which is located in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States. The artwork was cast a solid piece of plaster that has been painted white.

Spichern was a motor tanker that was built in Denmark for Norwegian owners and launched in 1935 as Krossfonn. In 1940 the German navy captured her, renamed her Spichern and converted her into an oiler. In 1944 she was damaged in an air raid and then scuttled.

Spichern was raised in 1947, rebuilt, renamed Ringfjell and returned to Norwegian merchant service in 1949. She was converted into a bulk carrier in 1955, renamed Ringsaker in 1961 and scrapped in 1964.

Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is a public, not-for-profit research unit in the Department of Community Health Sciences within the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences at the University of Manitoba (Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada). MCHP provides and uses population-based administrative data to inform health policy, social policy and health and social service delivery provincially, nationally and internationally. MCHP also creates population-based research on health and the social determinants of health.

Basugaon is a town in Chirang district, Assam, India. Located at an elevation of 47 meters, it has a population of 27,350 (2019). The town is governed by the Basugaon Municipal Board and is connected by National Highways 31, 31C, and 37, linking it to major cities in Assam and neighboring states. It has a railway station under the Northeast Frontier Railway zone, serving as a key transit point. Basugaon also has several schools, though few provide secondary education.

The Leader of Alderney is the highest civil authority in Alderney, a Channel Island. Since 1949, the position has been held by the President of the States of Alderney, elected for a four-year term. The president chairs the island's parliament and holds a deciding vote in tied decisions. Historically, leadership was held by feudal lords, governors, and judges before German occupation during World War II. After the war, Alderney was reintegrated into the Bailiwick of Guernsey, leading to the current democratic system.

STS-50 was a 1992 NASA Space Shuttle mission aboard *Columbia*, marking the first flight of the United States Microgravity Laboratory-1 (USML-1). The 14-day mission, the longest Shuttle flight at the time, conducted over 30 experiments in fluid dynamics, materials science, combustion, and biotechnology. It also introduced the Extended Duration Orbiter hardware, enabling longer missions.
The Reading by-election was a Parliamentary by-election. It returned one Member of Parliament to the House of Commons of the United Kingdom, elected by the first past the post voting system.

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a 2007 country song by James Otto, released as the lead single from his album *Sunset Man*. Co-written by Otto, Jim Femino, and D. Vincent Williams, the song was produced by John Rich and Otto. A romantic ballad in G major, it received positive reviews for its smooth, confident delivery. The accompanying music video, directed by Ryan Smith, premiered in late 2007. The track became Otto's first major hit, reaching No. 1 on *Billboard*'s Hot Country Songs chart.

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish politician who has served as Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston since 2015. A member of the Scottish National Party (SNP), she served as its spokesperson for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport from 2018 to 2019.

Lead Section:  

*MercyMe, It's Christmas!* (2015) is the first Christmas album by contemporary Christian band MercyMe. Featuring a mix of original songs and holiday classics, it received widespread critical acclaim for its festive and uplifting sound.

Velocity Tower is a residential skyscraper in Sheffield, England. Originally planned as the 22-storey Eclipse Tower, it was redesigned by AXIS Architecture and completed in 2010 with 30 floors. The glass-clad building features a rooftop wind turbine array.
Bradley F. Granger (March 12, 1825 – November 4, 1882) was an American lawyer and Republican politician who served one term in the U.S. House of Representatives from Michigan (1861–1863).

David Jonathan Heyman (born 26 July 1961) is a British film producer and the founder of Heyday Films. Heyman secured the rights to the Harry Potter film series in 1999 and went on to produce all eight installments of the franchise. He also received three Academy Award nominations for his work on the films Gravity, Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, and Marriage Story.

DecembeRadio is the eponymous major label debut album by the band DecembeRadio. Produced by Scotty Wilbanks, the album features a guitar-driven sound that quickly earned it comparisons to The Black Crowes, King's X, Aerosmith and Free. The album was nominated for a "Best Rock or Rap Gospel Album" Grammy Award, and won the 2007 Dove Award for "Rock Album of the Year". Two of the album's tracks were also nominated for Dove Awards: "Drifter" for "Song of the Year", and "Dangerous" for "Rock Recorded Song of the Year".

Ko. Si. Mani (13 September 1929 – 2 December 2016) was an Indian politician and longtime member of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK). He served five terms in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly and two in the Legislative Council, becoming the first DMK MLA elected from Kumbakonam. He faced corruption charges in 2003 and was acquitted in a 1984 assault case.

Harrisburg is a town in Lewis County, New York, United States. With a population of about 435 (2016), it was established in 1803 and features rural landscapes, including the Deer River and state routes NY-12, NY-26, and NY-177.
The Right (Lang-it) was a neofascist and national-conservative political party in Italy. Its leader and founder was Francesco Storace.

Colt Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American football tight end who played college football for the Oregon Ducks and briefly in the Arena Football League (AFL). A standout high school athlete in Oregon, he played for Oregon from 2011 to 2013, winning the Fiesta Bowl (2013) and Rose Bowl (2012). After going undrafted in the 2014 NFL Draft, he signed with the Green Bay Packers but suffered a career-ending knee injury. His career was overshadowed by legal troubles, including arrests for drug possession and assault.

Collective motion refers to the coordinated movement of self-propelled particles, such as micromotors or nanomotors, in response to external stimuli like chemical gradients or light. These particles can exhibit behaviors such as schooling (grouping together) and exclusion (avoiding each other), as seen in silver chloride and titanium dioxide microparticles under UV light. Some systems, like silver orthophosphate microparticles, can switch between these behaviors based on chemical inputs, enabling applications like logic gates. Additionally, chemotaxis—movement toward chemical gradients—has been observed in synthetic nanorods and enzyme-coated particles, mimicking biological systems. This behavior has potential uses in drug delivery, microfluidics, and lab-on-a-chip devices.

*Freeze Frame* is the 1979 studio album by British duo Godley & Creme. Known for experimental techniques like the Gizmo guitar device and pitch-shifted vocals, it blends rock, new wave, and art punk. The album includes the single "An Englishman in New York."

Bill Long (28 April 1932 – 21 May 2010) was an Irish writer and broadcaster. Known for his radio documentaries and books like *Bright Light, White Water* and *Change of Heart*, he also worked as a journalist for several Irish newspapers.

The Sanctuary is a late Neolithic and early Bronze Age stone and timber circle located on Overton Hill near Avebury in Wiltshire, England. Part of the Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites UNESCO World Heritage Site, it was constructed in several phases between 3000 and 2000 BCE, evolving from a small timber structure into concentric rings of posts and stones. The site connects to Avebury via the West Kennet Avenue, a 2.4-kilometer stone avenue, suggesting a ceremonial or processional link between the two monuments.  

Excavations in the 1930s revealed postholes and stone settings, indicating multiple construction phases, though much of the site was destroyed in the 18th century. Antiquarian William Stukeley recorded its appearance before its demolition, depicting it as part of a larger ritual landscape. The Sanctuary's exact purpose remains uncertain, with theories ranging from a mortuary house to a ceremonial gathering place. Today, its layout is marked by concrete posts, though little visible evidence of the original structure remains.
John Patrick (born February 29, 1968) is an American professional basketball coach and former professional basketball player.

The Kuban River is a major river in Eastern Europe, flowing through Russia for 870 kilometers (540 mi) from the Caucasus Mountains to the Sea of Azov. Originating near Mount Elbrus, it forms a delta covering 4,300 km² (1,700 sq mi) with numerous limans (shallow estuaries). The river's basin spans 57,900 km² (22,400 sq mi) and includes tributaries such as the Laba, Belaya, and Urup.  

Historically, the Kuban discharged into both the Black Sea and Azov Sea, but natural shifts redirected it entirely to the Azov. Its waters support rice farming, fishing, and hydroelectric power, including the Krasnodar Reservoir. The delta is a Ramsar wetland, rich in biodiversity, including sturgeon, migratory birds, and endemic fish like the Kuban barbel. The river has cultural significance, lending its name to the Kuban Cossacks.

Aristides Aquino (born April 22, 1994) is a Dominican professional baseball outfielder for the Chunichi Dragons of Nippon Professional Baseball. He previously played for the Cincinnati Reds (2018–2022), setting a National League rookie record with 14 home runs in a month.

David Loud (born November 28, 1961, in Cincinnati, Ohio) is an American music supervisor, music director, conductor, vocal and dance arranger, pianist and actor. He is best known for his collaborations with and interpretations of the music of both Kander and Ebb and Stephen Sondheim.

Police impersonation is the act of falsely posing as a law enforcement officer, often to commit crimes such as fraud, extortion, or assault. It undermines public trust in authorities and has been documented globally throughout history.

Fuxi or Fu Hsi (伏羲 ~ 伏犧 ~ 伏戲) is a culture hero in Chinese legend and mythology, credited along with his sister and wife Nüwa with creating humanity and the invention of music, hunting, fishing, domestication, and cooking as well as the Cangjie system of writing Chinese characters around 2,000 BC. Fuxi was counted as the first of the Three Sovereigns at the beginning of the Chinese dynastic period.

Sam's Strip was a metafictional comic strip created by Jerry Dumas and Mort Walker, syndicated from 1961 to 1963. It followed Sam, a self-aware comic strip character who interacted with classic cartoon figures while humorously breaking the fourth wall. Despite critical praise, the strip was canceled due to limited public appeal.

Police Academy is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson in his directorial debut, and distributed by Warner Bros. Pictures. Its story follows a new recruitment policy for an unnamed police department's academy that is required to take in any recruit who wishes to try out to be a police officer. The film stars Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, and G.W. Bailey.

The film was produced by The Ladd Company. It premiered on March 23, 1984. It grossed $8.5 million in its opening weekend and over $149 million worldwide, against a budget of $4.5 million, and remains the most successful film of the series as of 2022. The film spawned six sequels in the Police Academy franchise.

The Mourne Mountains (Irish: *Na Beanna Boirche*) are a granite mountain range in County Down, Northern Ireland. The highest peak, Slieve Donard, rises to 850 metres (2,790 ft). The range is known for its scenic landscapes, including the Mourne Wall, a 31.4 km (19.5 mi) dry stone wall crossing fifteen summits, built to protect Belfast's water supply. The area features forests like Tollymore Forest Park, reservoirs such as Silent Valley, and diverse flora and fauna. A popular destination for hiking and rock climbing, the Mournes have inspired literature and music, including Percy French’s song *"The Mountains of Mourne"*. Conservation efforts and debates over a proposed national park and wind farm continue to shape the region.

Velocity Tower is a mixed use development next to the Inner Ring Road in Sheffield City Centre, South Yorkshire, England, overlooking Ecclesall Road. The build is a continuation of a previous design to be built by Cala, titled 'Eclipse'.

The Medieval University of Dublin was a short-lived university established in 1320 by papal authority, with faculties in theology and law. Founded by Archbishop Alexander de Bicknor, it was closely tied to St. Patrick's Cathedral but struggled due to political instability and lack of funding. Despite later attempts to revive it, including royal and papal support, the university never flourished and disappeared during the Irish Reformation (1534–41). It remains a historical footnote in Ireland's educational history.
*Odostomia farallonensis* is a species of sea snail in the family Pyramidellidae, found in the Pacific Ocean off the Farallon Islands.

The Ideal 18 is a Canadian-designed one-design keelboat, first built in 1989. It features a fractional sloop rig, a fixed fin keel, and a self-tacking jib, primarily used for club racing. Over 300 have been constructed.

William F. De Saussure (February 22, 1792 – March 13, 1870) was an American politician and lawyer who served as a U.S. Senator from South Carolina (1852–1853) and as mayor of Columbia. A Democrat, he later signed South Carolina's Ordinance of Secession.

A lord proprietor is a person granted a royal charter for the establishment and government of an English colony in the 17th century. The plural of the term is "lords proprietors" or "lords proprietary".

Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I (before 682–734) was a Maya king of Tikal who ruled from 682 until his death. His reign marked Tikal's resurgence, including a decisive victory over Calakmul in 695. He was buried in Temple I, while his wife was interred in Temple II.

Rhyssa persuasoria, also known as the sabre wasp, is a large parasitic ichneumon wasp found across Europe, North Africa, and parts of Asia and North America. Females have a long ovipositor used to lay eggs inside wood-boring insect larvae, particularly horntails and longhorn beetles. Adults feed on honeydew and pine sap.

Hampton Hill is a suburban town in the London Borough of Richmond upon Thames, England. Historically part of Hampton parish, it developed in the 19th century with the expansion of railways. The area is bordered by Bushy Park and the Longford River, an artificial watercourse built for Hampton Court. Hampton Hill features a mix of residential and commercial areas, with its High Street hosting shops, restaurants, and an annual Christmas parade. The town was affected by bombings during World War II, including a 1940 Luftwaffe attack that killed five people. Local landmarks include St James's Church, Hampton Hill Theatre, and Hampton Hill Junior School. The area is served by nearby rail services and maintains active community organizations.

Clepsicosma is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae. As at 2022, this genus contains only one described species, Clepsicosma iridia, which is endemic to New Zealand. The species inhabits native forest in the North Island as well as the northern and western parts of the South Island down to Westland. The larval host of this species is assumed to be species of Cutty grass, possibly including Gahnia setifolia and Gahnia xanthocarpa, although the life history of this species is unknown. The adults of C. iridia are on the wing from December until May. They are nocturnal, and are attracted to light. During the day the adults rest on the underside of leaves, including those Cutty grass species that may possibly be their larval hosts.

Michael Hugh Meacher (4 November 1939 – 21 October 2015) was a British politician who served as a government minister under Harold Wilson, James Callaghan and Tony Blair. A member of the Labour Party, he was Member of Parliament (MP) for Oldham West and Royton, previously Oldham West, from 1970 until his death in 2015. He was also a 9/11 conspiracy theorist.

Before entering politics, Meacher was a lecturer in social administration at the University of Essex and the University of York.

Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (born 29 June 1966), commonly known as Ahok, is an Indonesian politician and businessman who served as Governor of Jakarta (2014–2017) and later as President Commissioner of Pertamina (2019–present). A member of the Indonesian Democratic Party of Struggle (PDI-P), he was the first ethnic Chinese and Christian governor of Jakarta.  

Ahok began his political career as Regent of East Belitung (2005–2006) before serving in the People's Representative Council (2009–2012). He became Deputy Governor of Jakarta (2012–2014) under Joko Widodo, succeeding him as governor after Widodo was elected president. His tenure focused on anti-corruption measures, infrastructure development, and bureaucratic reform, but also faced criticism for forced evictions of urban poor communities.  

In 2017, he was convicted of blasphemy and imprisoned for two years following a controversial trial widely seen as politically motivated. After his release in 2019, he joined Pertamina as president commissioner. His political career has been marked by both reformist policies and polarizing controversies, including racial and religious tensions in Indonesia.

The three-point turn (sometimes called a Y-turn, K-turn, or broken U-turn) is the standard method of turning a vehicle around to face the opposite direction in a limited space, using forward and reverse gears. This is typically done when the road is too narrow for a U-turn.

This manoeuvre is a common requirement in driving tests.

"Love Song" is a 2000 single by Japanese rock band Luna Sea, released on November 8, 2000, by Universal. Written by guitarist Sugizo, it was recorded during the *Lunacy* sessions but excluded from the album. The single includes B-sides "Into the Sun" and "Until the Day I Die", each composed by different band members. Its release coincided with the band's announcement of their impending disbandment. The song was later covered by Kannivalism in 2007.

The 1975 Council of Ministers of East Timor was the first government formed after Fretilin's unilateral declaration of independence from Portugal. Established on December 1, 1975, it was dissolved days later following Indonesia's invasion.
Urera is a genus of flowering plants in the nettle family, Urticaceae. It has a pantropical distribution.

"I'd Go to Jail" is a 2021 country song by Canadian artist Dean Brody, released as a single from his album *Boys*. Inspired by fatherhood, the song explores themes of love and protection. It reached No. 5 on *Billboard* Canada Country and No. 73 on the *Canadian Hot 100*.

The ZBD-03 is a Chinese airborne infantry fighting vehicle (IFV) designed for the People's Liberation Army. Introduced in 2002, it features a 30mm autocannon, HJ-73C anti-tank missiles, and amphibious capabilities, with variants including command and anti-tank versions.

A. J. Alan (1883–1941) was the pseudonym of Leslie Harrison Lambert, a British magician, intelligence officer, and pioneering radio broadcaster. Known for his witty, seemingly improvised short stories, he became a popular BBC personality in the 1920s and 1930s. He also worked in British naval intelligence during both World Wars.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics (FIS) is one of the six faculties of the University of Economics, Prague, established in 1991. It offers Bachelor's, Master's, and Doctoral programs in fields such as informatics, econometrics, operations research, and statistics. The faculty collaborates with institutions like the Czech Technical University, the Academy of Sciences of the Czech Republic, and the Czech Statistical Office. As of recent data, it has around 2,490 students. Its dean is prof. Ing. Jakub Fischer, Ph.D.

Manuel José de Jesus Silva (born 9 April 1946) is a Portuguese former footballer and manager, best known for his highly successful tenure with Egyptian club Al Ahly, where he won four CAF Champions League titles (2005, 2006, 2008, 2012) and five consecutive Egyptian Premier League titles (2004–2009).  

As a player, he spent most of his career in Portugal, featuring for Benfica and several other clubs. After retiring, he managed multiple Portuguese teams, including Sporting CP, Boavista, and Belenenses, winning domestic cups. However, his greatest achievements came in Egypt, where he became Al Ahly's most successful foreign manager, earning the CAF Coach of the Year award in 2006. He also briefly managed Angola at the 2010 Africa Cup of Nations and had short spells in Saudi Arabia and Iran before retiring.
Fredrik Bergenstråhle (born in Stockholm) was a Swedish diplomat who served as ambassador to Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and Colombia.

Baasskap (Afrikaans for "boss-ship") was a faction of apartheid ideology in South Africa that sought to maintain white, particularly Afrikaner, dominance in politics and the economy while allowing controlled black labor. Prominent supporters included Prime Minister J.G. Strydom and Minister C.R. Swart. Unlike stricter apartheid factions, baasskap proponents accepted limited economic integration as long as white supremacy was preserved. Hendrik Verwoerd later refined the concept, giving it intellectual justification.

Aleksei Losev (23 September 1893 – 24 May 1988) was a Soviet and Russian philosopher, philologist, and culturologist, known for his works on classical aesthetics, mythology, and the philosophy of language. A critic of Soviet ideology, he was imprisoned in the 1930s but later rehabilitated, continuing his scholarly work under state censorship. His major contributions include *The Dialectics of Myth* and the eight-volume *History of Classical Aesthetics*, for which he received the USSR State Prize in 1986.

Bill Sizemore (born June 2, 1951) is an American political activist and writer in Happy Valley, Oregon, United States. Sizemore has never held elected office, but has nonetheless been a major political figure in Oregon since the 1990s. He is considered one of the main proponents of the Oregon tax revolt, a movement that seeks to reduce taxes in the state. Oregon Taxpayers United, a political action committee he founded in 1993, has advanced numerous ballot initiatives limiting taxation, and has opposed spending initiatives. Sizemore made an unsuccessful run for Governor of Oregon in 1998. He also announced his intention to run for governor in 2010, but was indicted by the state on charges of tax evasion. The charges were later amended to failure to file tax returns.

Hopanoids are a diverse subclass of triterpenoids with the same hydrocarbon skeleton as the compound hopane. This group of pentacyclic molecules therefore refers to simple hopenes, hopanols and hopanes, but also to extensively functionalized derivatives such as bacteriohopanepolyols (BHPs) and hopanoids covalently attached to lipid A.

The first known hopanoid, hydroxyhopanone, was isolated by two chemists at The National Gallery, London working on the chemistry of dammar gum, a natural resin used as a varnish for paintings. While hopanoids are often assumed to be made only in bacteria, their name actually comes from the abundance of hopanoid compounds in the resin of plants from the genus Hopea. In turn, this genus is named after John Hope, the first Regius Keeper of the Royal Botanic Garden, Edinburgh.

Since their initial discovery in an angiosperm, hopanoids have been found in plasma membranes of bacteria, lichens, bryophytes, ferns, tropical trees and fungi. Hopanoids have stable polycyclic structures that are well-preserved in petroleum reservoirs, rocks and sediment, allowing the diagenetic products of these molecules to be interpreted as biomarkers for the presence of specific microbes and potentially for chemical or physical conditions at the time of deposition. Hopanoids have not been detected in archaea.

Common ownership refers to holding the assets of an organization, enterprise or community indivisibly rather than in the names of the individual members or groups of members as common property.

Forms of common ownership exist in every economic system. Common ownership of the means of production is a central goal of communist political movements as it is seen as a necessary democratic mechanism for the creation and continued function of a communist society. Advocates make a distinction between collective ownership and common property as the former refers to property owned jointly by agreement of a set of colleagues, such as producer cooperatives, whereas the latter refers to assets that are completely open for access, such as a public park freely available to everyone.

DXWT (92.3 FM), broadcasting as 92.3 Wild FM, is a radio station owned and operated by the UM Broadcasting Network. It serves as the flagship station of the Wild FM network. The station's studio is located at the UMBN Media Center, C. Bangoy cor. Palma Gil St., Davao City, and its transmitter is located along Broadcast Ave., Shrine Hills, Matina, Davao City.

Thomas P. Coates (1803 – October 11, 1895) was a 19th-century American musician who achieved initial prominence in Pennsylvania for his performances on the cornet and French horn. The director of Pomp's Cornet Band in Easton, Pennsylvania, he was commissioned as the first conductor of the regimental band of the 47th Pennsylvania Volunteer Infantry Regiment during the early months of the American Civil War. Post-war, he became a prolific and popular composer of band music, and was subsequently nicknamed "the Father of Band Music in America."

Robert Prosky (born Robert Joseph Porzuczek, December 13, 1930 – December 8, 2008) was an American actor. He became a well-known supporting actor in the 1980s with his roles in Thief (1981), Christine (1983), The Natural (1984), and Broadcast News (1987).

Prosky's other notable movies include Gremlins 2: The New Batch (1990), Hoffa (1992), Mrs. Doubtfire (1993), Last Action Hero (1993), Miracle on 34th Street (1994), Dead Man Walking (1995), and Mad City (1997). His most notable television role was of Sgt. Stan Jablonski on the TV police drama Hill Street Blues.

Baltoceratidae is an extinct family of slender, straight-shelled cephalopods from the Ordovician period. Initially classified within the Ellesmerocerida, they were later reassigned to the Orthocerida based on their thin connecting rings and lack of siphuncular diaphragms. The family includes rod-bearing and vacuosiphonate forms, with some genera giving rise to later orthoceratid groups.

Joseph Tanke (Ph.D., Boston College; born 1978) is an American philosopher and Professor of Philosophy at the University of Hawaii at Manoa. His work focuses on Continental philosophy, the history of philosophy, aesthetics, art theory, and historical ontology. Tanke is known for his analysis of major figures in the French philosophical tradition, such as Michel Foucault and Jacques Rancière. His book Foucault's Philosophy of Art: A Genealogy of Modernity is one of the first systematic presentations of Michel Foucault's writings on visual art. Tanke is the first scholar to publish a comprehensive critical introduction to the thought of Jacques Rancière.

Zoya Yunoevna Semenduyeva (October 20, 1929 – April 9, 2020) was a Soviet and Israeli poet who wrote in Juhuri (Mountain Jewish language). She published numerous poetry collections and contributed to the translation of the Tanakh into Juhuri.

Saint Overboard (1936) is a mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, featuring his iconic character Simon Templar, alias *The Saint*. While sailing off the French coast, Templar rescues detective Loretta Page, who is investigating modern-day pirates using advanced diving technology to loot shipwrecks. With the help of old allies, Templar confronts the ruthless Kurt Vogel, leading to a dangerous underwater salvage mission. The novel was adapted into a 1995 radio drama but never made into a film despite RKO Studios acquiring the rights.
Andrea Batista Schlesinger (born October 27, 1976) is an American political writer and campaigner. She was until 2009 the executive director of the Drum Major Institute.
Boubacar Moungoro (born September 22, 1994) is a Malian professional basketball player who has played for Baloncesto Fuenlabrada, Oklahoma City Blue, and the Mali national team.

Barasat Junction railway station is a major railway junction in Barasat, West Bengal, India, serving the Kolkata Suburban Railway network. Operated by Eastern Railway, it was established in 1906 and electrified in 1972. The station has five platforms and six tracks, facilitating connections to key routes like the Sealdah–Bangaon line. Historically, it was part of the narrow-gauge Barasat Basirhat Railway (1914–1955), later converted to broad gauge under Indian Railways. A proposed Kolkata Metro extension from Noapara to Barasat aims to ease congestion, though construction delays persist due to land encroachment issues. The station features a reservation counter, smart card ticketing, and a rail car shed for maintenance.
Madonna and Child Kissing is a 1520s oil on panel painting by the Flemish renaissance artist Quentin Matsys in the collection of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, on loan to the Mauritshuis.

Heinz Rein (pseudonym: Reinhard Andermann) was an influential German novelist writing before and after the Second World War. He became a major figure in the "rubble literature" period, and his famous novel Berlin Finale, published in 1947, was one of the first bestsellers of the German rebuilding period.

Ja Lama (c. 1862–1922), born Dambiijantsan, was a Kalmyk-Mongolian warlord and Buddhist monk who played a key role in Mongolia's 1911 revolution against Qing rule. Known for his brutality, he helped capture Khovd from Qing forces and later ruled western Mongolia through fear. After being imprisoned by Russian authorities, he returned to Mongolia but was hunted by the new communist government. In 1922, he was killed by Mongolian forces, and his severed head was displayed in Ulaanbaatar and later in Saint Petersburg. His legacy remains controversial due to his violent methods and separatist ambitions.

The SNCF Class BB 1800 (originally Z 5000) were early electric locomotives operated by the French railways. Introduced in 1901, they were among the first locomotives to run on France's newly electrified Paris-Invalides to Versailles-Rive-Gauche line. Initially classified as self-propelled baggage vans, they were later renumbered as BB 001–010 (1932) and BB 1801–1810 (1950). Equipped with 600 V DC third-rail power, they featured four traction motors and underwent modifications in the 1930s. Withdrawn by 1956, their duties were taken over by Z 5100 multiple units.

Dacrydium cupressinum, commonly known as rimu, is a slow-growing coniferous tree native to New Zealand. It can reach heights of up to 50 meters and lives for 800–900 years. Rimu is valued for its timber and ornamental use, though logging is now restricted. Its seeds are an important food source for birds like the kakapo.

The rusty-margined guan (*Penelope superciliaris*) is a Near Threatened bird in the Cracidae family, found in South America. It inhabits forests and woodlands, feeds mainly on fruit, and has distinctive reddish wing margins.

*Nothing's Real* is the debut studio album by British musician Shura, released on 8 July 2016 by Polydor Records. A blend of electropop and '80s-inspired synth-pop, the album received critical acclaim for its songwriting and production. It peaked at number 13 on the UK Albums Chart.

Dale Harris Van Sickel (November 29, 1907 – January 25, 1977) was an American college football, basketball and baseball player during the 1920s, who later became a Hollywood motion picture actor and stunt performer for over forty years. Van Sickel played college football for the University of Florida, and was recognized as the first-ever first-team All-American in the history of the Florida Gators football program.

Dmitry Lisanevich (1778–1825) was a Russian general who fought in the Russo-Persian War, Russo-Turkish War, and Caucasian War. Known for his role in the killing of Ibrahim Khalil Khan, he later commanded troops in the Caucasus before being assassinated in 1825.

Formula One 99 is a racing video game developed by Studio 33 and published by Psygnosis for PlayStation and Microsoft Windows. It is the sequel to the 1998 video game Formula 1 98 and was based on the 1999 Formula One World Championship.

Following the disappointment of Formula 1 98, and the subsequent split with Visual Science, Psygnosis hired Studio 33 to develop Formula One 99, after their successful development of Newman/Haas Racing the previous year. During production of the game, Sony completed a full acquisition of Psygnosis, making this the final Formula One game to be published under the Psygnosis name.

The game is unique in having substitute drivers appear in the game as they did in the real 1999 season (such as Mika Salo replacing Michael Schumacher for the races between Austria and Europe). A new grid editor tool was also introduced, allowing players to customise the starting grid to their own liking before a race. Despite the lack of an arcade mode that had featured in previous titles, the game was widely praised as an overwhelming improvement in comparison to the 1998 game.
KeeWeb is a free and open-source password manager compatible with KeePass, available as a web version and desktop apps. The underlying file format is KDBX (KeePass database file).

Larry Keel (born November 25, 1968) is an American bluegrass guitarist, singer, and bandleader from Rockbridge County, Virginia. Known for his progressive string band style, he has led multiple groups, including *Magraw Gap*, the *Larry Keel Experience*, and *Larry Keel & Natural Bridge*. A frequent collaborator with artists like Keller Williams and members of *Yonder Mountain String Band*, Keel has recorded numerous albums and performed at major festivals nationwide. Since 1994, he has been married to bassist Jenny Keel, who performs in his bands. His music blends traditional bluegrass with innovative improvisation.

In psychologist Hans Eysenck's P–E–N (psychoticism, extraversion and neuroticism) model of personality, psychoticism is a trait which is typified by aggressiveness and interpersonal hostility. In 2010, a paper titled "The nature of the relationship between personality traits and political attitudes" claimed to find a strong positive correlation between conservatism and psychoticism. This error was repeated in subsequent papers by the same authors; however, around 2015, the authors acknowledged the correlation is actually negative rather than positive, and began issuing corrections.

Persicaria minor is a creeping herb native to Southeast Asia, growing in wet lowlands and highlands. Its leaves are used in traditional dishes like *laksa* and in herbal medicine. Studies suggest it has antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, making it valuable in food preservation and health applications.

Occupy San Francisco was a collaboration that began with a demonstration event located at Justin Herman Plaza in the Embarcadero and in front of the Federal Reserve building on Market Street in the Financial District in San Francisco, California. It is based on the Occupy Wall Street movement that began in New York City on September 17, 2011 and is one of several "Occupy" protest sites in the San Francisco Bay Area; other sites include Occupy Oakland and Occupy San Jose.

As of June 2012, Occupy San Francisco had continued to engage in organized meetings, events and actions.

The Young Plan was a constitutional reform proposal carried out in 1946 attempting to introduce representative democracy in Colonial Hong Kong. Named after the then Governor, Mark Young, it was the first major reform proposal to give Hong Kong inhabitants a greater share of managing their own affairs by widening the base of Hong Kong's political system through the creation of a new Municipal Council. The proposed Council was to consist of an elected majority based on a fairly wide franchise, with powers and autonomy over all urban services, education, social welfare, town planning and other functions. It even allowed for indirect election of two Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council (LegCo) by the new Council.

Unofficial Members of the Legislative Council were opposed to the transfer of power to the new body and Young's successor, Governor Alexander Grantham who was opposed to the Young Plan, did not press this issue. Discussion dragged on but the continued opposition of Unofficials in LegCo in addition to the fear of the potential of penetration by Communist China finally killed the plan in 1952. This was the last move towards any form of major electorally-based government until in the 1980s during the Sino-British negotiations on Hong Kong's sovereignty.

Joseph Tate (born ?) is an American attorney who represented Lewis Libby during the CIA leak grand jury investigation. A graduate of Villanova University School of Law, Tate publicly denied Libby's involvement as Judith Miller's confidential source in the case. Special Counsel Patrick Fitzgerald confirmed that Libby's communications with Miller did not constitute obstruction of justice.
Freud's inner circle or Secret Committee consisted of Sigmund Freud's most trusted psychoanalysts.

Walton Castle was a Saxon Shore Fort in the Roman province of Britannia. The fort was reused by the Normans who used it as the bailey for a castle. It stood 30 metres above sea level but was destroyed by coastal erosion in the 18th century. It was located in the village of Walton, Suffolk, now part of Felixstowe (grid reference TM32253580).

The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program, in the United States, is an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) program under 26 U.S.C. § 7122, which allows qualified individuals with an unpaid tax debt to negotiate a settled amount that is less than the total owed to clear the debt. A taxpayer uses the checklist in the Form 656, OIC package to determine if the taxpayer is eligible for the program. The objective of the OIC program is to accept a compromise when acceptance is in the best interests of both the taxpayer and the government, and promotes voluntary compliance with all future payment and filing requirements.

Planet Earth is a television and film documentary franchise produced and broadcast by the BBC. The franchise began in 2001 with the success of The Blue Planet. As of 2017, The Blue Planet has spawned 5 series and one feature film.
Police impersonation is the act of falsely portraying oneself as a member of the police for the purpose of deception.

Anima Yell! is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Tsukasa Unohana, serialized in *Manga Time Kirara Carat* from 2016 to 2020. It follows Kohane Hatoya, a cheerful girl who starts a cheerleading club in high school. A 12-episode anime adaptation by Doga Kobo aired in 2018, featuring themes of friendship and teamwork. The series blends slice-of-life comedy with light sports elements.

Culbert Olson (November 7, 1876 – April 13, 1962) was an American lawyer and politician who served as the 29th governor of California from 1939 to 1943. A progressive Democrat and atheist, he was the first Democrat elected governor in 40 years, supporting labor rights and New Deal policies while clashing with conservatives.

Richard E. Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is a former American football player and coach. He served as the head football coach at Rutgers University from 1984 to 1989, compiling a record of 28–33–4. His highlight victory during that span was 21–16 win over Penn State in 1988—Rutgers' first win over the Nittany Lions in 70 years. He played college football at Penn State, and served there as an assistant football coach for many years under Joe Paterno.
HMS Modeste was a 64-gun third rate ship of the line of the Royal Navy. She was previously the 64-gun Modeste, of the French Navy, launched in 1759 and captured later that year.

Arbignieu is a former commune in the Ain department of eastern France. Since 2016, it has been part of the commune Arboys en Bugey. Located in the Bugey wine region, it is known for its vineyards producing Roussette du Bugey-Arbignieu wine. The area includes the hamlets of Peyzieu, Thoys, and Slignieu.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (Lang-ru; 10 December [O.S. 28 November] 1888 – 19 September 1964) was a soviet organic chemist, specializing in natural product chemistry. He studied essential oils and resins of plants, as well as terpene compounds extracted from them.

TIME'S UP is a non-profit organization that raises money to support victims of sexual harassment. The organization was founded on January 1, 2018, by Hollywood celebrities in response to the Weinstein effect and the Me Too movement. As of January 2020, the organization had raised $24 million in donations.

Time's Up collaborated with the National Women's Law Center to create the Time's Up Legal Defense Fund (TULDF), which provides legal and media support to individuals who have been subject to workplace sex discrimination, such as sexual harassment. The Time's Up Foundation raises money for the TULDF.

The organization came under fire after its leadership's involvement in the attempted cover-up of the Andrew Cuomo sexual harassment allegations came to light. In September 2021, Time's Up dissolved its 71-member advisory board, which included several prominent actors, as a result of the continued fallout from the group's handling of the controversy. It was also reported that its entire governing board would resign and be replaced.

In late 2022, the three board members were Ashley Judd, Nina Shaw, and financial executive Gabrielle Sulzberger.

The Sanctuary was a stone and timber circle near the village of Avebury in the south-western English county of Wiltshire. Excavation has revealed the location of the 58 stone sockets and 62 post-holes. The ring was part of a tradition of stone circle construction that spread throughout much of Britain, Ireland, and Brittany during the Late Neolithic and Early Bronze Age, over a period between 3300 and 900 BCE. The purpose of such monuments is unknown, although archaeologists speculate that the stones represented supernatural entities for the circle's builders.

The Sanctuary was erected on Overton Hill, overlooking older Early Neolithic sites like West Kennet Long Barrow and East Kennet Long Barrow. It was connected to the Late Neolithic henge and stone circle at Avebury via the West Kennet Avenue of stones. It also lies close to the route of the prehistoric Ridgeway and near several Bronze Age barrows.

In the early 18th century, the site was recorded by the antiquarian William Stukeley although the stones were destroyed by local farmers in the 1720s. The Sanctuary underwent archaeological excavation by Maud and Ben Cunnington in 1930, after which the location of the prehistoric posts was marked out by concrete posts. Now a scheduled monument under the guardianship of English Heritage, it is classified as part of the "Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites" UNESCO World Heritage Site and is open without charge to visitors all year round.

"If Love Was a Crime" is a song recorded by Bulgarian recording artist Poli Genova. Written by Borislav Milanov, Sebastian Arman, Joacim Bo Persson, Johnny K. Palmer and Genova, the track represented Bulgaria in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016. As a result, it came in fourth place in the final, with it becoming the highest placing in the contest for Bulgaria. An accompanying music video for "If Love Was a Crime" was released on 21 March 2016, followed by the launching of the single on 30 March 2016 through the Bulgarian National Television (BNT) and Universal. Upon its appearance in the contest, the recording charted at number 200 on the UK Singles Chart and peaked within the top eighty in Austria, Belgium, Scotland and Sweden.

Despite its success, the song was removed from digital music platforms after its initial distribution deal with Universal expired. A campaign by the Pop Activism website saw a new deal struck between the rights holders BNT and distributor Universal in April 2018 with the song returning to digital music platforms.

The 1945 College Football All-America team is composed of college football players who were selected as All-Americans by various organizations and writers that chose College Football All-America Teams in 1945. The nine selectors recognized by the NCAA as "official" for the 1945 season are (1) Collier's Weekly, as selected by Grantland Rice, (2) the Associated Press, (3) the United Press, (4) the All-America Board, (5) the American Football Coaches Association (AFCA), (6) the Football Writers Association of America (FWAA), (7) the International News Service (INS), (8) Look magazine, (9) the Newspaper Enterprise Association (NEA) and (10) the Sporting News.

The Hồ dynasty (Vietnamese: Nhà Hồ, chữ Nôm: 茹胡;Sino-Vietnamese: Hồ triều, chữ Hán: 胡朝) was a short-lived Vietnamese dynasty consisting of the reigns of two monarchs, Hồ Quý Ly (胡季犛) in 1400–01 and his second son, Hồ Hán Thương (胡漢蒼), who reigned the kingdom of Đại Ngu from 1401 to 1406. The practice of bequeathing the throne to a designated son (not simply passing it on to the eldest) was similar to what had happened in the previous Trần dynasty and was meant to avoid sibling rivalry. Hồ Quý Ly's eldest son, Hồ Nguyên Trừng, played his part as the dynasty's military general. In 2011, UNESCO declared the Citadel of the Hồ Dynasty in Thanh Hóa Province a world heritage site.
Howard Minion Radzely (born 1970) is an American lawyer who served as the United States Deputy Secretary of Labor from December 19, 2007 through February 2, 2009.
Urera is a genus of flowering plants in the nettle family (Urticaceae), comprising lianas, shrubs, and small trees, many with stinging hairs.

The Apostolic Faith Church, formerly the Apostolic Faith Mission, is a Holiness Pentecostal denomination of Christianity, with nationwide reach and headquartered in Portland, Oregon, United States. The Apostolic Faith Mission of Portland was founded in 1907 by Florence L. Crawford, who was affiliated with William J. Seymour and the Azusa Street Revival of Los Angeles, California. By 1908 Crawford had independently founded what would become the Apostolic Faith Church. Since July 2000, the Superintendent General of the Apostolic Faith Church has been Darrel Lee.

The Apostolic Faith Church has a presence in the United States, Canada and across Africa, Asia, Europe, Central and South America, and Australia. In 1997, the AFC had 2,013 members in 115 churches served by 188 clergy in the United States. There are ten churches in Canada, several hundred in Africa (six-hundred in Nigeria alone), twenty throughout the Philippines and Korea, nine in Europe (Romania and Norway), and over sixty in the West Indies. Church-affiliated groups also regularly assemble in other parts of the world and recently include India. In some locations, member churches carry the name Trinity Apostolic Faith Church in order to differentiate themselves from non-affiliated churches in the same area.

Infanta Beatriz of Spain (22 June 1909 – 22 November 2002) was the third child of King Alfonso XIII and Queen Victoria Eugenie. After the Spanish monarchy's exile in 1931, she settled in Italy and married Alessandro Torlonia, 5th Prince of Civitella-Cesi, in 1935, renouncing her succession rights. They had four children, including Olimpia, mother of Princess Sibilla of Luxembourg. Beatriz remained active in royal circles but was restricted from prolonged stays in Spain under Franco's regime. She died in Rome at 93, the last surviving child of Alfonso XIII.

Ruslan Khuchbarov (died 2004) was an Ingush militant and associate of Shamil Basayev. Accused of multiple attacks, including the 2000 Galashki ambush, he was killed during the 2004 Beslan school siege. Russian authorities initially misidentified him as other militants.

Pneumodesmus is an extinct genus of myriapod known from a single species, *Pneumodesmus newmani*, dating to the Late Silurian or Early Devonian (428–414 million years ago). Discovered in Scotland, it is the oldest known land-dwelling arthropod with evidence of a tracheal system, indicating it breathed air. Measuring about 1 cm long, it resembled a millipede but belonged to an early branch of myriapods. The fossil, found in 2004, provides key insights into the colonization of land by animals.

Altibbi is an Arabic-language digital health platform founded in 2008 in Amman, Jordan, by Jalil Labadi. It provides medical information, teleconsultations, and healthcare services across the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region. The platform, which evolved from an earlier medical glossary by Dr. Abdel Aziz Labadi, connects users with over 12,000 accredited doctors and receives millions of monthly visits. Altibbi secured investments from Middle East Venture Partners and other firms, enabling expansion into multiple Arab countries. Recognized for its contributions to Arabic medical content, it won the Arab E-Content Award in 2013.
Kaintuck' (Kentucky) is a 1935 symphonic poem for piano and orchestra by American composer William Grant Still.

Hasan Arat (born [year]) is a Turkish businessman, former professional basketball player, and sports administrator. He led Istanbul's bid for the 2020 Olympics and serves in leadership roles with the Turkish National Olympic Committee, European Olympic Committee, and World Athletics.
Estelle Desanges (born 8 March 1977) is a French former pornographic actress. During the 2000s, she was one of France's most popular porn actresses.

An Ziwen (Chinese: 安子文; 25 September 1909 – 25 June 1980) was a Chinese Communist Party (CCP) official who served as head of the CCP Central Organization Department from 1956 to 1966. He joined the CCP in 1927 and later held key roles in personnel and party discipline. During the Cultural Revolution, he was purged as one of the "61 Renegades" but was rehabilitated in 1978 under Deng Xiaoping, becoming vice president of the Central Party School. He died in Beijing in 1980.

Mercury Meltdown is a puzzle-platform game for the PlayStation Portable. It is the sequel to Archer Maclean's Mercury. Like the first game, the goal is to tilt the stage in order to navigate one or more blobs of mercury to the destination. In contrast to the original, Ignition Banbury had more time and experience developing the game and listened to player feedback, allowing the game to be easier and provide players with more freedom to choose levels. The game has new hazards, enemies, and minigames.

The game received a port to the PlayStation 2 titled Mercury Meltdown Remix released a month after the original, with improved graphics, new levels and optimized for PlayStation 2's controller. A second port for the Wii titled Mercury Meltdown Revolution was released in 2007 and also changing the levels, improving the graphics further from the Remix version and making use of Wii's motion controls.

All versions of Mercury Meltdown were well-received among critics. The game was praised by critics for being an overall improvement from the original in terms of difficulty and art style. Mercury Meltdown Remix received mixed reviews in regards to the PS2 controls with some criticism of removing multiplayer. Mercury Meltdown Revolution also criticized for lack of multiplayer but was praised for its motion controls.

Tulane University Law School is the law school of Tulane University in New Orleans, Louisiana. Established in 1847, it is the 12th oldest law school in the U.S. and offers Juris Doctor (J.D.), Master of Laws (LL.M.), and Doctor of Juridical Science (S.J.D.) degrees. The school is known for its programs in maritime law, environmental law, and international and comparative law, as well as joint degree programs such as JD/MBA and JD/MA in Latin American Studies.  

Housed in John Giffen Weinmann Hall on Tulane’s Uptown campus, the law school features a strong clinical program, study abroad opportunities, and a pro bono graduation requirement. It ranks 55th in the 2023 U.S. News & World Report law school rankings and has a first-time Louisiana bar passage rate of 84.3% (2021). Notable alumni include legal scholars, judges, and government officials.

The Yankee Dutch crossing is a complex square dance involving 16 dancers arranged in four pairs of couples. The dance consists of four major parts, each with two minor segments, where dancers alternate between interacting with adjacent groups and diagonally opposite partners. The formation divides into two teams of eight, with one team moving along the square's diagonals (the "Dutch crossing") while the other performs a grand right and left around the perimeter. Roles alternate between teams as the dance progresses, often leading to disintegration before completion—a characteristic part of its challenge and appeal.

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC) is the national high-performance computing centre in Ireland. It was established in 2005 and provides supercomputing resources, support, training and related services. ICHEC is involved in education and training, including providing courses for researchers.

"Cookie" is a song by South Korean girl group NewJeans, released on August 1, 2022, as the third single from their debut EP *New Jeans*. The track, described as a "weightless groove," faced controversy over alleged sexual innuendos in its lyrics, which the group's agency denied.
Johan Fredrik Göstasson (Gson) Bergenstråhle (23 September 1926 – 10 February 2005) was a Swedish diplomat.
Lugazi University was a private university in Uganda, operating from 2007 to 2010 before its closure and conversion into a military institution.

The *George Washington* bust is a 1932 plaster copy of Jean-Antoine Houdon's original 1785 sculpture, displayed at the Indiana Statehouse. Created for the George Washington Bicentennial, it depicts Washington in a classical toga, gazing upward. The bust, measuring 25 × 18 × 18 inches (64 × 46 × 46 cm), is located on the Statehouse's fourth floor. Houdon's original was sculpted from life at Mount Vernon.

Richardt Strauss (born 28 January 1986) is a former professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs and played for the Cheetahs in Super Rugby before moving to Ireland to play for Leinster Rugby in 2009. After completing three years' residence in Ireland, Strauss qualified to play for the Ireland national team; he made 17 appearances between 2012 and 2016, including three at the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He is the brother of South Africa international Andries Strauss and the cousin of South Africa international Adriaan Strauss.

The Swiss Social Archives (Lang-de, Lang-fr) in Zurich is a historical archive, an academic library, a collection of documentation and a research facility specialising in social issues and social movements. The Swiss Confederation recognizes the archives as the country's leading research facility for social issues and social movements. The Social Archives run their own research endowment fund, the Ellen Rifkin Hill Foundation. The SSA play an important role in communicating scholarship to the broader community. They have convened several exhibitions, published essay collections on Swiss social history and conduct lecture series, presentations and information sessions. The archives work with Swiss secondary and tertiary educational institutions, archives and libraries and with similar institutions abroad. The SSA are a founding member of the International Association of Labour History Institutions (IALHI).
Space Under Sun is the second album by American singer, Ari Gold.
Air Vice-Marshal Paul Douglas Luker, CB, OBE, AFC, DL is a former Royal Air Force officer who served as Commander of Joint Helicopter Command from 2002 to 2005.

Tbilisoba (Georgian: თბილისობა) is an annual festival in Tbilisi, Georgia, celebrating the city's history and culture. Established in 1979 under Soviet rule to promote socialist traditions, it became a popular event highlighting Georgian heritage. After a pause in the early 1990s due to civil unrest, it was revived in 1995 and is now held every October, featuring music, food, and traditional performances.

Sianto Sikawa (born 1990) is a Kenyan tourism coordinator, fashion model, and philanthropist. A former Miss Tourism Kenya, she works as Narok County's tourism marketing coordinator and promotes solar energy initiatives in her community.

Ottery St Mary, known as "Ottery", is a town and civil parish in the East Devon district of Devon, England, on the River Otter, about 10 miles (16 km) east of Exeter on the B3174. At the 2001 census, the parish, which includes the villages of Metcombe, Fairmile, Alfington, Tipton St John, Wiggaton, and (until 2017) West Hill, had a population of 7,692. The population of the urban area alone at the 2011 census was 4,898.

There are two electoral wards in Ottery (Rural and Town). The total population of both wards, including the adjacent civil parish of Aylesbeare, at the 2011 census was 9,022.

The town as it now stands has several independent shops, mainly in Mill Street, Silver Street and Yonder Street. An area known as 'The Square', is the heart of Ottery St Mary. There are pubs, restaurants, and coffee and tea rooms. Ottery provides services, employment, and a wide range of shopping for local residents and visitors from nearby villages and towns.

Ahmed Javed (born 2 January 1956) is an Indian diplomat and retired police officer who served as India's Ambassador to Saudi Arabia (2015–2019) and as Commissioner of Mumbai Police (2015–2016). A 1980-batch IPS officer, he held key positions in Maharashtra Police, including Joint Commissioner (Law & Order) and Director General of Police (Home Guards). He is known for modernizing Mumbai Police's public outreach, including launching its official Twitter handle.

Scott L. Thoele is a United States Army officer and veteran of the Gulf War, Iraq War, and War in Afghanistan. Commissioned in 1980, he served with the 82nd Airborne Division, 12th Special Forces Group, and later commanded in the 20th Special Forces Group. His decorations include the Legion of Merit, Bronze Star, and Combat Infantryman Badge. As a civilian, he works as a vice president and credit analyst at First Bankers Trust Company in Quincy, Illinois. He holds degrees from Quincy University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

Hatton Castle stands on the lower part of Hatton Hill, the most easterly of the Sidlaw Hills, to the south of Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. The castle overlooks the wooded Den of Newtyle, and its views extend across Strathmore and include Ben Lawers and Schiehallion as well as the Angus and Glenshee hills. The 16th-century castle was originally built in a typical Scottish "Z plan" tower house design, as a fortified country house or château. There was an earlier castle called Balcraig Castle which stood less than half a mile from the present building, also on Hatton Hill.

Alturas de Macchu Picchu is a studio album by the Chilean rock band Los Jaivas, released in October 1981 on SyM label. It is a concept album that musicalizes the homonymous poem by Pablo Neruda that appeared in his 1950 book Canto General. The album mixed electric, electronic and folk instruments. Prior to its launch, an television special recorded in the ruins of Machu Picchu was presented on Chilean and Peruvian television.
Oto Horvat is a writer whose works include poetry and novels, notably *Sabo je stao*, which won literary awards in 2015.

The Anatolian diagonal is a theoretical dividing line which runs diagonally across central and eastern Turkey from the northeastern corner of the Mediterranean Sea to the southeastern corner of the Black Sea, and roughly cuts across part of the course of the upper Euphrates River within Turkey's borders.

JoBeth Williams (born December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director. Known for her roles in films such as *Poltergeist* (1982), *The Big Chill* (1983), and *Kramer vs. Kramer* (1979), she has also appeared in *Stir Crazy* (1980), *The Day After* (1983), and *Dutch* (1991). Williams earned Emmy nominations for her performances in the TV movies *Adam* (1983) and *Baby M* (1988), as well as a guest role on *Frasier*. She made her directorial debut with the Oscar-nominated short film *On Hope* (1994). On television, she has had recurring roles in *Dexter* (2007), *Private Practice* (2009–2013), and *Extant* (2014). Williams is married to director John Pasquin, with whom she has two children.

Hopanoids are a diverse class of pentacyclic triterpenoid lipids produced by many bacteria, where they play roles in membrane stability, permeability, and stress tolerance. Structurally similar to eukaryotic sterols like cholesterol, hopanoids modify membrane properties by increasing rigidity and reducing permeability. They are synthesized from squalene via the enzyme squalene-hopene cyclase (SHC) and can undergo further functionalization, including methylation at C2 and C3 positions, which has made them useful as biomarkers in paleobiology.  

Hopanoids are among the most abundant natural products on Earth and persist in sediments as molecular fossils (hopanes). Their presence in ancient rocks has been used to study early life, though their interpretation as biomarkers for cyanobacteria and oxygenic photosynthesis has been debated. Some bacteria, including nitrogen-fixing and acid-tolerant species, produce hopanoids to adapt to extreme conditions. Beyond their biological roles, hopanoids have industrial and agricultural applications, including biofertilizers and biocatalysts. Additionally, they influence bacterial multidrug resistance by modulating membrane transport systems.

Randy Kaplan is an American musician, songwriter, and actor known for his work in children's music, blending folk, blues, and comedic storytelling. Born in Dix Hills, New York, he studied at the University of Michigan and UCLA before pursuing acting and music. Kaplan fronted the band *i* in Los Angeles in the 1990s and later released solo albums, including *Boyish Hips* (1997).  

Transitioning to children's music in 2005, Kaplan gained acclaim for his family-friendly albums such as *Five Cent Piece* (2006) and *The Kids Are All ID* (2010), which earned awards from NAPPA and Nickelodeon. His work has been praised by *Time Out New York Kids* and *People* for its wit and musical depth. Kaplan also performs covers of blues, ragtime, and Broadway tunes in live shows.  

Beyond children's music, he has released adult-oriented albums like *Songs for Old Lovers* (2011) and *Ancient Ruins* (2008), showcasing his eclectic style. Kaplan has collaborated with artists like Mike West and Brian Schey and continues to tour nationally. He resides in Michigan with his family.

Mansfield is a borough in Tioga County, Pennsylvania, United States. Settled in 1804 and incorporated in 1857, it is home to Mansfield University of Pennsylvania. The borough is notable for hosting the first night football game under electric lights in 1892. Its population was 2,839 as of the 2020 census.

Janice Charette is a Canadian civil servant and diplomat who has served as Clerk of the Privy Council and Secretary to the Cabinet since 2021, previously holding the role from 2014 to 2016. She was Canada’s High Commissioner to the United Kingdom (2016–2021) and has held senior positions in multiple federal departments. A graduate of Carleton University, she is the second woman to serve as Canada’s top civil servant.

Hallie Flanagan Davis (August 27, 1889 in Redfield, South Dakota – June 23, 1969 in Old Tappan, New Jersey) was an American theatrical producer and director, playwright, and author, best known as director of the Federal Theatre Project, a part of the Works Progress Administration (WPA).

Saint Overboard is the title of a 1936 mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, one of a long series of novels featuring Charteris' creation Simon Templar, alias "The Saint". An edited version was previously published in November 1935 in The American Magazine as The Pirate Saint. Some paperback editions append the article The to the title (The Saint Overboard).

The book was first published in the United Kingdom by Hodder & Stoughton in 1936 (followed by an American edition by The Crime Club).

Alberta Ballet (also known as the Alberta Ballet Company) was founded by Muriel Taylor and Dr. Ruth Carse in 1958 and became a professional company in 1966. The company is a resident company of both the Northern Alberta Jubilee Auditorium in Edmonton, Alberta and the Southern Alberta Jubilee Auditorium in Calgary, Alberta and performs its full season in both venues.

Aussie Gold Hunters is an Australian factual television series that follows gold miners across Western Australia, Victoria, and Queensland as they use various techniques—including metal detecting, sluicing, and dry blowing—to reach their seasonal gold targets. Premiering on Discovery Channel in 2016, the show has aired seven seasons and over 100 episodes. Produced by Electric Pictures, it has become a ratings success in Australia and internationally, ranking as one of Foxtel's top factual programs and a leading show on the UK's Quest channel.

Phussa (Pali: Phussa; Sanskrit: Puṣya) is one of the ancient Buddhas recognized in Theravada and Mahayana Buddhism. Born in Kāsika to King Jayasena and Queen Sirimā, he renounced his throne after ruling for 9,000 years and attained enlightenment under a Maha Bodhi tree. According to tradition, he predicted that King Vijitāvī (a past life of Gautama Buddha) would become a Buddha in the future. Phussa lived for 90,000 years before attaining parinirvana.

The SS William G. Mather (Official Number 224850) is a retired Great Lakes bulk freighter now restored as a museum ship in Cleveland, Ohio, one of five in the Great Lakes region. She transported cargo such as ore, coal, stone, and grain to ports throughout the Great Lakes, and was nicknamed "The Ship That Built Cleveland" because Cleveland's steel mills were a frequent destination.
Elfonda Mekel, SH, (born 21 May 1970), better known by his stage name Once Mekel (IPA-id, pronounced Ohn-chè), is an Indonesian singer and former lead vocalist of Dewa 19.

Barbil is a city and a Municipal Council in the Kendujhar district (also known as Iron City) of the state of Odisha, India. The region around Barbil has one of the largest deposit of iron ore and manganese ore in the world. It is a major source of revenue generation for both the Central and the State Governments.

According to sources, 45% to 48% ST and SCs are living here.It is a fifth scheduled area under the constitution of india.

Rangi Ruru Girls' School is an independent Presbyterian girls' school in Christchurch, New Zealand, offering Years 7–13 education with boarding facilities. Founded in 1889, it moved to its current Merivale site in 1923. The school is known for high academic achievement, consistently exceeding national averages in NCEA results.

Elizabeth Swaney (born July 30, 1984) is an American-born freestyle skier who competed for Hungary at the 2018 Winter Olympics in halfpipe skiing. She qualified by strategically entering events with few competitors, avoiding falls, and benefiting from Olympic quota rules. Her conservative Olympic runs, scoring among the lowest, sparked debate over qualification standards. Before skiing, she coxed for UC Berkeley's men's rowing team and briefly pursued skeleton and bobsleigh.

The Alligator Rivers are a system of three perennial rivers—the East Alligator, South Alligator, and West Alligator—located in the Northern Territory of Australia. Flowing through the Arnhem Land Plateau and into the Van Diemen Gulf, these rivers are part of the Kakadu National Park and are known for their rich biodiversity, including crocodiles, diverse birdlife, and unique ecosystems. The region has a monsoon climate, with distinct wet and dry seasons shaping its floodplains and wetlands.  

The area holds deep cultural significance for Aboriginal peoples, such as the Gagudju, Jawoyn, and Kunwinjku, who have inhabited the region for over 50,000 years, leaving behind rock art and sacred sites. European exploration began in the 19th century, with Phillip Parker King naming the rivers after mistaking crocodiles for alligators. Later, buffalo hunting, cattle stations, and uranium mining shaped the region's economic history. Today, the rivers remain vital to both ecological conservation and Indigenous heritage.

Michael O'Loughlin (born 20 February 1977) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Sydney Swans in the Australian Football League (AFL). A dual All-Australian (1997, 2000) and 2005 premiership player, he was the club's games record holder (303) until surpassed by his cousin Adam Goodes. Known for his skill as a forward, O'Loughlin won Sydney's best and fairest award in 1998 and twice led the club in goalkicking (2000–01). He was inducted into the Indigenous Team of the Century and later coached the Indigenous All-Stars. Off-field, he co-founded the GO Foundation, supporting Indigenous education. His career was briefly overshadowed by an unproven off-field controversy in 2000.
The Matt Lucas Awards was a British comedy entertainment show hosted by Matt Lucas, airing on BBC One from 2012 to 2013, featuring celebrity guests in humorous award categories.

State Route 202 (SR 202) is a state highway in the U.S. state of Washington, serving part of the Seattle metropolitan area.
