It runs southeasterly for 31 miles (50 km) in the Eastside region of King County, connecting Woodinville, Redmond, Fall City, and North Bend. The highway begins at SR 522 in Woodinville, intersects SR 520 in Redmond and SR 203 in Fall City, and terminates at Interstate 90 (I-90) in North Bend. The entire highway is designated as the Cascade Valleys Scenic Byway, a state scenic and recreational highway.

Railroads and roads in the Sammamish River and Snoqualmie River valleys, where modern-day SR 202 runs, were built during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The early roads were incorporated into the Yellowstone Trail in the 1910s and became a state highway in 1925. SR 202 was established in 1964 as part of a new state highway system, running from Woodinville to Monroe; SR 522 was designated to the Woodinville–North Bend highway, continuing west to Seattle. By 1970, the designations were reversed, with SR 202 moved to the Woodinville–North Bend highway, and SR 522 moved to the Bothell–Monroe highway.

A handscroll is a traditional East Asian format for displaying paintings or calligraphy, designed to be unrolled horizontally for viewing. Originating in China by the first century CE, it evolved from bamboo and wooden slip documents, later transitioning to silk and paper. Handscrolls typically consist of a frontispiece, artwork, and colophon section, often mounted with decorative silk and wooden rollers for storage. Introduced to Japan as *emakimono*, they became prominent in Buddhist narratives. This format remained dominant in Chinese art until the Tang dynasty (618–907).

Helene P. Foley is an American classicist and professor at Barnard College, Columbia University. A scholar of Greek tragedy, she served as president of the American Philological Association (1998) and as Sather Professor at UC Berkeley (2008). Her work explores modern adaptations of Greek drama, including themes of race, gender, and identity. She holds degrees from Swarthmore, Yale, and Harvard.

Robin Tyler (born Arlene Chernick, April 8, 1942) is the first lesbian or gay comic to come out on national television, a feminist and pioneer in the grassroots struggle for LGBTQ civil rights and marriage equality in the U.S., and a producer. She emceed and produced the main stage at three marches on Washington for LGBTQ rights, including the historic first National March On Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights in 1979. Tyler coined the phrase "We are everywhere" as a rallying cry for the LGBTQ community. This became the powerful signature chant of the 1979 march, appearing on banners and posters. The chant "We are everywhere" has continued as a popular rallying cry for LGBTQ equality.

Bilston is a village in Midlothian, Scotland, with a population of 1,440 (2022). Historically tied to coal mining, it lies near Bilston Glen, a former colliery, and features Dryden Tower, a 19th-century folly. The area includes woodland designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). A proposed bypass through the SSSI sparked a long-running protest camp.

Ariyalur (IPA-ta) is a town and district headquarters of Ariyalur district in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu and is rich in limestone, surrounded with seven cement factories and two sugar factories. The town is located at a distance of 310 km (190 mi) from the state capital Chennai.

Ariyalur was a part of the erstwhile Trichirapally District until India's independence in 1947 and Tiruchirappalli district until 1995, Perambalur district until 2007 and subsequently a part of the newly formed Ariyalur district. The town is a part of the fertile Cauvery Delta and the major profession in the town is agriculture.

Ariyalur is administered by a municipality established in 1994. As of 2011, the municipality covered an area of 7.62 km2 (2.94 sq mi) and had a population of 28,902. Ariyalur comes under the Ariyalur assembly constituency which elects a member to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly once every five years and it is a part of the Chidambaram constituency which elects its Member of Parliament (MP) once in five years. Roadways are the major mode of transportation to the town and it also has rail connectivity which acts as a main station after Tiruchirappalli junction. The nearest seaport is Karaikal port, located 95 km (59 mi) away, while the nearest airport is the Tiruchirappalli International Airport, located 76 km (47 mi) away from the town.

Joanna Mytkowska (born 1970) is a Polish curator, art critic, and museum director. She curated the Polish Pavilion at the 51st Venice Biennale (2005) and organized exhibitions at the Centre Pompidou. A member of the Documenta 14 selection committee, she received the 2018 Igor Zabel Award for her contributions to contemporary art.

Severn FM is an internet radio station in the United Kingdom, which started broadcasting online in June 2011, and officially launched from its studio in Hardwicke on 3 December 2011 with the Severn Bore Breakfast Show, the launch coincided with its first 28-day RSL broadcast on 87.9 FM. The brainchild of Andy Clarke (Managing Director of South Gloucester Media CIC and Ringtone.net Ltd) who used his skills gained as a mobile network consultant, it initially followed the standard community radio model but has evolved along the way under the leadership of Andy and his Board of Directors (Barry Kirby, Caro Kingsnorth and Fr Andrew James) along with the presenters and other volunteers who form part of this growing and evolving South Gloucester "Community Project".

Severn FM is a 24/7 Internet Radio Station broadcasting on the internet.

Georgia Blanche Douglas Camp Johnson, better known as Georgia Douglas Johnson (September 10, 1880 – May 15, 1966), was a poet. She was one of the earliest female African American playwrights, and an important figure of the Harlem Renaissance.

Annihilation in particle physics refers to the process in which a particle and its corresponding antiparticle collide and convert into other particles, often photons or mesons, releasing energy. The simplest case is electron–positron annihilation, typically producing two or more gamma-ray photons, conserving momentum and energy. For proton–antiproton annihilation, the composite nature of baryons leads to complex hadronization, producing multiple mesons (such as pions and kaons) that later decay. High-energy collisions, such as those in the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), can also result in Higgs boson production through quark-antiquark annihilation or gluon fusion. Annihilation processes are fundamental in particle physics, contributing to phenomena such as matter-antimatter asymmetry and nuclear interactions.

The Secret of Roan Inish is a 1994 fantasy drama film directed by John Sayles, based on Rosalie K. Fry's novel *Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry*. Set in 1946 Ireland, it follows Fiona (Jeni Courtney), a young girl sent to live with her grandparents in a coastal village. Through local folklore, she learns of her family’s ties to Roan Inish (Seal Island) and the legend of the selkies—seals that can transform into humans. Convinced her missing baby brother was taken by the seals, Fiona and her cousin work to restore the family’s abandoned island home, hoping to reunite with him.  

Filmed in County Donegal, Ireland, and the Isle of Mull, Scotland, the movie blends myth and reality with cinematography by Haskell Wexler. It received critical acclaim for its lyrical storytelling and atmospheric visuals, holding a 96% rating on Rotten Tomatoes. A restored version was released in 2020 by the UCLA Film & Television Archive.

WiQuest Communications, Inc., was a fabless semiconductor company that designed and developed Wireless USB (WUSB) products using WiMedia Alliance technology for high-speed, short-range applications. WiQuest offered integrated circuits, software, and reference designs. WiQuest was headquartered in Allen, Texas, and had operations in Bangalore, India.

Margarita Iosifovna Aliger (October 7, 1915 – August 1, 1992) was a Soviet poet known for her wartime verse, including the famous poem *"Zoya"* (1942) about Zoya Kosmodemyanskaya. Her works blended patriotic themes with personal and reflective poetry. She survived personal tragedies, including the loss of her children, and was buried in Peredelkino.

Washington State Route 202 (SR 202) is a 30.53-mile (49.13 km) state highway in King County, Washington, connecting Woodinville to North Bend. Designated as part of the Cascade Valleys Scenic Byway, it follows a scenic route through the Sammamish River Valley, Redmond, Fall City, and Snoqualmie, before terminating at Interstate 90 (I-90).  

Originally part of early 20th-century wagon roads and later the Yellowstone Trail, the route was incorporated into the state highway system in 1925. After highway renumbering in 1964, it became SR 202, replacing a segment of Primary State Highway 2. The route was extended in 1978 to its current terminus at I-90.  

SR 202 passes through wine country, Marymoor Park, and Snoqualmie Falls, with traffic volumes ranging from 40,000 vehicles daily near Woodinville to 7,500 in rural areas. The Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT) has implemented roundabouts and widening projects to improve safety and traffic flow.

The College of Dental Medicine is the dental school of the Medical University of South Carolina. It is located in the city of Charleston, South Carolina, United States. It is the only dental school in South Carolina.

A mercy rule, slaughter rule, knockout rule, or skunk rule ends a two-competitor sports competition earlier than the scheduled endpoint if one competitor has a very large and presumably insurmountable scoring lead over the other. It is called the mercy rule because it spares further humiliation for the loser. It is common in youth sports in North America, where running up the score is considered unsporting. It is especially common in baseball and softball in which there is no game clock and a dominant team could in theory continue an inning endlessly.

The rules vary widely, depending on the level of competition, but nearly all youth sports leagues and high school sports associations and many college sports associations in the United States have mercy rules for sports including baseball, softball, American football and association football.

However, mercy rules usually do not take effect until a prescribed point in the game (like the second half of an association football game). Thus, one team, particularly if it is decidedly better than a weaker opponent, can still "run up the score" before the rule takes effect. For instance, in American football, one team could be ahead by 70 points with three minutes left in the first half; in baseball, the better team could have a 20-run lead in the second inning, but the game would still continue.
Unison is an unincorporated community in Loudoun County, Virginia. Founded by Quakers in 1740, it was the site of Civil War skirmishes in 1862 and is now part of the Unison Historic District.

UFOria is a science fiction comedy film written and directed by John Binder and starring Fred Ward, Harry Dean Stanton, Harry Carey, Jr. and Cindy Williams. The film includes small appearances by Peggy McKay, Joe Unger, Hank Worden, Charlotte Stewart, and Alan Beckwith. Filming was completed in 1981, but the film was not theatrically released until 1985. It was released only on VHS in 1987 by MCA Home Video, now known as Universal Studios Home Entertainment.

Eleanor Spencer Stone Perényi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was a gardener and author. She wrote several books including Green Thoughts, a collection of essays based on her own gardening experiences.

Daniel Isn't Real is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer, from a screenplay by Mortimer and Brian DeLeeuw, based upon the novel In This Way I Was Saved by DeLeeuw. It stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, Mary Stuart Masterson, Hannah Marks, Chukwudi Iwuji and Peter McRobbie.

Daniel Isn't Real had its world premiere at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019. It was released on December 6, 2019, by Samuel Goldwyn Films in select theaters and digitally.

Acetoin, also known as 3-hydroxybutanone or acetyl methyl carbinol, is an organic compound with the formula CH3CH(OH)C(O)CH3. It is a colorless liquid with a pleasant, buttery odor. It is chiral. The form produced by bacteria is (R)-acetoin.
Sam Patrello (October 24, 1934 – August 15, 2009) was an American nightclub and movie comedian best known as a Jerry Lewis imitator.

Melchior Inchofer or Imhofer, in Hungarian: Inchofer Menyhért (c. 1584 – 28 September 1648) was an Austrian-Hungarian Jesuit. He played an important part in the trial of Galileo, by his arguments, later published in his Tractatus Syllepticus, that Galileo was an advocate of the Copernican system. His role in the Galileo affair is being reassessed in the light of fresh documentary evidence.

Villa Park is a neighborhood in Denver, Colorado. Its hilly topography provides panoramic views of downtown Denver and of the Rocky Mountains, particularly from Lakewood / Dry Gulch Park, which divides the neighborhood diagonally.

The Twelve Apostles is a collection of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park, by the Great Ocean Road in Victoria, Australia.

Their proximity to one another has made the site a popular tourist attraction. Seven of the original eight stacks remain standing at the Twelve Apostles viewpoint, after one collapsed in July 2005. Though the view from the promontory by the Twelve Apostles never included twelve stacks, additional stacks—not considered part of the Apostles group—are located to the west within the national park.

Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography written in 1896 by the Gaudiya Vaishnava scholar Bhaktivinoda Thakur at the request of his son, Lalita Prasad. Covering 56 years of his life, the work candidly describes Bhaktivinoda's struggles with poverty, health, doubt, and spiritual seeking before his eventual commitment to the teachings of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Notably, the text avoids self-glorification, instead portraying Bhaktivinoda with humility and introspection. Published posthumously in 1916 by Lalita Prasad, *Svalikhita-jivani* offers an honest account of his spiritual journey without claiming extraordinary sanctity or divine inspiration.

Beautiful Creatures is the debut studio album by American hard rock band Beautiful Creatures, released on August 16, 2001, by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by Sean Beavan, the album features a heavy metal and hard rock sound, drawing comparisons to Guns N' Roses. The band, formed by former BulletBoys guitarist DJ Ashba and Bang Tango vocalist Joe LeSte, gained attention after opening for Kiss. Reviews were mixed, with praise for its energetic rock style but criticism for its lack of originality. The lead single, "Wasted," was released in 2001. The album was followed by *Deuce* in 2005.

*Loop* (ループ, *Rūpu*) is a 1998 horror novel by Japanese writer Kōji Suzuki, the third book in the *Ring* series. It follows medical student Kaoru Futami as he investigates a deadly virus linked to a virtual reality experiment called the LOOP project. After discovering his own connection to the simulation, Kaoru must enter the digital world to stop the virus from spreading. Blending science fiction and horror, the novel explores themes of artificial life, disease, and reality. It was translated into English in 2006.

Uriah Francis Abshier (September 17, 1849 – May 23, 1934) was a state legislator from the state of Oregon. He served one two-year term in the Oregon House of Representatives as a Republican legislator, representing a large rural district in south-central Oregon. He was the postmaster for Silver Lake, Oregon and owned ranch property near that town. Later, he ran a hardware store in Centerville, Washington.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (December 19, 1874 – March 28, 1965) was the author of numerous theological writings and a Bible publisher.

Knoch founded the Concordant Publishing Concern and translated the Concordant Version of the Bible.

Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a distinguished Brazilian engineer and a signal processing researcher at Microsoft Research's largest laboratory in Redmond, Washington, United States. He was the managing director of the lab following the departure of long-time Managing Director Dan Ling in 2007, when he oversaw about 350 researchers. Currently, he is a Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft Research, heading a team that develops new user experiences with new devices and new input/output interaction modes, with special attention to inclusiveness and empowering people with disabilities.

Rico is also an Affiliate Professor at the Electrical and Computer Engineering Department at the University of Washington.
Hassan Askiali Haskins Jr. (born November 26, 1999) is an American football running back for the Tennessee Titans of the National Football League (NFL). He played college football at Michigan.

Dolphin Tale 2 is a 2014 American family drama film directed by Charles Martin Smith and a sequel to *Dolphin Tale* (2011). The film follows Sawyer Nelson (Nathan Gamble) and the staff of Clearwater Marine Aquarium as they rescue another dolphin, Mandy, while facing a crisis when Winter, their tailless dolphin, loses her companion. Under pressure from a USDA inspector, they must find Winter a new companion or risk her relocation. Meanwhile, Sawyer grapples with accepting a prestigious marine research scholarship. The film stars Harry Connick Jr., Ashley Judd, Morgan Freeman, and Kris Kristofferson, reprising their roles from the first film.  

Produced by Alcon Entertainment and distributed by Warner Bros., *Dolphin Tale 2* received mixed-to-positive reviews, praising its family-friendly appeal. It grossed $57.8 million worldwide against a $36 million budget. The film includes real-life footage of rescued marine animals, reinforcing its conservation themes. A potential third film was discussed but never materialized following the death of Winter in 2021.

The Ma Tante River is a tributary of the Franquelin River, flowing in the township of Franquelin, in the municipality of Franquelin, in the Manicouagan Regional County Municipality, in the administrative region of Côte-Nord, in the province of Quebec, in Canada.

Forestry is the main economic activity in this valley; recreational tourism, second.

The surface of this Middle North Shore river is usually frozen from the beginning of November to the end of April, except the rapids; however, safe circulation on the ice is generally from late November to early April.
John Patrick (born February 29, 1968) is an American basketball coach and former player, currently coaching the Chiba Jets Funabashi in Japan's B.League.
Odysseus is a 1980 tabletop role-playing game set in ancient Greece, published by Fantasy Games Unlimited.

SMS *Frankfurt* was a *Wiesbaden*-class light cruiser built for the Imperial German Navy during World War I. Launched in 1915, she participated in several major naval engagements, including the Bombardment of Yarmouth and Lowestoft and the Battle of Jutland, where she served as the flagship of II Scouting Group. She later took part in Operation Albion in the Baltic and the Second Battle of Heligoland Bight.  

After the war, *Frankfurt* was interned at Scapa Flow with the rest of the High Seas Fleet. She survived the scuttling of the fleet in 1919 when British forces beached her before she could sink. Transferred to the United States Navy as a war prize, she was commissioned as USS *Frankfurt* in 1920. The U.S. Navy studied her design before using her as a target ship in bombing tests conducted by General Billy Mitchell in 1921. She was sunk by aerial bombs on 18 July 1921 off Cape Henry, Virginia.

The center of gravity (CG) of an aircraft is a critical factor in flight stability and control. It must remain within manufacturer-specified limits to ensure safe operation. The CG is calculated along the longitudinal axis (fore-aft balance), and in some helicopters, lateral balance is also considered. An improperly loaded aircraft—either overweight or with a CG outside allowable limits—can lead to severe handling issues, reduced control effectiveness, or even loss of flight control. Fuel consumption and weight distribution must be carefully managed to maintain balance throughout the flight.

Amanda Jaqueline Paswall (née DaCosta; born 7 October 1989) is an American-born Portuguese former professional footballer who played as a midfielder for Washington Spirit, Chicago Red Stars and Boston Breakers in the National Women's Soccer League (NWSL), Liverpool Ladies in England's FA WSL, the Washington Freedom and MagicJack in the Women's Professional Soccer (WPS) and for the Portuguese national team.

Argentina at the 1960 Summer Paralympics competed in its first Paralympic Games in Rome, sending five swimmers who all won medals. The team earned two gold, three silver, and one bronze, finishing 10th in the medal standings. Notable performances included Sznitowski's gold in the men's 50m backstroke (class 5) and Perazzo's gold in the women's 50m crawl (class 3). All athletes competed in wheelchair events, as the 1960 Games were exclusively for athletes with spinal cord injuries.

Robert Altman (died September 24, 2021) was an American photographer best known for documenting the counterculture of the 1960s, including the Woodstock festival, the Summer of Love, and the rock music scene. His work captured the spirit of the era, portraying musicians, fans, and the free-spirited atmosphere of the time.  

Altman's photographs have been exhibited at venues such as Abbey Road Studios, the Newseum, and the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. His images are part of permanent collections at institutions including the Smithsonian, the Library of Congress, and the San Francisco Public Library. In 2009, his work was featured in Macy's "Art Under Glass" series in New York City.  

His book *The Sixties: Photographs* (2007) serves as a key visual record of the decade, emphasizing themes of freedom, music, and social change. Influenced by Ansel Adams, Altman's style focused on raw, candid moments, often using unique angles to convey emotion. He received an honorary doctorate from Digital Media Arts College in 2010.  

Altman died in 2021 from esophageal cancer. His legacy endures as a chronicler of 1960s culture.

Piero di Cosimo (2 January 1462 – 12 April 1522) was an Italian Renaissance painter known for his imaginative mythological scenes, detailed landscapes, and portraiture. Born in Florence, he trained under Cosimo Rosselli, assisting him in the Sistine Chapel. Influenced by Netherlandish art, particularly Hugo van der Goes, Piero developed a distinctive style marked by rich naturalism and fantastical elements, as seen in works like *The Death of Procris* and *Perseus Freeing Andromeda*. His later religious paintings, such as *The Immaculate Conception with Saints*, reflected the influence of Girolamo Savonarola.  

Piero was also a skilled portraitist, with his depiction of *Simonetta Vespucci* being among his most famous works. Described by Giorgio Vasari as eccentric—living in squalor and fearing thunderstorms—Piero's reputation for peculiarity has endured. He influenced contemporaries like Andrea del Sarto and died of plague in 1522. His legacy includes contributions to both secular and religious Renaissance art.

The giant skippers are butterflies in the disputed subfamily Megathyminae, which is part of the skipper family. Some authorities treat this as a distinct and separate subfamily, but more modern classifications tend to place them within the subfamily Hesperiinae. However, some works, such as the Integrated Taxonomic Information System, still treat is as a valid subfamily. The giant skippers include two tribes, Aegialini and Megathymini. There are three genera and about eighteen species in this subfamily. These butterflies typically live in the southwest United States and Mexico in desert areas.

WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial classical music radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York. Owned by Buffalo Toronto Public Media, it broadcasts at 94,000 watts, making it one of three grandfathered "superpower" FM stations in Buffalo. Its signal reaches parts of Western New York and southern Ontario, including Toronto.  

Originally signing on in 1960 as commercial station WEBR-FM, it underwent several call sign changes before becoming WNED-FM in 1976 under nonprofit ownership. Since 1977, it has aired a classical format, featuring local programming, collaborations with the Buffalo Philharmonic Orchestra, and listener-voted classical music rankings. The station also operates an HD Radio subchannel, JazzWorks, dedicated to jazz. Alongside sister stations WBFO and WNED-TV, it rebranded as "Buffalo Toronto Public Media" in 2020, reflecting its cross-border audience.

The United States Space Force's 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron (4 EWS) is an offensive space electromagnetic warfare unit formerly located at Holloman Air Force Base, New Mexico, stood up operations July 1, 2014 at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist, a designer of nuclear weapons, Doctor of Sciences, professor, Hero of Socialist Labour, Laureate of the Lenin Prize and the USSR State Prize.

The Maine Italian sandwich is a submarine-style sandwich made with a long bread roll, ham, American or provolone cheese, tomatoes, onions, green peppers, olives, and olive oil. Originating in Portland, Maine, it was popularized by Giovanni Amato, who sold it from his grocery cart before opening Amato's, a sandwich shop.

St. Mary's High School is a coeducational school in Belagavi, Karnataka, India, established in 1851. Originally founded for British soldiers' children, it later expanded to serve Indian students. The school offers education from LKG to SSLC (Grade 10) and features extracurricular activities such as NCC, sports, and academic competitions. It follows a house system and holds an annual passing-out parade for graduating students.

Rhydymwyn is a village in Flintshire, Wales, with a population of 537 (2011). Historically an industrial hub due to its limestone and mineral deposits, it housed the Valley Factory, a WWII mustard gas production site and part of the Tube Alloys nuclear project. The village features St. John the Evangelist Church (Grade II* listed) and the ancient Ffynnon leinw holy well. It also has connections to composer Felix Mendelssohn and author Charles Kingsley. Flood defenses were installed after severe flooding in 2000.

*Dance of the Tiger* is a 1980 novel by Björn Kurtén, set in Ice Age Scandinavia. It follows Tiger, a Cro-Magnon seeking revenge against Shelk, a hybrid tyrant. The story explores interactions between Neanderthals and Cro-Magnons, depicting cultural differences and interbreeding. The novel is noted for its scientific plausibility regarding early human coexistence.
The Mercedes-Benz S-Series (W06) was a successful line of sports cars produced from 1927-33 that bore the nickname, the "white elephants."

Ja Lama (Lang-mn, also known as Dambiijantsan, Lang-mn or Dambiijaa, Lang-mn; 1862–1922) was an adventurer and warlord of unknown birth and background who fought successive campaigns against the rule of the Qing dynasty in western Mongolia between 1890 and 1922. He claimed to be a Buddhist lama, though it is not clear whether he actually was one, as well as a grandson and later the reincarnation of Amursana, the Khoid-Oirat prince who led the last great Mongol uprising against the Qing in 1757. He was one of the commanders of Mongolian forces that liberated Khovd city from Qing control in 1912.
Drexel-Alvernon is a former census-designated place (CDP) in Pima County, Arizona, with a population of 4,192 as of the 2000 census.
Eileen Dennes (1 February 1898 – 22 January 1991) was an Irish-born actress of the silent era.
Oliver Bäte (born 1 March 1965) is a German business executive who is the CEO of Allianz, a position he has held since October 2014.

Progress MS-20 (Lang-ru), Russian production No.450, identified by NASA as Progress 81P, is a Progress spaceflight launched by Roscosmos to resupply the International Space Station (ISS). It is the 173rd flight of a Progress spacecraft.

Leslie Quirk (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004) was a Manx language activist and folklorist. A key figure in preserving the Manx language, he learned from the last native speakers and later taught new generations of learners. He worked with the Irish Folklore Commission and led the Manx Folk-Life Survey, documenting traditional Manx culture. Awarded the Reih Bleeaney Vanannan in 1997, Quirk played a vital role in the revival of Manx Gaelic.

A temporary crown (provisional crown, interim crown) is a temporary (short-term) crown used in dentistry. Like other interim restorations, it serves until a final (definitive) restoration can be inserted. Usually the temporary crown is constructed from acrylic resins (monomethacrylate-based/polymethacrylate-based) or, chemical-cure/light cure composite (dimethacrylate-based), although alternative systems using aluminium crown forms are occasionally used. Temporary crowns function to protect the tooth, prevent teeth shifting, provide cosmetics, shape the gum tissue properly, and prevent sensitivity.

Shikantaza (只管打坐, "just sitting") is a form of seated meditation in Zen Buddhism, emphasizing open awareness without focusing on any specific object. Rooted in the Chinese Caodong tradition of "silent illumination" (默照, *mòzhào*), it was popularized by the Japanese Zen master Dōgen. Unlike other meditation techniques, shikantaza involves sitting with full attention, free from goal-oriented effort. The practice is central to the Sōtō school, where it is considered an expression of inherent enlightenment. Dōgen described it as "single-minded sitting," reflecting direct awareness of reality.

Azerbaijan–France relations refer to the diplomatic, economic, and cultural ties between Azerbaijan and France. Established in 1992, bilateral relations have expanded through high-level visits, trade agreements, and cooperation in energy, defense, and education. France, as a co-chair of the OSCE Minsk Group, plays a role in mediating the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict. Cultural exchanges, including events organized by the Heydar Aliyev Foundation, and joint initiatives like the French-Azerbaijani University (UFAZ), further strengthen ties. Over 50 bilateral agreements have been signed, and numerous French companies operate in Azerbaijan.

Sam's Strip was a humorous comic strip created and produced by Mort Walker and Jerry Dumas. It was distributed by King Features Syndicate from October 2, 1961 to June 1, 1963. The series depended heavily on metahumor and appearances by famous comic-strip characters.

NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) is a constituency for Pakistan's National Assembly, covering the Hangu District. It was created from the former NA-16 (Hangu). The current member is Nadeem Khayal of Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI), who won the 2022 by-election following the death of Khayal Zaman Orakzai. The seat has historically seen close contests between PTI and JUI-F.

Ida S. Baker High School is a public high school in Cape Coral, Florida, serving grades 9–12. Established in 2004, it is part of the Lee County School District and named after Ida S. Baker, the first Black female high school principal in Lee County. The school offers programs in business, engineering, and ROTC.

Marie Fillunger (27 January 1850 – 23 December 1930) was an Austrian operatic singer and voice teacher, known for her performances of lieder and her long-term relationship with pianist Eugenie Schumann.
Dominic Michaelis (1938–2015) was a French architect and inventor known for pioneering solar-powered balloons and advocating renewable energy.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman and chairman of Delaware North. He has owned the Boston Bruins since 1975 and serves as chairman of the NHL Board of Governors. Under his ownership, the Bruins won the Stanley Cup in 2011.
Drexel-Alvernon was a census-designated place (CDP) in Pima County, Arizona, United States. The population was 4,192 at the 2000 census.

The Bora–Witoto languages are a proposed language family of northwestern Amazonia, linking the Boran and Witotoan families. The grouping remains controversial, with some linguists rejecting the connection. Andoque is sometimes included.

Maine's Italian sandwich, sometimes referred to as the Maine Italian sandwich, is an American submarine sandwich in Italian-American cuisine prepared on a long bread roll or bun with meats, cheese and various vegetables. The Maine Italian sandwich was supposedly invented in Portland, Maine.

Arenys de Mar (IPA-ca, arenys being Catalan for "sands (of a seasonal creek)", and de mar for "by the sea" as opposed to Arenys de Munt, "sands up hill") is one of the main municipalities of the comarca of Maresme, Barcelona Province, Catalonia, Spain. It is situated on the coast between Caldes d'Estrac and Canet de Mar, about 40 kilometres (25 miles) northeast of Barcelona. As of 2010, it had a population of 14,688.

Arenys' harbour was for many years the only existing port on the north coast of Barcelona. The beaches, framed by hills which run right down to the sea, give a preview of the neighbouring Costa Brava.
GE Power is a subsidiary of General Electric that manufactures power generation equipment, including gas turbines, nuclear reactors, and steam turbines.

Super Smash Bros. is a 1999 crossover fighting game developed by HAL Laboratory and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo 64. Directed by Masahiro Sakurai, it is the first entry in the Super Smash Bros. series, featuring characters from various Nintendo franchises. Unlike traditional fighting games, players aim to knock opponents off the stage rather than deplete a health bar, with damage increasing knockback. The game supports up to four players in multiplayer battles, with dynamic stages and items from Nintendo games influencing gameplay.  

Twelve playable characters are included, such as Mario, Link, Pikachu, and Donkey Kong, each with unique moves. The game also features single-player modes like Classic Mode and minigames such as "Break the Targets." Initially conceived as *Dragon King: The Fighting Game*, Sakurai later incorporated Nintendo characters to enhance appeal.  

Upon release, *Super Smash Bros.* received positive reviews for its innovative gameplay and multiplayer focus, though critics noted its limited single-player content. It was a commercial success, selling over 5 million copies worldwide and spawning a long-running franchise.

Yusef Lateef (born William Emanuel Huddleston; October 9, 1920 – December 23, 2013) was an American jazz multi-instrumentalist, composer, and educator known for blending Eastern and Western musical traditions. A pioneer of world music, he played tenor saxophone, flute, oboe, and various non-Western instruments, incorporating them into jazz. Born in Tennessee and raised in Detroit, he began his career in swing bands before converting to Islam in the 1950s and changing his name.  

Lateef gained prominence in the 1960s with albums like *Eastern Sounds* and collaborations with Cannonball Adderley. He rejected the label "jazz," preferring "autophysiopsychic music," and explored genres from post-bop to new age. His 1987 album *Yusef Lateef's Little Symphony* won a Grammy for Best New Age Album. A dedicated educator, he earned multiple degrees, including a doctorate, and taught at institutions like the University of Massachusetts Amherst.  

Lateef received the NEA Jazz Masters Fellowship in 2010 and continued performing until his death at 93. His legacy endures through his recordings, compositions, and influence on global music traditions.
Cary Castle was a 12th-century motte-and-bailey castle in Somerset, England, besieged during The Anarchy and later abandoned. Only earthworks remain today.

Sobelair (IATA: Q7) was a Belgian charter airline founded in 1946. Initially focused on cargo and African routes, it later shifted to holiday charters. Owned by Sabena (1949–2001) and later SN Brussels Airlines, it ceased operations in 2004 after financial struggles. Its fleet included Boeing 737s and 767s.

Angela Jackson is an American basketball coach and former player. She played guard at Odessa College and Old Dominion University before becoming a coach. She served as head coach at Saginaw Valley State and Chicago State, where she led her team to a conference championship and WBI Final Four appearance in 2011. Jackson emphasized academic success, with 91% of her players graduating. Despite some players turning professional, she was fired in 2018 after Chicago State's record-setting losing streak.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is an instrumental piece composed by Nino Rota for the 1972 film *The Godfather*. Originally written for the 1958 Italian film *Fortunella*, it gained fame as the film's romantic theme. The song, also known as *"Speak Softly Love"* with English lyrics by Larry Kusik, has been recorded in multiple languages. Though Rota's score was initially disqualified from an Academy Award due to its prior use, it became a chart success, with versions by Roger Williams, Andy Williams, and Al Martino reaching international popularity.

The 1973 World Snooker Championship was a professional snooker tournament held from 16 to 28 April 1973 at the City Exhibition Halls in Manchester, England. Sponsored by Park Drive, it featured a record 24 entrants and a £8,000 prize fund, with £1,500 awarded to the winner. Defending champion Alex Higgins was eliminated in the semi-finals by Eddie Charlton, while Ray Reardon staged a dramatic comeback from 12–19 down to defeat John Spencer 23–22 in the other semi-final.  

In the best-of-75 frames final, Reardon overcame Charlton 38–32 to claim his second world title, having previously won in 1970. The tournament marked a shift in snooker's popularity, with 25,000 spectators attending and partial BBC television coverage of the final. Reardon's victory was notable for his recovery from a 0–7 deficit in the opening session. The highest break of the event was 139, made by Spencer in the second round.

Rodney Bullock (born June 3, 1994) is an American professional basketball player for KB Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He played college basketball for Providence, earning Second-team All-Big East honors in 2017.
*Callulops fojaensis* is a frog species in the family Microhylidae, known only from the Foja Mountains of Indonesia. It inhabits mossy mid-montane forests at elevations around 1,600 m (5,200 ft).

William Forsythe (born June 7, 1955) is an American actor known for his roles in crime films and television. Born in Brooklyn, New York, he gained recognition for performances in *Once Upon a Time in America* (1984), *Raising Arizona* (1987), and *Dick Tracy* (1990). He later portrayed real-life figures such as Al Capone in *The Untouchables* (1993) and Sammy Gravano in *Gotti* (1996). Forsythe also starred in *Dear Mr. Gacy* (2010) as serial killer John Wayne Gacy. His career spans over four decades in film and television.

Susan Sonnenschein is a Professor of Psychology at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC), where she directs the Children and Families, Schooling and Development Lab. She has served as Graduate Program Director of the Applied Developmental Psychology doctoral program since 2006. Sonnenschein's research examines ways to promote the academic success of children. Her research explores the influence of both home and school, but with a focus on the associations between parental beliefs, practices, and children's academic development. She has edited and contributed to several books and is the author of over 70 peer-reviewed articles focusing on educational contexts of development for diverse populations.

Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a championship fight for the WBC Super Middleweight championship. The winner of the bout went on to the Finals of the Super Six World Boxing Classic and face WBA Champion Andre Ward, after his decision win over Arthur Abraham in May 2011. It was held on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall, in Atlantic City, New Jersey, United States, and televised on Showtime.

Carroll A. Campbell Jr. (July 24, 1940 – December 7, 2005) was an American Republican politician who served as the 112th governor of South Carolina from 1987 to 1995. Earlier, he was a U.S. Representative (1979–1987) and a state legislator. As governor, he led recovery efforts after Hurricane Hugo and attracted BMW’s first U.S. plant to South Carolina. He later chaired the National Governors Association (1993–1994). Campbell died from complications of Alzheimer's disease.

Larry Keel (born November 25, 1968 in Manassas, Virginia) is a bluegrass musician "reared in" Southwest Virginia who's been performing "seriously" since 1976. He was a founding member of Magraw Gap in 1990 with Danny Knicely and Will Lee. He performs with his wife, bassist Jenny Keel, in The Larry Keel Experience – and with other musicians in other groups. He has released over a dozen albums, the latest being Experienced in 2016, which he recorded with his group, Larry Keel Experience. He is married to his bass player, Jenny Keel.

Robinsons Summit Center, formerly known as the JG Summit Center, is an office skyscraper located in Makati, Philippines. It is owned by JG Summit Holdings, Inc., and developed by its real estate arm, Robinsons Land Corporation. It stands at 174 meters (571 ft), it is currently the 9th-tallest complete building in Makati, and is one of the tallest buildings in the Philippines.

Aqua is a Danish-Norwegian Europop and Dance-pop band, best known for their 1997 multi-platinum crossover and novelty single "Barbie Girl". The group formed in 1989 as Joyspeed and achieved international success around the globe in the late 1990s and early 2000s. The band released three albums: Aquarium in 1997, Aquarius in 2000 and Megalomania in 2011. The group sold an estimated 33 million albums and singles, making them the most profitable Danish band ever.

The group managed to top the UK Singles Chart with three of their singles. The group also caused controversy with the double entendres in their "Barbie Girl" single, with the Barbie doll makers Mattel filing a lawsuit against the group. The lawsuit was finally dismissed by a judge in 2002, who ruled "The parties are advised to chill".

The band's members are vocalists René Dif and Lene Nystrøm, keyboardist Søren Rasted, and guitarist Claus Norreen. During their split, Nystrøm, Dif and Rasted all achieved solo chart success, and Norreen continued in the music industry remixing other artists' material. At a press event on October 26, 2007, the group announced a reunion tour, as well as the release of a compilation album featuring new material. Their third album, Megalomania, was released on 3 October 2011.

Tales of Zestiria the X is a 2016–2017 anime television series produced by Ufotable, based on the *Tales of Zestiria* video game. Set in a world where humans and Seraphim once coexisted, the story follows a young man who can see Seraphim and dreams of restoring harmony between the two races. Directed by Haruo Sotozaki and featuring music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina, the series aired in two parts, with the first half premiering in July 2016 and the second in January 2017. The anime adapts elements from both *Tales of Zestiria* and *Tales of Berseria*, with most of the original game's voice cast reprising their roles. It was licensed internationally by Crunchyroll and Madman Entertainment.

George Washington's reception at Trenton occurred on April 21, 1789, during his journey to New York City for his presidential inauguration. Upon arriving in Trenton, New Jersey, he was greeted by a triumphal arch adorned with references to his Revolutionary War victories. Local women and children, dressed in white, sang and scattered flowers as he crossed the Assunpink Creek bridge. The event was later commemorated in art, including works by John Trumbull and N.C. Wyeth, and was reenacted during its 1989 bicentennial.
*Lodestar* is the 2016 comeback album by English folk singer Shirley Collins, released after a 38-year hiatus. Produced by Ian Kearey, it received widespread critical acclaim upon release.

*Great Bliss, Vol. 1* is a 1991 jazz album by saxophonist David S. Ware, recorded with his quartet featuring pianist Matthew Shipp, bassist William Parker, and drummer Marc Edwards. It marked the beginning of Ware's influential quartet and showcased his experimentation with multiple woodwind instruments, including flute and stritch.

The 2013 Kashiwa Reysol season was Kashiwa Reysol's 3rd season back in the J.League Division 1 since promotion in 2010, making it their 41st season in the top flight overall. They finished 10th in the league, won the J.League Cup and were knocked out of the Emperor's Cup and AFC Champions League at the fourth round Semifinals respectively.

General Motors Thailand was the Thai subsidiary of General Motors, operating from 1993 until its closure in 2020. Headquartered in Rayong, it manufactured and sold Chevrolet, Saab, and Holden vehicles, including the Zafira, Colorado, and Captiva. The company's Rayong plant, opened in 2000, also produced engines and transmissions for export. Despite early success, GM Thailand struggled with declining market share and shifted focus to SUVs and pickups before ceasing operations. In 2020, its assets were acquired by Great Wall Motors, marking the end of Chevrolet’s presence in Thailand and contributing to the discontinuation of the Holden brand in Australia and New Zealand.
E11EVEN Miami is a multilevel nightclub located in downtown Miami. The night club was opened February 5, 2014.

*I, Olga Hepnarová* (2016) is a Czech biographical drama directed by Tomáš Weinreb and Petr Kazda, starring Michalina Olszańska as Olga Hepnarová, a woman who committed a mass murder in 1973. The film explores her isolated life and psychological turmoil leading to the crime. It received mixed-to-positive reviews, praised for its stark cinematography and Olszańska's performance.

Web Therapy is an improvised online series starring Lisa Kudrow as Fiona Wallice, a therapist who has conceived of a new form of therapy, the titular "web therapy". The series debuted on LStudio.com on September 22, 2008.

CNN Pipeline was a subscription-based online streaming service launched by CNN on December 5, 2005, offering four live video feeds ("pipes") of breaking news, archived content, and special event coverage. It replaced parts of CNN Interactive but was discontinued on June 27, 2007, in favor of free ad-supported streaming. The service featured anchors such as Veronica De La Cruz and Nicole Lapin, with content available in over 40 countries. CNN Pipeline was later succeeded by CNNgo in 2014, which incorporated similar features.
*Chaos of the Senses* (1997) is an Arabic novel by Ahlam Mostaghanemi, following Hayat, an Algerian writer trapped in a loveless marriage during the civil war, who escapes into her fictional world.
Charles Parker JP (4 March 1809 – 29 June 1898) was a New Zealand politician and a carpenter.

Nutrisystem is a commercial provider of weight loss products and services headquartered in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania. Nutrisystem's initial product in 1972 was a liquid protein diet, but the company changed its offering after Slim-Fast came to prominence in that market. The company originally offered weight loss counseling and products in brick and mortar centers, but in 1999, Nutrisystem moved to a direct-to-consumer business model, selling its products and programs on the Internet and by telephone.

The company launched its mobile platform in 2010. WebDiet, Inc., a Silicon Valley startup, accused Nutrisystem of stealing their technology, but the case was dismissed in May, 2014.

In 2021, Stephen Mikulak was named President of Nutrisystem.

In 2015, the firm acquired the South Beach Diet brand. In December 2018, Tivity Health announced that it would acquire the Nutrisystem brand. On October 19, 2020, Kainos Capital acquired the brand from Tivity Health.

An eruption column or eruption plume is a cloud of super-heated ash and tephra suspended in gases emitted during an explosive volcanic eruption. The volcanic materials form a vertical column or plume that may rise many kilometers into the air above the vent of the volcano. In the most explosive eruptions, the eruption column may rise over 40 km (25 mi), penetrating the stratosphere. Stratospheric injection of aerosols by volcanoes is a major cause of short-term climate change.

A common occurrence in explosive eruptions is column collapse when the eruption column is or becomes too dense to be lifted high into the sky by air convection, and instead falls down the slopes of the volcano to form pyroclastic flows or surges (although the latter is less dense). On some occasions, if the material is not dense enough to fall, it may create pyrocumulonimbus clouds.
Lugazi University (LU), was a private university in Uganda. The university was founded in 2007. It closed in December 2010.

The 1973 World Snooker Championship (also known as 1973 Park Drive World Snooker Championship for sponsorship reasons) was a snooker tournament that took place from 16 to 28 April 1973 at the City Exhibition Halls in Manchester, England. The scheduling was a change of practice from championships in the preceding years, which had taken place over several months. The tournament was the 1973 edition of the World Snooker Championship established in 1927. The 1973 tournament was promoted by Peter West and Patrick Nally, and sponsored by tobacco brand Park Drive, with £8,000 prize money. There were 24 entrants, a new championship record.

Ray Reardon won the title by defeating Eddie Charlton 38 frames to 32 in the final. It was Reardon's second World Championship win, after his first in 1970. He went on to win a further four titles, the last of them in 1978. Defending champion Alex Higgins lost 9–13 to Charlton in the semi-finals. Higgins's quarter-final match against Fred Davis was interrupted when rainwater leaked onto the snooker table they were using. There were six century breaks during the competition, the highest of which was 139 by John Spencer.

The Cello Concerto is a composition for cello and orchestra by the Finnish composer Esa-Pekka Salonen. The work was co-commissioned by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, the New York Philharmonic, the Barbican Centre, and the Elbphilharmonie. It was completed in 2017 and was first performed by the cellist Yo-Yo Ma and the Chicago Symphony Orchestra under the direction of Salonen on March 9, 2017. The piece is dedicated to Yo-Yo Ma.
Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995) is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

StarUML is a software engineering tool for system modeling using the Unified Modeling Language, as well as Systems Modeling Language, and classical modeling notations. It is published by MKLabs and is available on Windows, Linux and MacOS.

Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection. (Danish: Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.) is a collection of nine fairy tales by Hans Christian Andersen. The tales were published in a series of three installments by C. A. Reitzel between May 1835 and April 1837, and represent Andersen's first venture into the fairy tale genre.

The nine tales of the three booklets were collected together and published in one volume and sold at seventy-two shillings. A title page, a table of contents, and a preface by Andersen were published in the volume.

The Great Sandy Desert (GSD) is an interim Australian bioregion, located in the northeast of Western Australia straddling the Pilbara and southern Kimberley regions and extending east into the Northern Territory. It is the second largest desert in Australia after the Great Victoria Desert and encompasses an area of 284,993 square kilometres (110,036 sq mi). The Gibson Desert lies to the south and the Tanami Desert lies to the east of the Great Sandy Desert.
Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was an American lawyer, Democratic politician, and New York Municipal Court judge.

Hilbert College is a private Franciscan college in Hamburg, New York. The college is named after Mother Colette Hilbert of the Franciscan Sisters of Saint Joseph, who founded the school in 1957 to train teachers. Hilbert College enrolls approximately 800 students and grants both undergraduate and master's degrees.

The Department of Social Security (the DSS) was a government department in Australia, which administered the Social Security system between 1972 and 1998. The department was one of several new departments established by the Whitlam Government and was managed by the Minister for Social Security.
Thaddäus Huber (8 May 1742 – 27 February 1798) was an Austrian violinist and composer.

"Jam" is a single by American singer-songwriter Michael Jackson. The song is the fourth single from his 1991 album Dangerous, where it is the opening track. It also appears as the second track on his 2009 This Is It compilation album. The single was re-released in 2006 as part of Jackson's Visionary: The Video Singles collection campaign, and it was remixed to the Cirque du Soleil's Immortal World Tour, releasing that remix on the soundtrack album. "Jam" is a new jack swing song whose bridge features a rap verse performed by Heavy D (of the group Heavy D & the Boyz), though no credit to him appears on the album. The music video of the song featured NBA basketball legend Michael Jordan. The song was also featured on the Chicago Bulls (Jordan's team at the time)'s 1992 NBA Championship video "Untouchabulls" and was also used in many promotional ads of the NBA in the said season. The single peaked at #26 on the Billboard Hot 100. The song re-entered the UK Singles Chart in 2006, reaching number 22.

Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is a former Democratic Iowa State Senator. Having served the 30th Senate District between 2013 and 2019, he had also previously served the 10th Senate District up until redistricting, between 2005 and 2013.

Danielson served on following committees: Appropriations (Vice Chair), Economic Growth, State Government (Chair), Transportation, Veterans Affairs (Vice Chair), and the Administration and Regulation Appropriations Subcommittee (Vice Chair). Permanent statutory committees he serves on include: Public Retirement Systems and State Government Efficiency Review. Danielson additionally serves on the Iowa Capital Investment Board.

His prior public service experience includes experience as the Commissioner of the Iowa Department of Transportation and the Waterloo Planning, Programming, and Zoning Commission. He is a U.S. Navy veteran and serves the community of Cedar Falls as a professional firefighter.

Dale Albert Kimball (born November 28, 1939) is a senior United States district judge for the District of Utah, appointed by President Bill Clinton in 1997. He previously worked as a law professor and commercial litigator.

Leigh Diffey (born March 3, 1971) is an Australian-American motorsports commentator for NBC Sports, known for his coverage of Formula One, IndyCar, NASCAR, and IMSA racing. Beginning his career in Australia with Network Ten and the BBC, he later moved to the U.S., working for Speed Channel before joining NBC in 2013. Diffey has also covered Olympic sports, including luge, bobsled, and track and field. He became a U.S. citizen in 2011.

Matthew Kantakouzenos (c. 1325 – 1383 or 1391) was a Byzantine emperor (1353–1357) and later Despot of the Morea (1380–1383). The son of Emperor John VI Kantakouzenos, he ruled jointly with his father before being deposed by John V Palaiologos. After his release, he governed the Morea until succeeded by his son, Demetrios I Kantakouzenos.

Muhammad bin Saud Al Muqrin (1687–1765) was the founder of the Emirate of Diriyah and the House of Saud, ruling from 1727 until his death. He formed a pivotal alliance with religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abdul Wahhab in 1744, establishing the foundation for the First Saudi State. His reign marked the beginning of Saudi-Wahhabi expansion in Arabia.

Eclipse Island is a 99-hectare (245-acre) granite island off the coast of Western Australia, named by Captain George Vancouver in 1791 after observing a solar eclipse there. Declared a Class 1A Nature Reserve in 2000, it hosts a lighthouse, a weather station, and a breeding colony of seabirds, including flesh-footed shearwaters and great-winged petrels. Introduced rabbits and arum lilies also inhabit the island.

Kreis Schwetz was a Prussian district that existed from 1818 to 1920, with its capital at Schwetz. The district was located on the western bank of the Vistula river in the part of West Prussia that fell to Poland after the First World War through the Treaty of Versailles in 1920.

Annette Krauss (born 1971) works as artist, writer and educator. Krauss is a member of the Read-in collective and her projects include Read the Masks. Tradition is Not Given, Hidden Curriculum, Sites for Unlearning, and Spaces of Commoning. Currently, she is course leader of the Master Fine Arts at the HKU and Elise-Richter-Peek Post-Doc researcher at Academy of Fine Arts Vienna.

Carl Froch vs. Glen Johnson was a professional boxing match for the WBC super-middleweight title on June 4, 2011, at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City. Froch, the defending champion, defeated Johnson by majority decision in the semifinals of the Super Six World Boxing Classic. The bout was part of a Showtime broadcast that also featured Mikkel Kessler's comeback fight. Froch advanced to the tournament final, where he faced Andre Ward.

Giant skippers are a subfamily (*Megathyminae*) of medium-sized, thick-bodied butterflies in the family *Hesperiidae*. They are larger than most skippers, typically brown with yellow markings, and have unhooked antennae. Males display territorial behavior and fast flight, rarely feeding as adults. Their larvae burrow into host plants, forming silk-lined tunnels where they pupate. The subfamily includes the genera *Agathymus*, *Megathymus*, and *Stallingsia*.

I, Olga Hepnarová (Lang-cs) is a 2016 internationally co-produced drama film, directed by Tomáš Weinreb and Petr Kazda, about Olga Hepnarová (1951–1975). She was a Czechoslovakian mass murderer, who on 10 July 1973, killed eight people with a truck in Prague. The film was shown in the Panorama section at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival.

Elizabeth Melise Jow, known professionally as Malese Jow and Melise, is an American actress. She is known for her roles as Geena Fabiano on the Nickelodeon sitcom Unfabulous, Lucy Stone on the Nickelodeon musical sitcom Big Time Rush and as Anna, a teenage vampire on The CW's fantasy drama The Vampire Diaries. She starred as Julia Yeung in the short-lived CW science fiction romantic drama Star-Crossed. Jow starred as Mareth Ravenlock in season two of The Shannara Chronicles. She voiced Dupli-Kate in the Amazon Prime Video animated series Invincible.

A customer data platform (CDP) is a software system that collects and unifies customer data from multiple sources into a single, standardized profile. Unlike data management platforms (DMPs), which use anonymized data, CDPs track identifiable individuals to enable personalized marketing. They differ from marketing automation systems by integrating with other tools and retaining historical data for analytics. The CDP industry emerged in the 2010s, growing rapidly as businesses sought alternatives to DMPs and data lakes.
E11EVEN Miami is a nightclub and bar in Miami, Florida, opened in 2014, known for hosting celebrities and accepting cryptocurrency.

Nick Horvath (born February 18, 1981) is an American-New Zealand former professional basketball player who played the majority of his career in the New Zealand National Basketball League (NZNBL). In 2010, he became the first person to win an NCAA championship (2001), an ANBL championship (2009) and a NZNBL championship (2010).

The Palm Court, also known at other times as the Franco-Italian Dining Room, the Grand Ballroom and the Continental Room, is a ballroom at the Hotel Alexandria in downtown Los Angeles, California. In its heyday from 1911 to 1922, it was the scene of speeches by U.S. Presidents William Howard Taft and Woodrow Wilson and Gen. John J. Pershing. It is also the room where Paul Whiteman, later known as the "Jazz King", got his start as a bandleader in 1919, where Rudolph Valentino danced with movie starlets, and where Hollywood held its most significant balls during the early days of the motion picture business. Known for its history and its stained-glass Tiffany skylight, noted Los Angeles columnist Jack Smith called it "surely the most beautiful room in Los Angeles". The Palm Court was designated as a City of Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument (HCM#80) in 1971.

The white collar--2 (wc-2) gene in Neurospora crassa encodes the protein White Collar-2 (WC-2). WC-2 is a GATA transcription factor necessary for blue light photoreception and for regulating circadian rhythms in Neurospora. In both contexts, WC-2 binds to its non-redundant counterpart White Collar-1 (WC-1) through PAS domains to form the White Collar Complex (WCC), an active transcription factor.

The WCC has two major and distinct roles in the cell. In the light, the WCC acts as a photoreceptor to mediate acute regulation of light-induced genes involved in various physiological processes such as carotenoid (type of pigment) biosynthesis and conidiation. In a separate and distinct role in the dark, WCC acts as the positive element in the autoregulatory transcription-translation negative feedback loop that controls circadian rhythmic behaviors in Neurospora. In this context, WCC regulates expression of the Frequency (FRQ) gene, a light-induced clock protein.

Berlin, also known as the Long-a-Coming Depot, is located in Berlin, Camden County, New Jersey, United States, between East Taunton and Washington Avenues, abutting the New Jersey Transit Atlantic City Line tracks. The station was built in 1856 and added to the National Register of Historic Places on February 14, 1997 for its significance in transportation.
Satannish is a demonic Marvel Comics supervillain who battles Doctor Strange and wields vast supernatural powers.

Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack) is the soundtrack to the 2021 American monster film Godzilla vs. Kong. A sequel to Kong: Skull Island (2017) and Godzilla: King of the Monsters (2019), it is the fourth film in Legendary's MonsterVerse, the 36th film in the Godzilla franchise, the 12th film in the King Kong franchise, and the fourth Godzilla film to be completely produced by a Hollywood studio. Directed by Adam Wingard, the film features musical score written and composed by Tom Holkenborg (Junkie XL), the fourth film composer to work on the MonsterVerse franchise. He was announced as a part of the film in June 2020, before a planned meeting with Wingard, two years (when the film was under pre-production), expressing his interest on working on the score due to his personal love for the Godzilla film series during his teen years. The recording of the score took place for nearly two years, and was bundled into a soundtrack album, released by WaterTower Music in digital and physical formats on March 31, 2021, coinciding with the film's United States theatrical release.

Camping World Stadium (formerly known as the Orlando Citrus Bowl and Florida Citrus Bowl) is a multi-purpose stadium in Orlando, Florida. Opened in 1936 as Orlando Stadium, it has undergone multiple renovations and expansions, with its most recent major overhaul completed in 2014. The stadium has hosted numerous events, including college football bowl games (such as the Citrus Bowl and Cheez-It Bowl), professional football teams (including the XFL’s Orlando Guardians and the USFL’s Orlando Renegades), and international soccer matches (including the 1994 FIFA World Cup and Copa América Centenario). It has also been the site of major WWE events, including WrestleMania XXIV and WrestleMania 33, the latter setting an attendance record of 75,245. With a current capacity of 60,219 (expandable to 65,194), the stadium features modern amenities such as 360-degree concourses, a party deck, and chair-back seating. Owned by the City of Orlando, it remains a key venue for sports and entertainment in Central Florida.

Songs of the Pixies was composed by Samuel Taylor Coleridge during 1793. The poem describes Coleridge's summer vacation and his childhood home. It also incorporates Coleridge's own view of himself as a young poet.

Parmalat is an Italian multinational food and dairy corporation, founded in 1961 by Calisto Tanzi. Headquartered in Collecchio, Italy, it produces milk, dairy products, fruit juices, and tea. Initially a small pasteurization plant, Parmalat expanded into a global conglomerate, acquiring businesses in sports, media, and tourism. It became publicly traded in 1990 and was valued at €3.7 billion by 2002.  

In 2003, Parmalat collapsed in one of Europe's largest financial frauds, revealing €14.3 billion in hidden debt. Tanzi and other executives were implicated in falsifying accounts, leading to criminal convictions. After bankruptcy, the company restructured under government supervision. In 2011, French dairy giant Lactalis acquired Parmalat in a €2.5 billion takeover, later delisting it from the stock exchange. Today, Parmalat operates as a subsidiary of Lactalis, though its Italian operations have been scaled back.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (10 March 1863 – 2 January 1910) was an English painter who spent most of her career in Paris. Few of her works have survived, but she is notable for her 1886 painting Fantine, which hangs in the Walker Art Gallery, Liverpool, England. The subject of the painting is Fantine, a character in Victor Hugo's 1862 novel Les Misérables.

Serbs in Germany (German: *Serben in Deutschland*) are one of the largest foreign communities in the country, with over 300,000 people of Serbian descent. Most live in North Rhine-Westphalia, Baden-Württemberg, and Bavaria, particularly in cities like Munich, Stuttgart, and Düsseldorf. They primarily speak German and Serbian and are predominantly Serbian Orthodox.
James Bouknight (born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the NBA. A standout at UConn, he was selected 11th overall in the 2021 NBA draft.

Charles J. Givens (1941 – July 12, 1998) was an American financial advisor and best-selling author, known for his books *Wealth Without Risk* and *Financial Self-Defense*. He founded the Charles J. Givens Organization, which grew to over 600,000 members and generated $104 million in revenue by offering financial education programs. Despite his success, Givens faced multiple lawsuits, including fraud cases in California and Florida, where he was accused of misleading customers about his wealth-building strategies. He settled these cases, including one involving his controversial advice to drop insurance coverage. Givens died in 1998 from prostate cancer.

The Materfer CMM 400-2 is an Argentine diesel multiple unit (DMU) built by Materfer for Trenes Argentinos. Designed for various track gauges, it features air conditioning, reclining seats, and LED lighting. Used on multiple railways, including the Mitre Line and Tren del Valle, it operates urban and interurban services across Argentina.

The 1956 Queensland state election was held on 19 May 1956, with the Labor Party, led by Vince Gair, retaining government. However, internal party divisions led to Gair's expulsion in 1957, splitting Labor and triggering an early election that ended its 26-year rule. Turnout was 93.01%.

Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a Brazilian-American electrical engineer and computer scientist known for his contributions to signal processing and data compression. He developed the lapped transform and Malvar wavelets, and played a key role in creating the Windows Media Audio codec and JPEG XR image format. After earning his PhD from MIT in 1986, he worked at Microsoft Research, where he led teams in multimedia compression and interaction technologies. A Fellow of the IEEE (1997) and member of the U.S. National Academy of Engineering (2012), he has authored over 180 papers and 120 patents.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (also known as the Gershman Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (GPJFF)) is an annual film festival in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, in the United States that presents movies and film-related programs about the Jewish experience, culture, values, and legacy.

Save Our Waterways Now (SOWN) is an Australian environmental organization focused on habitat restoration and tree planting in the Enoggera and Ithaca Creek catchments in Brisbane. Founded in 1994 by Alderman Brian Hallinan as a project of *Men of the Trees*, SOWN expanded with National Heritage Trust funding, engaging hundreds of volunteers in planting native trees and restoring waterways. By the late 1990s, it had become a major conservation group, planting over 20,000 trees annually. In 2007, it merged with the Brisbane Rainforest Action and Information Network (BRAIN) and later launched *Ithaca Intact*, a project to rehabilitate Ithaca Creek. SOWN remains active in ecological restoration and community engagement.

Annette Beard, also known as Annette Helton or Annette Sterling, is an American R&B and soul singer. Beard is best known for her work with Motown and as an original member of the singing group Martha and the Vandellas during the 1960s. Beard is currently known as a member of the singing group The Original Vandellas.

Geoffrey Sayre-McCord (born December 10, 1956) is an American philosopher and professor at the University of North Carolina, where he directs the Philosophy, Politics, and Economics Program. Known for his work on moral realism and metaethics, he has contributed to the *Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy* and edited the journal *Noûs*.

*Gayopakhyanam* (Telugu: గయోపాఖ్యానం) is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890. It follows the conflict between Gaya, a Gandharva king, and Lord Krishna, leading to a confrontation with Arjuna. The play blends Hindu mythology with dramatic storytelling and was highly popular, selling over 100,000 copies. It was later adapted into the 1963 film *Sri Krishnarjuna Yudham*.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis FRES FGS (28 June 1869-15 October 1943) was a British entomologist.

GAL (born 1940) is a Belgian cartoonist known for his socially engaged and painterly style. Since 1984, his satirical cartoons have appeared in *Knack*, often sparking controversy. An advocate for human rights, his work has supported causes like Amnesty International and Oxfam. In 2019, he received an honorary doctorate from the Free University of Brussels.

The 1945 Katsuyama killing incident was the murder of three African-American United States Marines in Katsuyama near Nago, Okinawa after the Battle of Okinawa on July 10, 1945 to August 13, 1946. Residents of Katsuyama had reportedly killed the three Marines for their repeated rape of village women during occupation of Okinawa and hid their bodies in a nearby cave out of fear for retaliation. The Katsuyama incident was kept secret until August 16, 1997 when the bodies and identities of the Marines were discovered.

Spirito DiVino is the seventh studio album by the Italian blues rock singer-songwriter Zucchero Fornaciari, released on 27 May 1995 by Polydor Records. The album was also released in partial Spanish language edition, and English edition titled Spirito DiVino: Stray Cat in a Mad Dog City.

Microsoft DNS is the Domain Name System (DNS) implementation in Windows, including client-side resolution and server-side hosting, supporting dynamic updates, caching, and Active Directory integration.

Great Bliss, Vol. 1 is an album by American jazz saxophonist David S. Ware recorded in 1990 and released on the Swedish Silkheart label. Great Bliss was conceived as a two-installment project and marks the debut of the David S. Ware Quartet, one of the most highly acclaimed musical groups of the decade.

Francisco de Holanda (6 September 1517 – 19 June 1585) was a Portuguese Renaissance artist, architect, and writer. Born in Lisbon, he trained as an illuminator under his father, António de Holanda, before studying in Italy from 1538 to 1547. There, he associated with Michelangelo and Vittoria Colonna, absorbing classical and Renaissance artistic ideals.  

Upon returning to Portugal, he served under Kings John III and Sebastian, producing illuminated manuscripts, architectural designs, and theoretical works. His treatise *Da Pintura Antiga* (1548) articulated Renaissance aesthetics and included dialogues with Michelangelo. He also created drawings documenting Italian antiquities and designed the façade of the Church of Our Lady of Grace in Évora.  

A pioneer in urbanism, he wrote *Da fábrica que falece à cidade de Lisboa*, the first Iberian essay on city planning. His works, blending classical influence with Portuguese tradition, established him as a key figure in 16th-century European art and intellectual history.

William Methwold (c. 1590 – 5 March 1653) was an English merchant, linguist, and administrator for the East India Company. He served as president of Surat (1633–1638), where he negotiated the Goa Convention (1635) with the Portuguese. Earlier, he became the first Englishman to visit the Golconda diamond mines, documenting his travels in *Purchas His Pilgrims* (1626). After returning to England, he became deputy governor of the East India Company (1643–1653) and facilitated its merger with the Assada Company (1649). He died at his London home, Hale House.

The Sugar Loaf Islands (Māori: *Ngā Motu*) are a group of small volcanic islands and rock formations off the coast of New Plymouth, New Zealand, near Port Taranaki. The islands are divided into inner and outer groups, with notable formations including Mataora (Round Rock), Motumahanga (Saddleback Island), and Moturoa. Historically, they were important fishing and gathering sites for Māori, particularly the Taranaki and Te Āti Awa iwi.  

The area holds geological significance as remnants of Taranaki's oldest volcanic activity, dating back 1.7 million years. In 1986, the islands and surrounding waters were designated a marine protected area, later strengthened by the Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Park Act (1991), safeguarding marine life such as fur seals, little blue penguins, and orcas. The islands are also the type locality of taranakite, a mineral formed from bird guano. Human impact includes historic oil drilling (New Zealand's first oil well in 1865) and land reclamation for industrial development.

Uncle Jam Wants You is the eleventh studio album by American funk band Funkadelic, released on September 21, 1979, by Warner Bros. Records. Produced by George Clinton, the album serves as a more militant follow-up to *One Nation Under a Groove*, with themes encouraging a conversion to "Funkadelia." Its cover art, depicting Clinton in a Black Panthers-inspired pose, reflects its assertive tone. The album includes the hit single *"(Not Just) Knee Deep,"* which topped the *Billboard* Soul singles chart and later became widely sampled in hip-hop. *Uncle Jam Wants You* also influenced West Coast hip-hop, particularly the DJ collective Uncle Jamm's Army. The album aimed to revitalize dance music, as declared on its cover: "to rescue dance music from the blahs."
Paladin Career and Technical High School is a Minnesota public charter school in Coon Rapids, Minnesota, in the United States. It opened in September 2002 and teaches grades 9–12.

The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was a nonprofit housing initiative established in 1968 to promote racial integration in University City, Missouri, a suburb of St. Louis. Founded by local activists and investors, including Kay Drey and Rev. William G. Lorenz, the trust purchased and rented homes to white families with school-age children to prevent white flight and maintain diversity in the Pershing Elementary School area. Despite financial struggles and criticism over selective tenant recruitment, the UCHRT grew to 35 homes by 1979 and was credited with sustaining integration in the neighborhood. By the mid-1980s, the trust began selling its properties, returning profits to investors before dissolving in 1990.

Vagif Mustafazadeh (March 16, 1940 – December 16, 1979) was an Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer, widely regarded as the founder of jazz mugham, a fusion of traditional Azerbaijani mugham with modern jazz. Despite Soviet-era restrictions on jazz, he gained international acclaim, winning the Monaco Jazz Competition in 1978. His innovative style influenced generations of musicians. Mustafazadeh died of a heart attack at 39, leaving behind a legacy celebrated in Azerbaijan and beyond.

The Troy Slapshots were a minor league ice hockey team in the Atlantic Coast Hockey League (ACHL) during the 1986–87 season. Based in Troy, New York, they played at Houston Field House before folding after just four home games due to financial struggles.

Sir Daril Watson (17 October 1888 – 1 July 1967) was a British Army general who served in both World Wars. He commanded the 2nd Infantry Division, became Assistant Chief of the Imperial General Staff, and later led Western Command. He retired in 1947 as Quartermaster-General to the Forces.

Dan Dotson (born November 23, 1962) is an American auctioneer and television personality, best known as the fast-talking auctioneer on the reality series *Storage Wars* (2010–present). He co-owns American Auctioneers with his wife, Laura, specializing in storage unit, estate, and foreclosure auctions. Trained by his grandfather, Dotson began auctioneering at age 11 and later founded his company in 1983. In 2014, he survived a life-threatening double aneurysm.

Necedah Shrine, officially the Queen of the Holy Rosary, Mediatrix of Peace Shrine, is a Marian shrine located in Necedah, Wisconsin. On November 12, 1949, Mary Ann Van Hoof (1909–1984) reported receiving a vision from the Blessed Virgin Mary. She claimed that in subsequent visions she was told to "bring the truth to people" through prayer and the rosary. The Roman Catholic Church investigated and found the reported visions and other phenomena indisputably faked, and when Van Hoof and her followers refused to desist, put her under interdict. Van Hoof and her followers then left the Roman Catholic Church and joined themselves to the Old Catholic movement.

Anne Ebbs (born 1940) is an Irish former Paralympic table tennis player and sports administrator. She won three Paralympic medals (1972, 1980, 1984) and later became Sports Director of the Irish Wheelchair Association. She co-founded the Paralympic Council of Ireland and received the Paralympic Order in 2008.
Ladies Legend Pro-Wrestling, also known as LLPW and currently as LLPW-X, is a Japanese independent women's professional wrestling promotion founded in 1992 by Rumi Kazama and Shinobu Kandori.
