The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States. The band consists of members Irwin Sparkes (lead vocals, lead guitar) and Alan Sharland (drums, percussion, vocals).

Their first single "Worried About Ray" reached number 5 on the UK Singles Chart in July 2007. In October 2007, they released their debut album The Trick to Life, which reached number one in the album charts. The band released their second top ten album The Illusion of Safety on 16 August 2010 and was preceded by the lead single "Choices", released on 1 August 2010, and which reached the UK top 20.

In July 2015, bassist Martin Skarendahl left the band in what was described by the remaining members as "one of the most amicable decisions in music history".

Bali Botanic Garden (Indonesian: *Kebun Raya Bali*) is a 157.5-hectare (389-acre) botanical garden located in Bedugul, Bali, Indonesia. Established in 1959 by President Sukarno, it was originally named *Eka Karya Botanic Garden* ("First Creation") as Indonesia's first post-independence botanical garden. Initially focused on conifer conservation and recreation, its development was halted in 1965 due to political instability before reopening in 1975 with an expanded role in conserving eastern Indonesia's montane flora.  

The garden features over 2,300 plant species, including extensive collections of orchids, begonias, bamboo, cacti, carnivorous plants, and traditional Balinese medicinal plants. It also contains remnant rainforest, three Hindu temples, and scenic views of Lake Bratan. Popular attractions include a giant ficus tree, a fern garden, and one of the world's largest begonia collections. The garden supports diverse wildlife, including birds, treeshrews, and macaques from the adjacent Batukaru Nature Reserve.  

Receiving over 500,000 visitors annually, the Bali Botanic Garden serves scientific, cultural, and recreational purposes while promoting plant conservation. It operates daily from 8 AM to 6 PM.

Greenhill, Edinburgh is a historic area in southern Edinburgh, originally part of the Burgh Muir estate feued in 1586. It was owned by merchant Thomas Aikenhead and later by apothecary John Livingston. Remnants include a stone carving of the former manor house and Livingston’s 17th-century mausoleum. Since 2006, parking has been restricted to residents under the "S2" controlled parking zone.

Down High School is a co-educational grammar school in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland. Established in 1933, it serves around 1,080 students aged 11 to 18. The school occupies a historic site, originally part of a 19th-century county gaol, with remnants such as the gatehouse and lowered stone walls still visible. Its crest, inspired by Norman ruler John de Courcy and the Cromwell family, reflects local heritage. The school motto, *Floreat Dunum, Absque Labore Nihil* ("May Down flourish, nothing is achieved without effort"), emphasizes academic diligence. Students are divided into five houses: Down, Lecale, Rathkeltair, Mourne, and Strangford. The current principal is Mrs M. Perry.

Rhizomatic learning is an educational theory inspired by Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari's concept of the rhizome, which contrasts hierarchical structures with decentralized, interconnected models of knowledge. It emphasizes organic, non-linear learning processes, drawing connections to John Dewey's progressive pedagogy and later feminist and critical pedagogy scholars. The approach has been applied to digital and networked learning, including massive open online courses (MOOCs), though critics argue it may undermine formal education structures. Some, like George Siemens, question its practical advantages over traditional network-based learning models.
56 Field Regiment is an artillery regiment of the Indian Army, raised in 1964. It participated in the 1971 Indo-Pakistani War, notably in the Battle of Kamalpur.

Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a Swabian Catholic priest who became a Protestant preacher, reformer and hymn-writer. In 1523, he helped Martin Luther to create the First Lutheran hymnal, published in 1524 and called Achtliederbuch.

Oxalis latifolia, commonly known as broadleaf woodsorrel, is a perennial herb with heart-shaped leaflets and pink (or white) flowers. Native to the Americas, it has become an invasive weed in many regions, spreading via bulbils and disrupting agriculture.

The Chalfont Viaduct (also known as the Misbourne Viaduct) is the first of two five-arch brick railway viaducts on the Chiltern Main Line in south-east England. It is located between Gerrards Cross and Denham Golf Club stations. The M25 motorway passes beneath it between junctions 16 and 17 at Gerrards Cross near Chalfont St Peter, from where the bridge gets its name. The bridge is known as Chalfont No. 1 Viaduct; the longer Chalfont No. 2 Viaduct is a short distance to the west and spans the A413.

The bridge is noted as a local landmark because for many years it bore a graffiti slogan, "give peas a chance" painted in large white letters on the south-facing parapet.

Vellattanjur is a village in Thrissur district, Kerala, India. Located near the city of Thrissur, it had a population of 6,637 as of the 2001 census. The village is known for its religious sites, including four major temples—Kootumuchi, Kurooramma Srikrishna, Erakad Subramanya Swami, and Sree Rama—each hosting annual festivals with cultural events, elephant processions, and traditional music. Vellattanjur is also home to the Fathima Matha Church, the first church in India dedicated to Our Lady of Fatima, which holds grand celebrations in October. The village is governed by a grama panchayat and falls under the Alathur Lok Sabha constituency.
Doris Mary Stenton (1894–1971) was an English medieval historian and editor, known for her work on Lincolnshire records and the Pipe Roll Society.

The Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire was a British order of chivalry established in 1878 by Queen Victoria to honor British and Indian officials serving in British India. Originally a single-class order (Companion), it expanded to three classes in 1887: Knight Grand Commander (GCIE), Knight Commander (KCIE), and Companion (CIE). Intended as a less exclusive counterpart to the Order of the Star of India, it had broader membership, including Indian princes and officials. Appointments ceased after India's independence in 1947, and the order became dormant in 2010. The British monarch remains its sovereign, though no living members exist. Notable recipients included rulers of Indian princely states and foreign dignitaries. The order's insignia avoided Christian symbols to respect India's diverse traditions.

Sajek Tripuri Valley is one of the most popular tourist spots in Bangladesh situated among the hills of the Kasalong range of mountains in Sajek union, Baghaichhari Upazila in Rangamati District. The valley is 2,000 feet (610 m) above sea level. Sajek Tripuri Valley is known as the Queen of Hills & Roof of Rangamati.
Lucius Julius L. f Vop. n. Iulus was a member of the patrician house of the Julii at ancient Rome. He was military tribune with consular powers in 401 and 397 BC.
Oberhünigen is a municipality in the Bern-Mittelland administrative district of Switzerland, with a population of 316 (2020).

Hastings is a city in Mills County, Iowa, United States, with a population of 152 as of the 2020 census. Founded in 1872 along the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad, it is part of the East Mills Community School District.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs Sr. (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman, the owner of the Boston Bruins and chairman of Delaware North. Forbes magazine ranks him as 481st richest person in the world.

Ro-45 was a *Kaichū*-type submarine of the Imperial Japanese Navy, commissioned in January 1944. Assigned to intercept U.S. forces near Truk, she was sunk by American destroyers on 30 April 1944 with all hands lost. Stricken from the navy list that July.

The Jamaat al Muslimeen coup attempt was a failed 1990 insurrection in Trinidad and Tobago led by the Islamist group Jamaat al Muslimeen, under Yasin Abu Bakr. On July 27, militants stormed the Red House (Parliament) and seized Prime Minister A. N. R. Robinson and his cabinet, while others attacked state media outlets. After six days of negotiations, the insurgents surrendered under an amnesty agreement. The coup attempt resulted in 24 deaths, widespread looting, and millions in property damage. Though tried for treason, the militants were released after courts upheld the amnesty. The event severely weakened the government and led to long-term political and economic repercussions.

Prospect Hill is a historic neighborhood in Omaha, Nebraska, originally platted in the 1850s as part of the Scriptown land scandal. It became the site of Prospect Hill Cemetery, where early Omaha settlers, including over 800 African Americans and Buffalo Soldiers, were buried. In the 1940s, the U.S. government built nearly 700 public housing units in the area, including Hilltop Homes, Spencer Homes, and Pleasantview Homes. Today, Salem Baptist Church stands where Hilltop Homes once existed. The cemetery remains a significant historical landmark.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality, known for her work on *Entertainment Tonight Canada*, *CP24*, and *The Weather Network*.

Vishwa Thulasi is a 2004 Indian Tamil-language romantic drama film directed by Sumathy Ram. Starring Mammootty and Nandita Das, it follows the emotional reunion of two former lovers haunted by their past. The film features music by M. S. Viswanathan and Ilaiyaraaja.

Brown Boy 2 is a reissue of Canadian rapper Nav's third studio album *Good Intentions*, released on May 11, 2020, through XO and Republic Records. Serving as a deluxe edition, it includes previously leaked tracks, snippets, and new material, following the original album's release by just a few days. The title references Nav's nickname, "Brown Boy," which pays homage to his Punjabi heritage. The project expands on his 2019 EP *Brown Boy*, which was later incorporated into the deluxe version of his second album, *Bad Habits*. Production credits include contributions from Nav himself, along with notable producers such as Tay Keith, Wheezy, and Nick Mira. The reissue maintains Nav's signature melodic trap style while continuing his trend of blending personal themes with mainstream hip-hop sounds.
The Willard Gallery was a contemporary art gallery operating in New York City from 1940 until 1987. It was founded by Marian Willard Johnson.

The Children's Museum of the Arts (CMA) is a New York City-based museum dedicated to engaging children with art through exhibitions and educational programs. It features works by notable artists such as Keith Haring, Christo and Jeanne-Claude, and Swoon, alongside interactive installations like Ian Berry’s *Secret Garden* (2017), made from denim. CMA offers art programs for diverse groups, including children with autism, those in foster care, and low-income families, both on-site and through outreach initiatives. Past exhibitions have explored themes like public art, WPA-era works, and sensory experiences.
Christa Peters-Lidard is an American earth scientist at NASA, known for developing the Land Information System (LIS) and her research in hydrology and land-atmosphere interactions.

Hristo Chernopeev (Lang-bg) (1868, Dermantsi – 6 November 1915, Krivolak) was a Bulgarian Army officer and member of the revolutionary movement in Macedonia. He was among the leaders of the Bulgarian People's Macedonian-Adrianople Revolutionary Organization.
Karlo Matković (born 30 March 2001) is a Croatian professional basketball player for Cedevita Olimpija of the ABA League. He represents the Croatia national team internationally.
"Like a Man" is a 2017 tropical house single by Onhel featuring Lil Wayne. The song was leaked before its official release on August 8, 2017.
Thaddäus Huber (c. 1740–1798) was an Austrian violinist and composer, active in Vienna.

Gracefield is a city in the Outaouais region of Quebec, Canada, located in the La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality. Founded in 1840, it was officially constituted in 2002 through the merger of Wright, Gracefield, and Northfield. Situated along the Gatineau River, the city has faced natural disasters, including a major flood in 1974 and a 5.0 magnitude earthquake in 2010. Gracefield's economy historically relied on mica mining, and it remains a small community with a population of 2,376 (2021). It is accessible via Route 105.
Lodestar is the seventh studio album by the English folk musician, Shirley Collins. The album is Collins's first in 38 years, making it one of the longest gaps between studio albums.

The Mizoram Peace Accord, 1986 was an official agreement between the Government of India and the Mizo National Front (MNF) to end insurgency and violence in Mizoram, India, that started in 1966. The Mizo National Front was an organisation of Mizo secessionists led by Laldenga to fight for independence from India. The movement was basically due to lack of support from the government during the great famine (called Mautam) in Mizoram in the late 1950s. Political insurgency and social unrest ensued in the next decades. After a number of negotiations, the document titled Mizoram Accord, 1986: Memorandum of Settlement was finally signed on 30 June 1986. It was signed by Laldenga for MNF, R.D. Pradhan, Home Secretary, Government of India, and Lalkhama, Chief Secretary, Government of Mizoram. It is remarked as the most and only successful peace agreement in India after its independence from British Empire in 1947.

The WU polyomavirus (also known as Human polyomavirus 4) is a virus of the *Polyomaviridae* family, first identified in 2007 in respiratory secretions from a child with pneumonia. It has a circular double-stranded DNA genome encoding five viral proteins. WU virus is globally distributed, with high seroprevalence in adults, suggesting widespread exposure. While initially detected in respiratory infections, its role in disease remains unclear, with no confirmed links to cancer or kidney disease. It is classified under the genus *Betapolyomavirus*, alongside other human polyomaviruses like BK and JC viruses.

The Palm Court is a historic ballroom in the Alexandria Hotel in Los Angeles, California. Designed by John Parkinson and completed in 1911, it became the city's most prestigious event venue, hosting presidents, celebrities, and early Hollywood figures like Rudolph Valentino, Mary Pickford, and Douglas Fairbanks. The Palm Court was where United Artists was founded in 1919. After the Biltmore Hotel opened in 1923, the Alexandria declined, and the ballroom was later used for boxing training in the mid-20th century. Restored in the 1980s, it was designated a Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument in 1971. In 2009, the Blankenship Ballet Company began performing there, reviving its cultural significance.

Annette Beard (born in Detroit, Michigan) is an American R&B singer, best known as an original member of Martha and the Vandellas. Joining in 1957, she contributed contralto vocals to early hits like *"(Love Is Like A) Heat Wave"* before leaving in 1964. She later reunited with the group for performances and their Rock & Roll Hall of Fame induction in 1995. Since 2011, she has performed as part of The Original Vandellas.

The 1999 Brickyard 400 was the 20th race of the 1999 NASCAR Winston Cup Series, held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway. Dale Jarrett won after leading 117 laps, becoming the event's second two-time winner. Jeff Gordon started on pole, while Bobby Labonte nearly stole the win on fuel strategy before a late caution. The race aired on ABC, drawing a 4.6 Nielsen rating.

Putte is part of the Dutch municipality of Woensdrecht, and had 3751 inhabitants as of 1 January 2008. The village lies on and extends over the border between the Netherlands and Belgium, the Belgian part being Putte, Kapellen.

The Portuguese Faculdade de Ciências Sociais e Humanas (Faculty of Social and Human Sciences - FCSH) is an organic unit of the Universidade NOVA de Lisboa (NOVA). According to its statutes, “the Faculty of Social and Human Sciences of NOVA University of Lisbon is an institution dedicated to education, scientific research and cultural creation". The Faculty's own identity stems from the coexistence of social sciences with humanities, allowing an unusual interdisciplinarity in the Portuguese higher education panorama.

Ariyalur is a town and the headquarters of Ariyalur District in Tamil Nadu, India. Located in the Chola Nadu region, it lies about 267 km from Chennai and 65 km from Tiruchirappalli. Historically significant as part of the Chola dynasty, Ariyalur later came under Vijayanagara Nayak rule before being annexed by the British in the 19th century.  

The town has a semi-arid climate and is a major hub for cement production, hosting factories of companies like UltraTech Cement, India Cements, and Dalmia Cement. Agriculture, particularly sugarcane and cashew cultivation, also contributes to its economy.  

Ariyalur is governed by a second-grade municipality and serves as an administrative and political center, represented in both the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly and the Lok Sabha. As of the 2011 census, it had a population of 28,902, with a literacy rate of 76.04%.  

The town is well-connected by road and rail, with the Ariyalur railway station being a key junction on the Chennai-Tiruchirappalli route. It also has basic infrastructure, including water supply, healthcare facilities, and a bus stand facilitating local and intercity transport.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent that is native to southwestern Angola, western Namibia, and western South Africa. It is also known as the Kaoko ground squirrel or the Damara ground squirrel.

It is the closest relative of the Cape ground squirrel (Latin name Geosciurus inauris), which is so similar in appearance that the two are difficult to distinguish in the field. Both species have long bushy black and white tails with a white stripe from the shoulder towards the rump. Geosciurus princeps is slightly larger, on average, than G. inauris, although there is considerable overlap in body size. Differences in skull morphology also distinguish the two species, and the incisors are yellow to orange rather than white as in G. inauris.

Tamara Bowie (born June 3, 1981) is an American former professional basketball player. A two-time MAC Player of the Year at Ball State (2002, 2003), she was drafted by the Washington Mystics in 2003. She later played overseas, winning a Bulgarian League title (2011) and earning Úrvalsdeild Foreign Player of the Year honors (2007) in Iceland.

Aloysius Edward "Pie" Piechota (January 19, 1914 – June 13, 1996) was a professional baseball player whose career spanned 15 seasons, two of which were spent with the Major League Baseball (MLB) Boston Bees / Braves from 1940 to 1941. Piechota, a pitcher, compiled an earned run average (ERA) of 5.66, allowing 39 earned runs off of 68 hits, 6 home runs, and 42 walks while recording 18 strikeouts over 62 innings pitched. Piechota also played in 14 seasons of minor league baseball. He made his MLB debut at the age of 26 and was officially listed as standing 6 feet 0 inches (183 cm) and weighing 195 pounds (88 kg).

Johann F. C. Hessel (1796–1872) was a German mineralogist and crystallographer who derived the 32 possible crystal classes and contributed to the understanding of feldspar composition. His work, though initially overlooked, later became foundational in crystallography.

The Hồ dynasty (Vietnamese: *Nhà Hồ*; 1400–1407) was a short-lived Vietnamese dynasty that replaced the Trần dynasty. Founded by Hồ Quý Ly, it renamed the country Đại Ngu ("Great Peace") and moved the capital to Tây Đô. The dynasty promoted Chữ Nôm, a vernacular script, over Classical Chinese. Hồ Quý Ly implemented economic reforms, including paper currency and land redistribution, but faced internal opposition from Trần loyalists. In 1407, the Ming dynasty invaded under the pretext of restoring the Trần, leading to the dynasty's collapse. The Hồ rulers were captured and exiled, marking the beginning of the Fourth Era of Northern Domination. Despite its brief rule, the dynasty introduced significant administrative and cultural changes.

Ruby Cabernet is a red Olmo grape variety that is a cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Carignan, it can produce wines with good colour and a pleasant cherry flavour, but is mostly blended into bulk wines.

The purpose for the creation of the crossing of the grape varieties utilized to produce Ruby Cabernet was to obtain the superior quality of a Cabernet wine, and the resistance to heat of the Carignan combined in an inexpensive table wine. Even though the wine made from these grapes does not possess the distinctive flavor and the overall structure of other types of Cabernet wines, it does carry their fruitful essence.

The grape for this type of red wine was developed for California's hot climate, specially for regions such as the San Joaquin and the Napa Valleys. Ruby Cabernet has improved the quality of the bulk wines produced in these areas thanks to its natural, special acidity.
Hugh Cecil Lowther, 5th Earl of Lonsdale, KG, GCVO, DL (25 January 1857–13 April 1944) was an English peer and sportsman.

Melchior Inchofer (1585 – 28 September 1648) was a Hungarian Jesuit theologian and scholar. He played a key role in the 1633 trial of Galileo, where his report strongly condemned Galileo's *Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems* for promoting Copernicanism. Inchofer wrote on theology, history, and linguistics, including *Annales ecclesiastici regni Hungariae*, a pioneering work in Hungarian church history. His later years were marked by controversy, including accusations of contributing to an anti-Jesuit tract.

Joseph Tanke is an American philosopher and academic specializing in aesthetics and critical theory. He has taught at the California College of the Arts and the University of Hawaii at Manoa, where he directs the International Cultural Studies Graduate Certificate Program. Tanke is the editor of the *Global Aesthetic Research* book series and has authored works on Michel Foucault and Jacques Rancière. He also co-edited the *Bloomsbury Anthology of Aesthetics*.

The American International School of Abuja (AISA) is a co-educational international school in Abuja, Nigeria, offering a U.S.-based curriculum from preschool to grade 12. Founded in 1993, it serves a diverse student body and features modern facilities, technology-integrated classrooms, and extracurricular activities. The school is governed by an elected Board of Governors.

Might and Magic: Heroes Kingdoms was a free-to-play, browser-based massively multiplayer online strategy game developed and published by Ubisoft in 2009. Set in the *Heroes of Might and Magic* universe, players controlled cities, recruited heroes, and battled for dominance in persistent online worlds. The game featured resource management, alliances, and turn-based combat before its servers shut down in 2014.

Sir John Wallace Pringle (1863–1938) was a British military engineer and railway inspector. He served in the Third Anglo-Burmese War and led surveys for the Uganda Railway. As Chief Inspecting Officer of Railways for the Board of Trade and later the Ministry of Transport, he investigated accidents and promoted railway safety. Knighted in 1925, he retired in 1929.
Joseph McLaughlin (June 9, 1867 – November 21, 1926) was a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania.

A Fazenda 4 was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television series *A Fazenda*, which aired on Rede Record in 2011. Lasting 86 days, the season featured 16 contestants, including three returning participants from previous seasons. The winner was Joana, with Monique as runner-up. A notable twist involved former contestants Franciely Freduzeski, Ana Paula Oliveira, and Monique Evans returning for another chance at the R$2 million prize. The season included a team challenge where the winning group split an additional R$500,000. Controversies arose, including the ejection of Duda Yankovich for violent behavior and backlash against Thiago Gagliasso over alleged animal mistreatment. The season was the first to be filmed and broadcast in high-definition.

Rarefaction (ecology) is a statistical technique used to compare species richness across datasets with differing sample sizes. Developed in 1968 by Howard Sanders for marine benthic ecosystems, it adjusts for sampling bias by constructing curves that estimate expected species diversity at smaller sample sizes. The method has since been refined to address issues such as overestimation and has been applied to various ecological studies, including microbial diversity and pollution impact assessments.  

Rarefaction assumes random species distribution and similar sampling methods, making it most reliable when no single species is extremely rare or abundant. Unlike raw species counts, rarefaction curves allow smoother comparisons but do not account for species composition or relative abundance. While useful for standardizing diversity measures, it cannot predict total species richness in larger samples and may be skewed by non-random spatial distributions. The technique remains widely used in community ecology for assessing biodiversity in unevenly sampled environments.

Murphy's sign is a clinical test used to detect gallbladder inflammation (cholecystitis). It involves palpating the right upper abdomen during deep inspiration; pain causing the patient to halt breathing indicates a positive result. Named after surgeon John Benjamin Murphy, it has high sensitivity but lower specificity, especially in elderly patients.

Levi General (March 15, 1873 – June 27, 1925), commonly known as Deskaheh, was a Haudenosaunee hereditary chief and appointed speaker noted for his persistent efforts to get recognition for his people. He is most famous for bringing Iroquois concerns before the League of Nations in the 1920s.

Uriah F. Abshier (September 17, 1849 – May 23, 1934) was an American politician, businessman, and postmaster. A Republican, he served in the Oregon House of Representatives from 1885 to 1886, representing Lake and Klamath counties. He later became a prominent rancher, postmaster, and hardware store owner in Washington before retiring to California. Abshier died in a car accident at age 84.

Greenhill is a small area of Edinburgh, the capital of Scotland. Situated south of the city centre, Greenhill is normally taken to be part of Bruntsfield, which skirts it to the north. Greenhill borders Marchmont and The Grange to the east, Morningside to the south, and Merchiston, beyond Holy Corner, to the west. It comprises a mixture of Georgian and Victorian villas and some tenement housing.
Louis of Brienne (fl. 13th century) was a French nobleman, son of John of Brienne, and Viscount of Beaumont through marriage.

The Harold Peary Show was a short-lived American radio sitcom starring Harold Peary, airing on CBS from 1950 to 1951. A successor to *The Great Gildersleeve*, it featured Peary as "Honest Harold," a radio host, but failed to match its predecessor's success.

Burma VJ (2008) is a Danish documentary film directed by Anders Østergaard, chronicling the 2007 Burmese anti-government protests through secretly recorded footage by citizen journalists. It received critical acclaim, including a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, and won awards such as the Sundance Film Festival's World Cinema Documentary Film Editing Award. The film highlights media censorship and the struggle for free speech in Myanmar.

Jhajhar is a village in Jhunjhunu district, Rajasthan, India. Historically part of the Shekhawat clan's territory, it was ruled by Thakurs under Jaipur's suzerainty. The village celebrates traditional festivals like Teej, Gangaur, and Dussehra.

Mindy Lubber is an American environmental advocate and business strategist. She served as Regional Administrator of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and later became CEO of Green Century Capital Management. Her work focuses on corporate sustainability and partnerships with major companies.

Pridwen was, according to the 12th-century writer Geoffrey of Monmouth, King Arthur's shield; it was adorned with an image of the Virgin Mary. Geoffrey's description of it draws on earlier Welsh traditions found in Preiddeu Annwfn, Culhwch and Olwen, and the Historia Brittonum. The shield is also named and described by Wace, Layamon, Roger of Wendover and Robert of Gloucester among other medieval writers, and it directly inspired the description of Sir Gawain's shield in Sir Gawain and the Green Knight.

Malice Mizer was a Japanese visual kei band active from 1992 to 2001, known for their gothic rock, neoclassical, and synthpop sound, as well as elaborate costumes and theatrical performances. Formed by guitarists Mana and Közi, the band went through three distinct eras with different vocalists: Tetsu (1992–1994), Gackt (1995–1999), and Klaha (2000–2001). Their music evolved from darkwave and progressive rock to art rock and electronic influences, with albums like *Merveilles* (1998) and *Bara no Seidou* (2000) gaining critical acclaim.  

The band's lineup also included bassist Yu~ki and drummer Kami, whose death in 1999 deeply affected the group. Malice Mizer disbanded in 2001, with members pursuing solo careers—most notably Gackt as a pop star and Mana with his gothic fashion label Moi-même-Moitié and band Moi dix Mois. Since 2008, partial reunions under the Deep Sanctuary events have featured Mana, Közi, and Yu~ki performing together. Malice Mizer remains influential in the visual kei and J-rock scenes.

Messier 83 or M83, also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy and NGC 5236, is a barred spiral galaxy approximately 15 million light-years away in the constellation borders of Hydra and Centaurus. Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille discovered M83 on 23 February 1752 at the Cape of Good Hope. Charles Messier added it to his catalogue of nebulous objects (now known as the Messier Catalogue) in March 1781. It is one of the closest and brightest barred spiral galaxies in the sky, and is visible with binoculars. Its nickname of the Southern Pinwheel derives from its resemblance to the Pinwheel Galaxy (M101).

Dangaioh (破邪大星ダンガイオー) is a 1987–1989 Japanese mecha OVA series produced by AIC and Artmic. Directed by Toshiki Hirano, it follows four psychic warriors who combine their powers to pilot the giant robot Dangaioh against the tyrannical Captain Galimos. The series spawned manga, video games, and a 2001 sequel, *Great Dangaioh*.

Capsule hotel (Lang-ja), also known in the Western world as a pod hotel, is a type of hotel developed in Japan that features many small bed-sized rooms known as capsules. Capsule hotels provide cheap, basic overnight accommodation for guests who do not require or who cannot afford larger, more expensive rooms offered by more conventional hotels.

The first capsule hotel in the world opened in 1979 and was the Capsule Inn Osaka, located in the Umeda district of Osaka, Japan and designed by Kisho Kurokawa. From there, it spread to other cities within Japan. Since then, the concept has further spread to various other territories, including Belgium, China, Hong Kong, Iceland, India, Indonesia, Poland and Canada.

The European Data Portal is a centralized platform providing access to open public sector data from EU member states and EFTA countries. It aggregates metadata from national portals to promote data reuse and was consolidated into data.europa.eu in 2021.

Fredric Sheldon Brandt (June 26, 1949 – April 5, 2015) was an American physician, researcher, lecturer, author, and radio host specializing in cosmetic dermatology. Among the first to use botulinum toxin ("botox") and fillers, Brandt was noted for his role in the FDA approval of numerous fillers and botulinum toxins for cosmetic use in the United States.

Brandt, who hosted a radio show called Ask Dr. Brandt on SiriusXM radio and whose celebrity patients included Madonna, was called the "Baron of Botox" by W magazine and "King of Collagen" for using more botox and collagen than any other dermatologist in the world.

Yann Weymouth (born 1941) is an American architect known for his work on the National Gallery of Art East Wing and the Louvre Pyramid under I.M. Pei. He later designed museums, including the Salvador Dalí Museum and expansions at the Ringling Museum of Art.
Satannish is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.

Alturas de Macchu Picchu is the sixth studio album by Chilean rock band Los Jaivas, released in October 1981. A rock opera adaptation of Pablo Neruda's poem from *Canto General*, it blends Andean folk instruments with progressive rock. Recorded in Paris and inspired by Machu Picchu, the album was accompanied by a televised concert filmed at the historic site. Widely praised, it is regarded as one of the greatest Chilean albums of all time.

Somalia at the Olympics  

Somalia first participated in the Summer Olympics in 1972 and has since competed in most editions, though it has never won a medal or taken part in the Winter Games. The Somali Olympic Committee, founded in 1959, was recognized by the IOC in 1972. Due to the Somali Civil War, many athletes have emigrated and competed for other nations, including British distance runner Mo Farah. Somalia has often sent small delegations, with its best result being Abdi Bile's sixth-place finish in the 1500m in 1996. Since 2000, Somalia has consistently sent one male and one female athlete.

The murder of Urban Höglin and Heidi Paakkonen refers to the disappearance and deaths of two Swedish backpackers in New Zealand in April 1989. Their bodies were later found in separate locations, with Höglin's remains discovered in 1991. The case led to the conviction of David Tamihere, who was sentenced to life imprisonment in 1990 based largely on jailhouse informant testimony. However, doubts about his guilt arose when key witness Robert Conchie Harris (known as "Secret Witness C") later admitted to lying under police pressure. Tamihere was granted parole in 2010 and continues to maintain his innocence. In 2020, he was granted a royal prerogative of mercy, referring the case back to the Court of Appeal. The investigation and trial have been the subject of significant media scrutiny, including documentaries and allegations of police misconduct.
Breandán Ó hEithir (18 January 1930 – 26 October 1990) was an Irish writer and broadcaster.

Winter Haven Hospital is a 527-bed non-profit hospital in Winter Haven, Florida, founded in 1926. It joined the BayCare Health System in 2013 and serves as a major healthcare provider in Polk County. The hospital has expanded over the years, adding specialized centers such as the Bostick Heart Center, Cassidy Cancer Center, and an Advanced Primary Stroke Center. Its main campus includes multiple buildings, including the Regency Center for Women and Infants, and it operates several outpatient Family Health Centers in surrounding communities. The hospital is a key regional employer with over 2,500 staff.

The 1950 World Snooker Championship was a snooker tournament held at the Tower Circus in Blackpool, England.

For the fourth year running the final was contested by Fred Davis and Walter Donaldson. Donaldson defeated Davis 51–46 to win his second and last world title. Donaldson won the 7th frame of the final day to lead 49–42 with only 6 frames to play. Davis made a break of 101 in frame 46 of his semi-final match against George Chenier.

Satellite in the Sky is a 1956 British CinemaScope science fiction film in Warner Color, produced by Edward J. Danziger and Harry Lee Danziger, directed by Paul Dickson, and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell, Donald Wolfit, and Bryan Forbes. The film was distributed by Warner Bros. Pictures. Special effects were by Wally Veevers, who would later work on Stanley Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968).

NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) (این اے-36، ہنگو-کم-اورکزئی) is a constituency for the National Assembly of Pakistan. It covers the whole of the district Hangu. The constituency was formerly known as NA-16 (Hangu) from 1977 to 2018. The name changed to NA-33 (Hangu) after the delimitation in 2018. NA-36 (Hangu-cum-Orakzai) and Orakzai District were also included after the delimitation in 2022.

Petra Kronberger (born 21 February 1969, in Pfarrwerfen) is an Austrian former alpine skier, who participated in all disciplines. She was the first female alpine skier to win in all five World Cup events.

Yamamiya Sengen Shrine (山宮浅間神社) is a Shinto shrine in Fujinomiya, Shizuoka, Japan, dedicated to the worship of Mount Fuji. Built without a *honden* (main hall), it features an altar aligned for direct veneration of the mountain. In 2013, it was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site as part of *Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration*.

Petra Kronberger (born 21 February 1969) is an Austrian former alpine skier. A three-time overall World Cup champion (1990–1992), she won two Olympic gold medals (1992) and a World Championship title (1991) before retiring in 1992.

Andrea Nguyen (born 1969) is a Vietnamese-born, American teacher, food writer, cookbook author and chef living in the San Francisco area. An expert on Asian cuisine and cooking methods, Nguyen has written numerous cookbooks on the food of her native Vietnam, as well as an account of her family's escape during the Fall of Saigon. She writes an active blog, as well as articles for newspapers and food magazines and teaches cooking classes throughout the country.

Dacrydium cupressinum, commonly known as rimu, is a large evergreen coniferous tree endemic to the forests of New Zealand. It is a member of the southern conifer group, the podocarps. The former name "red pine" has fallen out of common use.

Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875) was a Scottish peer and landowner. He served as Lord Lieutenant of Inverness from 1853 to 1873 and was made a Knight of the Thistle in 1865. Inheriting the Lovat estates in 1815, he was later confirmed as 12th Lord Lovat after the reversal of his ancestor's attainder.

The Republic of Lakotah or Lakotah is a proposed independent republic in North America for the Lakota people. Proposed in 2007 by activist Russell Means, the suggested territory would be enclaved by the borders of the United States, covering thousands of square miles in North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Wyoming, and Montana. The proposed borders are those of the 1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie between the United States government and the Lakota. These lands are now occupied by Indian reservations and non-Native settlements. None of the existing Lakota tribal governments support the proposed republic, and they were not consulted about the proposal.

The Kuban is a river in Russia that flows through the Western Caucasus and drains into the Sea of Azov. The Kuban runs mostly through Krasnodar Krai for 660 kilometres (410 mi), but also in the Karachay–Cherkess Republic, Stavropol Krai and the Republic of Adygea.

The Kuban flows 870 kilometres (540 mi) north and west from its source near Mount Elbrus in the Caucasus Mountains, eventually reaching Temryuk Bay in the Sea of Azov. It is navigable up to Krasnodar. Major cities on the river are Karachayevsk, Cherkessk, Nevinnomyssk, Armavir, Novokubansk, Kropotkin, Ust-Labinsk, Krasnodar and Temryuk. Despite its name, Slavyansk-na-Kubani lies not on the Kuban River, but on its distributary the Protoka.

Wang Dan (c. 957 – 6 October 1017), courtesy name Ziming, was a major politician in the Song dynasty, serving as the grand councilor from 1006 until shortly before his death in 1017. Well trusted by Emperor Zhenzong, Wang Dan was given plenipotentiary authority over some matters after 1008.

Wang Dan is generally remembered as a faithful and virtuous official, mainly because he kept a low profile, recruited talented men to the bureaucracy while blocking his notoriously unscrupulous colleague Wang Qinruo from advancing for most of his tenure. But Wang Dan also flattered and encouraged Emperor Zhenzong's excessive and ridiculous Taoist pursuits, for which he expressed regret on his deathbed.

The CMM 400-2 is a diesel multiple unit produced by Materfer in Córdoba Province, Argentina. The units are produced in Argentina's three primary track gauges and the standard gauge variants were the first to be produced for General Urquiza Railway services in Entre Ríos Province.

Airblue is a Pakistani private airline headquartered in Islamabad. Founded in 2003, it began operations in 2004 and serves domestic and international destinations, including the UAE and the UK. The airline operates an all-Airbus fleet and was the first in Pakistan to introduce e-ticketing.

Tamika Tremaglio is the current executive director of the National Basketball Players Association (NBPA). Tremaglio was selected by the players to replace Michele Roberts on September 22, 2021, and officially stepped into the role upon Roberts'retirement in early January 2022. Prior to joining the NBPA as executive director, Tremaglio, who retired from Deloitte LLP, served as the Managing Principal for Deloitte's Greater Washington offices.

Paleontology in New Hampshire refers to paleontological research occurring within or conducted by people from the U.S. state of New Hampshire. Fossils are very rare in New Hampshire because so much of the state's geology is highly metamorphic. The state's complicated geologic history has made dating its rocks and the fossils they contain "a difficult task." The state's Devonian rocks are especially metamorphosed, yet its Mississippian rocks formed too recently to have been subject to the same metamorphism. Nevertheless, despite the geologic complications some fossils have been discovered in the state.

During the early Paleozoic, New Hampshire was covered by a warm, shallow sea that would come to be inhabited by creatures like brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites. Geologic forces raised the local elevation during the Devonian, and local sediments were eroded away from the state rather than deposited, leading to a gap in the local rock record. Erosion continued through the Mesozoic and the early to mid Cenozoic, leaving few rocks of that age, and no fossils. By the Ice Age, the state was being worked over by glaciers and had similar wildlife to the modern Canadian Arctic.

New Hampshire fossils had attracted scientific attention by the late 1800s when C. H. Hitchcock reported local invertebrate remains preserved near Littleton. Other notable finds include fossils preserved in metamorphic rock that are still recognizable despite the extreme geologic forces exerted on these rocks.

Aleksei Fedorovich Losev (Lang-ru; 23 September 1893 – 24 May 1988) was a Russian philosopher, philologist and culturologist, one of the most prominent figures in Russian philosophical and religious thought of the 20th century.
In mathematics, Casey's theorem, also known as the generalized Ptolemy's theorem, is a theorem in Euclidean geometry named after the Irish mathematician John Casey.

Pope Benedict XVI and ecumenism  

Pope Benedict XVI (r. 2005–2013) engaged in ecumenical efforts while maintaining traditional Catholic doctrine on the nature of the Church. In 2007, he reaffirmed that the Catholic Church is the sole true Church, recognizing Orthodox communities as "churches" but describing Protestant denominations as "ecclesial communities." This stance drew criticism from some Protestant leaders. Benedict sought reconciliation with traditionalist Catholics, lifting excommunications on leaders of the Society of St. Pius X in 2009. Relations with the Eastern Orthodox Church were mixed, with tensions over papal titles and jurisdiction, though he held dialogues with Patriarch Bartholomew I. He also facilitated the creation of Anglican ordinariates, allowing groups of Anglicans to enter full communion with Rome while retaining elements of their liturgy. His approach emphasized continuity with Catholic tradition while cautiously pursuing Christian unity.

Messier 83 (also known as the Southern Pinwheel Galaxy or NGC 5236) is a barred spiral galaxy located approximately 14.7 million light-years away in the constellation Hydra. Classified as SAB(s)c, it features loosely wound spiral arms and a weak central bar. M83 is a grand design spiral galaxy with active star formation, particularly along its spiral arms. It has hosted six observed supernovae and is the central galaxy of a subgroup within the Centaurus A/M83 Group. The Galaxy Evolution Explorer mission discovered young stars in its outer regions, challenging previous assumptions about star formation in such areas.
The Harold Peary Show is a radio situation comedy broadcast in the United States September 17, 1950-June 13, 1951 on CBS. Some sources refer to the program as Honest Harold or The Hal Peary Show.

William H. Belpitt (May 3, 1860 – November 29, 1912) was an Australian-born U.S. Navy sailor awarded the Medal of Honor for rescuing a drowning man in Fuzhou, China, in 1884 while serving aboard the USS *Monocacy*.
Gamawan Fauzi (born 9 November 1957) is an Indonesian politician.

Hambleden Lock is a lock on the River Thames in Berkshire, England, managed by the Environment Agency. First built in 1773 and last rebuilt in 1994, it has a length of 61 meters and a fall of 1.44 meters. Historically, the site included a mill and weir mentioned in the Domesday Book, with a flash lock in use until the 19th century. The lock was rebuilt in 1870 due to poor condition and later upgraded with new weirs in 1884. Accessible from Aston or Mill End, it lies near Henley-on-Thames and is part of the Henley Royal Regatta course. The Thames Path runs nearby, and the weir is used for kayaking.

Hotel Kazakhstan (Lang-kk, Lang-ru) is the third-tallest building in the city of Almaty, Kazakhstan. It is 102 m (335 ft) tall, with 26 floors. It is situated in the southern center of Almaty. It is located on Dostyq Avenue, a major road in the east part of the city which runs all the way from Medeo to Panfilov Park, in the north part of town.

The total area is 44887.5 sq. meters.

The building was erected in 1977. It is constructed to stand an earthquake that measures 9.0 on the Richter scale. It is a famous landmark all over Almaty, and serves as a symbol of the city. It is also the eighth-tallest building in all Kazakhstan.

In October 2018, architectural lighting was installed on the hotel building.

The "Friendship" public garden adjoins the hotel territory, where the Camels panel, a monument to Shamshi Kaldayakov and a fountain are located. Convenient access to the embankment of the Malaya Almatinka River has been arranged.
Gerhard vom Rath (died 1888) was a German mineralogist and geologist, known for his research on minerals and crystallography.
Lawton is a city in Ramsey County, North Dakota, United States. Founded in 1902, it had a population of 15 as of the 2020 census.

DecembeRadio is the self-titled second studio album by American Christian rock band DecembeRadio, released on June 27, 2006, by Slanted Records. Produced by Scotty Wilbanks, the album features a harder rock sound compared to their previous work, with tracks like *"Love Found Me (Love’s Got a Hold)"* and *"Dangerous."* It debuted on Billboard's Christian charts and later re-entered during their 2008 tour with Third Day. The album won a Dove Award for Rock Album of the Year in 2007. An expanded edition, including new tracks and live content, was released in 2007.

Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Catalan economist and politician who is current serving as the First Vice President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2021 and as Acting President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2022.

Anna Magdalena Appel (8 February 1846 – 19 December 1917) was a German ballet dancer and the morganatic wife of Louis III, Grand Duke of Hesse. Ennobled as *Freifrau von Hochstädten*, she influenced the arts in the Grand Duchy before retiring to Wiesbaden after his death.

The French Covered Court Championships its original name also known as the French Covered Court Open Championships and the French Indoors was a tennis event held from 1895 through 1971 in Paris, France and Lyon, France.
The Willard Gallery was a New York City art gallery founded in 1940 by Marian Willard Johnson. It operated until 1987, showcasing contemporary artists.

Long-chain-alcohol oxidase (EC 1.1.3.20) is an oxidoreductase enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of long-chain alcohols using oxygen as an acceptor. It is involved in omega-oxidation pathways in yeast and plants, converting alcohols to aldehydes and carboxylic acids. The enzyme, containing heme and flavin cofactors, varies in structure and substrate specificity across species. Industrially, it is used in producing dicarboxylic acids for fragrances, polymers, and pharmaceuticals.

Frederik Wilhelm Stabell (25 May 1763 – 2 June 1836) was a Norwegian military officer and politician. He was a member of the Norwegian Constituent Assembly in 1814, and ended his military career with the rank of general.

A. Judson Wells (1917–2008) was an American chemist who worked at DuPont for nearly five decades, retiring as director of its industrial products division. He later published influential research on the health effects of secondhand smoke, including studies estimating tens of thousands of annual U.S. deaths linked to passive smoking. He held a doctorate in chemistry from Harvard University.

Paladin Career and Technical High School is a public charter school in Coon Rapids, Minnesota, serving at-risk students in grades 9–12. Founded in 2002, it offers career-focused education with a diploma track.

"Ambush" is the fourth-season premiere of the American medical drama *ER*. Originally airing on September 25, 1997, the episode was directed by Thomas Schlamme and written by Carol Flint. Filmed live twice for East and West Coast broadcasts, it follows the staff of County General Hospital as they are documented by a PBS film crew. Key events include Dr. David Morgenstern suffering a heart attack, new surgeon Elizabeth Corday (Alex Kingston) joining the team, and John Carter learning he must repeat his internship. The episode received mixed reviews, with praise for its technical execution but criticism for its script. It drew 42.71 million viewers, becoming the most-watched drama season premiere in television history at the time. At the 50th Primetime Emmy Awards, it won for Outstanding Technical Direction and received additional nominations.
Leslie Walter Gandar (26 January 1919 – 16 December 1994) was a New Zealand politician of the National Party.
Benjamin Hoffman (April 15, 1864 – May 20, 1922) was a Jewish-American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.

The Jamaat al Muslimeen coup attempt was an attempt to overthrow the government of Trinidad and Tobago, instigated on Friday, 27 July 1990. Over the course of six days, Jamaat al Muslimeen, a radical extremist Islamist group, held hostages (including Prime Minister A. N. R. Robinson and other government officials) at the Red House and at the headquarters of the state-owned national television broadcaster, Trinidad and Tobago Television (TTT). On 1 August, the insurgents surrendered. They were charged with treason, but were ordered released by the Court of Appeal. Twenty four people were killed and many more were injured in the coup.

The ZBD-03 or Type 03 (industrial designation WZ506) is a Chinese airborne infantry fighting vehicle. It features a light-weight chassis and hydropneumatic suspension for airborne operations. Early prototypes received the designation ZLC-2000.

Joel Spitzer (born 1957) is an American smoking cessation educator known for his advocacy of quitting smoking "cold turkey." Since 1971, he has conducted hundreds of seminars and clinics, promoting his free book *Never Take Another Puff*. He opposes nicotine replacement therapy, arguing that abrupt cessation is more effective.

Barbara Keiler (born April 7, 1953 in New York City, United States), known more widely by her pseudonym Judith Arnold, is a best-selling American author of crime fiction and over eighty-five romance novels. She has been writing since 1983, and has also been published under the pen names Ariel Berk and Thea Frederick.

An antifuse is an electrical device that performs the opposite function of a fuse—it starts with high resistance and becomes conductive when triggered by high voltage. Commonly used in low-voltage Christmas lights, antifuses bypass failed bulbs to keep the circuit operational. In integrated circuits, they enable one-time programmable (OTP) configurations in devices like programmable logic arrays (PLDs) and read-only memory (PROM). Different types include dielectric antifuses, amorphous silicon antifuses, and Zener antifuses, each suited for specific semiconductor processes. Historically, antifuses were also used in series-wired street lighting systems.

The Redan Football Netball Club (nicknamed the Lions) is an Australian rules football and netball club located in the city of Ballarat, Victoria. The football team currently competes in the Ballarat Football League. Redan has a history of 59 Premierships including 11 Senior Football Premierships and was named the Victorian Country Football League Most Disciplined Club in 2004 & 2005.

Redan players have also won the Henderson Medal (Ballarat Football League's best player award) on 14 occasions with two players, Michael Smith (1994, 1994) and Jarrod Edwards (2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009) winning the award in consecutive years. Edwards is the first player in BFL history to win four consecutive Henderson Medals.

The club also has four winners of the Tony Lockett Medal, for the leading goalscorer in the Ballarat Football League. Redan currently play their home matches at City Oval in Ballarat and play in a predominantly maroon strip with a maroon top band and a lion logo on the chest.

The first Redan game was reported in The Ballarat Star Saturday September 2, 1871 against the Ballarat Football Club. The Redan team was made up from miners from the Band of Hope mine and their average weight was 14 Stone (90 kg). The Star reported that the Redan team were everywhere, kicking and tripping regardless of the consequences.

Former Adelaide Crows coach, the late Phil Walsh, played for Redan prior to be being recruited by Collingwood.

Mindy Lubber is President of Ceres, a non-profit sustainability advocacy organization based in Boston, Massachusetts. Lubber became President of Ceres in January 2014 and was a speaker at the Sustainable Innovation Forum of the 2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference.

Jessie Joe Parker (born 22 August 1985) is a Papua New Guinean rugby league coach and former player. A centre, he played for clubs in England, including Featherstone Rovers and Wakefield Trinity, and represented Papua New Guinea in the 2008 and 2013 World Cups. He later became a player-coach for Hensingham ARLFC.

Barry Took (19 June 1928 – 31 March 2002) was a British comedy writer, performer, and broadcaster. Best known for his collaborations with Marty Feldman, he co-wrote *Round the Horne* and contributed to *The Army Game* and *Bootsie and Snudge*. As a BBC comedy advisor, he helped form *Monty Python’s Flying Circus*. Later, he chaired *The News Quiz* and hosted *Points of View*. Took also wrote several books on comedy and an autobiography.

Cuba–Suriname relations are the bilateral ties between Cuba and Suriname. Established in 1981, relations have included diplomatic exchanges, Cuban medical aid, and limited trade, though tensions arose after the 1983 Grenada crisis.
The FC Ingolstadt 04 II is a German association football club from the city of Ingolstadt, Bavaria. It is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt.

Fluorenone is an aromatic organic compound with the chemical formula C13H8O. It is bright fluorescent yellow in color and is a solid at room temperature.

It can be synthesised from fluorene with the addition of glacial acetic acid and sodium hypochlorite solution, undergoing an oxidation reaction.

It is used to make antimalaria drugs. It is used as a fragrance or odor agent in candles.

Henry I of Cyprus (3 May 1217 – 18 January 1253) was King of Cyprus from 1218 until his death. Crowned at age eight, his early reign was marked by the War of the Lombards, a conflict between his regents and Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II. Henry assumed full power in 1232 and later served as regent of Jerusalem (1246–1253). He married three times, including Plaisance of Antioch, with whom he had his successor, Hugh II. He was buried in Nicosia.

Kihikihi, a small town located in the Waikato region of the North Island of New Zealand, serves as a satellite community of Te Awamutu, five kilometres to the north, and lies 35 kilometres south of Hamilton. The 2018 New Zealand census recorded a population of 2,808 people The main reason for the large increase since 2013 is the construction of a large number of new dwellings.

The town's outer rim has merged with the expanding rim of Te Awamutu, rendering the boundary between the two towns difficult to perceive.

Kihikihi is a Māori-language word meaning "cicada"; the name imitates the sound made by the insect. A large statue of a cicada stands at the northern entrance to the town.

Kihikihi's multi-purpose sports domain hosts national and international equestrian events such as the FEI Eventing World Cup.

The town is also home to the historic Kihikihi Polo Club, founded in 1892 by the Kay family.

A Fazenda 4 was the fourth season of the Brazilian reality television series A Fazenda which premiered Tuesday, July 19, 2011 at 11:15 p.m. on RecordTV.

This season was confirmed on late December 2010, before the finale of the third season. Britto Junior and Chris Couto reprise their hosting stints for the show. Tina Roma makes her debut as the show's new special correspondent.

For the first time ever, four women reached the finals, making this the first reality show in Brazilian television history with public voting to do so. No Limite 1 (2000) and Aprendiz 6 − Universitário (2009) also managed to create all female final fours but there was no public vote involved.

On October 12, 2011, personal trainer Joana Machado won the competition with 48% of the public vote over TV host Monique Evans (44%) and DJ Raquel Pacheco (8%).

*Fiction* is a 2020 EP by Canadian experimental rock band Suuns. Released on October 30, it features six tracks blending avant-rock, noise, and psychedelic influences, including the singles "Pray," "Breathe," and "Fiction."

Brian Edward O'Connor (born February 14, 1953), known professionally as Brian Brucker O'Connor or Brian O'Connor, is an American actor, comedian and guidance counselor. His best known roles include Biddle in Beverly Hills Cop II (1987) and Schemer on Shining Time Station (1989–1993). O’Connor briefly reprised his role as Schemer in a 2020 parody of Thomas and the Magic Railroad entitled “The Magic Railroad Parody” which aired exclusively on YouTube.

Edmonton Public Schools (EPS) is the largest public school district in Alberta, Canada, serving over 105,000 students across 212 schools. Established in 1881, it operates with a $1.21 billion budget and is governed by nine elected trustees. The district pioneered site-based decision-making, allowing schools greater autonomy in programming. EPS offers diverse programs, including alternative, special education, and language immersion, as well as early learning and kindergarten. Its historic McKay Avenue School, now an archives and museum, was Alberta’s first legislative meeting site.

Design studies is an interdisciplinary field examining both the practice of design and its broader societal, cultural, and environmental impacts. It integrates perspectives from the humanities, social sciences, and sciences while exploring topics such as design history, ethics, sustainability, and decolonization. The field employs diverse research methods, including design ethnography, actor-network theory, and semiotics, to analyze how design shapes human experience. Key journals like *Design Studies* and *Design Issues* reflect its dual focus on design processes and critical theory.

Calamus is a genus of flowering plants in the palm family Arecaceae that are among several genera known as rattan palms. There are an estimated 400 species in this genus, all native to tropical and subtropical Asia, Africa, and Australia.

The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd official season, or 76th season of top-tier football in Albania (including three unofficial championships of WW2) and the fourteenth season under the name Kategoria superiore. The season began on 10 September 2011 and ended on 12 May 2012. The defending champions were Skënderbeu, who won their second Albanian league championship last season and third in their history.

The size of the league was expanded from twelve to fourteen teams this season.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality, currently the host of HGTV's Home to Win and an on-air personality of Entertainment Tonight Canada.

Lead Section:  

*¡Que viva la lucha!* (2007) is a Mexican documentary film directed by Gustavo Vázquez, exploring the cultural significance of *lucha libre* in Tijuana. The film follows the lives of local wrestlers—including *técnicos* (heroes) like Extreme Tiger and *rudos* (villains) like Ángel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo—as they balance their working-class identities with their wrestling personas. It examines themes of good versus evil, the symbolism of masks, and the diverse participants in *lucha libre*, such as women, *exóticos* (gay wrestlers), and referees. The documentary also highlights the passionate fan culture, where spectators actively engage with the spectacle. Filmed over two years with multiple cameras, the production faced challenges, including injuries, but captured the raw energy of Tijuana’s wrestling scene.
Tom Lawton (born 27 November 1962) is an Australian former rugby union hooker who played for Queensland and the Wallabies, including at the 1987 Rugby World Cup.
Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is an Australian former rugby league halfback who played for the Gold Coast Titans and was a Queensland Cup champion with the Tweed Heads Seagulls.
Eric Mattson is a Democratic member of the Illinois Senate representing the 43rd district since 2022. A Joliet fire captain, he was appointed to fill a vacancy after John Connor's resignation.

Svalikhita-jivani is an autobiography written in 1896 by Kedarnatha Datta (also known as Bhaktivinoda Thakur), a prominent thinker of Bengali Renaissance and a leading philosopher, savant and spiritual reformer of Gaudiya Vaishnavism who effected its resurgence in India in late 19th and early 20th century and was hailed by contemporary scholars as the most influential Gaudiya Vaisnava leader of his time.

Written in Bengali on the request of Bhaktivinoda's son Lalita Prasad, Svalikhita-jivani gives a detailed autobiographical account that spanned most of his life from his birth in 1838 until retirement in 1894. The book was published by Lalita Prasad in 1916, after Bhaktivinoda's death.

Ruby Cabernet is a red wine grape variety, a cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Carignan, developed in 1936 by Harold Olmo at the University of California, Davis. Bred for heat tolerance and quality, it is primarily grown in California, South Africa, and Australia, though smaller plantings exist in Argentina, Brazil, Mexico, Chile, and Israel. The vine is drought-resistant but susceptible to powdery mildew and poor fruit set in some regions. In California’s San Joaquin Valley, it is often used in bulk wines, though careful viticulture can produce structured, aromatic wines. Notable producers include Van Loveren in South Africa and Andrew McPherson in Australia. The variety thrives in deep clay loam soils with open canopies.

Marguerite Louppe (1902–1989) was a French painter, best known for her still lifes and landscapes. Her work synthesized the styles of Cubism and Purism. Louppe was married to the French artist Maurice Brianchon.

The Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is a research institute at the University of Manitoba, established in 1991 by Drs. Noralou and Leslie Roos. It maintains the Manitoba Population Research Data Repository, a comprehensive collection of anonymized health, social, and economic data on Manitoba residents. MCHP conducts population health research, focusing on health services, social determinants of health, and policy evaluation. The center collaborates with government agencies and provides secure data access for researchers.

A fish flake is a wooden rack used for drying salted cod in the open air, traditionally found in fishing villages, particularly in Newfoundland. Constructed with poles and sometimes covered with spruce boughs, flakes allow fish to cure under sunlight. Permanent flakes were built near fishing stages, while temporary "hand flakes" were used during abundant catches. This method was essential before refrigeration, ensuring fish preservation for market. Flakes remained in use in some communities into the late 20th century.

The spectacle case pearly mussel (*Cumberlandia monodonta*) is an endangered freshwater mussel native to the Mississippi, Ohio, and Missouri River basins. It has an oblong, smooth shell that darkens with age and can exceed 9 inches in length. Its life cycle includes a parasitic larval stage (glochidia) that requires specific fish hosts, including *Hiodon tergisus* and *Hiodon alosoides*.  

Historically found in 44 streams, its range has declined by 55%, with remaining populations fragmented and vulnerable. Major threats include habitat degradation from dams, mining, sedimentation, invasive zebra mussels, and climate change. Listed as endangered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2012, conservation efforts include habitat restoration, genetic research, and laboratory propagation. Despite challenges, some populations remain stable, and ongoing monitoring aims to prevent further decline.

True Liberty is an American Christian hardcore band, and they primarily play hardcore punk and punk rock. They come from Richmond, Virginia. The band started making music in 2005, and their members are lead vocalist and lead guitarist, Mike Liberty, bassist, Tom Liberty, and drummer and background vocalist, Aaron Liberty. Their first studio album, This Is War, was released in 2007 by Raging Storm Records. They independently released, Brotherhood, that came out in 2008. The studio album, City of the One, was released by Wounded Records, in 2010. Their subsequent studio album, Marked for Life, was released by Thumper Punk Records, in 2012. They released a live album, Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death, with Thumper Punk Records, in 2014.
Q.S. Serafijn (born February 19, 1960) is a Dutch conceptual artist and author, who is working as sculptor, photographer, and installation artist.

Trelawne is a historic manor in Cornwall, England, dating back to Norman times. Originally held by the de Vautort family, it passed through several noble families before being acquired by the Trelawnys in 1600. The Grade II* listed Trelawne House incorporates medieval elements, with later additions in the 18th and 19th centuries. The estate was long associated with the Trelawny and Salusbury-Trelawny baronets, who served as MPs, sheriffs, and lord-lieutenants of Cornwall. In the 1960s, the estate was converted into a holiday caravan park.

The 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron is a United States Space Force unit under Space Delta 3, based at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado. It conducts offensive counterspace operations and provides space superiority effects for global and theater campaigns.

Daniël Termont (born 19 May 1953) is a Belgian politician who served as the mayor of Ghent from 2007 to 2018. A longtime member of the Socialist Party (SP.a), he began his political career in local youth movements before being elected as a municipal councillor in 1976. He later became an alderman, overseeing the port of Ghent and economic development. After leading his party to victory in the 2006 municipal elections, he succeeded Frank Beke as mayor in 2007.  

Beyond his mayoral duties, Termont held leadership roles in energy and infrastructure organizations, including as vice president of Fluxys and FARYS, and as a board member of the Port of Ghent. He also served as vice-chairman of Eurocities, a network of major European cities. In 2014, he was a finalist for the World Mayor Prize, placing second. His tenure was marked by economic initiatives and strong ties to local institutions, including football club AA Gent, of which he was a prominent supporter.

The Bali Botanic Garden (Lang-id) is the largest botanic garden in Indonesia and is located in the mountainous region of Bedugul, Tabanan Regency, central Bali, around 90 minutes drive north of Denpasar. The Garden was established on 15 July 1959 and is situated around 1300 metres above sea level overlooking Bratan Lake and the Ulun Danu Temple on the slopes of Tapak Hill. The Garden is a centre for botanical research, conservation, education and recreation. It is operated by the Indonesian Institute of Sciences (LIPI).

The Garden has an area of 157.5 hectares (389 acres) and daytime temperatures range from 17 - 25 °C and 10 - 15 °C at night. The humidity averages around 70-90%.

The Garden contains more than 21,000 living specimens belonging to 2,400 species, representing various species from mountainous areas of eastern Indonesia: Bali, Nusa Tenggara, Sulawesi, Maluku and Papua. In addition its herbarium contains 10,000 preserved plant specimens ranging from algae to flowering plants.

Apart from plant collections that include orchids, ferns, cacti and carnivorous plants, there is also a traditional Balinese style guesthouse that functions as a guest house for visitors. One of the world's largest displays of begonias is also on display in the conservatory building.

Once Mekel (born Elfonda Mekel; May 21, 1970) is an Indonesian pop-rock singer, best known as the former lead vocalist of Dewa 19. He has also released solo hits, including *"Dealova"* and *"Separuh Nafasku."*

Joseph Buckland (born 27 October 1992) is a British ice dancer. He has competed with multiple partners, including Olivia Smart, with whom he won the 2015 British national title. Since 2016, he has skated with Robynne Tweedale.
Masʿūd Zazoyo or Masʿūd of Zaz (c.1430/31 – 1509/1512) was a Syriac Orthodox author, hermit, monk and prelate.

New areas (Chinese: 新区) are special economic-development zones in China. State-level new areas receive preferential policies from the central government to attract investment and boost economic growth. Provincial and prefectural-level new areas are administered by local governments rather than the central government.

The Swiss Social Archives (SSA) is a memory institution in Zurich, Switzerland, specializing in the documentation of social history. Founded in 1906 by social reformer Paul Pflüger, it was originally known as the *Centre for Switzerland's Social Literature*. The archives became a key resource for political refugees, including figures like Lenin and Trotsky. Recognized as a research facility in 1974, the SSA moved to its current location in 1984. Funded by the Swiss Confederation, the Canton of Zurich, and other sources, it employs historians, archivists, and librarians to preserve and study social movements. Designated a Swiss cultural property of national significance, it remains an important center for social research.

Four Lane Ends Interchange is a Tyne and Wear Metro station in Longbenton, North Tyneside, England. Opened in 1980, it serves as a key transport hub with a multi-storey car park, bus interchange, and step-free access. The station features public art installations and connects to major employment sites and business parks in the area.

Pashchimottanasana (Lang-sa), Seated Forward Bend, or Intense Dorsal Stretch is a seated forward-bending asana in hatha yoga and modern yoga as exercise. Janusirsasana is a variant with one knee bent out to the side; Upavishthakonasana has the legs straight and wide apart.

Basharat Mirbabayeva (Lang-ru, Lang-uz; 5 December 1916 – 20 March 2010) was a locomotive operator in the Uzbek SSR. In addition to being the first woman in the Uzbek SSR to drive a train, she was also the first Uzbek woman to parachute from an airplane.

Hochschild Kohn's, also known as Hochschild-Kohn or simply Hochschild's, was a 20th-century American department store chain based in Baltimore, Maryland. It was started in 1897 as a partnership between Max Hochschild, Benno Kohn, and his brother Louis B. Kohn. Hochschild-Kohn & Company opened that year with a downtown-Baltimore store on the northwest corner of Howard and Lexington Streets. The chain closed in 1984.

SPINDLE (Sub-glacial Polar Ice Navigation, Descent, and Lake Exploration) is a 2-stage autonomous vehicle system consisting of a robotic ice-penetrating carrier vehicle (cryobot) and an autonomous submersible HAUV (hovering autonomous underwater vehicle). The cryobot is designed to descend through an ice body into a sub-surface ocean and deploy the HAUV submersible to conduct long range reconnaissance, life search, and sample collection. The HAUV submersible will return to, and auto-dock with, the cryobot at the conclusion of the mission for subsequent data uplink and sample return to the surface.

The SPINDLE designed is targeted at sub-glacial lakes such as Lake Vostok and South Pole Lake in Antarctica. SPINDLE would develop the technologies for a Flagship-class mission to either the shallow lakes of Jupiter's moon Europa, the sub-surface ocean of Ganymede, or the geyser sources on both Europa and Enceladus. The project is funded by NASA and is being designed at Stone Aerospace under the supervision of Principal Investigator Bill Stone.

James David Bouknight (/ˈbʊknaɪt/; born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.
Crystina Poncher (born July 20, 1984) is currently a commentator, host and reporter for Top Rank Boxing on ESPN. She previously served as host, reporter and correspondent for NFL Network and NFL.com.

The Medical University of South Carolina College of Dental Medicine is a public dental school in Charleston, South Carolina. Established in 1953, it awards the DMD degree and operates in the James B. Edwards Dental Clinics Building, a state-of-the-art facility opened in 2009. The college is known for its competitive admissions and strong clinical training.
Lawton is a city in Ramsey County, North Dakota, United States. The population was 15 at the 2020 census. Lawton was founded in 1902.

"Flash" is a song by Japanese girl group Perfume, released on March 16, 2016, as a single from their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the J-pop and EDM track features martial arts-inspired choreography. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light effects, becoming Perfume's most-viewed video on YouTube with over 60 million views. The song was also included in a re-arranged version on *Cosmic Explorer*.

The Black sermonic tradition, or Black preaching tradition, is an approach to sermon (or homily) construction and delivery practiced primarily among African Americans in the Black Church. The tradition seeks to preach messages that appeal to both the intellect and the emotive dimensions of humanity. The tradition finds its roots in the painful experiences of blacks during slavery in the United States, as well as experiences during the Jim Crow era and subsequent discrimination.

KNBN (channel 21) is an NBC-affiliated television station licensed to Rapid City, South Dakota, owned by Rapid Broadcasting Company. Launched in 2000, it broadcasts local news under the NewsCenter 1 brand and carries MyNetworkTV on a secondary subchannel. KNBN originated in 1996 as a low-power station after KEVN-TV switched to Fox, leaving Rapid City without an NBC affiliate. The station operates a network of translators across western South Dakota and Wyoming.
Dolní Bečva is a municipality and village in Vsetín District in the Zlín Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 1,900 inhabitants.

Heinz Rein (9 April 1906 – 16 January 1991) was a German writer known for his postwar *rubble literature*. His best-known novel, *Finale Berlin* (1947), became an early postwar bestseller. Banned from writing under the Nazis, he later worked in East Germany before moving to West Germany in the 1950s.

McKenzie is a city in Carroll, Weakley, and Henry counties in Tennessee, United States. Incorporated in 1869, it had a population of 5,529 as of the 2020 census. Located along U.S. Route 79, it serves as a regional hub in northern Tennessee.
Lucie Papineau (born April 26, 1962) is a writer living in Quebec, Canada who mainly writes children's books.

PRNP (prion protein) is the human gene encoding for the major prion protein PrP (prion protein, Pr for prion, and P for protein), also known as CD230 (cluster of differentiation 230). Expression of the protein is most predominant in the nervous system but occurs in many other tissues throughout the body.

The protein can exist in multiple isoforms: the normal PrPC form, and the protease-resistant form designated PrPRes such as the disease-causing PrPSc(scrapie) and an isoform located in mitochondria. The misfolded version PrPSc is associated with a variety of cognitive disorders and neurodegenerative diseases such as in animals: ovine scrapie, bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, mad cow disease), feline spongiform encephalopathy, transmissible mink encephalopathy (TME), exotic ungulate encephalopathy, chronic wasting disease (CWD) which affects cervids; and in humans: Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD), fatal familial insomnia (FFI), Gerstmann–Sträussler–Scheinker syndrome (GSS), kuru, and variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (vCJD). Similarities exist between kuru, thought to be due to human ingestion of diseased individuals, and vCJD, thought to be due to human ingestion of BSE-tainted cattle products.

Milltown (Irish: Baile an Mhuilinn, meaning "town of the mill") is a village in County Kildare, Ireland. The village is in the townland of the same name in the civil parish of Feighcullen It is 7km from the town of Newbridge. It is on the R415 regional road between Allenwood and Crookstown.

"Eu Quero Tchu, Eu Quero Tcha" is a 2012 sertanejo-pop song by Brazilian duo João Lucas & Marcelo, written by Shylton Fernandes. The soccer-themed music video features footballer Neymar and became a viral hit. The track gained further popularity through its inclusion in the telenovela *Avenida Brasil* and was referenced in *The Simpsons*. It inspired multiple covers, including Pitbull and Enrique Iglesias' "Tchu Tchu Tcha." A version by Flavel & Neto also became popular in Europe.
