Santiago de Cuba railway station (officially *Santiago de Cuba General Senén Casas*) is the main eastern rail terminus in Cuba, located in Santiago de Cuba. Rebuilt in 1997, it serves as a hub for long-distance trains, including the *Tren Francés* connecting Havana to Santiago, as well as regional services to cities like Guantánamo and Bayamo. The station features a modern building with vaulted roofs, a clock tower, and multiple platforms. It also includes rail sidings for the nearby harbor.

John Boudreaux (December 10, 1936 – January 14, 2017) was an American drummer known for his work in New Orleans rhythm and blues. A key studio musician in the 1960s, he played on hits by Ernie K-Doe, Lee Dorsey, and Professor Longhair, and co-founded AFO Records. He later worked in Los Angeles with artists like Sam Cooke and Sonny & Cher.

HNoMS Rauma was an Otra-class minesweeper built in 1939 for the Royal Norwegian Navy. Captured by the Germans during the 1940 invasion of Norway and renamed Kamerun, she was returned to the Norwegians after the end of the Second World War and recommissioned in 1947. Rauma remained in service until being sold for scrapping in 1963.

Matthew Daniels (born September 27, 1989) is a former American football safety and current special teams coach of the Minnesota Vikings of the National Football League (NFL). He was signed by the St. Louis Rams as an undrafted free agent in 2012. He played college football for Duke.

The Parapetí River is a river in Bolivia that has its source on the eastern side the Andes, flows through the Gran Chaco of Bolivia and ends in the marshes of the Bañados de Izozog depression. The drainage basin is 25,300 km2, and with the inclusion of the Bañados of Izozog is 61,903 km2.

Karli Coburger (born 4 October 1929) was a German Stasi officer who served as head of Section VIII (Surveillance, Investigations, and Arrests). Known for his role in political interrogations, he was later convicted for complicity in perverting justice. He joined the Stasi in 1952 and remained active in historical revisionist groups after German reunification.
Wilkinson Heights is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, with a population of 1,946 as of the 2020 census.

The new areas or new districts of the People's Republic of China are new urban districts that are given special economic and development support by the Chinese Central Government or regional government. New areas are divided into two varieties: administrative or management and further divided into levels: state-level, provincial-level, and prefectural-level.
"Like a Man" is a song recorded by Onhel, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne. It was released as a single on August 8, 2017.

Augustus Jones (c. 1757 – November 16, 1836) was a British Crown surveyor and farmer in Upper Canada, best known for his extensive land surveys that shaped early settlement patterns in southern Ontario. Born in New York's Hudson River Valley, he fled to Canada as a Loyalist in the 1780s and became Deputy Surveyor General for the Nassau District (later the Home District). His surveys included key townships along Lake Ontario, the baseline for what became Yonge Street, and the Toronto Purchase boundaries. Jones also played a role in early relations with Indigenous communities, particularly the Mississauga and Mohawk nations, and was briefly involved in tensions following the murder of Chief Wabakinine.  

After retiring from surveying in 1800, he farmed in Saltfleet Township and later moved to Brantford, living among the Mohawk. He had children with both his Mohawk wife, Sarah Tekarihogen, and his earlier Mississauga partner, Tuhbenahneequay, including the prominent Methodist missionary Peter Jones. Honored with a statue in Hamilton in 2005, Jones' surveys laid the groundwork for southern Ontario's development.

The Finse Tunnel (Lang-no) is a 10,300-metre-long (6.4 mi; 11,300 yd) railway tunnel west of the village of Finse in Eidfjord Municipality in Vestland county, Norway. The tunnel is part of the Bergen Line between the cities of Oslo and Bergen. It is the longest tunnel on the line, and the third longest railway tunnel in the kingdom. Inside the tunnel, at 1,237 metres (4,058 ft) elevation, is the highest point on the Norwegian railway network.

The tunnel was constructed to increase the regularity of the line past the Finse peak which was often blocked by snow. Planning started during the 1980s to remove this section that was regularly snowed down, and at the same time reduce the length and increase the speed of the railway. Construction started in 1990, and it was opened by King Harald V on 16 May 1993. Following the construction of the tunnel, 32 kilometres (20 mi) of railway around this area was also rebuilt to achieve higher train speeds, a shorter overall distance, and to maximize the natural protection from the weather. This was built in five stages, opening between 1995 and 1998.

The tie is a symbol in the shape of an arc similar to a large breve, used in Greek, phonetic alphabets, and Z notation. It can be used between two characters with spacing as punctuation, non-spacing as a diacritic, or (underneath) as a proofreading mark. It can be above or below, and reversed. Its forms are called tie, double breve, enotikon or papyrological hyphen, ligature tie, and undertie.

Hoang Anh Gia Lai Group (Lang-vi). Group also Hoang Anh Gia Lai Joint Stock Company (Lang-vi), abbreviated as HAGL or HAG) is a diversified company headquartered in Pleiku, Vietnam.

From a small furniture producer the company diversified into other industries such as rubber, finances a football club (Hoang Anh Gia Lai Club) and was Vietnam's largest property developer in 2010 and the second largest in 2011 (behind Vingroup). It has also grown significantly outside of Vietnam, with investments of around $1bn in Laos, hundreds of millions in Myanmar, $100m in Cambodia and tens of millions of dollars in Thailand.

Antiscopa elaphra is a species of moth in the family Crambidae, endemic to New Zealand. First described by Edward Meyrick in 1884, it was later reclassified into the genus *Antiscopa*. Adults are small with distinctive triangular forewings and are active from August to March. They are attracted to light.

Rhyssa persuasoria, also known as the sabre wasp, is a species belonging to the family Ichneumonidae subfamily Rhyssinae. Members of this subfamily, including those of Rhyssa and the allied Megarhyssa, are also known collectively as giant ichneumonid wasps or giant ichneumons.
Louis of Brienne, also known as Louis of Acre (died in 1297), was viscount of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe in Maine, France.
Doris Mary Stenton, Lady Stenton, FBA (1894–1971) was an English historian of the Middle Ages.

Kentucky Route 8 is a 114.202-mile-long (183.790 km) east–west state highway divided into two distinct segments across northern Kentucky. The western terminus of the route is at KY 237 near Francisville. The eastern terminus is at U.S. Route 23 in South Portsmouth. The two distinct segments of this route were not meant to be connected together. KY 8 from its west end in Boone County to Augusta in Bracken County is designated as a part of an identified corridor for bikes named the Ramblin' River Tour by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).

KY 8 is named the Mary Ingles Highway for part of its length. It is rumored that she was the first white woman in Kentucky. Captured by Indians in Virginia in 1755 and taken to Ohio, she later escaped a salt-making party at Big Bone Lick and made her way across the Kentucky wilderness back home to Virginia.
Oksana Kostina (15 April 1972 – 11 February 1993) was a Russian rhythmic gymnast who won five gold medals at the 1992 World Championships. She died in a car crash at age 20.
MyPertamina is a loyalty program and mobile app by PT Pertamina, launched in 2017, offering electronic payments and rewards at Pertamina gas stations and merchandise outlets.

In Search of Sunrise is a trance music compilation series, initially mixed by Tiësto (1999–2009), later by Richard Durand (2010–2015), and currently by Markus Schulz (2018–present). Released under Black Hole Recordings, the series features location-themed subtitles and expanded multi-disc formats.
Wodan Timbur Coaster is a wooden roller coaster, located in the Europa-Park in Rust, Baden-Württemberg. The coaster opened on March 31, 2012.

Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American civil engineer, educator, and apostle of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church). Born in Fillmore, Utah Territory, he was a descendant of prominent early LDS leaders, including his father, Francis M. Lyman, and grandfather, Amasa M. Lyman, both apostles. After earning degrees from the University of Michigan, the University of Chicago, and Cornell University, he worked as a teacher and engineer.  

Lyman was ordained an apostle in 1918, filling a vacancy in the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles. However, in 1943, he was excommunicated for unlawful cohabitation after secretly entering a plural marriage in defiance of church policy. He was later rebaptized in 1954 but never reinstated to his former position. His wife, Amy Brown Lyman, served as Relief Society general president during his excommunication. Lyman died in Salt Lake City at age 93.

Paul Speratus (13 December 1484 – 12 August 1551) was a German Lutheran minister, hymnwriter, and early supporter of Martin Luther. He co-authored the first Lutheran hymnal, *Achtliederbuch* (1524), and wrote the hymn *Es ist das Heil uns kommen her*. Later, he served as bishop of Pomesania.

Stuyvesant Street is one of the oldest streets in the New York City borough of Manhattan. It runs diagonally from 9th Street at Third Avenue to 10th Street near Second Avenue, all within the East Village, Manhattan, neighborhood. The majority of the street is included in the St. Mark's Historic District.

Although the street runs diagonally in relation to the Manhattan street grid, geographically it is one of the few true east–west streets in Manhattan, since most of the grid runs southeast–northwest at a 28.9 degree offset. It is a one-way street, running eastbound.
William Henry Belpitt (born May 3, 1860, date of death unknown) was a United States Navy sailor and a recipient of the United States military's highest decoration, the Medal of Honor.

The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the franchise's 106th year in Major League Baseball. Managed by Roger Craig and owned by Bob Lurie, the team played at Candlestick Park in San Francisco. The season was broadcast on KTVU and KNBR, with notable announcers including Duane Kuiper and Joe Morgan.

White Collar-2 (WC-2) is a key protein in the circadian rhythm and light-sensing mechanisms of the fungus *Neurospora crassa*. It forms the White Collar Complex (WCC) with WC-1, a photoreceptor that binds flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) to detect blue light. WC-2 contains a GATA-type zinc finger domain for DNA binding and a PAS domain for protein-protein interactions. The WCC regulates the expression of the *frequency (frq)* gene, essential for maintaining circadian rhythms, by binding to promoter elements such as the Clock Box (for circadian regulation) and the Proximal Light Regulatory Region (PLRE) (for light induction). Mutations in WC-2 disrupt rhythmicity and light responses, demonstrating its critical role in the fungal circadian clock. Research continues to explore its interactions with transcription factors and its broader role in *Neurospora*'s biological rhythms.

Teresa Ogilvy Dent CBE (born 1959) is the CEO of Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust, a British charitable organisation promoting game and wildlife management as an essential part of nature conservation, since 2001.

Publius Sulpicius Quirinius (c. 51 BC – AD 21), also translated as Cyrenius, was a Roman aristocrat. After the banishment of the ethnarch Herod Archelaus from the tetrarchy of Judea in AD 6, Quirinius was appointed legate governor of Syria, to which the province of Judaea had been added for the purpose of a census.

Bertil Stjernfelt (24 July 1917 – 21 January 2017) was a Swedish Navy lieutenant colonel and military historian. He served in coastal artillery, commanded Sweden's UN peacekeeping contingent in Cyprus (1966–1967), and later headed the Naval Staff's Intelligence Department. A prolific writer, he authored works on military history, including *Alarm i Atlantvallen*.

Ben Christo (born 22 March 1980) is an English rock musician, guitarist, songwriter, vocalist, lyricist and session musician, he is best known for his tenures with The Sisters of Mercy, Night By Night and Diamond Black.

Bellevue Plantation is a historic plantation house in Leon County, Florida, now part of the Tallahassee Museum. Built in the 19th century, it was named after the Hotel de Belle-Vue in Brussels by its original owners, Catherine and Achille Murat. The house has been restored with period furnishings and includes reconstructed outbuildings such as a plantation kitchen and slave cabin. Bellevue was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1971. The plantation is notable for its association with Catherine Murat, who contributed to the preservation of Mount Vernon.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG; Lang-sv) is a scientific learned society founded in December 1877. It was established after a rearrangement of various sections of the Anthropological Society, which was formed in 1873 by Hjalmar Stolpe, Hans Hildebrand, Oscar Montelius, and Gustaf Retzius.

The society functions as a link between science and the public, especially in the subjects of anthropology and geography. It awards research fellowships, organizes excursions and lectures, and hands out awards including the Vega Medal and Retzius Medal. In 1880, the society published the first edition of the Swedish yearbook Ymer, and it has published the international journal Geografiska Annaler since 1919, a publication that is divided between physical geography and human geography.

In 2018, it established Kritisk Etnografi, an academic journal of ethnography.

The Secret of Roan Inish is a 1994 independent fantasy-adventure film written and directed by John Sayles. It is based on the 1957 novel Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry, by Rosalie K. Fry.

It is centered on the Irish and Orcadian folklores of selkies—seals that can shed their skins to become human. The story, set on the west coast of Ulster in the north-west of Ireland, is about Fiona, a young girl who is sent to live with her grandparents and her cousin Eamon near the island of Roan Inish, where the selkies are rumored to reside. It is a family legend that her younger brother was swept away in his infancy and raised by a selkie. Part of the film takes place in Donegal Town.

Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Yugoslav Partisan recognized as a People's Hero of Yugoslavia for her part in the struggle against the Axis powers during World War II. She was the only Bosnian Muslim woman to receive the order.

Born to a Banja Luka qadi, Maglajlić was denied a higher education by her father. She took up various social and humanitarian causes, including women's rights. After Yugoslavia was invaded by the Axis powers in April 1941, Bosnia became part of the Independent State of Croatia, a fascist puppet state. Maglajlić entered the Communist Party of Yugoslavia in May and joined the growing Partisan resistance movement. Her high social status allowed her to inconspicuously shelter and equip local Partisans for several months, but she was eventually discovered by the authorities and sent to prison. After months of torture, Maglajlić escaped and joined the Partisans. She was an influential political figure in Bosanska Krajina until her death in combat with German troops in Mala Krupska Rujiška.

After the war, her remains were exhumed from Mala Krupska Rujiška and reburied at the Partisan cemetery in Banja Luka. Her image has been displayed on the cover of leaflets distributed by the Palestine Liberation Organization, which has sought to promote her as an example for Palestinian women.

David Jonathan Heyman (born 26 July 1961) is a British film producer and founder of Heyday Films. Best known for producing the *Harry Potter* film series, he has also worked on *Gravity*, *Paddington*, *Fantastic Beasts*, and *Once Upon a Time in Hollywood*, winning a Golden Globe and earning multiple Academy Award nominations. Heyman studied at Harvard University before beginning his career in film production.

Isaac Hawkins Browne (died 30 May 1818) was a British coal owner, landowner, and Tory politician who served as MP for Bridgnorth (1784–1812). He developed coal mines in Shropshire and expanded Badger Hall, his estate.

Manuel José de Jesus Silva ComM (born 9 April 1946), simply known as Manuel José (IPA-pt), is a Portuguese football manager.

Some of the teams he has coached include Vitória de Guimarães, Sporting CP, Sporting de Braga, Boavista, Benfica, Al Ahly, Belenenses, and the Angola national team. He is the living coach with most games in the Primeira Liga.

He is regarded as one of the most successful club coaches in CAF competitions, having won the main African club tournament, the CAF Champions League, a record of four times and guiding his team to four consecutive CAF Champions League finals between 2005 and 2008, winning three of those finals. He has also won the CAF Super Cup in 2002, 2006, 2007 and 2009 and was the first manager to take an African team to the medal positions in the FIFA Club World Cup in 2006.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic Gothic Revival plantation house near Uniontown, Alabama. Built between 1857 and 1861 for Joseph Selden and his wife Elizabeth, it was designed by local builder Theophilus G. Fowler. The house features intricate Gothic Revival details, including bargeboards, hood moldings, and a cast-iron veranda. Selden, a Confederate officer, owned 80 enslaved people in 1860. The plantation remained in the Selden family for over a century. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1992 and is one of few surviving Gothic Revival residences in Alabama.

The Paris Gregory (BnF Grec 510) is an illuminated manuscript of the Homilies of Gregory of Nazianzus commissioned in Constantinople by Patriarch Photios I as a commemoration to the Emperor Basil I between 879 and 883. The illustrations from the manuscript are held today in the Bibliothèque nationale de France in Paris as part of their collection of Greek manuscripts.
James Harold Bradley (Mar 4, 1906 – Nov. 10, 1984), was a pianist and the Founder and Principal of the Bradley Institute for Music Education Research.

Lucius Julius Iulus was a Roman politician and military leader who served as consular tribune in 401 BC and 397 BC. He played a key role in Rome's campaigns against Veii, Falerii, and Capena, while also dealing with internal political conflicts.

The Open Siddur Project is a free-culture, open-source initiative that provides digital tools and a collaborative platform for creating, sharing, and printing Jewish liturgical texts (*siddurim*). Founded in 2009 by Aharon Varady and developed by Efraim Feinstein, it hosts a growing repository of historical and contemporary Jewish prayers in multiple languages, licensed under Creative Commons or public domain terms. The project promotes user autonomy in spiritual practice, supports XML-based digital publishing, and fosters a global community of contributors. It has been recognized for its innovative approach to preserving and revitalizing Jewish liturgical traditions.

Vahida Maglajlić (17 April 1907 – 1 April 1943) was a Bosnian Muslim resistance fighter and communist activist during World War II. Born into a prominent family in Banja Luka, she became one of the first Bosnian Muslim women involved in the labor and women's rights movements. After the Axis invasion of Yugoslavia in 1941, she joined the Yugoslav Partisans, using her family's influence to organize support, shelter fighters, and gather supplies.  

Captured and tortured by the Ustaše, she escaped and continued her resistance work, eventually becoming a political organizer among women in northwestern Bosnia. She was killed in combat against German forces in 1943. Posthumously awarded the Order of the People's Hero in 1951, she remains the only Bosnian Muslim woman to receive this honor. Her legacy endures as a symbol of resistance, with her image later used by the Palestine Liberation Organization as an inspiration for women fighters.

Violet Dandridge (1878–1956) was an American scientific illustrator, naturalist, and suffragist. She worked at the Smithsonian Institution and was active in women's rights, later living as a farmer and artist.
Joseph McLaughlin (June 9, 1867 – November 21, 1926) was an Irish-born Republican congressman from Pennsylvania, serving in the U.S. House from 1917 to 1919 and 1921 to 1923.

Argentina was one of the seventeen nations that competed at the inaugural Summer Paralympic Games in 1960 held in Rome, Italy from September 19 to 24, 1968. Preparations for the Games began two years prior in 1958 to stage what was at the time called the 9th Annual International Stoke Mandeville Games. The team finished tenth in the medal table with a total of six medals, two gold, three silver and one bronze. The Argentinian team consisted of five athletes, one man and four women.

Error concealment refers to techniques used to minimize the impact of data loss or corruption in digital and analog transmissions. Receiver-based methods include interpolation, zero insertion, and buffering, while transmitter-based approaches involve retransmission, forward error correction, and interleaving. These techniques are applied in audio, video, and internet communications to maintain signal integrity.

The red flour beetle (Tribolium castaneum) is a species of beetle in the family Tenebrionidae, the darkling beetles. It is a worldwide pest of stored products, particularly food grains, and a model organism for ethological and food safety research.

Benedicto "Bong" Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino painter and educator known for his vibrant nature-themed works, including *The Joyful Moods of Nature*. A graduate of the University of Santo Tomas (UST), he has taught at UST's College of Fine Arts and Design and other institutions. His career spans sculpture, visual arts, and handicraft design.

Yūka Nishio (西尾 夕香, born March 31, 1994) is a Japanese musician, singer, voice actress, and DJ. After debuting as a singer in 2016 under the name Haruca, she joined Hibiki agency in 2019 and adopted her current stage name. Nishio is best known for voicing Rinku Aimoto in *D4DJ* and Nanami Hiromachi in *BanG Dream!*, where she also plays bass in the band Morfonica. A former violinist, she studied at Keio University before pursuing a career in entertainment.

Sanjog (1972) is a Hindi drama film directed by S. S. Balan and starring Mala Sinha, Amitabh Bachchan, and Aruna Irani. The story follows a man torn between his first wife, presumed dead, and his second wife, leading to tragic consequences. The film was produced by Gemini Studios and featured music by R. D. Burman.
Sergio Garavini (1926–2001) was an Italian trade unionist, politician, and co-founder of the Communist Refoundation Party.

*Cuck* is a 2019 American psychological drama film directed by Rob Lambert and co-written with Joe Varkle. Starring Zachary Ray Sherman as a mentally unstable loner radicalized by online extremism, the film explores themes of toxic masculinity and far-right indoctrination. It premiered at the Cleveland International Film Festival and received mixed reviews for its provocative subject matter.

Road Rules: Down Under was the sixth season of the MTV reality series *Road Rules*, airing from June 22 to September 7, 1998. The season featured six cast members traveling through Australia and New Zealand, completing missions. Several cast members later appeared in *The Challenge* and other MTV shows.

Robin Tyler (born April 8, 1942) is a Canadian-American comedian, producer, and LGBTQ+ rights activist. A pioneering figure in lesbian comedy, she was the first openly lesbian comic on U.S. national television. Tyler produced and emceed the first National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights (1979) and organized mass protests for marriage equality, including a symbolic same-sex wedding at the 1987 march. In 2001, she and her wife, Diane Olson, successfully sued California for marriage rights, becoming one of the first legally married lesbian couples in the state. Tyler also co-founded the comedy duo Harrison and Tyler and produced trans-inclusive women's music festivals. Her activism includes campaigns against homophobic media and nationwide protests for LGBTQ+ rights.

Mysterious Girlfriend X (Japanese: *謎の彼女X*, *Nazo no Kanojo Ekkusu*) is a Japanese romantic comedy manga series written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. Serialized in *Monthly Afternoon* from 2006 to 2014, it follows high school student Akira Tsubaki, who becomes obsessed with the drool of his mysterious classmate Mikoto Urabe, leading to an unconventional romance. A 13-episode anime adaptation by Hoods Entertainment aired in 2012, followed by an original video animation. The manga was licensed in English by Vertical.

Trichomonas gallinae is a parasitic protozoan that infects birds, causing avian trichomoniasis. It primarily affects the upper digestive and respiratory tracts, leading to lesions in the mouth, esophagus, and crop. Transmission occurs through contaminated food, water, or direct contact, particularly in pigeons feeding crop milk to their young. Virulent strains can cause severe disease, with symptoms including weight loss, difficulty swallowing, and death. The parasite is diagnosed via microscopic examination of oral excretions and treated with nitroimidazole antibiotics. *T. gallinae* has a global distribution, excluding polar and extreme northern regions. Outbreaks have been reported in finches, pigeons, and raptors, with prevention relying on hygiene and controlling wild bird contact.

AlTibbi (Arabic: الطبي) is a digital health platform in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA). Launched by both Jalil Labadi and his father, Dr. Abdel Aziz Labadi, in 2008 in Amman, Jordan. The platform aims to present reliable, up-to-date and simplified medical information to users in the region in Arabic, according to their proclaimed mission. Today, the website features thousands of medical articles, a medical glossary, a section that is dedicated to questions and answers, the latest news in medicine, telehealth services and consultations and a lot more. AlTibbi has also partnered with Mawdoo3, a leading online Arabic content provider, to provide high quality content to a growing audience in the region.

Pit-Fighter is a 1990 arcade fighting game by Atari Games which uses digitized live actors. It was Atari's first fighting game. The Japanese arcade release was published by Konami. Home versions were published by Tengen.

The graphical animations for the player character and opponents were created through a bluescreen process, where the various poses and moves were performed by real actors in front of a video camera. The game's on-screen character animation is replays of the actual footage, not a rotoscoped (redrawn) animation. Pit-Fighter is the second fighting game to use digitized sprites, after Home Data's Reikai Dōshi: Chinese Exorcist.

The red flour beetle (*Tribolium castaneum*) is a small, rust-colored beetle that infests stored grains and food products. A major pest worldwide, it is also a model organism in genetics and developmental biology due to its sequenced genome and suitability for RNAi and CRISPR studies.

The Holliday Transfer Facility (TDCJ Identification Code: NF, also referred to as the Holliday Transfer Unit), is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice transfer facility for men located in Huntsville, Texas. Holliday is along Interstate 45 and.5 miles (0.80 km) north of Texas State Highway 30. The unit, on a 1,412-acre (571 ha) plot of land, is co-located with the Wynne Unit. 

Holliday, one of the largest transfer facilities in Texas, is across the street from the Texas Prison Museum. Holliday is one of two prisons in the TDCJ that, as of 2003, is named after an African-American.
William Ford De Saussure (February 22, 1792 – March 13, 1870) was a United States senator from South Carolina. Born in Charleston, the son of Henry William de Saussure and Elizabeth Ford De Saussure.

Sir Constantijn Huygens, Lord of Zuilichem (/ˈhaɪɡənz/ HY-gənz, also US: /ˈhɔɪɡənz/ HOY-gənz, 4 September 1596 – 28 March 1687), was a Dutch Golden Age poet and composer. He was secretary to two Princes of Orange: Frederick Henry and William II, and the father of the scientist Christiaan Huygens.

Trichomonas gallinae is a cosmopolitan parasite of birds including finches, pigeons, doves, turkeys, chickens, parrots, raptors (hawks, golden eagle, etc.). The condition in birds of prey is called frounce. It is believed to be an ancient pathogen causing frounce-like symptoms in theropod dinosaurs. The same condition in pigeons is commonly called canker.

The protozoa are small (5 to 20 μm size) oval or spherical flagellates with four free flagella found on the anterior aspect of the parasite. They also have an axostyle found on the posterior end as well as an undulating membrane on one side. An important diagnostic feature is the lack of a free posterior flagellum.

The Republic of Benin was a short-lived unrecognized secessionist state in West Africa that existed for one day in 1967. It was established on 19 September 1967 during the Nigerian Civil War as a puppet state of Biafra, following its occupation of Nigeria's Mid-Western Region, and named after its capital, Benin City, with Albert Nwazu Okonkwo as its head of government.

The new state was an attempt by Biafra to prevent non-Igbo residents of the neighbouring Mid-Western Region from siding with Nigeria following regional ethnic tensions early in the war. The Republic of Benin was officially declared even as the Nigerian federal forces were reconquering the region and ended the following day as they entered Benin City.: 369 The occupation of the Mid-Western Region turned residents against the secessionist cause, and was used by the Nigerian government as justification to escalate the war against Biafra.

Brontotheriidae is an extinct family of large, herbivorous mammals that lived during the Eocene (56–34 million years ago). Known as brontotheres, they evolved from small, hornless ancestors into massive animals, some reaching 2.5 meters (8.2 ft) tall with bony nasal horns. Their teeth were adapted for shearing soft vegetation, and they likely went extinct due to environmental changes. They are closely related to modern horses, rhinos, and tapirs.

Paul Pressler (born [year]) is an American business executive who served as CEO of Gap Inc. (2002–2007) and chairman of Walt Disney Parks and Resorts (2000–2002). He later held leadership roles at Avon, David’s Bridal, and eBay, where he became board chairman in 2020.
Isaac Hawkins Browne, FRS (7 December 1745 – 30 May 1818) was a British Tory politician, industrialist, essayist, and a lord of the manor of Badger, Shropshire.

Ruslan Tagirovich Khuchbarov (Lang-ru; 12 November 1972 – 3 September 2004), was an Ingush terrorist presumed to be a militant nicknamed "Polkovnik" (the Russian for Colonel) notorious for his leading role in the 2004 Beslan school hostage crisis.

George Richmond (28 March 1809 – 19 March 1896) was a British portrait painter and a key figure in the 19th-century art world. A student at the Royal Academy, he was deeply influenced by William Blake and became part of the artistic group known as "The Ancients." Initially known for imaginative works, he later gained fame as a portraitist, producing crayon, watercolor, and oil paintings of prominent Victorian figures, including politicians, scientists, and writers. His subjects included William Wilberforce, Charles Darwin, and Charlotte Brontë. Richmond was elected a Royal Academician in 1866 and contributed to major exhibitions. He also worked in sculpture, creating memorials for St. Paul’s Cathedral. He died in London, leaving a lasting legacy in British portraiture.

The 1999 Brickyard 400, the 6th running of the event, was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series race held on August 7, 1999 at Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana. Contested at 160 laps on the 2.5 mile (4.023 km) speedway, it was the 20th race of the 1999 NASCAR Winston Cup Series season. Dale Jarrett of Robert Yates Racing won the race.
Shonan Maru No. 7 (Japanese: 第七昭南丸) was a Japanese whaling ship that was requisitioned by the Imperial Japanese Navy during World War II and converted into an auxiliary subchaser.

Peaked Mountain is a 1,227-foot (374 m) mountain in Massachusetts, known for its diverse ecosystems, including forests, wetlands, and rocky outcrops. Its summit offers panoramic views of Connecticut’s Shenipsit State Forest, Mount Monadnock, and Mount Wachusett. Historically used for charcoal production and agriculture, the area has since reforested and is now managed by The Trustees of Reservations. The mountain features hiking trails, a fire pond, and remnants of past land use, such as logging and farming. It is a popular destination for hiking, birdwatching, and winter sports.

April Carrión (born April 23, 1989) is a Puerto Rican drag performer and artist, best known as a contestant on *RuPaul's Drag Race* (season 6). They have appeared in music videos, documentaries, and television shows, including *Mala Mala* and *Versus Drag Queens*.

David Lodge (author)  

David John Lodge (born 28 January 1935) is a British novelist, critic, and academic, best known for his satirical campus novels, including *Changing Places* (1975), *Small World* (1984), and *Nice Work* (1988). Educated at University College London and the University of Birmingham, he became a professor of English literature before retiring to write full-time in 1987. His works often explore themes of Catholicism, academia, and post-war Britain, blending humor with literary experimentation. Lodge has won the Hawthornden Prize and the Whitbread Book of the Year, and two of his novels were shortlisted for the Booker Prize. Several of his books have been adapted for television, and he has also written plays and literary criticism.

The Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963 (c. 41) was a UK law that extended workplace health and safety protections to employees in offices, shops, and certain railway premises. Introduced by John Hare, Minister of Labour, it came into force on 18 February 1964, covering an additional 8 million workers. The Act established general requirements for safe and healthy working conditions, including safeguards for machinery, restrictions on hazardous tasks for young workers, and limits on manual lifting.  

Many of its provisions were later repealed and replaced by subsequent regulations, including the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992 and the Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations 1992. The Act was influenced by the 1949 Gowers Report, which had previously led to reforms in mining, agriculture, and factory safety laws. While largely superseded, some provisions may still apply to premises not covered by later legislation.
Mount Gould is a thirteener on the Sierra crest, just north of the Kearsarge Pass.

According to Theravada Buddhism's Pali canon's Buddhavamsa and its commentary, Phussa is the twenty-first of twenty-seven Buddhas who preceded the historical Gotama Buddha. He was also the second Buddha of the Maṇḍa kalpa.

In the Buddhavamsa, he is described as:

Phussa Buddha removed the darkness, calmed the world and gave the Dhamma rain.

Phussa was 58 cubits, or 87 feet tall and was frequently compared with the sun and the moon.

Arkady Adamovich Brish (May 14, 1917 – March 19, 2016) was a Soviet and Russian physicist and partisan during World War II. A recipient of numerous state honors, including Hero of Socialist Labour, he later worked in scientific research and was commemorated with a memorial plaque in Moscow.

Sendle is an Australian-based company which provides courier services within the country, focusing primarily on small to medium-sized businesses and online retailers. It claims to be the first courier company in Australia to offer fully carbon neutral services. While its delivery services are worldwide - with the exception of Russia and Belarus - its operational staff are based in Australia, New Zealand, the Philippines and the United States.
Dolní Bečva is a municipality in the Vsetín District of the Czech Republic, known for its location in the Moravian-Silesian Beskids and the Radhošť mountain.
Deerfoot (1828 – 18 January 1896, Cattaraugus Reservation) was an American Seneca runner. His most noted achievements took place in England.

Kamaal the Abstract is the third studio album by American hip hop artist Q-Tip, released September 15, 2009, on Battery Records. Recorded in 2001, the album is a departure from his solo debut album Amplified (1999). Kamaal the Abstract is an eclectic album that features Q-Tip rapping, singing, and exploring his jazz influences. The album contains introspective and life-related lyrical themes.

The album was originally planned for release in 2002 as the follow-up to Amplified. However, it was shelved by Q-Tip's record label at the time, Arista, who doubted its commercial potential. It eventually leaked onto the internet, while the distribution of promotional copies led several publications to run reviews of the album.

After its official release in 2009, the album debuted at number 77 on the U.S. Billboard 200 chart, selling 6,000 copies in its first week. Upon its release, Kamaal the Abstract received positive reviews from most music critics.

Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness (died 28 November 1746) was a Scottish landowner and Jacobite supporter. The son of Alexander Wedderburn, 4th Baronet, he lived in poverty before joining the 1745 Jacobite rising as a colonel. Captured after the Battle of Culloden, he was convicted of treason and executed at Kennington Common. He married Jean Fullerton in 1724 and had several children, including sons John and James. His descendants included the portrait painter Catherine Read, his niece.

Howard M. Radzely (born 1970) is an American attorney who served as Acting U.S. Secretary of Labor (2009) and Deputy Secretary of Labor (2007–2009). He previously held roles as Solicitor of Labor and worked in private practice.
Marie Fillunger (27 January 1850 – 23 December 1930) was an Austrian singer, and the longtime partner of Eugenie Schumann, who was a daughter of Robert and Clara Schumann.

Bernard Vince (born 2 October 1985) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Adelaide and Melbourne Football Club in the Australian Football League (AFL). He has since become involved in the football media, working for Triple M and Fox Footy.

At the time of his retirement, Vince was only one of seven players in VFL/AFL history to have played 100 or more games at two clubs and win the best and fairest award at both clubs.

Helene Hewitt (née Banks) is a British oceanographer and climate scientist. She developed the HadGEM3 climate model and led the Met Office's Ocean Modelling group. Hewitt coordinated the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report on oceans and was appointed OBE in 2022. She studied at Cambridge and Southampton.

Vishwa Thulasi is a 2004 Tamil-language drama film directed by Sumathy Ram, starring Mammooty and Nandita Das. This film's score and soundtrack were composed by Ilayaraaja and M. S. Viswanathan. The film was notable for having a female director.

The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament held in Bratislava, Slovakia, from 25 June to 1 July 2017. Eight teams competed, with Slovenia winning its second Division I title and earning promotion to the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship. Latvia finished as runner-up, while Australia took bronze. The tournament featured 23 games with 227 goals scored. Teams qualified through regional tournaments, including New Zealand (Asia/Oceania) and Latvia (Europe). Brazil and Hungary were relegated to future qualification rounds. The event was held alongside the top-tier World Championship in the same city.
Rechtboomssloot is a historic canal in Amsterdam, running from Geldersekade to Oudeschans, with bridges and notable landmarks along its route.

KeeWeb is a free, open-source password manager for Linux, macOS, Windows, Android, and iOS. It supports KeePass files, encrypts data locally, and syncs with cloud storage. Written in JavaScript, it runs in browsers or as a desktop app.

China's Special Envoy on the Middle East Issue is a diplomatic position established in 2002 to promote peace in the Israeli–Palestinian conflict. The envoy advocates for China's Five Principles in Support of the Middle East Peace Process, emphasizing negotiations, Palestinian statehood, and regional stability. As of 2022, five envoys have held the role, engaging with regional stakeholders and addressing broader Middle Eastern conflicts.
Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959 in South Tyrol) is an Italian luger who competed between the late 1970s and the late 1980s.

Leslie Quirk RBV (30 September 1914 – 20 October 2004), also known as Y Kione Jiarg (IPA-gv, "the red head") was a Manx language activist and teacher who was involved with the language's revival on the Isle of Man in the 20th century. His work recording the last native speakers of the language with the Irish Folklore Commission and the Manx Museum helped to ensure that a spoken record of the Manx language survived.

Mysterious Girlfriend X (Japanese: 謎の彼女X, Hepburn: Nazo no Kanojo Ekkusu) is a Japanese manga written and illustrated by Riichi Ueshiba. It was originally published as a one-shot story in 2004 before becoming a serialized comic in Kodansha's Monthly Afternoon magazine in 2006. The manga is licensed in North America by Vertical. An anime adaptation by Hoods Entertainment aired in Japan from April to June 2012, with an original video animation episode released in August 2012.

An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that aims itself by listening for characteristic sounds of its target or by searching for it using sonar (acoustic homing). Acoustic torpedoes are usually designed for medium-range use, and often fired from a submarine.

The first passive acoustic torpedoes were developed nearly simultaneously by the United States Navy and the Germans during World War II. The Germans developed the G7e/T4 Falke, which was first deployed by the submarines U-603, U-758 and U-221 in March 1943. Few of these torpedoes were actually used and quickly phased out of service in favor of the T4's successor, the G7es T5 Zaunkönig torpedo in August 1943. The T5 first saw widespread use in September 1943 against North Atlantic escort vessels and merchant ships in convoys.

On the Allied side, the US Navy developed the Mark 24 mine, which was actually an aircraft launched, anti-submarine passive acoustic homing torpedo. The first production Mk. 24s were delivered to the U.S. Navy in March 1943, and it scored its first verified combat kills in May 1943. About 204 torpedoes were launched against submarine targets, with 37 Axis submarines being sunk and a further 18 damaged.

Since its introduction, the acoustic torpedo has proven to be an effective weapon against surface ships as well as serving as an anti-submarine weapon. Today, acoustic torpedoes are mostly used against submarines.

The 2014 Harris County shooting occurred on July 9, 2014, near Spring, Texas, when Ronald Lee Haskell killed six members of his ex-wife's family in an execution-style attack. Disguised as a FedEx employee, Haskell forced his way into their home, tied up the victims, and shot them. A 15-year-old girl survived by playing dead and alerted police, leading to Haskell's arrest after a standoff. He was later convicted of capital murder and sentenced to death.

Cumberlandia monodonta (common name spectaclecase) is a freshwater mussel endemic to the United States. Currently, C. monodonta is listed as an endangered species by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the International Union for Conservation of Nature.

Cumberlandia monodonta was first reported in a newspaper titled The Disseminator (New Harmony, Ind.) by Thomas Say in 1829 under the name Unio monodonta. The spectaclecase was then reclassified into the genus Cumberlandia by A. E. Ortmann in 1912.

Cumberlandia monodonta has experienced a drastic decline in population due to human activity, resulting in its listing as "Endangered" by the Endangered Species Act and the IUCN. In particular, the construction of dams has caused significant problems that disrupt C. monodonta's habitat and food supply. The Endangered Species Act classifies C. monodonta as a highly threatened species with a low potential for recovery.

"Ambush" is the premiere episode of the fourth season of the American medical drama ER. Written by executive producer Carol Flint and director Thomas Schlamme, it was first broadcast on NBC on September 25, 1997, as a live episode, filmed twice for the East and West Coast.

Shown through the perspective of a PBS documentary film crew, the emergency room is filmed as a day in the life of the doctors. The episode introduced British actress Alex Kingston as Elizabeth Corday to the cast. Inspiration for the episode came from production staffers having worked in documentaries, with Anthony Edwards and George Clooney suggesting to do it live.

In its initial airing, "Ambush" received the highest rating of the week. Seen by a total of 42.71 million viewers, it became the most watched season premiere episode for a drama series. Reception to the episode was mixed from critics. Praise was given to the live performances but the story and script writing was criticized being lackluster. "Ambush" received four nominations at the 50th Primetime Emmy Awards, winning one for Outstanding Technical Direction, Camerawork, Video Control for a Series.

Bunyan and Babe is a 2017 American animated adventure film loosely based on the folklore of Paul Bunyan. Directed by Louis Ross, the film follows two children who discover Bunyan (voiced by John Goodman) and his blue ox Babe (Jeff Foxworthy) in a hidden forest. Together, they must stop a greedy land developer from destroying a small town. Produced by Exodus Film Group and Toonz Entertainment, the film was distributed by Cinedigm and received mixed reviews.
Neopleurotomoides rufoapicata is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Raphitomidae.
Tun Tun (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003) was the screen name of Indian playback singer and actress-comedienne, Uma Devi Khatri, who was called "Hindi cinema's first-ever comedienne".

The National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) is a U.S. federal agency under the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that provides vital and health statistics. Established in 1960, it collects data through surveys, vital records, and health examinations, covering topics such as births, deaths, diseases, nutrition, and healthcare access. Major programs include the National Vital Statistics System, National Health Interview Survey, and National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey.
When World War II was declared in 1939, it had a negative effect on association football; competitions were suspended and players signed up to fight, resulting in the deaths of many players.

"Adrift" is the second episode of the second season of Lost and the 27th episode overall. The episode was directed by Stephen Williams and written by Steven Maeda and Leonard Dick. It first aired on September 28, 2005, on the American Broadcasting Company. In the episode, flashbacks depict Michael Dawson's struggles for the custody of his son Walt Lloyd. The realtime events show Michael and Sawyer, who have just had their raft destroyed, becoming hostile towards each other as they drift back to shore, while Kate Austen and John Locke enter the mysterious hatch and encounter Desmond Hume.

The storyline picks up the raft storyline which was left hanging after season one's finale, "Exodus", and also revisits the events seen in the previous episode, "Man of Science, Man of Faith". Originally envisioned as a Sawyer episode, the episode was rewritten to focus on Michael. The sea scenes were shot on the Hawaiian shoreline. "Adrift" had 23.17 million American viewers and stands as the second largest audience on the series' run, but received negative reviews, which focused criticism on the flashbacks, the raft scenes, and the lack of plot advancement.

The Big Red Machine was the dominant Cincinnati Reds baseball team of the 1970s, known for its powerful lineup and back-to-back World Series championships in 1975 and 1976. Managed by Sparky Anderson, the team featured Hall of Famers Johnny Bench, Joe Morgan, Tony Pérez, and Pete Rose, along with key players like Dave Concepción, George Foster, César Gerónimo, and Ken Griffey Sr. The "Great Eight" starting lineup led the Reds to four National League pennants and two World Series titles. The team's success was marked by strong rivalries, particularly with the Los Angeles Dodgers. Though the core disbanded by the late 1970s, the Big Red Machine remains one of baseball's most celebrated dynasties.

Backes & Strauss is a British-Swiss luxury watch and jewelry company, founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany. Originally a diamond and jewelry firm, it became the world's oldest diamond company. In 2006, it partnered with Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller to produce high-end diamond-encrusted timepieces. Headquartered in London and Geneva, the company is known for its flawless diamonds and signature "Jewel in the Crown" design. All watches are crafted in Franck Muller's Geneva workshops.
John Doyley (1602–1660) was an English politician who sat in the House of Commons from 1646 to 1648.

Zeitenspiegel is a German journalism and publishing company founded in 1976 by Uli Reinhardt and based in Weinstadt. It operates the Zeitenspiegel-Reportageschule Günter Dahl, a journalism academy ranked among the best in Germany, offering one-year courses in feature writing and photojournalism. Since 1998, Zeitenspiegel has awarded the Hansel Mieth Prize, honoring outstanding reporting on social issues, along with the Gabriel Grüner Grant, which supports emerging journalists. The organization is named after its honorary member, photojournalist Johanna "Hansel" Mieth (1909–1998). Zeitenspiegel focuses on in-depth storytelling and training the next generation of journalists.

Sir William Pole (1561–1635) of Colcombe House in the parish of Colyton, and formerly of Shute House in the parish of Shute (adjoining Colcombe), both in Devon, was an English country gentleman and landowner, a colonial investor, Member of Parliament and, most notably, a historian and antiquarian of the County of Devon.

Brooke High School is a public high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, United States. It is the only high school in the Brooke County School District. Athletic teams compete as the Brooke Bruins in the WVSSAC Class AAA as well as the Ohio Valley Athletic Conference.

Ma Zhou (601–648), courtesy name Binwang, was a Tang dynasty politician who rose from humble origins to become a trusted advisor to Emperor Taizong. Known for his insightful petitions on governance, frugality, and imperial succession, he served as head of the legislative bureau and minister of civil service. Despite chronic illness, he remained influential until his death, after which he was posthumously honored as Duke of Gaotang and enshrined in Emperor Gaozong's temple.

A customer data platform (CDP) is a collection of software which creates a persistent, unified customer database that is accessible to other systems. Data is pulled from multiple sources, cleaned and combined to create a single customer profile. This structured data is then made available to other marketing systems. According to Gartner, customer data platforms have evolved from a variety of mature markets, "including multichannel campaign management, tag management and data integration."

Colt Keliikoa Lyerla (born November 13, 1992) is a former American arena football wide receiver and convicted felon. He played tight end at the University of Oregon for the Ducks but left in October 2013 due to personal reasons. He signed with the Green Bay Packers after going undrafted during the 2014 NFL Draft. He was a member of the Arizona Rattlers and Portland Steel of the Arena Football League. He also played for AS Carcassonne in the Elite One Championship rugby league.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) is a generic term that covers a suite of anti-terrorist protective measures that are often employed around buildings or publicly accessible spaces/venues of particular significance. The design of these various vehicle security barriers and landscape treatments came about as security authorities across the globe sought to mitigate the effects of vehicle borne improvised explosive devices (VBIED). The sorts of places that warrant consideration as potential terrorist targets in need of HVM include: government buildings, airports, large railway stations, sports venues, concentrations of entertainment and crowded night time economy, etc.

Jawadia Arabic College is a Shia Muslim seminary in Banaras, India, founded in 1928 by Maulana Syed Mohammad Sajjad Al-Husaini. It focuses on Islamic jurisprudence, theology, and literature, producing scholars and clerics.
Fitchville Township is a township in Huron County, Ohio, United States. Established in 1828, it has a population of 1,056 (2010) and is governed by a three-member board of trustees.

Daniel Isn't Real is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer, based on Brian DeLeeuw's novel *In This Way I Was Saved*. The film stars Miles Robbins as Luke, a troubled college student haunted by his childhood imaginary friend Daniel (Patrick Schwarzenegger), who re-emerges with sinister intentions. The story explores themes of mental illness, toxic masculinity, and supernatural possession. Premiering at SXSW, it received positive reviews for its stylish direction and performances. The film is the first installment in Mortimer's planned "Vortex Trilogy."

General Motors (Thailand) Limited (GMT) was a holding company of sales and manufacturing subsidiaries of General Motors (GM) in Thailand. The company was registered in 1993 as a sales company and opened its manufacturing plant in 2000. At its height, GM Thailand exported vehicles to most regions in the world, including South America, Central America, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, Australia and Japan. In February 2020, GM announced that it would withdraw from the Thai market and the Rayong plant would be acquired by Great Wall Motors by the end of 2020. The company continued to support existing Chevrolet owners for ongoing aftersales, warranty and service.

"Imagine" is a 2012 pop duet by Norwegian singer Tone Damli and Swedish singer Eric Saade, released as a single from Damli's album *Looking Back*. The song's music video, filmed in Ibiza, depicts a love story between the two artists. They performed it live on Norwegian TV shows and during the VG Lista 2012 tour.

Sammy Petrillo (October 24, 1934 – August 15, 2009) was an American comedian best known for his uncanny impersonation of Jerry Lewis and his role in the cult film *Bela Lugosi Meets a Brooklyn Gorilla* (1952). He later worked in television, stand-up comedy, and low-budget films.

Raymond Cannon (born Ulises Tildmann Cannon;: 270 September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, film director, screenwriter, journalist, and author known for his work with D. W. Griffith and Buster Keaton.

Leslie Harrison Lambert (11 November 1883 – 13 December 1941), known in public as A. J. Alan, was an English magician, intelligence officer, short story writer and radio broadcaster. At the beginning of World War II he worked in naval intelligence at Bletchley Park.
Wigstan (died c.840 AD), also known as Saint Wystan, was the son of Wigmund of Mercia and Ælfflæd, daughter of King Ceolwulf I of Mercia.

The Sugar Loaf Islands (often Sugarloaf; Lang-mi, lit. 'the islands') are a collection of five small uninhabited islands and several sea stacks near Port Taranaki, New Zealand.

The largest, Moturoa Island, covers approximately 1.4 hectares (3.5 acres). Motumahanga is the island furthest from shore, at approximately 1.5 kilometres (0.93 miles).

Ngā Motu was one of the first areas inhabited by descendants of Te Whiti o Rongomai, and the islands and reefs were all named by Ngāti Te Whiti. The island group was given its English name in 1770 by James Cook because the reminded him of the way sugar was stored in heaps in Europe.

The Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Protected Area (SLIMPA) was established in 1991 to protect the area from oil exploration. This strengthened the protection that had been in place since the formation of a marine park in 1986.

In 2013 New Plymouth district councillors unanimously agreed to gift the protected area back to the government for treaty settlement negotiations with Taranaki and Te Āti Awa iwi.

The Diptych by Giovanni da Rimini is a 14th-century Italian Gothic painting, likely part of a larger work. The left wing, now in the National Gallery, London, depicts scenes from the lives of saints, while the right wing, in Rome's Palazzo Barberini, shows episodes from Christ's life. Both panels reflect Byzantine and Giotto influences.

Gurie Georgiu (31 December 1968 – 21 October 2021) was a Romanian Orthodox bishop. He served as the first bishop of the Diocese of Deva and Hunedoara from 2009 until his death. Educated at the University of Craiova, he previously worked as vicar of the Archdiocese of Craiova. He died from COVID-19 during the pandemic in Romania.
Ari is a given name in many languages and cultures, for both men and women. It also may be a nickname for a wide variety of unrelated names.

Polytrauma and multiple trauma are medical terms describing the condition of a person who has been subjected to multiple traumatic injuries, such as a serious head injury in addition to a serious burn. The term is defined via an Injury Severity Score (ISS) equal to or greater than 16. It has become a commonly applied term by US military physicians in describing the seriously injured soldiers returning from Operation Iraqi Freedom in Iraq and Operation Enduring Freedom in Afghanistan. The term is generic, however, and has been in use for a long time for any case involving multiple trauma.

William Forsythe (born June 7, 1955) is an American actor. He is best known for his portrayal of tough-guy, criminal characters, and has starred in films such as American Me (1992), Raising Arizona (1987), Dick Tracy (1990), Gotti (1996), The Rock (1996), and The Devil's Rejects (2005). He has also played recurring characters in television series such as Boardwalk Empire (2010) and Justified (2010).

Forsythe was nominated for the Independent Spirit Award for Best Supporting Male for his performance in the indie film The Waterdance.

The COVID-19 pandemic in Tunisia was part of the worldwide pandemic caused by SARS-CoV-2. The first case was confirmed on 2 March 2020 in Gafsa. By early 2021, Tunisia had recorded over 1.1 million cases and 29,000 deaths, with significant economic impacts.

The Katkari people are an Adivasi community primarily found in Maharashtra, India, with smaller populations in Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Karnataka. Traditionally forest-dwellers, they were known for producing catechu from khair trees and practicing shifting cultivation. However, forest laws and land restrictions forced many into landlessness and bonded labor, particularly in brick kilns near Mumbai.  

Today, the Katkari face displacement due to rising land values and social exclusion, reinforced by caste-based discrimination. Their unique cultural practices include hunting rodents, celebrated in the festival *Undir Navmi*, and deep knowledge of forest resources. Despite their historical ties to the land, they remain marginalized, struggling for secure livelihoods and housing.

San José Castle (Spanish: *Castillo de San José*), also known as Santa Bárbara Fort, is a 19th-century military fortress in Guatemala City, Guatemala. Built between 1843 and 1846 under President Rafael Carrera, it served as a strategic defense post and later as an ammunition depot. The fort played a key role in the 1920 revolution that ousted President Manuel Estrada Cabrera, when it was a major stronghold before falling to rebel forces. Today, it houses the Museo del Ejército (Army Museum).

Eskişehirspor is a Turkish professional football club based in Eskişehir, competing in the TFF Third League. Founded in 1965 through the merger of three local teams, the club quickly rose to prominence, finishing as Süper Lig runners-up three times (1969, 1970, 1972) and winning the Turkish Cup in 1970. Known for their passionate fanbase, including the BandoESES supporters' band, Eskişehirspor has played in multiple divisions, experiencing promotions and relegations. The club previously played at Eskişehir Atatürk Stadium but now uses the New Eskişehir Stadium, a modern 32,500-capacity venue. Despite financial and competitive struggles in recent years, the club remains a historic symbol of Anatolian football.

William Le Baron Jenney (September 25, 1832 – June 14, 1907) was an American architect and engineer who is known for building the first skyscraper in 1884.

In 1998, Jenney was ranked number 89 in the book 1,000 Years, 1,000 People: Ranking the Men and Women Who Shaped the Millennium.
Casey's theorem is a generalization of Ptolemy's theorem in Euclidean geometry, relating the lengths of common tangents of four circles tangent to a fifth circle. The converse is also true.

A temporary crown is a short-term dental restoration used to protect a prepared tooth while a permanent crown is being fabricated. It can be made from preformed plastic, composite, or metal shells, or custom-formed from acrylic or bis-acrylic resin. Temporary crowns maintain function, aesthetics, and gum health, and are cemented with provisional cement for easy removal. Proper oral hygiene is essential to prevent complications before the final crown is placed.

The Lady in the Lake is a 1943 detective novel by Raymond Chandler featuring the Los Angeles private investigator Philip Marlowe. Notable for its removal of Marlowe from his usual Los Angeles environs for much of the book, the novel's complicated plot initially deals with the case of a missing woman in a small mountain town some 80 miles (130 km) from the city. The book was written shortly after the attack on Pearl Harbor and makes several references to America's recent involvement in World War II.
Babócsa is a village in Somogy County, Hungary, near the Croatian border.

Hilbert College is a private Franciscan Catholic college in Hamburg, New York. Founded in 1957, it offers bachelor's and graduate programs. The college competes in NCAA Division III athletics as the Hawks. Its 60-acre campus includes residence halls, academic buildings, and athletic facilities. Enrollment consists of about 800 undergraduates.
John's River or St. John's River is a small river that snakes its way through Waterford city before joining the River Suir at Adelphi Quay, Ireland.

Web Therapy is an American comedy series created by and starring Lisa Kudrow as Fiona Wallice, a self-serving therapist who conducts short, unorthodox sessions via webcam. Originally an online show (2008–2014), it was adapted into a TV series on Showtime (2011–2015), featuring guest stars like Meryl Streep and Julia Louis-Dreyfus.

"Lottery" (Hindi: लॉटरी) is a 1933 Hindi short story by Premchand. It follows a schoolteacher and his friend Vikram, who dream of winning a large lottery prize but face greed and family conflict when they pool money for a ticket. The story ends with an ironic twist and a moral lesson. It has been included in textbooks, anthologies, and adapted into plays.
*Cinéaste* is a quarterly American film magazine founded in 1967 and based in New York City.

Bristol, Tennessee, is a city in Sullivan County, Tennessee, United States, located along the state's border with Virginia. Incorporated in 1856 and named after Bristol, England, it is part of the Tri-Cities region of Northeast Tennessee. Known as the "Birthplace of Country Music," Bristol gained national recognition for its pivotal role in early country music recordings, including the historic 1927 Bristol Sessions featuring the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers. The city celebrates this heritage with the annual Bristol Rhythm & Roots Reunion festival and the Birthplace of Country Music Museum.  

Bristol is home to Bristol Motor Speedway, a major NASCAR venue, and has a population of 27,147 (2020 census). The city features a humid subtropical climate with cooler temperatures due to its elevation. Governed by a mayor and city manager, Bristol operates its own school system, including Tennessee High School, and maintains a police department with 69 sworn officers. The downtown area, divided by the Tennessee-Virginia state line, is a cultural and economic hub for the region.

DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States. With a population of 10,811 (2020), it is named after Isaac De Forest, who purchased the land in 1856. The village is located near Interstate 39/90/94.

Norma Aleandro (born 2 May 1936) is an Argentine actress. She is considered one of the most celebrated and prolific Argentine actresses of all time and is recognized as a cultural icon in her home country.

Aleandro starred in the 1985 film The Official Story, a role that earned her the Cannes Award for best actress. She has performed in other successful films like The Truce (1974), Cousins (1989), Autumn Sun (1996), The Lighthouse (1998), Son of the Bride (2001) and Cama Adentro (2005). For her performance as Florencia Sánchez Morales in the 1987 film Gaby: A True Story, she received a Golden Globe nomination and an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress.

Aleandro wrote the 1970 film The Inheritors and performed in various plays such as August: Osage County. Aleandro also appeared in the Argentine adaptation of BeTipul, the critically successful En terapia.
Wilkinson Heights is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, United States. The population was 3,068 at the 2000 census.

The Anatolian diagonal is a biogeographical boundary in Turkey, first identified by botanist Peter Hadland Davis in 1971. It divides plant species distribution, with distinct flora on either side, and hosts numerous endemic species. Ecological and climatic differences, rather than geological factors, are believed to drive this separation.

Joanne M. Garvey (1935–2014) was an attorney in San Francisco, California, and was a nationally recognized expert in field of taxation. She was the first woman to serve on the California State Bar Board of Governors from 1971–1974, and was one of the founders of the California Women Lawyers organization in 1974. Garvey also was the first female president of the San Francisco Barristers, and in 1981 became the first female president of the Bar Association of San Francisco since the Association’s founding in 1872. In addition, she served in a number of leadership positions with different sections and committees of the American Bar Association, and as a member of its Board of Governors and House of Delegates. Moreover, Garvey helped form the State Bar of California's Taxation Section, from which she also received the Section’s lifetime achievement award in 1994—the Joanne M. Garvey Award—which was named for her. In 2003, Garvey received the American Bar Association Commission on Women's 2003 Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award. After Garvey’s death in 2014, Charles Rettig—prior to becoming Commissioner of the Internal Revenue Service—stated that: "From every perspective, Joanne Garvey was a legend. She was among the most highly respected lawyers in the United States (tax or otherwise)".

Divya Dhayal (Hindi: दिव्या धायल) is an Indian compound archer. Born in Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh, she began competing in 2014 and won gold at the 2017 Senior National Archery Championship. She has represented India internationally since 2016, reaching a career-high world ranking of 21. Her father, Colonel Vikram S. Dhayal, is an army officer.

"Lottery" (Lang-hi, Lang-ur) is a Hindustani short story. It was written by Indian author Premchand. The story is told in narrative form from the perspective of an unnamed school teacher. The story has been adapted into play format, with theatrical performances presented by Lalit Parimoo's Natsamaj Theatre Group, as well as Mujeeb Khan's Ideal Drama and Entertainment Academy.

Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist who lived in Hamburg. Very little is known about this naturalist.

Many of Röding's descriptions (often simply a German rendition of the Latin binomial name) are of species which were first named by earlier authors such as Johann Hieronymus Chemnitz, Friedrich Wilhelm Martini and Martin Lister. Röding's references to pre-existing descriptions and figures make these names also valid, since they are unequivocally recognizable, and were (after Röding) subsequently adopted by many later authors.

VMM-363 (Marine Medium Tiltrotor Squadron 363), nicknamed the "Lucky Red Lions," is a United States Marine Corps squadron operating the MV-22B Osprey. Activated in 1952, it has participated in major conflicts, including the Vietnam War, Operation Iraqi Freedom, and Operation Enduring Freedom. Based at MCB Hawaii, it provides assault support under Marine Aircraft Group 24.
Matheus Antunes Ribeiro (born 23 October 1993) is a Brazilian footballer who plays as a right back for Vasco da Gama.
Masʿūd II of Ṭur ʿAbdin (fl. 15th–16th century) was a Syriac Orthodox patriarch and bishop, known for his monastic reforms and controversial leadership.

O RLY? is an Internet phenomenon, typically presented as an image macro featuring a snowy owl. The phrase "O RLY?", an abbreviated form of "Oh, really?", is popularly used in Internet forums in a sarcastic manner, often in response to an obvious, predictable, or blatantly false statement. Similar owl image macros followed the original to present different views, including images with the phrases "YA RLY" (Yeah, really.), "NO WAI!!" (No way!), and NO RLY. (Not really.)

Cecil L. Alexander (born August 2, 1935) is an American businessman and Democratic politician from Arkansas. He served in the Arkansas House of Representatives from 1963 to 1979, including as Speaker from 1975 to 1977. After retiring, he became a prominent lobbyist and was appointed to the Arkansas Racing Commission. He resides in Heber Springs, Arkansas.

StarUML is a proprietary UML modeling tool developed by MKLabs, supporting UML 2.0, SysML, and legacy notations. Initially released in 2006 as open-source, it transitioned to a commercial model in 2014. It runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

Joseph Tate is an American attorney who represented government official Lewis Libby in the CIA leak grand jury investigation. Tate is a partner with the law firm Dechert LLP in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. He specializes in defending clients against charges of complex white collar crime, in particular, antitrust and other regulatory issues. Previously, Tate worked in the Antitrust Division of the United States Department of Justice.

Hunsrückisch is a German dialect spoken in the Hunsrück region of Germany, with regional variations. It is also spoken in southern Brazil, where it is known as Hunsrik, influenced by Portuguese and other immigrant languages. The dialect has distinct phonetic and grammatical features, differing from Standard German in pronunciation, gender, and case usage.
1. FC Kaan-Marienborn is a German association football club based in Siegen, that competes in Regionalliga West.

Rupa Goswami (1489–1564) was a key figure in the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition and one of the Six Goswamis of Vrindavana. A disciple of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, he authored foundational theological texts and helped restore sacred sites in Vrindavana. His works, including *Bhakti-rasamrita-sindhu*, systematized devotional practices. He is revered as an incarnation of Rupa Manjari, an eternal associate of Radha-Krishna. His *samadhi* (tomb) is in Vrindavana.

TransPeshawar or Peshawar BRT is a bus rapid transit system in Peshawar, capital of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province. TransPeshawar BRT system consists of two parts: the first encompasses an east–west corridor served by 32 stations on a dedicated lane for exclusive use by buses, while the second part consists of a network of feeder routes in which buses can enter and exit the system to travel on city streets. The system was inaugurated on August 13, 2020, and is the fourth BRT system in Pakistan.

The medieval University of Dublin (Lang-la) was an early but generally unsuccessful attempt to establish a university in Dublin, the capital city of the Lordship of Ireland. Founded in 1320, it maintained an intermittent existence for the next two centuries, but it never flourished, and disappeared for good at the Reformation in Ireland (1534–41). It was located in Saint Patrick's Cathedral, Dublin. It had no connection with the present University of Dublin, better known as Trinity College Dublin, which was founded in 1592.

Peter Hollins (born in Hendon, Middlesex) is a British businessman. He held executive roles at ICI and British Energy, later serving as director general of the British Heart Foundation and chairman of University Hospital Southampton NHS Foundation Trust.

Dragan Bender (born November 17, 1997) is a Croatian professional basketball player for Monbus Obradoiro of the Spanish Liga ACB. Standing at 7 ft 0 in (2.13 m), he plays as a power forward and center. Bender was selected fourth overall by the Phoenix Suns in the 2016 NBA draft, becoming the highest-drafted Croatian player at the time. He also played for the Milwaukee Bucks and Golden State Warriors before returning to Europe, where he won championships with Maccabi Tel Aviv in Israel. Internationally, he has represented Croatia at youth and senior levels, earning a bronze medal at the 2014 FIBA Europe Under-18 Championship.

Hristo Chernopeev (c. 1868 – 6 November 1915) was a Bulgarian revolutionary and military officer. A key figure in the Macedonian liberation movement, he fought in the Balkan Wars and World War I, where he was killed in battle. Chernopeev Peak in Antarctica is named after him.

Dolphin Tale 2 is a 2014 American family film written and directed by Charles Martin Smith as the sequel to his 2011 film Dolphin Tale which in-turn was based on the true story about a rescued bottlenose dolphin named Winter who made her final on-screen appearance in this film. In addition to Winter, most of the cast from the first film also reprise their roles including Harry Connick Jr., Ashley Judd, Nathan Gamble, Cozi Zuehlsdorff, Kris Kristofferson, Morgan Freeman, Austin Stowell, Tom Nowicki, Betsy Landin and Juliana Harkavy while Hope made her film debut. It was released on September 12, 2014, and tells the story of another dolphin at the Clearwater Marine Aquarium named "Hope". After Winter's elderly companion and surrogate mother, Panama, dies, Winter's future is in jeopardy, unless Sawyer, Clay, Hazel, and the rest of the team can find a new companion for her. The film received generally mixed to positive reviews from critics and grossed $57.8 million.

CBS Playhouse was an American anthology drama series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. It featured original teleplays by prominent writers, including Ron Cowen and Reginald Rose, and won multiple Emmy and Peabody Awards. The series aimed to elevate television drama but ended due to lack of sponsorship.

Walton Castle was a late 3rd-century Saxon Shore fort located on the cliffs of Felixstowe, Suffolk, England. Likely built as part of Roman coastal defenses, it may have later served as the early medieval bishopric of Dommoc. The Normans repurposed it as a motte-and-bailey castle, but it was slighted in the 12th century. Coastal erosion destroyed the ruins by the 18th century, though remnants are occasionally visible at extreme low tides.
Software was a German electronic music duo active from 1984 to 1999, known for their releases on the IC label. After disbanding, member Michael Weisser revived the name for later solo projects.
The Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face is a Catholic religious congregation founded in 1950 by Abbot Hildebrand Gregori, dedicated to devotion and reparation to the Holy Face of Jesus.

Ronaldo Soares Giovanelli (born 20 November 1967) is a Brazilian former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper, best known for his 12-year career with Corinthians, where he made over 600 appearances. He later pursued a music career as a singer and radio host.
The Taylor Titch is a British fixed-wing homebuilt aircraft, developed in the 1960s by J.F. Taylor. As of 2015, examples are still being built and flown.

Down High School is a controlled co-educational grammar school located in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland. The school has students from the ages of eleven to eighteen in the senior school — of which there are approximately 970 pupils. On 21 July 2014, John O'Dowd, the education minister, approved a development proposal to close the preparatory department of Down High School and it closed on 30 June 2014. There are roughly 250 pupils engaged in A-level study in the sixth form. In July 2015, some £20 million was granted by the education minister to begin the new build project in 2017.

The mountain ground squirrel (*Geosciurus princeps*) is a large-bodied rodent native to the arid regions of southwestern Africa, ranging from southern Angola to Namibia and South Africa. It has a distinctive appearance, with short cinnamon-brown fur, a white underbelly, and a striped tail. Adults measure 23–29 cm (9.1–11.4 in) in head-body length, with tails of 21–28 cm (8.3–11.0 in), and weigh between 490–710 grams (1.1–1.6 lb). Unlike the closely related Cape ground squirrel, this species is less social, with solitary males and small female groups. Strictly diurnal, they forage up to 1 km (0.6 mi) from their burrows. The IUCN lists them as Least Concern.

The 400th Missile Squadron is an inactive United States Air Force unit. It was last assigned to the 90th Operations Group at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, Wyoming, where it was inactivated in 2005.

The squadron was first activated as the 10th Reconnaissance Squadron in 1942. Soon renamed the 400th Bombardment Squadron, it flew Consolidated B-24 Liberators in the Pacific during World War II, where it earned two Distinguished Unit Citations and a Philippine Republic Presidential Unit Citation for its actions in combat. After VJ Day, the squadron remained in the Philippines until January 1946, when it was inactivated.
