In films, Lemasters has appeared in the thriller film The Stepfather (2009), the romantic comedy film Easy A (2010), the family comedy film A Christmas Story 2 (2012) and the horror film Totem (2017).

Valsartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and left ventricular dysfunction after a heart attack. It works by blocking the effects of angiotensin II, leading to blood vessel relaxation and reduced blood pressure. Common side effects include dizziness, low blood pressure, and diarrhea.  

Valsartan is available under brand names such as Diovan and in combination with drugs like hydrochlorothiazide, amlodipine, or sacubitril for enhanced treatment effects. However, it carries a black box warning for fetal harm and should not be used during pregnancy.  

In 2018, global recalls occurred due to contamination with N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA), a potential carcinogen, leading to shortages. Research suggests valsartan may also reduce the risk of type 2 diabetes and slow Alzheimer's disease progression, though further studies are needed.

The National Premier League, also known for sponsorship purposes as the Red Stripe Premier League, is sanctioned by the Jamaican Football Federation as the top division for men's association football in Jamaica. Contested between 12 clubs, it operates on a system of promotion and relegation with Jamaica's regional Super Leagues where the two lowest placed teams are relegated to their corresponding local second division league.

Redan Football Netball Club, nicknamed the Lions, is an Australian rules football and netball club based in Ballarat, Victoria. Founded in 1871, it competes in the Ballarat Football League (BFL).

The club has won 11 senior premierships, with its most successful periods coming in the 1970s (three consecutive flags from 1975–1977) and the 2000s (five premierships between 2002–2011). Redan has produced multiple Henderson Medal (BFL best-and-fairest) winners, including Jarrod Edwards, who won the award four times (2006–2009). The club has also had several Tony Lockett Medal (leading goalkicker) winners, with W. Wells topping the league's goal tally in 1952 and 1953.

Redan's senior team plays home games at City Oval, while its junior and female football teams are based at Western Oval. The club maintains a strong presence in the Ballarat sporting community and continues to compete at a high level in both football and netball.

Dmitry Tikhonovich Lisanevich (Lang-ru, 1778 – 1825) was a prominent general of Imperial Russian Army. He is best known for negotiation of The Treaty of Kurakchay and killing of Ibrahim Khalil Khan of Karabakh.

Matt Johnson (born 15 August 1961) is an English singer, songwriter, and producer, best known as the frontman of the band The The. Active since 1979, he has released critically acclaimed albums such as *Soul Mining* (1983) and *Infected* (1986). His work blends alternative rock, post-punk, and new wave.

Brian C.J. Moore FMedSci, FRS (born 10 February 1946) is an Emeritus Professor of Auditory Perception in the University of Cambridge and an Emeritus Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge. His research focuses on psychoacoustics, audiology, and the development and assessment of hearing aids (signal processing and fitting methods).

Moore is a fellow of the Royal Society, the Academy of Medical Sciences, the Acoustical Society of America, the Audio Engineering Society, the British Society of Audiology, the Association for Psychological Science, and the Belgian Society of Audiology, and the British Society of Hearing Aid Audiologists. He has written or edited 21 books and over 750 scientific papers and book chapters.

The Camp Beauregard Memorial, outside Water Valley, Kentucky on Kentucky state road 2422 northeast of town, marks the site of a Confederate States Army encampment named for General P. G. T. Beauregard. The camp was situated to protect the right flank of the Confederate encampment at Columbus, Kentucky.

Audubon Aquarium of the Americas is an aquarium in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States.

It is run by the Audubon Nature Institute, which also supervises Audubon Zoo, Audubon Louisiana Nature Center, Freeport-McMoRan Audubon Species Survival Center, Audubon Center for Research of Endangered Species (ACRES), Coastal Wildlife Network, Audubon Wilderness Park, and Audubon Park. It is located along the banks of the Mississippi River by the edge of the historic French Quarter off Canal Street, at the upper end of Woldenberg Park. It opened on September 1, 1990.

Colegio San Benito ("CSB") is a private Roman Catholic, university-preparatory and elementary school, founded in Mayagüez, Puerto Rico in 1965. The school was the first boys-only Catholic school founded in the area of Mayagüez.
C. Harrie Gooden (d. 1907) was an Australian civil servant, artist, and secretary of the Adelaide Easel Club.
Marko Čakarević (born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player, known for his time with Partizan and as the 2017 Serbian Super League MVP.

The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was an experiment in maintaining racial integration of a neighborhood. It took place in University City, Missouri from 1968 to 1990. One of its leading investors was Leo Drey. His wife, Kay Drey, was its chief spokesperson. The UCHRT bought houses in a part of the city into which African Americans were moving, as whites were moving out. The houses were rented to white families with school-age children. The goal was to keep the neighborhood and the local public school integrated. In 1979, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch said, "The project--which experts say may be the only one of its kind in the United States--appears to have promoted integration successfully."

Unconscious (or intuitive) communication is the subtle, unintentional, unconscious cues that provide information to another individual. It can be verbal (speech patterns, physical activity while speaking, or the tone of voice of an individual) or it can be nonverbal (facial expressions and body language). Some psychologists instead use the term honest signals because such cues are involuntary behaviors that often convey emotion whereas body language can be controlled. Many decisions are based on unconscious communication, which is interpreted and created in the right hemisphere of the brain. The right hemisphere is dominant in perceiving and expressing body language, facial expressions, verbal cues, and other indications that have to do with emotion but it does not exclusively deal with the unconscious.

Little is known about the unconscious mind or about how decisions are made based on unconscious communications except that they are always unintentional. There are two types of unconscious communications: intrapersonal and interpersonal.

Research has shown that human conscious attention can attend to 5–9 items simultaneously. All other information is processed by the unconscious mind. For example, the unconscious mind sometimes picks up on and relates nonverbal cues about an individual based on how they have arranged their settings such as their home or place of work.

Ethel Page (née Blunt; 20 September 1875 – 26 May 1958) was an Australian nurse and political activist. She was the wife of Sir Earle Page, briefly serving as Spouse of the Prime Minister of Australia during his 19-day caretaker government in 1939. A trained nurse, she later became involved in women's organizations, founding the Women's Country Party and advocating for rural women's rights. She was also a director of the *Sunraysia Daily* newspaper. Despite suffering a stroke in 1933, she remained active in public life until her death.

Beenie Gunter (born Crescent Baguma, February 23, 1993) is a Ugandan dancehall and reggae musician. Known for hits like *Pon Mi* and *Olina Work*, he has collaborated with artists such as Eddy Kenzo, Sheebah Karungi, and Skales. Since 2013, he has released music under labels including Savy Music, Guntalk City, and Talent Africa Group. His debut album, *No Fear*, was released in 2019.

Crystina Poncher is an American sports broadcaster, currently serving as a play-by-play commentator for Top Rank Boxing and ESPN+. She previously worked with the NFL Network, Fox Sports West, and FoxSports.com.

USCGC *Robert Yered* (WPC-1104) is a *Sentinel*-class fast response cutter of the United States Coast Guard, commissioned in 2013. Built by Bollinger Shipyards, it is designed for coastal patrol, interdiction, and search-and-rescue missions. Named after Engineman First Class Robert J. Yered, a Silver Star recipient, the cutter has participated in multiple rescues and migrant interdictions. It remains in active service.

John Bullock O.S.A. (d. 1439 × 1440) was an Augustinian canon and prelate active in the 15th century Kingdom of Scotland. While earning a university degree between 1409 and 1417, Bullock gained several benefices in Scotland, and claimed the headship of St Andrews Cathedral Priory before becoming Bishop of Ross in 1418. He held the latter position until his death, which occurred in either 1439 or 1440.

The 2014 WNBA Finals was the playoff series for the 2014 season of the Women's National Basketball Association (WNBA), and the conclusion of the season's playoffs. The Phoenix Mercury, champions of the Western Conference, faced the Chicago Sky, champions of the Eastern Conference.

The WNBA Finals were under a 2–2–1 rotation. The Mercury held home-court advantage as they had a better regular season record (29–5) than the Sky (15–19). The Mercury swept the Sky in three games to win their third title in franchise history.

Stuyvesant Street is a historic street in Manhattan, New York City, originally part of Petrus Stuyvesant's 17th-century farm. Running east from Fourth Avenue, it once led to the Stuyvesant manor house, later replaced by St. Mark's Church in-the-Bowery. The street retains its 19th-century character, with landmarks such as the Hamilton Fish House and buildings within the St. Mark's Historic District. Unlike most Manhattan streets, it defies the 1811 grid plan. A former section near St. Mark's Church is now Abe Lebewohl Park, named after a local deli owner and activist.

Brooke High School is a public high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, serving grades 9–12. Established in 1969 through the consolidation of three local schools, it is part of the Brooke County School District. The Bruins compete in the Ohio Valley Athletic Conference and have won state championships in football, basketball, and cheerleading.

Bajrang Punia (born 26 February 1994) is an Indian freestyle wrestler, who competes in the 65-kg weight category. At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Punia won a bronze medal by defeating Kazakhstan's Daulet Niyazbekov with a wide 8-0 margin. Punia is the only Indian wrestler to win 4 medals at the World Wrestling Championships.

The University of St. Thomas School of Law is a private Catholic law school in Minneapolis, Minnesota. Established in 1999 (reviving an earlier law school that closed in 1933), it received ABA accreditation in 2006. The school is known for its Mentor Externship Program, which pairs students with legal professionals, and its emphasis on ethics, social justice, and practical training. It offers J.D., LL.M., and joint-degree programs, including an online LL.M. in organizational ethics and compliance. The school operates legal clinics and publishes two law journals. Its downtown campus features a four-story atrium, chapel, and moot court room.

Walter G. Alexander (December 3, 1880 – February 5, 1953) was an American physician and politician. A leader in African-American medical organizations, he became the first Black legislator in New Jersey when elected to the General Assembly in 1920. He also served as acting Assembly Speaker in 1921.

Stephanie Zimmermann (October 29, 1973-November 10, 2020) was a German physicist who worked on the ATLAS experiment at the Large Hadron Collider at CERN. As a researcher from the University of Freiburg, she was involved in the Muon Detector Control System activities, and she served as muon run-coordinator. She was elected and served as ATLAS Run-Coordinator in 2012–2014. She then became Project Leader of the New Small Wheel project (NSW), part of an extensive upgrade, the largest phase 1 upgrade project for the ATLAS detector.

Caserma Ederle is a U.S. Army garrison in Vicenza, Italy, serving as the headquarters of the United States Army Southern European Task Force (SETAF) since 1965. Established in 1955 following the withdrawal of U.S. forces from Austria, it initially housed nuclear-capable units during the Cold War. The base supports military personnel and families with standard amenities, including housing, a commissary, and a Post Exchange. In the 21st century, it expanded with the Del Din annex, consolidating the 173rd Airborne Brigade Combat Team and U.S. Army Africa headquarters. The garrison also oversees Camp Darby near Pisa, which was downgraded to a sub-garrison in 2012. Caserma Ederle remains a key U.S. military installation in Southern Europe.

Andrew Keith Leigh (born 3 August 1972) is an Australian politician, author, lawyer and former professor of economics at the Australian National University. He currently serves as the Assistant Minister for Competition, Charities and Treasury. He briefly served as the Parliamentary Secretary to Prime Minister Julia Gillard in 2013 and then served as Shadow Assistant Treasurer from 2013 to 2019. He has been a Labor member of the Australian House of Representatives since 2010 representing the seat of Fraser until 2016 and Fenner thereafter. Leigh is not a member of any factions of the Labor Party.

The Slutsk uprising (Lang-be) or the Slutsk defence (Lang-be) was an unsuccessful armed attempt to establish an independent Belarus. It took place in late 1920, near the end of the Polish-Soviet War, in the region of the town of Slutsk. It involved a series of clashes between irregular Belarusian forces loyal to the Belarusian People's Republic and the Soviet Red Army, ending in a Soviet victory.
The Troy Slapshots were a professional ice hockey team based in Troy, New York. They were a member of the Atlantic Coast Hockey League in the 1985–86 season.

"Love Theme from The Godfather" is an instrumental theme from the 1972 film The Godfather, composed by Nino Rota. The piece was lyricized in English by Larry Kusik into "Speak Softly, Love", a popular song released in 1972. The highest-charting rendition of either version was by vocalist Andy Williams, who took "Speak Softly Love" to number 34 on Billboard magazine's Hot 100 and number seven on its Easy Listening chart.

Pit-Fighter is a 1990 arcade fighting game developed by Atari Games. Players choose one of three fighters to battle opponents in a pit, using punches, kicks, and special moves while contending with crowd interference. The game features multiplayer modes, including a "Grudge Match" and a final battle against the Masked Warrior. It was ported to multiple home consoles and computers, though some versions omitted features like interactive audiences and certain characters. While praised for its multiplayer action, it received mixed reviews for its controls and graphics. A planned sequel was never released, though some elements appeared in *Guardians of the 'Hood*.

Wafa Mustafa (b. 1989 or 1990 (age 34–35)) is a Syrian journalist and activist who campaigns for the release of Syrian detainees. As an activist and former member of Families for Freedom, Mustafa has extensively lobbied the United Nations Security Council to call for the release of the names and locations of all those that Syrian authorities have in captivity. She calls for all detainees in Syria to be freed, whether they are held by the Assad regime or by opposition groups, though she also supports the public demonstrations that began in 2011 against the government of Bashar al-Assad.

Mustafa also campaigns for international recognition of Syrian refugees and against normalized international relations with the Assad regime.

The Fairchild 9440 MICROFLAME, also known as the F9440 and μFLAME, was a 16-bit microprocessor introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor in 1977. The 9440 implemented the Data General Nova 2's instruction set in a single-chip 40-pin DIP. The name "MICROFLAME" was part of a wider branding exercise called "FIRE", which was a development software system.

An updated version, the 9445, was announced in 1978 but did not reach market until late 1981. By this time the 16-bit designs were being surpassed by 32-bit designs and hybrids like the Motorola 68000, and Fairchild began turning their attention to their own 32-bit Fairchild Clipper design. The underlying core of the 9445 was also used to implement the 9450, which used new microcode to implement the MIL-STD-1750A instruction set.

The 9440 and 9445 were subject to constant lawsuits from Data General (DG) that dragged on both companies. DG finally settled all ongoing litigation in September 1986 by paying Fairchild over $52 million. Fairchild was purchased and sold several times during this period, ending up at National Semiconductor in 1987. National ended production of the 9445, leaving several users in the lurch. A final version, the IDC9445, was introduced to fill this need.

La Spinalba (Spinalba, ovvero Il vecchio matto, in English Spinalba or The Mad Old Man) is an opera (dramma comico) in three acts, with music by Francisco António de Almeida to an Italian-language libretto whose author is unknown. It was premiered in the Portuguese Carnival season of 1739 at the Palácio da Ribeira, Lisbon.

Backes & Strauss is an English company that designs and produces high-end timepieces and jewellery. Founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany, it is the oldest diamond company in the world.

The company's London office was established in 1814, and the company hallmark was registered at Goldsmith's Hall in London in 1878. In November 2006, Backes & Strauss entered into a strategic partnership with the Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller Group, launching its flagship collections of Regent, Berkeley and Piccadilly watches.
Workpoint TV is Thailand's television station, owned by Workpoint Entertainment, broadcasting on channel 23 of Thailand's digital television.

Trelawne (Lang-kw, meaning elm-tree homestead) is an historic manor in the parish of Pelynt in Cornwall, England, situated 20 miles (32 km) west of Plymouth, Devon and four miles (6.4 km) west-northwest of Looe, Cornwall. It was long the seat of the Trelawny family, later Trelawny baronets, one of the most eminent of Cornish gentry families, much of whose political power derived from their control of the pocket borough of nearby East Looe. The surviving grade II* listed manor house known as Trelawne House is today used for holiday accommodation and entertainment, under the name "Trelawne Manor Holiday Park".

Dinkar D. Patil (6 November 1915 – 23 October 2005) was an Indian filmmaker, screenwriter, and director known for his contributions to Marathi cinema. Over his five-decade career, he directed 35 films and wrote scripts for over 60, often focusing on rural themes and social issues. A pioneer in introducing Lavani folk dance to cinema, he also played a key role in establishing Kolhapur's Marathi Film City. His autobiography, *Patlache Por*, remains influential.
Ukhra Assembly constituency was an assembly constituency in what is now Paschim Bardhaman district in the Indian state of West Bengal. It covered the Ukhra area.

ESET NOD32 is an antivirus software suite developed by ESET, first released in 1987. Originally designed for DOS, it evolved into a multi-platform solution for Windows, macOS, Linux, and mobile devices. Known for its heuristic analysis and lightweight performance, it includes features like real-time scanning, exploit blocking, and anti-phishing. The suite has received multiple awards, including over 100 VB100 certifications.

Harmony, also known as Node 2, is a module of the International Space Station (ISS) that serves as a connecting hub for science laboratories, including Columbus and Kibō. Launched aboard Space Shuttle Discovery on 23 October 2007, it was installed on the forward end of the Destiny laboratory. Harmony was built by Thales Alenia Space in Italy under a NASA-ESA agreement and handed over to NASA in 2003.  

The module has six Common Berthing Mechanisms (CBMs) to attach other modules and visiting spacecraft, including Pressurized Mating Adapters (PMAs) for Space Shuttle and Commercial Crew dockings. It also houses crew quarters and avionics racks. Harmony expanded the ISS's living space, enabling the addition of international labs and supporting cargo missions via Cygnus, Dragon, and HTV spacecraft. Its name was chosen through a student competition in 2004, symbolizing international cooperation in space.

Fredric Brandt (June 26, 1949 – April 5, 2015) was an American dermatologist and author, known for his pioneering work in cosmetic dermatology and skincare. Born in Newark, New Jersey, he earned his medical degree from Hahnemann Medical College and trained at NYU and the University of Miami. Brandt established practices in Miami and New York City, where he conducted clinical research on injectables, lasers, and skincare treatments. He authored two books on anti-aging and founded his own skincare line. Brandt died by suicide at age 65 in his Florida home.

The 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship Division I was an international inline hockey tournament run by the International Ice Hockey Federation. The Division I tournament ran alongside the 2017 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship tournament and took place between 25 June and 1 July 2017 in Bratislava, Slovakia at the Ondrej Nepela Arena Rink 2 and Ondrej Nepela Arena. The tournament was won by Slovenia who upon winning gained promotion to the 2019 IIHF Inline Hockey World Championship. While New Zealand and Brazil were relegated to the Qualifications after losing their placement round games along with Hungary who lost the relegation game against Argentina.

The second season of the American reality competition show Top Chef Masters was announced on October 22, 2009. In addition, it was announced that Gail Simmons would be added as a critic. The season premiered on April 7, 2010 with 22 chefs competing against each other in weekly challenges. In the season finale that premiered on June 9, 2010, Marcus Samuelsson was crowned Top Chef Master.

Occupy San Francisco was a protest movement in San Francisco, California, from September 17 to December 11, 2011, as part of the broader Occupy movement against economic inequality and corporate influence in government. Protesters initially camped outside a Bank of America before relocating to the Federal Reserve Building and later Justin Herman Plaza. Police repeatedly dismantled encampments, citing public safety concerns, leading to multiple arrests. Demonstrations included marches, sit-ins, and solidarity actions with student protests. The movement also inspired later initiatives, such as a proposed credit union. Local officials, including Supervisor John Avalos, expressed support, while city authorities enforced park regulations, ultimately clearing the last major encampment in December 2011.

The inSpiral Lounge was a vegetarian and vegan café, music venue, and arts space in London, operating from 2007 to 2016. Known for its organic food, live music, and sustainability initiatives, it also hosted festivals and won awards for its eco-conscious approach.
The Sehol A5 (Chinese: 江淮嘉悦A5), named the JAC Jiayue A5 until 2021, is a compact liftback produced by JAC Motors under the Sehol sub brand since 2019.

Horace Edward Dobbs (born 14 January 1933) is a British scientist, author, and dolphin researcher. A former chemist with the UK Atomic Energy Authority, he later became known for his work on dolphin-human interactions, founding *International Dolphin Watch* and writing books such as *Follow a Wild Dolphin*. He advocates against dolphin captivity and promotes dolphin-assisted therapy.

Roberto Poljak (1929–2020) was an Argentine biophysicist known for determining the first 3D antibody structure and studying antigen-antibody interactions. He worked at Johns Hopkins, the Pasteur Institute, and the University of Maryland.

The DR Class 52.80 was a series of 200 rebuilt steam locomotives developed by East Germany's Deutsche Reichsbahn (DR) between 1960 and 1967. Based on the wartime DRB Class 52, they were modernized with Typ 50E boilers, new cylinders, and improved cabs. Some later received Giesl ejectors and Trofimoff valves. Several remain preserved, including 52 8055, which was further upgraded with oil firing and roller bearings for Swiss operation.

Emilie Tolnay (née Müller; 1899–1944) was an Austrian communist resistance fighter during World War II. Arrested in 1942 for aiding the anti-Nazi opposition, she was convicted of treason and executed by guillotine in 1944. Her husband, Anton Tolnay, received a prison sentence but survived the war.
Patrick Lichty is a conceptual media artist, activist, curator, and educator. Lichty is currently a Creative Digital Media professor at Winona State University.

Rami Mehmed Pasha (1645–1706) was an Ottoman statesman, poet, and Grand Vizier from January to August 1703 under Sultan Mustafa II. He previously served as reis ül-küttab (foreign minister) and negotiated the Treaty of Karlowitz. After his brief grand vizierate, he governed Egypt and Cyprus before dying in exile. A suburb of Istanbul bears his name.
Barrington J. Bayley (9 April 1937 – 14 October 2008) was an English science fiction writer.
Freienfeld (IPA-de; Lang-it IPA-it) is a comune (municipality) in the province of South Tyrol in northern Italy, located about 45 kilometres (28 mi) north of the city of Bolzano.

Grace Yun Yi is a professor of the University of Western Ontario where she currently holds a Tier I Canada Research Chair in Data Science. She was a professor at the University of Waterloo, Canada, where she holds a University Research Chair in Statistical and Actuarial Science. Her research concerns event history analysis with missing data and its applications in medicine, engineering, and social science.
Rodney Bullock Jr. (born 3 June 1994) is an American professional basketball player for Prishtina of the Kosovo Basketball Superleague. He competed in college basketball for Providence.

McKenzie is a city at the tripoint of Carroll, Henry, and Weakley counties in Tennessee, United States. The population was 5,310 at the 2010 census.

It is home to Bethel University and the Tennessee College of Applied Technology at McKenzie.

The Coaldale Big Green was an early professional football team based in Coaldale, Pennsylvania. The club played as an independent until joining the Anthracite League in 1924. After leaving the league in 1924, the team spent its 1925 season as an independent, then joined the short-lived Eastern League of Professional Football in 1926. While the most well known Anthracite League team is the Pottsville Maroons, which jumped to the National Football League in 1925 and immediately established itself as a championship contender, the most consistently successful club in the coal region was the Big Green. Coaldale won the coal region's Curran Cup in 1921, 1922 and 1923.
Unison is an unincorporated community village in Loudoun County, Virginia. It is located approximately five miles from Middleburg in the Loudoun Valley close to the Fauquier County border.

O RLY? is an internet slang phrase and meme, often used sarcastically to express skepticism. Originating in online forums, it became widely recognized through image macros featuring a snowy owl. The phrase has been referenced in media, including *World of Warcraft*, and inspired a 2006 computer worm that printed "O RLY?" images. It has also been parodied, notably in mock-ups mimicking O'Reilly Media's book covers.

1,3,2,4-Dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides are a class of organophosphorus, four-membered ring compounds which contain a P2S2 ring, many of these compounds are able to act as sources of the dithiophosphine ylides. The most well known example of this class of compound is Lawesson's reagent.

Other examples of this class of compound have been made; many inorganic chemists are now using Fc2P2S4 (Fc = ferrocene) as a starting material in reactions investigating the general chemistry of the 1,3,2,4-dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides, one reaction for this is that the Fc2P2S4 compound and all its derivatives are red which make column chromatography of the products more easy. Also the ferrocenyl groups provide an electrochemical handle which provide another means of investigating the properties of the products.

Deerfoot (c. 1828–1897), also known as Lewis Bennett, was a Seneca long-distance runner who dominated professional racing in the 1860s, setting world records and gaining fame in England for his speed and showmanship.
Claude Frederic T'Serclaes, Count of Tilly, was a soldier and later general in the Dutch States Army.
Lunella undulata, common name the common warrener or the lightning turban, is a species of sea snail, marine gastropod mollusk in the family Turbinidae.

The Love of Hypnosis (Chinese: 南烟斋笔录; pinyin: Nán Yānzhāi Bǐlù; lit. 'Southern Mist House Records') is an upcoming Chinese television series based on the eponymous manhua Nan Yanzhai Bilu (南烟斋笔录) by Ke Xiaosha and Zuo Xiaoling. It stars Liu Yifei and Jing Boran.
Mount Gould is a 13,011-foot (3,966 m) peak in California's Sierra Nevada, straddling Fresno and Inyo counties.
Babócsa (Lang-de, Lang-hr) is a village in Somogy County, Hungary.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival, founded in 1981, is the second-oldest Jewish film festival in the U.S. Hosted by the Gershman Y, it screens international films and awards audience favorites. The festival runs annually, featuring works from multiple countries.

"7empest" (pronounced "tempest") is a song by American rock band Tool. Clocking in at over 15 minutes in length, it is the final song on the band's fifth studio album, Fear Inoculum. The song peaked at number 6 on the Billboard Hot Rock Songs chart and was cited by critics as a standout track from the album. It later won the Grammy Award for Best Metal Performance.

Margaret JoBeth Williams (born December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director. Her directorial debut with the 1994 short film On Hope earned her an Academy Award nomination for Best Live Action Short Film. In 2009 she began serving as president of the Screen Actors Guild Foundation; she is President Emeritus of the foundation.

Williams rose to prominence appearing in such films as Stir Crazy (1980), Poltergeist (1982), The Big Chill (1983), The Day After (1983), Teachers (1984), and Poltergeist II: The Other Side (1986). A three-time Emmy Award nominee, she was nominated for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Miniseries or a Movie for her work in the TV movie Adam (1983) and the TV miniseries Baby M (1988). Her third nomination was for her guest role in the sitcom Frasier (1994). She also starred in the TV series The Client (1995–96) and had recurring roles in the TV series Dexter (2007) and Private Practice (2009–11).

NBA Street Vol. 2 is a basketball video game, published by Electronic Arts under the EA Sports BIG label and developed by NuFX and EA Canada. It is the sequel to NBA Street and the second game in the NBA Street series. It was released on April 29, 2003 for the PlayStation 2, GameCube, and, for the first time in the series, on the Xbox. Japan was only able to see a PlayStation 2 release of this game. The GameCube version was also planned to be released in that region, but it was canceled for unknown reasons.

Persicaria minor is species of herb in the family Polygonaceae. Common names include pygmy smartweed, small water pepper and swamp willow weed. This herb is native to Asia, but distributed widely in Europe and Australia. It is used in South East Asian cooking.

The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the Giants' 106th season in Major League Baseball, their 31st season in San Francisco since their move from New York following the 1957 season, and their 29th at Candlestick Park. The team finished in fourth place in the National League West with an 83–79 record, 111⁄2 games behind the Los Angeles Dodgers.

Robinsons Summit Center is a 38-story office skyscraper in Makati, Philippines, completed in 2001. Designed by Hellmuth, Obata + Kassabaum and W.V. Coscolluela & Associates, it stands at 174 meters (570 ft) and features a double-height lobby, rooftop helipad, and a Fitness First branch. Located on Ayala Avenue, it serves as a hub for call centers and corporate tenants, including Hewlett-Packard Philippines.
Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris (IPA-fr) is a station on Line 13 of the Paris Métro in the commune of Saint-Denis.

Michael St John Trend, CBE (born 19 April 1952) is a British former Conservative politician and journalist who was a member of parliament from 1992 to 2005, when he stood down after an expenses scandal.

Ladies Legend Pro-Wrestling (LLPW-X) is a Japanese joshi puroresu promotion founded in 1992 by Rumi Kazama and Shinobu Kandori after the split of Japan Women's Pro-Wrestling. It later shifted to occasional events.

A Zirkel (German for "circle", as in "circle of friends") is a monogram used in European student societies in countries such as Germany, Austria, Switzerland, Belgium, Hungary, Latvia, Estonia (Studentenverbindungen).

El Camino High School is a public high school in Oceanside, California, established in 1975. Part of the Oceanside Unified School District, it serves nearly 3,000 students with a student-teacher ratio of 26:1. The school's mascot is the Wildcat, and its colors are brown and gold. It competes in the CIF San Diego Section's Avocado West League, with Oceanside High School as its rival. El Camino High gained national attention in 2008 for a staged drunk-driving accident as part of the "Every 15 Minutes—Extreme" program. Earlier, in 1986, it faced controversy when students unsuccessfully petitioned to remove books on witchcraft and satanism from the library. The school's newspaper is *The Sun*.

The C. A. Holliday Transfer Facility is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice prison in Huntsville, Texas, named after African-American pastor and activist C. A. Holliday. Opened in 1994 with a capacity of 2,000 inmates, it serves as a transfer unit for prisoners awaiting assignment to other facilities. The industrial-style complex, noted for its sheet metal construction, was among the first TDCJ facilities to ban smoking before a statewide prison smoking prohibition took effect in 1995. Critics have described its design as resembling a "giant tin barn" with minimal security features beyond razor wire and guard towers.

Poilão is a small, uninhabited island in the Bijagós Archipelago of Guinea-Bissau, covering just 0.43 km² (0.17 sq mi). It holds deep cultural significance as a sacred site for the Bijagós people, traditionally owned by the community of Roxa. Part of the João Vieira and Poilão Marine National Park, it is one of the world's most important nesting sites for green sea turtles, hosting around 5% of the global population. The island's pristine beaches and undisturbed forests support a thriving ecosystem, including dolphins and monitor lizards. Access is restricted, with limited exceptions for traditional ceremonies and conservation efforts.

Al Piechota (January 19, 1914 – June 13, 1996) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Boston Bees/Braves from 1940 to 1941. Born in Chicago, he began his career in the minor leagues in 1933, leading the Mississippi Valley League in wins as a rookie. After stints with several minor league teams, including the Kansas City Blues, he made his MLB debut in 1940. His career was interrupted by military service in World War II, during which he played for service teams. After retiring from baseball in 1951, he worked as a Chicago police officer for 25 years.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG) is a scholarly organization that promotes research in anthropology and geography. Founded in the 19th century, it awards the Vega Medal for achievements in physical geography and the SSAG Medal (formerly the Anders Retzius Medal) for contributions to anthropology or human geography. These awards are presented by the King of Sweden on April 24, commemorating Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld's return from the Vega expedition.  

Since 2018, the society has published *Kritisk Etnografi: Swedish Journal of Anthropology*, a biannual, open-access, peer-reviewed journal focusing on critical ethnography. The society has distanced itself from controversial historical figures, renaming its anthropology medal in 2015 due to Anders Retzius's association with racial studies.

Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve is a protected area in the Australian Capital Territory, established in 1971 and covering 54.5 km² (21.0 sq mi). It is part of the Australian Alps National Parks and Reserves, listed on the National Heritage List. The reserve has deep Indigenous significance, with archaeological evidence of Aboriginal occupation dating back over 20,000 years. The name "Tidbinbilla" derives from the Ngunnawal word *Jedbinbilla*, meaning "a place where boys become men."  

European settlement in the 19th century left historic homesteads, including Nil Desperandum and Rock Valley Homestead, though both were damaged in the 2003 Canberra bushfires. The reserve plays a key role in wildlife conservation, breeding endangered species such as the Northern Corroboree Frog and Southern Brush-tailed Rock-wallaby. It features diverse walking trails, ranging from short family-friendly loops to challenging hikes like Gibraltar Peak, offering views of the surrounding landscape. The reserve is also adjacent to the Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex.

The AN/FPS-24 was a Cold War-era early warning radar system used by the United States Air Force. Operating in the VHF band, it had a range of 250 nautical miles (460 km) and a massive 120-by-50-foot (37 by 15 m) antenna. Twelve units were built between 1958 and 1962, but mechanical issues, including bearing failures due to the antenna's weight, led to operational challenges. Some were later converted into Air Route Surveillance Radars (ARSR). A few remaining radar towers, including one at Almaden Air Force Station, have been designated as historic structures.
Gerhard vom Rath (20 August 1830 – 23 April 1888), was a German mineralogist, born at Duisburg in Prussia.

"Cookie" is the third single recorded by South Korean girl group NewJeans from their debut extended play (EP) New Jeans. It was released as their debut single by ADOR ahead of the EP as one of the its lead singles on August 1, 2022.
William Tempest (1682–1761) was an English barrister and Fellow of the Royal Society.

Gurie Georgiu (31 December 1968 – 21 October 2021) was a Romanian prelate and member of the Holy Synod [ro] of the Romanian Orthodox Church. He served as the first Bishop of the Diocese of Deva and Hunedoara from its establishment in 2009 until his death from COVID-19 complications in October 2021, amid the COVID-19 pandemic in Romania.

Moses Rischin (born in Brooklyn, New York) was an American historian and professor at San Francisco State University. He earned his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 1957 and later directed the Western Jewish History Center at the Judah L. Magnes Museum. Rischin served on the board of the *Journal of American Ethnic History* and the American Jewish History Society. In 1996, a collection of essays was published in his honor. A character in Helen Hudson’s 1967 novel *Meyer Meyer* may have been inspired by him.

Civil parishes in Cambridgeshire are the lowest tier of local government, with roots in medieval ecclesiastical divisions. Established as civil administrative units under the Local Government Act 1894, they gained responsibilities such as road maintenance and poor relief. Modern parishes can elect councils, become towns, or adopt alternative names like "community council." The Local Government Act 1972 preserved rural parishes, while recent reforms allow unparished areas to establish new parishes.
Hanna Atik (born February 7, 1959) is a Lebanese politician and former military commander of the Lebanese Forces during the Civil War.

Elizabeth Marian Swaney OLY (born 30 July 1984) is an American born-and-raised freestyle skier who competed for Hungary in the 2018 Winter Olympics in the women's halfpipe based on her Hungarian ancestry. She was unable to qualify for the 2014 Winter Olympics for Venezuela in both skeleton and freestyle skiing, and qualified for the 2018 Winter Olympics in the women's halfpipe, in which she placed last.

The POW/MIA flag is a symbol honoring American prisoners of war (POW) and those missing in action (MIA). Designed in 1971 by Newt Heisley, it features a white silhouette of a POW before a guard tower with the words "POW/MIA" and "You Are Not Forgotten" on a black field. Adopted by the National League of Families, it was later recognized by Congress and is now flown at federal and military sites. Since 2019, federal law requires its display alongside the U.S. flag on certain days.

Homaro Cantu (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef, inventor, and restaurateur known for his pioneering work in molecular gastronomy. Born in Tacoma, Washington, he overcame homelessness in his youth before training at the Western Culinary Institute. After working under Charlie Trotter in Chicago, Cantu opened Moto in 2004, earning a Michelin star for his experimental dishes, such as edible menus and laser-cooked food. He later founded iNG, focusing on "flavor-tripping" using miracle berries, and Berrista, a coffeehouse.  

A prolific inventor, Cantu filed over 100 patents, including edible printing technology and sustainable food solutions aimed at combating hunger. He appeared on *Iron Chef America*, defeating Masaharu Morimoto, and hosted *Future Food* on Discovery’s Planet Green. Cantu also founded the Trotter Project, supporting culinary education in underserved communities.  

His death by suicide in 2015 shocked the culinary world. Remembered as a visionary, Cantu was praised for blending science and cuisine, with *The New York Times* calling him the "Buck Rogers of food." His legacy includes influencing modern gastronomy and efforts to revolutionize global food accessibility.

*Gambling Lady* is a 1934 American romantic drama film directed by Archie Mayo and starring Barbara Stanwyck, Joel McCrea, and Pat O'Brien. It follows a professional gambler navigating love and crime.

Meir Tobianski (20 May 1904 – 30 June 1948) was an Israeli soldier wrongfully executed for espionage during the 1948 Arab-Israeli War. Born in Kovno, Lithuania, he served in the Lithuanian Army before immigrating to Mandatory Palestine in 1925. He later joined the British Army in World War II, rising to the rank of major. After the war, he worked as an engineer for the Jerusalem Electric Corporation before enlisting in the newly formed Israel Defense Forces.  

Accused of passing military information to the British, Tobianski was subjected to a drumhead court-martial led by intelligence officer Isser Be'eri and summarily executed without legal defense. Months later, an inquiry exonerated him, and he was posthumously reinstated with full military honors. His case became a symbol of wartime judicial overreach, inspiring literary and media works. Be'eri was later convicted of manslaughter but received only a symbolic sentence.
Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959) is an Italian former luger who won a bronze medal at the 1979 World Championships and the 1985–86 Luge World Cup.

*Swing Kids* is a 1993 American historical drama film directed by Thomas Carter. Set in Nazi Germany, it follows a group of teenagers who embrace swing music and resist the Hitler Youth. Starring Robert Sean Leonard, Christian Bale, and Frank Whaley, the film received mixed reviews and underperformed at the box office.

Godzilla vs. Kong (soundtrack) is the film score composed by Tom Holkenborg for the 2021 monster film *Godzilla vs. Kong*. Released on March 31, 2021, by WaterTower Music, the soundtrack features original themes for both Godzilla and King Kong, departing from Akira Ifukube's classic Godzilla motifs to create a distinct identity for the MonsterVerse. Holkenborg employed deep brass and massive percussion for Godzilla's theme to convey power, while Kong's theme incorporated organic and electronic elements to reflect his more human-like nature. Two tracks, "Pensacola, Florida (Godzilla Theme)" and "Skull Island (Kong Theme)," were released as singles ahead of the full album.  

Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the new themes but criticism for the absence of Ifukube's iconic motifs and reliance on electronic elements. Additional songs, including tracks by The Hollies and Man with a Mission (exclusive to the Japanese release), were used in the film but not included in the official soundtrack. The score follows Holkenborg's work on *Zack Snyder's Justice League* (2021) and precedes *Army of the Dead* (2021).

"Her Strut" is a 1980 hard rock song by Bob Seger & The Silver Bullet Band from their album *Against the Wind*. Inspired by Jane Fonda, the song drew mixed reviews for its lyrics and energetic sound. It later appeared on compilation albums and in video games like *Grand Theft Auto IV*.
Taylor Titch is a British single-seat, low-wing monoplane designed by John Taylor for the 1964 Midget Racer competition. Over 40 have been built since its first flight in 1967.
General Sir Daril Gerard Watson GCB CBE MC (17 October 1888 − 1 July 1967) was a senior British Army officer who saw service during both World War I and World War II.

UFOria is a 1985 American comedy film written and directed by John Binder. Starring Fred Ward, Cindy Williams, and Harry Dean Stanton, it follows a con man who falls for a UFO-obsessed supermarket clerk, leading to the rise of a pseudoreligious cult. Despite positive reviews from critics like Roger Ebert, the film struggled in theaters due to limited distribution. Its extensive country music soundtrack delayed home media releases, and it remains unavailable on DVD or Blu-ray.

Pudliszki is a Polish food company specializing in fruit and vegetable products, founded in 1920 and headquartered in Pudliszki. Originally a sugar refinery (1847), it later shifted to distilling and, by the 1920s, began producing tomato-based goods. Nationalized after WWII, it became a leading state-owned enterprise before privatization in 1994. Since 1997, it has been majority-owned by Heinz.

Azerbaijan—France relations are the bilateral relations between the Republic of Azerbaijan and the French Republic in the political, socio-economic, cultural, and other spheres.

Cooperation is carried out in such areas as energy (Total), environment (Suez CNIM), transport (Alstom, Thales, Iveco, SYSTRA), agriculture (Lactalis, Danone), space industry (Airbus, Thales), gas sector, security, culture, hotel business (Accor), banking (Societe Generale), aviation, etc.

Clepsicosma is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae, containing the single species *Clepsicosma iridia*. Described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, it is endemic to New Zealand, found in native forests across the North Island and parts of the South Island. The species is named after the Roman goddess Iris, referencing the iridescent scales on its forewings. Adults are nocturnal, active from December to May, and are attracted to light. Larvae are presumed to feed on *Gahnia* species, though this has not been confirmed in captivity. An undescribed *Clepsicosma* species has also been identified in northern New Zealand. The holotype is held at the Natural History Museum, London.

Seventeen: A Tale of Youth and Summer Time and the Baxter Family Especially William is a humorous novel by Booth Tarkington that gently satirizes first love, in the person of a callow 17-year-old, William Sylvanus Baxter. Seventeen takes place in a small city in the Midwestern United States shortly before World War I. It was published as sketches in the Metropolitan Magazine in 1915 and 1916, and collected in a single volume by Harper and Brothers in 1916, when it was the bestselling novel in the United States.

"Tsunagu" is the 52nd single by Japanese boy band Arashi. It was released on June 28, 2017 under their record label J Storm. "Tsunagu" is the theme song for the film Shinobi no Kuni starring Arashi member Satoshi Ohno. The single sold over 380,000 copies in its first week and topped the weekly Oricon Singles Chart. The single was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of Japan (RIAJ).

Celanese Corporation, formerly known as Hoechst Celanese, is an American technology and specialty materials company headquartered in Irving, Texas. A Fortune 500 corporation, the company is the world’s leading producer of acetic acid, producing about 1.95 million tonnes per year, representing approximately 25% of global production. Celanese is also the world's largest producer of vinyl acetate monomer (VAM).

Celanese operates 25 production plants and six research centers in 11 countries, mainly in North America, Europe, and Asia. The company owns and operates the world's three largest acetic acid plants: one in the Clear Lake area of Pasadena, Texas, one on Jurong Island in Singapore, and a third in Nanjing, China.

Maitreya-nātha is a figure in Mahayana Buddhism traditionally regarded as the celestial bodhisattva Maitreya, who is said to have transmitted teachings to the scholar Asaṅga in the Tuṣita heaven. Some scholars debate whether Maitreya-nātha was a historical teacher or a divine figure. Several key Buddhist texts, including the *Five Dharmas of Maitreya*, are attributed to him, though their authorship remains disputed between Maitreya-nātha and Asaṅga.
April Carrión is the stage name of Jason Carrión (born April 22, 1989), a Puerto Rican drag queen and television personality best known for their appearance on the sixth season of RuPaul's Drag Race.

Nick Horvath (born February 18, 1981) is an American-New Zealand former professional basketball player. A 6'10" center/power forward, he played college basketball at Duke, winning an NCAA championship in 2001. He spent most of his professional career in Australia and New Zealand, winning an NBL title (2009) and NZNBL MVP (2012). He also briefly represented New Zealand internationally.
Ronaldo Soares Giovanelli (born 20 November 1967) is a Brazilian football pundit and retired footballer. Although as a child he was a forward, he spent his career as a goalkeeper.

Jumping Flash! 2: Big Trouble in Little Muu (Japanese: ジャンピングフラッシュ! 2 アロハ男爵大弱りの巻, Hepburn: Janpingu Furasshu! Tsū: Aroha Danshaku Ō Yowari no Maki) is a first-person shooter platform video game developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation in 1996. It is the direct sequel to Jumping Flash!, which was released the previous year. It was later re-released for the PlayStation 3 and PlayStation Portable via the PlayStation Network in 2009.

The game revolves around the robotic rabbit Robbit as he searches for lost MuuMuus, which are scattered throughout different levels in the game by Captain Kabuki. Robbit must explore each expansive new world to recapture all the lost MuuMuus. As with its predecessor, the game is presented in a first-person perspective. Much of the interface was transferred from the first Jumping Flash! to the second in order to save time during development. New features include a much longer draw distance, updated enemy AI and a meter displaying whether or not Robbit can triple-jump.

Similar to its predecessor, Jumping Flash! 2 was well received by critics, who praised the game for its updated interface, advanced graphics and gameplay. However, also like its predecessor, it was criticised for its short length, lack of challenging gameplay and close similarities to the first game. Despite this, Jumping Flash! 2 gained enough popularity to spawn a final sequel, Robbit Mon Dieu.

George Stiebel (1820–1896) was a Jamaican businessman of mixed German-Jewish and African descent. A former arms trader, he became wealthy through gold mining and real estate, owning 99 properties, including Devon House in Kingston. He was appointed a Companion of the Order of St Michael and St George in 1891.

Susan Gordon (July 27, 1949 – December 11, 2011) was an American child actress known for her roles in *The Twilight Zone*, *The Five Pennies*, and films directed by her father, Bert I. Gordon, including *Tormented*. She retired from acting in 1967.

An Ziwen (Chinese: 安子文; pinyin: Ān Zǐwén; 25 September 1909 – 25 June 1980), born as An Zhihan (安之瀚), was a Chinese politician and member of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party. He served as minister of the CCP Central Committee Organization Department, the Central People's Government Minister of Personnel, deputy secretary of the Central Discipline Inspection Commission, the Standing Committee of the CPPCC National Committee, among other roles. He authored the book "Revolutionary successors training is a strategic task of the party".

Hunsrückisch is a German dialect spoken in the Hunsrück region of Germany (Rhineland-Palatinate). This mountainous region of Germany has long been an exporter of emigrants to Brazil, United States, Canada, Australia and other parts of the world.

Hunsrückisch was spoken in Edgar Reitz's acclaimed television series Heimat.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis was a British entomologist and naturalist, known for his coleoptera collection.

The National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) is a U.S. government agency that provides statistical information to guide actions and policies to improve the public health of the American people. It is a unit of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and a principal agency of the U.S. Federal Statistical System. It is headquartered at University Town Center in Hyattsville, Maryland, just outside Washington, D.C.

Association football during World War II saw widespread disruption, with many leagues suspended or replaced by regional competitions. England, Germany, and Scotland adapted with wartime tournaments, while Italy's Serie A continued until 1943. International matches persisted unofficially, and football served as a morale booster. Many players enlisted, and Jewish footballers were among Holocaust victims. Post-war, football gradually resumed normal operations.

Callington was a rotten borough in Cornwall, England, that elected two Members of Parliament (MPs) from 1585 until its disfranchisement under the Reform Act 1832. Controlled by the Rolle and Coryton families, it had a small electorate and was abolished due to its lack of genuine representation.

Mercury Meltdown is a puzzle-platform game developed by Ignition Banbury and published by Ignition Entertainment (with Atari handling the European release). Released in 2006 for the PlayStation Portable (PSP), it was later ported to the PlayStation 2 as *Mercury Meltdown Remix* (2006) and the Wii as *Mercury Meltdown Revolution* (2007). A sequel to *Archer Maclean's Mercury*, the game tasks players with navigating blobs of mercury through tilting stages, using color-changing mechanics and different states (liquid, semi-solid, and solid) to solve puzzles. It features single-player and multiplayer modes, along with unlockable party minigames.  

The game received generally positive reviews, with critics praising its improved difficulty curve, vibrant cel-shaded visuals, and intuitive controls—particularly the Wii version's motion-based gameplay. However, some criticized the lack of multiplayer in later ports. The PSP and Wii versions were later included in *1001 Video Games You Must Play Before You Die*. An iOS version was announced in 2011 but never released.

Contour crafting is a building printing technology being researched by Behrokh Khoshnevis of the University of Southern California's Information Sciences Institute (in the Viterbi School of Engineering) that uses a computer-controlled crane or gantry to build edifices rapidly and efficiently with substantially less manual labor. It was originally conceived as a method to construct molds for industrial parts. Khoshnevis decided to adapt the technology for rapid home construction as a way to rebuild after natural disasters, like the devastating earthquakes that have plagued his native Iran.

Using a quick-setting, concrete-like material, contour crafting forms the house's walls layer by layer until topped off by floors and ceilings set in place by the crane. The notional concept calls for the insertion of structural components, plumbing, wiring, utilities, and even consumer devices like audiovisual systems as the layers are built.

Kamaal the Abstract is the second studio album by American rapper and producer Q-Tip, released on September 15, 2009, by Battery Records. Initially recorded in 2001 and shelved by Arista Records due to concerns over its commercial viability, the album blends neo-soul, jazz fusion, funk, and hip hop, marking a departure from Q-Tip's more mainstream debut, *Amplified* (1999). Critics noted its experimental, improvisational style, drawing comparisons to artists like Prince and Stevie Wonder. The album features introspective lyrics, with themes ranging from social commentary to love and existentialism. Despite its delayed release, *Kamaal the Abstract* received positive reviews, debuting at No. 77 on the Billboard 200 and earning praise for its genre-defying sound. The lead single, "Barely in Love", was released on September 1, 2009.

The Jamaica Premier League (JPL) is the top-tier professional football league in Jamaica, founded in 1973. It consists of 12 clubs and operates on a promotion-relegation system with regional second-division leagues. The most successful club is Portmore United, with seven titles. The league's top teams qualify for the CFU Club Championship, a pathway to the CONCACAF Champions League and CONCACAF League. The current champions (2021) are Cavalier F.C.
The 1994–95 Atlante F.C. season was the 4th season since the team's last promotion to Primera División. Atlante competed in Primera División and Copa México

Lu Rongjing (Chinese: 卢荣景; born August 1933) is a Chinese politician who served as governor of Anhui from 1988 to 1989, party secretary of Anhui from 1988 to 1996, and chairman of the Anhui Provincial Committee of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference from 1996 to 2000.

He was a delegate to the 7th National People's Congress. He was a member of the Standing Committee of the 9th and 10th Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference. He was a member of the 13th, 14th and 15th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party.
56 Field Regiment (Jitra) is part of the Regiment of Artillery of the Indian Army.

¡Que viva la lucha! is a 2007 film directed and produced by Gustavo Vazquez about Lucha libre in Tijuana, Mexico, considered to be a form of extreme lucha libre. The film follows Extreme Tiger, an up-and-coming professional luchador from Tijuana, on a journey that places him in a mask vs. hair match against Joe Líder. In addition, he follows other new talent, Angel Negro Jr. and Pancho Cachondo. In the process, the film also interviews other luchadores, promoters, commissioners, families, and fans who discuss the cultural significance of lucha libre as sport, ritual, and spectacle. The film includes music by Carne Cruda, Marziano, White Pine, and Nortec Collective. ¡Que viva la lucha! premiered at the 30th annual Mill Valley Film Festival on Saturday, Oct. 13, 2007, and has won two awards; at the Latino Film Festival and at the San Francisco International Film Festival.

Tales of Zestiria the X (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X, Teiruzu Obu Zesutiria Za Kurosu, pronounced "Tales of Zestiria the Cross") is a Japanese anime television series based on both Tales of Zestiria and Tales of Berseria video games developed by Bandai Namco Studios and tri-Crescendo, and published by Bandai Namco Entertainment. It is produced by Ufotable, directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Hikaru Kondo, featuring character designs by Akira Matsushima and music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina. The anime series is split into two halves: the first half premiered on July 3, 2016 and finished airing on September 25, 2016. The second half premiered on January 8, 2017 and finished airing on April 29, 2017.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was a United States Marine Corps colonel and a Medal of Honor recipient for his actions during World War II. A fighter pilot in the Pacific Theater, he earned the Medal of Honor for shooting down five Japanese aircraft in a single mission over the Solomon Islands on January 31, 1943, despite being wounded and forced to bail out. After evading capture with the help of indigenous islanders, he was rescued and returned to combat, finishing the war with nine aerial victories.  

After the war, DeBlanc earned multiple degrees in education and taught math and physics. He remained in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972, retiring as a colonel. He later appeared on the History Channel series *Dogfights*, recounting his wartime experiences. DeBlanc was the last surviving Medal of Honor recipient from Louisiana during World War II. He died in 2007 and was buried in Saint Martinville, Louisiana.

Kachche Heere is a 1982 Bollywood Hindi action film directed by Narender Bedi, starring Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, Aruna Irani and Shakti Kapoor. The movie was produced by Netra Pal Singh and the music was composed by Rahul Dev Burman. The film was somewhat a sequel to the 1974 Blockbuster Khote Sikkay. The film had the same star cast and almost the exact story line. Among few changes were that Ranjeet was replaced by Shakti Kapoor. The film was released on 12 February 1982.

Benedicto “Bong” Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino impressionist painter. He is now teaching at the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts and Design. He is noted for his renderings of plants, flowers and nature in droplet-like brush strokes and bright colors. In college, Ducat won the University of Santo Tomas Benavides Civic Award.

El Camino High School is one of two high schools of the Oceanside Unified School District, located at 400 Rancho Del Oro Drive in Oceanside, California. Declared a California Distinguished School in the early 2000s, it was originally named "Oceanside High School East", and officially became El Camino High School in 1976. El Camino's Truax Theatre was built in the early 1980s and houses a large performance venue, an adjacent classroom with stage and large music and drama rooms. It also recently built a new Science and Technology Building and is undergoing constant construction to clean up the campus. El Camino maintains a close athletic rivalry with Oceanside High School. The ECHS mascot is the "Wildcat" and the school colors are brown and gold.

Bellevue Plantation was the U.S. home of Catherine Willis Gray Murat, located in Tallahassee, Florida. It was purchased in 1854 after Catherine's second husband Prince Achille Murat (son of Joachim Murat, Napoleon's brother-in-law and King of Naples from 1808 to 1815) died in 1847. Murat was the great-grandniece of George Washington. She was a daughter of Colonel Byrd C. Willis (August 29, 1781 – 1846) and his wife Mary Lewis. Mary Lewis was the granddaughter of Fielding Lewis, George Washington's brother-in-law. Through the Lewis family, she was also a relative of explorer Meriwether Lewis.

Nikaho (にかほ市, Nikaho-shi) is a city located in Akita Prefecture, Japan. As of 31 December 2019, the city had an estimated population of 24,291 in 9,371 households, and a population density of 100 persons per km2. The total area of the city is 241.13 square kilometres (93.10 sq mi).

Richardt Strauss (born 29 January 1986) is a South African-born Irish former rugby union player who played as a hooker. He began his career with the Free State Cheetahs, winning the 2006 Currie Cup, before joining Leinster in 2009, where he won two Heineken Cups. After qualifying for Ireland through residency, he made his international debut in 2012 against his native South Africa and later represented Ireland at the 2015 Rugby World Cup. Strauss retired in 2018 following a career that included overcoming a heart condition in 2013. His brother Andries and cousin Adriaan also played professional rugby.
General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL) is an American aerospace company, known as a pioneer of hypersonic propulsion.

Bustard Island is an island in the Regional Municipality of Wood Buffalo, Alberta, Canada. Located in Lake Athabasca, it features sandy shores, rock points, and two internal lagoons. The island supports colonies of California Gulls and is part of the Athabasca Plains sub-region of the Canadian Shield.

Ben Christo (born March 22, 1980) is an English guitarist, vocalist, and songwriter, best known as the lead guitarist of The Sisters of Mercy since 2006. He has also fronted bands AKO, Night by Night, and Diamond Black, and has worked as a session musician for artists including Lord of the Lost and PIG.

The Irving–Williams series is an empirical trend that predicts the relative stability of transition metal complexes, typically following the order: Mn²⁺ < Fe²⁺ < Co²⁺ < Ni²⁺ < Cu²⁺ > Zn²⁺. While originally explained by factors such as ionic radius and crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE), recent studies suggest that a combination of covalent and electrostatic interactions governs this trend. The series has been influential in coordination chemistry, helping validate crystal field theory and later ligand field theory. It also applies to metal-binding affinities in natural proteins, though recent protein engineering has demonstrated ways to circumvent this trend, enabling selective binding of metals outside the predicted sequence.

Walter Gilbert Alexander I (December 3, 1880 – February 5, 1953) was an American physician and Republican Party politician from New Jersey. He was president of the National Medical Association and the first African American to serve in the New Jersey Legislature.

Super Smash Bros. (retroactively referred to as Super Smash Bros. 64 or Smash 64) is a 1999 crossover fighting video game developed by HAL Laboratory and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo 64. It was first released in Japan on January 21, 1999, in North America on April 26, 1999, and in Europe on November 19, 1999. The first installment in the Super Smash Bros. series, it is a crossover between several different Nintendo franchises, including Mario, The Legend of Zelda, Star Fox, Yoshi, Donkey Kong, Metroid, F-Zero, Mother, Kirby, and Pokémon. It presents a cast of characters and locations from these franchises and allows players to use each character's unique skills and the stage's hazards to inflict damage, recover health, and ultimately knock opponents off the stage.

Super Smash Bros. received mostly positive reviews upon its release. It was a commercial success, selling over five million copies worldwide by 2001, with 2.93 million sold in the United States and 1.97 million sold in Japan. It was given an Editors'Choice award from IGN for the "Best Fighting Game", and also became a Nintendo 64 Player's Choice title. The game spawned a series of sequels for each successive Nintendo console, starting with Super Smash Bros. Melee which was released for the GameCube in 2001.
Konstantin (born Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev; 17 July 1976) is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. Since his 1999 debut with the hit *"Cherna Roza"*, he has remained a prominent figure in Bulgarian music.

Cine City (originally named the Scala Cinema) was a cinema in Withington, Manchester, England located at 494 Wilmslow Road, Withington, Manchester, M20 3BG. It opened in 1912 as The Scala, and was the third cinema to open in Britain. When the popularity of picture houses reached its peak in the 1930s, The Scala was one of 109 cinemas in Manchester.

During the Second World War, the cinema escaped with minor damage when the road outside was hit by a small bomb in 1940. After the war, television led to a decline in cinema attendances, and by 1965, only 40 cinemas remained in Manchester. Cine City closed in July 2001, making it the third-longest running cinema in England.

By 2005 the building was in a bad state of repair, and was threatened with demolition. Although heritage groups won a stay of execution, the cinema was demolished in spring 2008. A new residential building has been constructed on the site.

Muthoot Microfin Limited, the microfinance arm of Muthoot Pappachan Group, is an Indian microfinance institution focused on providing microloans to women entrepreneurs with a focus on rural regions of India.

E. S. Rose Park is a 25-acre (10 ha) multi-sport facility in Nashville, Tennessee, owned by Metro Parks & Recreation. Renovated in 2010–2011 at a cost of $8 million, it features artificial turf baseball and soccer fields, as well as a natural grass softball field. It serves as the home venue for Belmont University's baseball, softball, soccer, and track & field teams, as well as local high school and club sports. The park opened in April 2011 after negotiations with the Edgehill neighborhood over shared access and community benefits.

USS *Commencement Bay* (CVE-105) was a *Commencement Bay*-class escort carrier of the United States Navy, commissioned in 1944. Primarily used for training pilots and crews during World War II, she was decommissioned in 1946, reclassified as a helicopter and cargo carrier, and later scrapped in 1972.
Oliver Bäte (born [year]) is a German business executive who has served as CEO of Allianz since 2015, after holding leadership roles at McKinsey & Company and Allianz.

Vladimir Lenin monument in Azov (Lang-ru) is a monument dedicated the founder of USSR Vladimir Lenin which is situated in the city of Azov, Rostov Oblast, Russia. It is officially declared as an object of cultural heritage of regional importance.

Muthoot Microfin is an Indian microfinance institution and a subsidiary of the Muthoot Pappachan Group. It was established in 2012 after acquiring Panchratna Securities and received RBI approval as an NBFC-MFI in 2015.

State Route 159 (SR 159) is a northeast-southwest state highway (signed north–south) in the south-central portion of the U.S. state of Ohio. Its southern terminus is in Chillicothe at the U.S. Route 50 and State Route 104 concurrency; its northern terminus is at U.S. Route 22 approximately 4 miles (6.4 km) southeast Lancaster.

The Martin PBM Mariner was an American patrol bomber flying boat of World War II and the early Cold War era. It was designed to complement the Consolidated PBY Catalina and PB2Y Coronado in service. A total of 1,366 PBMs were built, with the first example flying on 18 February 1939 and the type entering service in September 1940, with the last of the type being retired in 1964.

Moses Rischin (1925-2020) was an American historian, author, lecturer, editor, and emeritus professor of history at San Francisco State University. He coined the phrase new Mormon history in a 1969 article of the same name.

Rischin is considered an authority on American ethnic and immigration history and a pioneer in the field of American Jewish history. Historian Selma Berrol, however, has challenged the minimal treatment Rischin has given to the tensions between earlier German Jews and later Russian Jews in America.

Lilian Wyles (1885–1975) was a pioneering British police officer, serving in the Metropolitan Police from 1919 to 1949. As one of the first attested female officers, she advocated for women's role in policing, particularly in handling sexual assault cases. She later wrote a memoir about her career.
Claude Frédéric t'Serclaes, Count of Tilly (1648–1723) was a Dutch field marshal who fought in the Franco-Dutch War, Nine Years' War, and War of the Spanish Succession.

The C. H. Moore House is a historic Second Empire and Italianate mansion in Clinton, Illinois, built in 1865. It was home to attorney Clifton H. Moore, who worked with Abraham Lincoln. Now the DeWitt County Museum, it features a preserved Victorian interior, a large library, and hosts the annual Apple 'n Pork Festival. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979.

Portugal is generally considered as successful in upholding the civil liberties and protecting the human rights of its citizens. Portugal has proved to be determined in promoting and respecting human rights at an international and national level. The country's minister of Justice as of September 2018, Francisca Van Dunem, said that Portugal has had "a good track record" on human rights but violations still do persist.

Portugal is a member state of the European Union and therefore its citizens are protected by its Charter of Fundamental Rights. In addition to being a member of the European Union, Portugal is also a member state of the United Nations and has signed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

The Cizeta-Moroder V16T is a mid-engine sports car produced by Cizeta Automobili from 1991 to 2003. Designed by Marcello Gandini and Claudio Zampolli, it features a transversely mounted 6.0-liter V16 engine producing 540 hp. Only 13 units were built, including one convertible. Initially a collaboration between Zampolli and composer Giorgio Moroder, the car was known for its high performance, luxury features, and exclusivity, with a top speed of 204 mph (328 km/h).

Waiau is a small town in north Canterbury, in the South Island of New Zealand. It lies 30 kilometres (19 mi) east of Hanmer Springs on the northern bank of the Waiau Uwha River, some 30 kilometres (19 mi) from the river's mouth. There is a small supermarket, a DIY store and a petrol station.

Desiderio Macías Silva was a Mexican poet, educator, and university administrator, best known for winning the National Poetry Prize Aguascalientes and serving as president of the Autonomous University of Aguascalientes.
Kisnána is a village in Heves county, Hungary, located 25 kilometres (16 mi) from Gyöngyös to the northeast. There is a castle in the village that was built in the 11th or 12th century.

Fight Sports is a Grappling and Brazilian jiu-jitsu academy and team based out of Miami, Florida. It was founded in 2011 by multiple time world champion Roberto "Cyborg" Abreu. Fight Sport's competition team includes a number of jiu-jitsu's top competitors, including many ADCC qualifiers. Fight Sports is also an internationally known Jiu Jitsu affiliation with 32 schools worldwide.
San Michele, locally called in dialect, Gésa Granda, is the Roman Catholic parish church located in the city center at Piazza Dante Alighieri in the town Cameri, province of Novara, Piedmont, Italy.

Henrique Dumont (July 20, 1832 – August 30, 1892) was a Brazilian engineer and coffee planter, known as the "Coffee King" for owning one of Brazil's largest coffee farms. A graduate of École Centrale Paris, he modernized coffee production with railroads and machinery. He was the father of aviation pioneer Alberto Santos Dumont.
Les Gandar (1919–1994) was a New Zealand politician, farmer, and diplomat who served as Minister of Education (1975–1978) and High Commissioner to the UK (1979–1982).
Jasaw Chan Kʼawiil I also known as Ruler A, Ah Cacao and Sky Rain, (before 682-734), was an ajaw of the Maya city of Tikal. He took the throne on May 3, 682 and reigned until his death.

The Faculty of Social and Human Sciences (Portuguese: *Faculdade de Ciências Sociais e Humanas*, NOVA FCSH) is a public university faculty in Lisbon, Portugal, part of NOVA University Lisbon. Established in 1977, it offers undergraduate, master's, and doctoral programs in social sciences and humanities. With over 5,000 students, it is one of the largest faculties at NOVA and hosts 16 research centers. Its main campus is located on Avenida de Berna, with plans for expansion to the Campolide Campus. The faculty is known for its strong research output and international exchange programs.

Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (Chinese: 鐘萬學; pinyin: Zhōng Wànxué, Pha̍k-fa-sṳ: Chûng Van-ho̍k; born 29 June 1966) is an Indonesian businessman, politician, and former governor of Jakarta. He is also known by his Hakka Chinese nickname Ahok (Chinese: 阿學). He was the second minority governor of Jakarta with Chinese ancestry and also of the Evangelical faith, following Henk Ngantung, who was governor of Catholic faith from 1964–65.

Ahok was a legislator in the Indonesian People's Representative Council (DPR) and Regent of East Belitung. He was elected to the House of Representatives for the 2009–2014 term but resigned in 2012 to run for the deputy governorship of Jakarta, to which he was elected. In November 2014, he became governor of Jakarta, as his predecessor Joko Widodo had become president. Ahead of the 2017 Jakarta gubernatorial election, Ahok's political rivals aligned themselves with Islamic extremists to exploit religious and racial intolerance, resulting in Ahok being accused of blasphemy in October 2016. He then lost the election to former Education Minister Anies Baswedan and was imprisoned for blasphemy.

Human rights in Portugal are protected under the country's 1976 Constitution, which guarantees fundamental rights and freedoms following the end of the Estado Novo dictatorship in 1974. Portugal is a signatory to major international human rights treaties, including the European Convention on Human Rights, the Convention on the Rights of the Child, and the Refugee Convention. The country has made significant progress in LGBT rights, including legalizing same-sex marriage (2010) and adoption (2016), as well as banning discrimination based on sexual orientation. However, challenges remain, including child poverty, gender inequality, and reports of police brutality and poor prison conditions. Portugal also faces criticism over inadequate housing for Roma and people of African descent, despite constitutional protections. The government has implemented policies to combat domestic violence and improve refugee integration, though reception conditions remain a concern.

The Mizoram Peace Accord was signed on June 30, 1986, in New Delhi, ending a 20-year insurgency led by the Mizo National Front (MNF) against the Indian government. The conflict began in 1966 when the MNF declared independence, leading to a violent uprising and military crackdown. After years of failed negotiations, church leaders and civil society groups mediated talks, culminating in an agreement under Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi.  

Key terms included the MNF surrendering arms, renouncing secessionist demands, and accepting Indian sovereignty. In return, Mizoram was granted statehood in 1987, and the MNF transitioned into a political party, with its leader Laldenga becoming Chief Minister. The accord also led to the establishment of Mizoram University and the recognition of June 30 as "Remna Ni" (Peace Day). The agreement is considered one of India’s most successful peace deals, restoring stability in the region.

La Spinalba is a 1739 comic opera by Portuguese composer Francisco António de Almeida. The only one of his three operas to survive intact, it reflects the influence of Giovanni Battista Pergolesi.
