Thought lost in the 1755 Lisbon earthquake, it was rediscovered in the 1960s and has since been performed in Portugal, Britain, and Spain, with a 2012 studio recording released by Naxos.

Karli Coburger (born 4 October 1929) is a former Major general in the East German Ministry for State Security (MfS / "Stasi"). In 1984 he became, as matters turned out, the last head of the service's Central Department for surveillance, fact finding and arrests ("Hauptabteilung / Section VIII").

Sid Hudson (January 3, 1915 – October 10, 2008) was an American professional baseball pitcher who played 12 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Washington Senators and Boston Red Sox. A two-time All-Star (1941, 1942), he compiled a 104–152 record with a 4.28 ERA. After his playing career, he became a longtime pitching coach for the Senators/Texas Rangers and later Baylor University. At his death, he was one of the oldest living former MLB players.

Asia Aluminum was a Hong Kong-based aluminum extrusion company founded in 1992. It expanded rapidly, issuing $450 million in bonds in 2004 and undergoing a leveraged buyout in 2006. However, the 2008 financial crisis and costly investments led to insolvency by 2009, with $1.5 billion in debt. After failed restructuring attempts, its management bought the company for $475 million, leaving creditors with significant losses. The case highlights risks in China's corporate debt market.

Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1339), probably the same person known as Angelino de Dalorto (fl. 1320s), and whose real name was probably Angelino de Dulceto or Dulceti or possibly Angelí Dolcet, was an Italian-Majorcan cartographer.

He is responsible for two notable 14th-century portolan charts, the "Dalorto" chart of 1325 and the "Dulcert" chart of 1339. The latter is the first portolan known to have been produced in Palma, and considered the founding piece of the Majorcan cartographic school. He is also believed to be the author of a third undated and unsigned chart held in London.

Living Colour is an American rock band formed in New York City in 1984 by guitarist Vernon Reid. Known for their fusion of hard rock, funk, and metal, the band gained mainstream success with their 1988 debut album *Vivid*, featuring the Grammy-winning hit "Cult of Personality". The original lineup included Corey Glover (vocals), Muzz Skillings (bass), and Will Calhoun (drums), later replaced by Doug Wimbish in 1992.  

Living Colour achieved critical and commercial success with albums like *Time's Up* (1990) and *Stain* (1993) before disbanding in 1995. They reunited in 2000 and have since released several albums, including *Collideøscope* (2003), *The Chair in the Doorway* (2009), and *Shade* (2017). Their music blends political themes, jazz, punk, and hip-hop influences, earning them recognition as funk metal pioneers.  

The band remains active, touring and recording new material. Their song "Cult of Personality" has endured as a cultural staple, notably used as the entrance theme for wrestler CM Punk in WWE, UFC, and AEW. Living Colour was ranked among VH1's 100 Greatest Artists of Hard Rock and continues to influence rock and alternative music.

Hyper Combat Unit Dangaioh (破邪大星ダンガイオー, Haja Taisei Dangaiō) is a Japanese anime OVA series produced and animated by AIC and Artmic and released in Japan in 1987. Dangaioh featured character designs by creator Toshiki Hirano, mechanical designs by Shoji Kawamori, and animation direction by Masami Ōbari.

Franco-Albertans (Lang-fr) are francophone residents of the Canadian province of Alberta. Franco-Albertans may also refer to residents of Alberta with French Canadian ancestry, although publications from the government of Alberta use the term Franco-Albertan to refer to its francophone residents. In the 2016 Canadian Census, there were 86,705 Albertans that stated their mother tongue was French. In the same census, there were 268,615 Albertans that claimed to be able to speak French.

Francophones were the first Europeans to visit the province, with French Canadian voyageurs employed in the fur trade exploring the region in the late 18th century. French Canadians settled into a number of communities in the Northwest Territories during the 19th century, including communities in present day Alberta. Several French toponyms exist in Alberta, exemplifying the Francophone presence in the region. In 1928, the Association canadienne-française de l'Alberta was formed to promote francophone rights, and to lobby the interests of Franco-Albertans to the province. Following the enactment of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms in 1982, Franco-Albertans pushed for further linguistic rights within the province, eventually resulting in the province establishing an independent public francophone school system in 1994.

Approximately 72 per cent of Franco-Albertans are situated within the province's two largest cities, Edmonton, and Calgary. The province is home to more than 100 francophone non-profit organizations. Radio-Canada, the country's French-language public broadcaster, serves as the main French-language media broadcaster in Alberta.

The Prospect Hill neighborhood in North Omaha, Nebraska is one of the oldest neighborhoods in the city. In addition to being home to some of the city's oldest structures, the neighborhood is also the site of the Prospect Hill Cemetery which is located between North 31st and 33rd Streets and Parker and Grant Streets. The neighborhood's boundaries are North 30th Street from Hamilton Street to Lake and up to Creighton Boulevard; then over to Blondo and up to North 38th Street to Hamilton. The Omaha Belt Line ran near the northwest corner of the neighborhood. The neighborhood is also home to the historic Franklin Elementary School.

The Maharishi Institute of Management (MIM) is a private business school in India, established in 1993 under Maharishi Shiksha Sansthan. It has campuses in Bhopal, Indore, Noida, Bangalore, Hyderabad, and Chennai, offering AICTE-approved programs such as BBA, BCA, MBA, and MCA. The institute integrates Consciousness-Based Education, including Transcendental Meditation, into its curriculum. Affiliated with various universities, MIM emphasizes Vedic Management to develop leadership and problem-solving skills in students.

Qualified teacher status (QTS) or Qualified teacher learning and skills status (QTLS) is required in England and Wales to work as a teacher of children in state schools under local authority control, and in special education schools. A similar status exists under a different name in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

Clidastes is an extinct genus of marine lizard belonging to the mosasaur family. It is classified as part of the Mosasaurinae subfamily, alongside genera like Mosasaurus and Prognathodon. Clidastes is known from deposits ranging in age from the Coniacian to the early Campanian in the United States.

Clidastes means "locked vertebrae", which originates from the Greek noun κλειδί, or kleid meaning key (akin to Latin claudere meaning to shut). This refers to how the vertebral processes allow the proximal heads of the vertebrae to interlock for stability and strength during swimming.

It was one of the earliest hydropedal mosasaurs, representing one of the first properly marine predatory forms alongside other early hydropedal genera like Tylosaurus and Platecarpus. It was likely an agile swimmer that preyed upon cephalopods, fish and other small vertebrates in shallow water. Isotopic analysis on teeth specimens has suggested that this genus and Platecarpus may have entered freshwater occasionally, just like modern sea snakes.
Thomas Anthony Lawton (born Darwin, 1 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player. He played as a hooker.
MercyMe, It's Christmas is the second Christmas album by MercyMe, released on October 9, 2015, through Fair Trade Services. The album was produced by Brown Bannister and Ben Shive.

Gretta Kehoe-Quigley is a former camogie player, captain of the All Ireland Camogie Championship winning team in 1975, the day after she was married to Ray Quigley, the trainer of her club camogie team.

The Togliattiazot affair was a major Russian legal case involving allegations of fraud, tax evasion, and corporate misconduct at Togliattiazot (ToAZ), one of the world's largest ammonia producers. The case centered on Vladimir Makhlai, the company's former owner, and his associates, who were accused of orchestrating schemes to sell products below market value through offshore entities, depriving shareholders and the state of revenue. After years of investigations and trials, five individuals, including Makhlai and Swiss businessman Andreas Zivy, were convicted in absentia in 2019 and sentenced to prison terms. The court also ordered compensation of over 87 billion rubles in damages.
Tasneem Roc (born 11 December 1978) is an Australian television and film actress born in Sydney.

Paterson is a New Jersey Transit commuter railway station located on an elevated viaduct above Market Street in downtown Paterson, New Jersey. The railway through the station is double tracked, for north and south traffic on the NJT Main Line.

"If Love Was a Crime" is a dance-pop song by Bulgarian singer Poli Genova, released on 21 March 2016 as her country's entry for the Eurovision Song Contest 2016. Written by Borislav Milanov, Sebastian Arman, Joacim Persson, and Genova herself, the song was internally selected by Bulgaria's national broadcaster, BNT. It competed in the second semi-final on 12 May 2016 in Stockholm, Sweden, qualifying for the grand final, where it placed 4th with 307 points—Bulgaria's best result at the time. The song became a streaming hit, amassing over 4 million plays on Spotify and nearly 15 million YouTube views for its official music video. A Greek version, titled *"Isovia Mazi"*, was later recorded by singer Demy.

Ahmed Javed (born 2 January 1956) is a retired Indian police officer and civil servant who was the 39th Commissioner of Police, Mumbai, India. He was succeeded by Dattatray Padsalgikar. He is former Indian Ambassador to Saudi Arabia. Javed is the second former Mumbai police commissioner to get a diplomatic posting after J. F. Ribeiro who served as Indian ambassador to Romania between 1989–1993.

Neodiprion abietis, the balsam fir sawfly, is a species of sawfly whose larvae feed on conifers, particularly balsam fir. It undergoes five to six larval instars, exhibits arrhenotokous parthenogenesis, and causes periodic outbreaks in eastern Canada, leading to tree defoliation and growth reduction.

May Song Vang (February 5, 1951 – August 5, 2013) was a Laotian-born Hmong American community leader, educator, and healthcare worker. She became a pioneering figure in Laos as a trained midwife and physician assistant before marrying General Vang Pao in 1973. After fleeing to the U.S. in 1975, she advocated for Hmong refugees, promoted education, and helped establish Vang Pao Elementary School in Fresno, California. She founded the General Vang Pao Foundation and remained a key leader in the Hmong American community until her death.

Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Norwegian politician for the Labour Party. She served as Minister of Culture (2012–2013) and later as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion (2021–2022). Tajik was the first Muslim cabinet member in Norway and the youngest minister at the time of her appointment. She has been a Member of Parliament since 2009, representing Oslo and later Rogaland. Tajik also served as deputy leader of the Labour Party from 2015 to 2022. Born to Pakistani immigrants, she holds degrees in journalism, human rights, and law. Her tenure as minister was marked by efforts to strengthen workers' rights, though her resignation in 2022 followed controversies over tax and housing benefits.

Aqua is a Danish-Norwegian pop group formed in Copenhagen in 1989, originally known as Joyspeed. The band consists of Lene Nystrøm (vocals), René Dif (rap vocals), and Søren Rasted (keyboards), with former member Claus Norreen (keyboards) departing in 2016. Aqua rose to international fame in the late 1990s with their bubblegum pop and Eurodance sound, particularly with their 1997 hit "Barbie Girl", which topped charts worldwide and sparked controversy over its satirical lyrics. Their debut album, Aquarium (1997), produced multiple hits, including "Doctor Jones" and "Turn Back Time", solidifying their status as one of the most successful Scandinavian pop acts of the era.  

After a hiatus in 2001, Aqua reunited in 2008, releasing their third studio album, Megalomania (2011), and continuing to tour internationally. Known for their upbeat, playful style and energetic performances, the group remains a prominent figure in 1990s nostalgia tours. Their music blends dance-pop with Eurodance influences, drawing comparisons to acts like ABBA and Ace of Base.

Longwood House is a mansion in St. Helena and the final residence of Napoleon Bonaparte, the former Emperor of the French, during his exile on the island of Saint Helena, from 10 December 1815 until his death on 5 May 1821.

Koro is a possibly Sino-Tibetan language spoken by around 1,500 people in Arunachal Pradesh, India. It was recognized as distinct from neighboring languages like Hruso Aka in 2009 due to low lexical similarity. Some linguists classify it within the proposed Siangic branch, alongside Milang.  

Koro has a complex consonant system with voiceless and voiced distinctions, though some phonemes like the glottal stop (ʔ) may serve grammatical rather than contrastive roles. Its syllable structure varies, commonly following CV patterns but also allowing consonant clusters. Nouns use suffixes like -le (for some animals) and -me (plural marker), while verbs employ suffixes such as -ro (imperative) and -ŋa (negative).  

The language follows a subject-object-verb word order, with noun phrases typically structured as demonstrative-noun-adjective-numeral. Question words appear after the subject or object. Research on Koro remains ongoing, with debates over vowel length and influences from neighboring languages.
Oliver Samuels (born 9 November 1948) is a Jamaican comedian and actor. He is often described as the Jamaican "King of Comedy", performing both stand-up and comic theatre.

Araxá is a municipality in Minas Gerais, Brazil, known for its mineral-rich spa tourism and niobium mining. With a population of 107,337 (2020), it lies at an elevation of 973 meters (3,192 ft). The city's economy also includes agriculture, phosphate production, and industry. It is home to the historic Grande Hotel and served by Araxá Airport.

A capsule hotel is a type of budget accommodation where guests stay in small, stacked sleeping pods. Each capsule typically includes basic amenities such as a bed, television, and air conditioning, while shared facilities like bathrooms, showers, and lounges are communal. Originating in Japan, these hotels cater primarily to travelers seeking affordable, short-term lodging, often used by salarymen who miss the last train home. Prices are low, usually between ¥2000–4000 (USD 18–36) per night. However, the confined space and lack of soundproofing can be uncomfortable for some guests. While traditionally male-dominated, some capsule hotels now offer separate sections for women.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship was the fourth season of the FIA GT3 series, featuring GT3-specification sports cars. New entrants included the Audi R8 LMS and Alpina B6, while the Ford Mustang FR500GT3 was discontinued. Ferrari introduced the 430 Scuderia GT3, replacing the F430 GT3, and Morgan debuted the Aero Super Sport. Teams were limited to two cars per manufacturer, with a maximum of three teams per brand. Manufacturers such as Ascari, Dodge, Jaguar, Lamborghini, and Morgan were ineligible for the Manufacturers Cup due to insufficient entries. Points were awarded to the top eight finishers, with half-points given for incomplete races. The championship also featured a Manufacturer Cup for brands supplying at least two full-season teams.

Violet Dandridge, was the pseudonym for Serena Katherine Dandridge (1878–1956), she was an American scientific illustrator, painter, naturalist, and suffragist. She was the Smithsonian Institution’s first female scientific illustrator.
B.A. Van Sise is an American photographer and author. He has worked as a travel photographer, and collections of his fine art photography has been exhibited in public installations by US museums.

The C. H. Moore Homestead, in Clinton, Illinois, is one of two Registered Historic Places in DeWitt County. The other, the Magill House, was added to the Register in 2003. CH Moore Homestead DeWitt County Museum was added in 1979.

Sidney Charles Hudson (January 3, 1915 – October 10, 2008) was a starting pitcher in Major League Baseball who played for the Washington Senators (1940–42, 1946–52) and Boston Red Sox (1952–54) who had a lengthy post-playing career as a pitching coach and scout. Born in Coalfield, Tennessee, he batted and threw right-handed, stood 6 feet 4 inches (1.93 m) tall and weighed 180 pounds (82 kg).
Oto Horvat (born 1967) is a Serbian poet and writer. He lives and works in Florence, Italy.

Our Lady and St Peter's Church is a Roman Catholic parish church in Bridlington, East Riding of Yorkshire, England. It was built from 1893 to 1894 in the Gothic Revival style. It is located on the corner of Victoria Road and Wycliffe Lane, close to the town centre. It is a Grade II listed building.
Marko Čakarević (Serbian Cyrillic: Марко Чакаревић, born May 13, 1988) is a Serbian professional basketball player for Beograd (3x3) team.

In the Night Room is a 2004 horror-thriller novel by American author Peter Straub, a sequel to his 2003 book Lost Boy, Lost Girl. The work was first published in hardback on October 26, 2004 through Random House and it won the 2004 Bram Stoker Award for Novel. Straub encountered some difficulties while writing In the Night Room and had written several different passages for the work before growing bored with each version before writing it using the same technique he used for its predecessor in which he "reached down inside the book and turned it inside out."

Ajantrik (known internationally as The Unmechanical, The Mechanical Man or The Pathetic Fallacy) is a 1958 Indian Bengali film written and directed by revered parallel filmmaker Ritwik Ghatak. The film is adapted from a Bengali short story of the same name written by Subodh Ghosh.

A comedy-drama film, Ajantrik is one of the earliest Indian films to portray an inanimate object, in this case an automobile, as a character in the story. It achieves this through the use of sounds recorded post-production to emphasize the car's bodily functions and movements.

The film was considered for a special entry in the Venice Film Festival in 1959.
Lamakaan (Lang-ur) is an open cultural center in Banjara Hills, Hyderabad, India. The non-profit organisation provides space for events such as arts, literature, theater, music, and debates.
The Museo MAGI ‘900 is a private museum in Pieve di Cento, Emilia-Romagna, Italy.

Hardoi is a village in Maharajganj block of Rae Bareli district, Uttar Pradesh, India. Located on the road from Maharajganj to Bachhrawan, Hardoi historically served as the seat of a pargana and has a now-ruined fort built in the early 1400s under the Jaunpur Sultanate. As of 2011, its population is 2,308, in 415 households.

The Hôtel de la Marine is a historic neoclassical building on the Place de la Concorde in Paris, France. Designed by Ange-Jacques Gabriel and completed in 1774, it originally served as the Garde-Meuble de la Couronne (Royal Furniture Repository), housing France’s royal collections. During the French Revolution, it became the headquarters of the French Navy (1792–2015) and was the site of key events, including the theft of the French Crown Jewels in 1792.  

The building features lavish interiors, including the restored apartments of the Intendant and the Salon des Amiraux, adorned with maritime-themed decor. After the Navy’s departure in 2015, it underwent a major restoration and reopened as a public museum in 2021. It now also hosts the Al Thani Collection, showcasing global artworks on long-term loan from Qatar’s royal family.  

A landmark of 18th-century French architecture, the Hôtel de la Marine remains a symbol of Paris’s cultural and political history.

The 2009 FIA GT3 European Championship season was the fourth season of the FIA GT3 European Championship. The season began on 2 May at Silverstone and ended on 14 November 2009 at Zolder. The season featured six double-header rounds, with each race lasting for a duration of 60 minutes. Most of the events were support races to the 2009 FIA GT Championship season, the 2009 GT4 European Cup and the 2009 Lamborghini Super Trofeo.

Phoenix Racing pairing Christopher Haase and Christopher Mies claimed the overall title, with a fourth place in the final race of the season at Zolder. In the Teams Championship, Hexis Racing were champions ahead of Matech Racing and Team Rosberg.
Fatma Nur Serter (born 10 November 1948) is a female Turkish academic and politician.

Deskaheh (March 15, 1873 – June 27, 1925), born Levi General, was a Cayuga hereditary chief and statesman of the Six Nations of the Grand River. As a prominent Indigenous rights advocate, he petitioned the League of Nations in the 1920s to challenge Canadian policies, seeking international recognition of Haudenosaunee sovereignty. His efforts, though unsuccessful, made him a key figure in Indigenous resistance.

Ultraman Powered is a 1994 fighting game developed by Tose and published by Bandai for the 3DO Interactive Multiplayer. Based on the 1993 series *Ultraman: The Ultimate Hero*, the game features one-on-one battles where players control Ultraman or various monsters, using punches, kicks, and special attacks powered by a replenishing energy meter. The game includes single-player modes with FMV cutscenes and rail shooter segments, as well as a multiplayer versus mode.  

Released exclusively in Japan as a 3DO launch title, *Ultraman Powered* received mixed reviews. Critics praised its digitized graphics and zoom-in combat mechanics, comparing them favorably to *Art of Fighting*, but criticized its slow gameplay and clunky controls. While some reviewers found it enjoyable for fans of the franchise, others considered it too sluggish for serious fighting game enthusiasts.

Eumenes fraternus is a species of potter wasp in the subfamily Eumeninae of the family Vespidae. It is native to the eastern United States and Canada. The female builds a miniature pot out of mud in which it lays an egg and places a live caterpillar. Its developing larva feeds on this whereas the adult wasp feeds primarily on nectar.

The Rodeo–Chediski Fire was a wildfire that burned in east-central Arizona beginning on June 18, 2002, and was not controlled until July 7. It was the worst forest fire in Arizona's recorded history until June 14, 2011 when the Wallow Fire surpassed it. Several local communities, including Show Low, Pinetop-Lakeside, Heber-Overgaard Clay Springs and Pinedale, were threatened and had to be evacuated.
Michel Deguy (1930–2022) was a French poet, translator, and literary editor.

"D.A.N.C.E." is the second single by Justice and the first from their album †. It includes edited and extended versions of "D.A.N.C.E", a rougher mix in the style of their earlier releases, "B.E.A.T", and the track "Phantom" which was previously issued in limited quantities twice on 12"vinyl preceding the release of"D.A.N.C.E.".

Atlantic Coast Line 1504 is a preserved USRA Light Pacific steam locomotive built in 1919 by the American Locomotive Company (ALCO) for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad (ACL). Originally numbered 497, it was renumbered 1504 in 1920 and primarily hauled premier passenger trains like the *Florida Special* and *Dixie Flyer* before being reassigned to freight service in the late 1940s. Retired in 1952, it was preserved in Jacksonville, Florida, becoming the only surviving USRA Light Pacific in near-original condition. Designated a National Historic Mechanical Engineering Landmark in 1990, it underwent cosmetic restoration in 2015. In 2021, it was acquired by U.S. Sugar Corporation for restoration to operational condition on the Sugar Express tourist train in Clewiston, Florida. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2018.

Adriaan van Dis (Bergen aan Zee, 16 December 1946) is a Dutch author. He debuted in 1983 with the novella Nathan Sid. In 1995 his book Indische Duinen (My Father's War), which in its narrative is a follow up to his debut novella, was also awarded several prestigious literary awards.

He is also known as the host of his own award-winning television talkshow named Hier is. Adriaan van Dis, that lasted from 1983 to 1992 and several successful award-winning television documentaries.

With the publication of his Indies inspired compilation book De Indie boeken (The Indies books) in 2012, van Dis establishes himself as one of the most significant second generation authors of Dutch Indies literature.

Petro Pysarchuk (born June 6, 1955) is a Ukrainian politician and entrepreneur. A member of the Party of Regions, he served in the Verkhovna Rada (Ukraine's parliament) for three consecutive convocations (2002–2012). He also founded businesses, including Pivdennyj Market, a major trade complex in Lviv.

Angela Jackson is a collegiate women's basketball coach. She coached the Chicago State University women's basketball team, the Cougars, for 15 years. Jackson led them to win the 2010-2011 Great West Conference Championships and to the Women's Basketball Invitational Final Four, for which she was named Coach of the Year. Among the coaches of Chicago State athletic teams, she has the strongest career record, 123 wins. She coached several 1,000 point scorers, including six players who went on to professional contracts.

The Gao Empire preceded the Songhai Empire in the region of the Middle Niger. It owes its name to the town of Gao located at the eastern Niger bend. In the ninth century CE, it was considered to be the most powerful West African kingdom.

Gao was founded in the 7th century. Because of its placement along the Niger River, it was a profitable location for fisherman to settle. Gao is one of the oldest trading centers in western Africa. Because of the placement of Gao, Gao became the capital for the Songhai (or Songhay) Empire in the early 11th century. Gao was able to flourish as the capital of the Songhai Empire, most notably via the trans-Saharan trade of gold, copper, slaves, and salt. Gao was annexed by the rulers of the kingdom of Mali in 1325, but Songhai would eventually retain control of it 40 years later.

There are no surviving indigenous written records that date from before the middle of the 17th century. Our knowledge of the early history of the town relies on the writings of external Arabic geographers living in Morocco, Egypt and Andalusia, who never visited the region.

*Zhenskii vestnik* (Russian: Женский вестник, *Women's Herald*) was a feminist monthly magazine published in Saint Petersburg from 1904 to 1917. Founded by Mariia Pokrovskaia, it promoted women's independence and served as the Union of Women's official organ until 1907. The magazine ceased publication after the Bolshevik Revolution.

A quick coupler is a device used on construction and earth-moving machinery, such as excavators and loaders, to rapidly switch between attachments like buckets or tools. They come in manual, semi-automatic, and automatic types, with safety concerns prompting regulatory scrutiny in some countries. International standards are being developed to address risks, including accidental detachment. The market includes various manufacturers, with some designs requiring specialized attachments while others are more universal.

Brown Boy 2, also known as Good Intentions (Brown Boy 2 Deluxe Version), is a reissued studio album by Canadian rapper Nav. It was released on May 11, 2020, as the deluxe edition of his third studio album, Good Intentions, which was released three days prior, on May 8, 2020. The album also serves as the sequel to Nav's unofficial 2019 extended play, Brown Boy, that his manager Cash released on Audiomack to help Nav come out of retirement at the time. It features additional guest appearances from Quavo and Lil Duke. The original album features guest appearances from Young Thug, Future, Gunna, Travis Scott, Lil Uzi Vert, the late Pop Smoke, Don Toliver, and Lil Durk.

The deluxe edition contributed to Good Intentions debuting at number one on the Billboard 200. The relatively short span of time between the release dates of the re-issue and the standard edition of Good Intentions was a subject of controversy, with journalists viewing the back-to-back releases as being a form of chart manipulation.

Islesboro is a town in Waldo County, Maine, United States, comprising Islesboro Island and several smaller islands. The population was 583 at the 2020 census. It has a summer colony accessible by state ferry service from Lincolnville Beach three miles to the west, by private boat, or by air taxi service. Home to Warren Island State Park, Islesboro includes the village of Dark Harbor.

Raymond Cannon (September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, screenwriter, and director in silent films. He later became a sport fishing author and columnist, writing *How to Fish the Pacific Coast* (1952) and *The Sea of Cortez* (1965).

Grange Barn is a historic timber-framed building in Coggeshall, Essex, England. Grange Barn was built by the Cistercians in the 13th century to serve Coggeshall Abbey. It underwent significant structural alteration in the 14th century. It is Grade I listed.

The barn is 36.57 metres long, 13.71 metres wide and 10.67 metres high. For comparison, England's largest medieval barn, Harmondsworth Great Barn, is 58.55 metres long, 11.3 metres wide, and 11.9 metres high. The barn has a nave and two aisles. There are two midstreys (gabled porches) which would have provided access for wagons: these are 18th century.

*Eumenes fraternus* is a species of potter wasp native to the eastern United States and Canada. It is black with ivory markings and measures 15–20 mm (0.6–0.8 in) in length. Females construct mud nests, provisioning them with paralyzed caterpillars for their larvae. Adults feed on nectar and pollen. The species inhabits glades, shrublands, and forest edges, active from spring to late fall.

Marley Sianto Sikawa (born c. 1990), is a Kenyan communications and marketing professional, who serves as the tourism marketing coordinator for Narok County, one of the 47 administrative Kenyan counties.

Ilias Haddad (born 1 March 1989) is a Dutch-Moroccan professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Union Touarga. He has previously played for clubs in the Netherlands, Scotland, Bulgaria, and Morocco.

Urbanus is a Flemish celebrity comic strip created by Willy Linthout and loosely based on Flemish comedian and singer Urbanus. The stories are written by Willy Linthout, Urbanus and Ann Smets, and drawn by Linthout. The first story was published in 1982 and is still in syndication as of this day, being the longest-running and most successful Flemish comic strip based on a celebrity. It is also the longest-running celebrity comic in the world made by the same writer/artist team. The series sells well in the Netherlands too, due to Urbanus' popularity there.

Day of Defeat is a multiplayer first-person shooter developed by Valve and released in 2003. Set in World War II, the game focuses on squad-based combat between the Allies (United States and Great Britain) and the Axis (Germany). Players engage in objective-based battles across historically inspired maps, using authentic WWII-era weapons with realistic mechanics like recoil and limited sprinting. Originally a Half-Life mod, it was later acquired by Valve and released as a standalone game with improved graphics and new maps. The game received positive reviews for its teamwork-driven gameplay but was criticized for its dated visuals. A Source engine remake, *Day of Defeat: Source*, was released in 2005.
Saint-Denis–Porte de Paris is a Paris Métro station in Saint-Denis, France, serving Line 13 and Tramway Line 8, located near the Stade de France.

Contour Crafting is a large-scale 3D printing construction technology developed by Behrokh Khoshnevis. Funded in part by Caterpillar Inc. and later NASA, the method aims to automate building construction, reducing waste and environmental impact. Proponents claim it could construct a house in a single day. NASA has explored its potential for building lunar and Martian structures using mostly local materials. In 2017, the Contour Crafting Corporation partnered with Doka Ventures to commercialize the technology, with plans to deliver the first printers in 2018. The technique has been featured in media, including the *Science Channel*, for its potential to revolutionize construction.
Desiderio Macías Silva (March 31, 1922-February 1995) was a Mexican poet and medical doctor born in Asientos, Aguascalientes. He served as President of the Autonomous University of Aguascalientes.

L.A. Law: The Movie (also known as *L.A. Law: Return to Justice*) is a 2002 American television reunion film based on the legal drama series *L.A. Law*. Directed by Michael Schultz and written by William M. Finkelstein, the film follows former partners of the law firm McKenzie Brackman as they reunite to handle new cases, including a death row appeal and personal legal battles. It aired on NBC on May 12, 2002, featuring returning cast members from the original series.

The German tanker *Spichern* was a Norwegian-built oil tanker launched in 1935 as *Krossfonn*. Seized by Germany in 1940, she was renamed *Spichern* and served as a naval oiler, notably refueling the cruiser *Prinz Eugen* during World War II. Scuttled in 1944, she was later salvaged, rebuilt, and returned to merchant service as *Ringfjell* (1949) and *Ringsaker* (1961) before being scrapped in 1964.
Roberto Juan Poljak (17 September 1932, Buenos Aires; – 30 May 2019, Baltimore County) was an Argentine biophysicist and immunologist.

Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1325–1340) was a medieval cartographer believed to be of Ligurian origin who later worked in Majorca. He is known for influential portolan charts, including the 1325 (revised to 1330) Dalorto map, which bridges Genoese and Majorcan cartographic styles, and the 1339 Dulcert map, considered foundational to the Majorcan school. His works introduced new geographic details, such as the first depiction of the mythical island of Brasil and early representations of the Canary and Madeira islands. His identity remains debated among scholars.
Hamiota Municipality is a rural municipality in Manitoba, Canada, formed in 2015 through amalgamation.
Ilyas Cherchar (born January 18, 1992) is an Algerian professional footballer who plays as a defender for RC Arbaâ.
The Roland RE-201, commonly known as the Space Echo, is an audio effects unit that produces delay and reverb effects. It was produced by the Roland Corporation from 1974 to 1990.

The Henschel Hs 126 was a German reconnaissance and observation aircraft used by the Luftwaffe during World War II. Introduced in 1938 after evaluation in the Spanish Civil War, it served as a key short-range reconnaissance plane in early campaigns, including the invasions of Poland and the Soviet Union. Though largely replaced by the Focke-Wulf Fw 189 by 1942, it remained in secondary roles such as glider tug and night ground attack. The Hs 126 was also used by Greece's Royal Hellenic Air Force during the Greco-Italian War. Production ended in 1941, and it was retired from frontline service the following year.

Paris Gregory is a Byzantine illuminated manuscript of the *Homilies of Gregory of Nazianzus*, commissioned by Patriarch Photios I as a gift for Emperor Basil I. Celebrating the triumph of Orthodoxy over Iconoclasm, it features elaborate illustrations and symbolic imagery linking Gregory’s 4th-century sermons to contemporary theological debates. The manuscript, notable for its artistic sophistication, reflects Photios’s efforts to reinforce Orthodox tradition under Basil’s rule.

Courtney Walsh (born 30 October 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer who played as a right-arm fast bowler for the West Indies from 1984 to 2001. One of the greatest fast bowlers in cricket history, he was the first to take 500 Test wickets, finishing his career with 519—a record at the time. Known for his accuracy, endurance, and sportsmanship, Walsh formed a feared bowling partnership with Curtly Ambrose.  

Walsh made his Test debut in 1984 and played 132 Tests, taking 22 five-wicket hauls. He also held the record for most Test ducks (43) while being a notoriously poor batsman. His best ODI performance was 5/1 against Sri Lanka in 1986. After retiring, he became a coach, serving as bowling coach for Bangladesh and later as head coach of the West Indies women's team. Inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010, Walsh remains one of Jamaica's most celebrated cricketers.

The Charleston Dirty Birds are a professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia, playing in the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball. Founded in 1987 as the Charleston Wheelers, the team has undergone multiple name changes, including the Alley Cats (1995–2004) and West Virginia Power (2005–2020). They play at GoMart Ballpark and are owned by Andy Shea. The franchise has previously been affiliated with various MLB teams and won the South Atlantic League championship in 1990.

Paul Brinegar (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American actor best known for his roles in Western films and television, particularly as Wishbone in *Rawhide* (1959–1966) and Mayor "Dog" Kelley in *The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp*. He appeared in over 100 films and TV shows.

Ernest Carlos Camacho (born February 1, 1955) is a former professional baseball player who pitched in the major leagues from 1980 to 1981 and 1983 to 1990. His best season came with the 1984 Cleveland Indians, when he led the team with 23 saves.

Freeze Frame is the third album by Godley & Creme. The album was recorded at Nigel Gray's Surrey Sound Studios, Leatherhead, Surrey. The cover art, designed by Hipgnosis, identifies the duo as'Godley Creme'.

Dan Dotson (born November 23, 1962) is an American auctioneer who has been in the auctioneering business since 1974. Dan and his wife, Laura Dotson, run American Auctioneers, a full service auction company in Riverside, California. Dan is best known for being the auctioneer on A&E Network's Storage Wars. In 2018, he auctioned off a unit containing $7.5 million in cash in a safe.
The Sehol A5 is a compact liftback produced by JAC Motors since 2019. It features a 1.5T engine and has an electric variant, the JAC iC5. The car is also sold internationally as the JAC J7.

Fadi Sawan (Arabic: فادي صوّان, born in 1960) is a Lebanese military investigative judge, who became known after being assigned with the immense task of investigating the 2020 Beirut Port explosion, one of the largest non-nuclear blasts in history, the 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate that killed more than 200 people, wounded over 6,000 and destroyed much of Beirut, the capital of Lebanon, on August 4, 2020.

In a country where political influence over the judiciary is a problem, which is part of corruption in Lebanon, he was known for his honesty and integrity and for not receiving instructions from politicians, thus many doubted that he could accomplish his task. As a result, in February 2021, he was removed from the probe, which will further delay the investigation. This in turn sparked a new wave of demonstrations, as part of the 2019–2021 Lebanese Revolution, with the participation of the families of the victims. The move was widely condemned, as being planned by the Lebanese authorities, who were under of extensive pressure to provide answers for the crime, which have not yet seen the light six months after the explosion.
Brad Davis (born 30 April 1982) is a rugby league footballer who previously played for the Gold Coast Titans in the National Rugby League. He played as a half-back.

Yann Weymouth is a St. Petersburg, Florida-based architect and the designer of the Salvador Dalí Museum. Early in his career, he served as chief of design for I. M. Pei on the Grand Louvre Project in Paris.

The qualifying competitions for the 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship were handled by two regional of CONCACAF's bodies; the Caribbean Football Union (Caribbean zone) and the Central American Football Union (Central American zone).

Representative teams from Canada, Mexico and the United States automatically qualified for the final competition.

Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve is a 54.5 square kilometres (21.0 sq mi) protected area, on the fringe of Namadgi National Park. Tidbinbilla is a short drive from the capital city of Australia, Canberra, in the Australian Capital Territory.

The nature reserve consists of a large valley floor, the Tidbinbilla Mountain and the Gibraltar range. The sides of the valley are steep and relatively undisturbed. The lower slopes of the valley are partly cleared and have a significant history of Aboriginal and European use. Tidbinbilla Mountain is believed to have been used for Aboriginal initiation ceremonies. The word 'Tidbinbilla' is Aboriginal in origin and comes from the word Jedbinbilla – a place where boys become men.

Known sites of Aboriginal significance at Tidbinbilla include the Birriagi Rock Shelter, which is the oldest Aboriginal site within the Australian Capital Territory. Bogong Rocks is a shelter where the oldest evidence of Aboriginal occupation was found at a bogong moth resting site.

The nature reserve is classified as an IUCN Category II protected area. It is the traditional Country of the Ngunnawal people.

Basharat Mirbabayeva (5 December 1916 – 20 March 2010) was an Uzbek railway worker and pioneering aviator. She became the first Uzbek woman to parachute from an airplane (1935) and later led a women's locomotive brigade during World War II. Recognized for her contributions, she was awarded the Medal "For Labour Valour" and named an Honorary Railway Worker of the USSR.

Marco Pinotti (born 25 February 1976 in Osio Sotto, Lombardy) is an Italian former road racing cyclist, who competed as a professional between 1999 and 2013. An individual time trial specialist, Pinotti was a six-time Italian Time Trial Champion (2005, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010 and 2013).

Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American transportation advocate and former elected official. A retired U.S. Navy officer, he served on the Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors (1975–1995) and chaired multiple regional transit agencies. Known as the "father of modern transit" in Silicon Valley, he led efforts to establish light rail, CalTrain, and the renaming of San Jose's Diridon Station in his honor. He later chaired the California High-Speed Rail Authority (2001–2003) and co-founded the Mineta Transportation Institute, where he served as executive director (1993–2014). A longtime civic leader, he has also been active in environmental, educational, and philanthropic organizations.

The Open Siddur Project (Lang-he, IPA: pʁojeqt hassidduʁ hapatuaħ) is an open-source, web-to-print publishing and digital humanities project intent on sharing the semantic data of Jewish liturgy and liturgy-related work with free-culture compatible copyright licenses and Public Domain dedications. The project collaborates with other efforts in open-source Judaism in sharing content and code, advocates among related user-generated content projects to adopt Open Content licensing, and solicits copyright owners of related liturgical materials to share their work under free-culture compatible terms.

Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British vehicle manufacturer founded in 1912, originally as a subsidiary of the London General Omnibus Company. It became a major producer of buses, coaches, and lorries, including military vehicles during both World Wars. Known for models like the Regent bus and Matador artillery tractor, AEC merged into Associated Commercial Vehicles (ACV) in 1948 and was later acquired by Leyland Motors in 1962. Production ceased in 1979 after British Leyland closed its Southall factory.

Michael Kevin O'Loughlin (born 20 February 1977) is a former professional Australian rules footballer, who played his entire Australian Football League career with the Sydney Swans.

O'Loughlin was named a member of the Indigenous Team of the Century. He was the third player with Indigenous heritage to play 300 AFL games. He twice achieved All-Australian selection, played for Australia twice in the International Rules Series, and was a Fos Williams Medallist as best player for South Australia in State of Origin. O'Loughlin was the first Sydney Swans player to play more than 300 career games. In 303 games he kicked 521 career goals.
The Time is the second album and first full-length studio album of Chinese singer-songwriter Xu Weizhou. It is divided into four parts.

Password synchronization is a method of managing access to multiple IT systems by ensuring that a user's password is identical across all platforms. This approach simplifies authentication for users, reducing the likelihood of forgotten passwords and insecure practices like writing them down. By requiring fewer passwords, organizations can enforce stronger password policies while decreasing reliance on IT support for password resets.  

However, password synchronization poses security risks: if a single password is compromised (through guessing, interception, or social engineering), all linked systems become vulnerable. Some synchronization systems transfer password hashes between systems with matching encryption, though most require plaintext access. Unlike single sign-on (SSO) or password vaults, which store unique credentials behind a master password, synchronization maintains uniformity across systems, making it similarly susceptible to widespread breaches if the master password is exposed.  

Password changes can be triggered by user actions, administrative updates, or centralized software interfaces. Vendors like Hitachi ID Systems provide solutions to automate this process.
Oberhünigen is a municipality in the Bern-Mittelland administrative district in the canton of Bern in Switzerland.

Wrocław Global Forum (WGF) is an annual transatlantic conference centered on topics related to democracy, prosperity and security. Launched in 2010, the event is held in Wrocław, Poland. It is organized by the Atlantic Council, the Polish Institute of International Affairs and the city of Wrocław. The most recent Forum took place on June 2–3, 2016.

The Henschel Hs 126 was a German two-seat reconnaissance and observation aircraft of World War II that was derived from the Henschel Hs 122. The pilot was seated in a protected cockpit under the parasol wing and the gunner in an open rear cockpit. The prototype aircraft frame was that of a Hs 122A fitted with a Junkers engine. The Hs 126 was well received for its good short takeoff and low-speed characteristics which were needed at the time. It was put into service for a few years, but was soon superseded by the general-purpose, STOL Fieseler Fi 156 Storch and the medium-range Focke-Wulf Fw 189 "flying eye".

Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is an English singer and presenter. She was briefly part of the original lineup of the Spice Girls (then called Touch) before leaving in 1994. She later worked as a TV presenter and session singer, recording backing vocals for artists like Ricky Martin and releasing her own EP in 2003.

A mobile (UK: /ˈmoʊbaɪl/,US: /ˈmoʊbiːl/) is a type of kinetic sculpture constructed to take advantage of the principle of equilibrium. It consists of a number of rods, from which weighted objects or further rods hang. The objects hanging from the rods balance each other, so that the rods remain more or less horizontal. Each rod hangs from only one string, which gives it the freedom to rotate about the string. An ensemble of these balanced parts hang freely in space, by design without coming into contact with each other.

Mobiles are popular in the nursery, where they hang over cribs to give infants entertainment and visual stimulation. Mobiles have inspired many composers, including Morton Feldman and Earle Brown who were inspired by Alexander Calder's mobiles to create mobile-like indeterminate pieces. John Cage wrote the music for the short film Works of Calder that focused on Calder's mobiles. Frank Zappa stated that his compositions employ a principle of balance similar to Calder mobiles.

Hambleden Lock is a lock with a long weir situated on the River Thames in England, about 2 miles downstream of Henley Bridge. The lock is on the Berkshire bank between Aston and Remenham. Built by the Thames Navigation Commission in 1773, the lock is named after the village of Hambleden, a mile (1.5 km) to the north.

The great weir is impressive and there are walkways over it from the lock to the small village of Mill End on the Buckinghamshire bank. Here is situated the picturesque Hambleden Mill, and the site of a Roman villa is nearby.

Joseph Hamilton (August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer. He was best known as one half of the tag team The Assassins where he was called "Assassin #1". When the Assassins ended, He wrestled as "The Assassin" and "The Flame" both while wearing a mask.

Hamilton teamed up with Tom Renesto in late 1961 to form the masked heel (bad guy) team known as "The Assassins" and, for over a decade, he and Renesto wrestled all over the world with great success. Once Renesto retired, Hamilton kept the Assassins team alive by partnering with wrestlers such as Randy Colley and "Hercules" Hernandez under the trademark gold and black masks of the Assassins.

In the late 1980s, Hamilton founded Deep South Wrestling (DSW), where he also wrestled until a back injury forced him to retire in 1988. After Deep South Wrestling closed, he worked extensively for World Championship Wrestling both on the booking team and later on as a trainer at WCW's Power Plant training facility. In 2005, he reopened Deep South Wrestling, this time as an affiliate of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), to train wrestlers the WWE had signed to developmental contracts. In 2007, WWE abruptly ended their contract with DSW and the promotion suspended operations later in the year.

Ching Cheung-ying is a Hong Kong educator and politician. A former Chinese Language and History teacher at Diocesan Boys' School, he opposed its transition to a fee-paying model. He has served on the Sha Tin District Council since 1985 and was awarded the Medal of Honour in 2007 for community service.

The Hoosiers are an English pop rock band formed in 2003 in Congresbury, England. Originally consisting of Irwin Sparkes (vocals/guitar), Alan Sharland (drums), and Martin Skarendahl (bass), the band gained recognition with their 2007 debut album *The Trick to Life*, featuring hits like "Goodbye Mr A." Known for their indie-pop sound, they released subsequent albums, including *The Illusion of Safety* (2010) and *The News from Nowhere* (2014), before transitioning to independent releases. After lineup changes, including Skarendahl's departure, the duo of Sparkes and Sharland continued, collaborating on projects like the 2021 charity single *"Route 66."* The band remains active, touring and releasing music independently.

Bernie Vince (born 2 October 1985) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Adelaide Crows (2006–2013) and Melbourne (2014–2018). A midfielder, he won Adelaide's best and fairest award in 2009 and Melbourne's in 2015. Vince also represented Australia in international rules football in 2011. Originally from Stansbury, South Australia, he was drafted from Woodville-West Torrens in the SANFL.

Horace Edward Dobbs (born 14 January 1933, in London), and commonly referred to as Horace Dobbs /ˈhɒrɪs dɒbz/ is a British scientist, researcher, author, and television producer, who is regarded as an expert on dolphins and their behaviour. In 1978, Dobbs sets up the International Dolphin Watch. He also founded the Oxford Underwater Research Group.

The Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic (Lang-ro, Moldovan Cyrillic: Република Аутономэ Советикэ Cочиалистэ Молдовеняскэ; Lang-uk), shortened to Moldavian ASSR, was an autonomous republic of the Ukrainian SSR between 12 October 1924 and 2 August 1940, encompassing the modern territory of Transnistria (today de jure in Moldova, but de facto functioning as an independent state; see Transnistria conflict) as well as much of the present-day Podilsk Raion of Ukraine. It was an artificial political creation inspired by the Bolshevik nationalities policy in the context of the loss of larger Bessarabia to Romania in April 1918. In such a manner, the Bolshevik leadership tried to radicalize pro-Soviet feelings in Bessarabia with a goal to return it in the presence of favorable conditions and creation of geopolitical "place d'armes" (bridgehead) to execute a breakthrough in the Balkan direction by projecting influence upon Romanian Bessarabia, which was eventually occupied and annexed in 1940 after the signing of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact.

Polytrauma refers to multiple severe injuries sustained simultaneously, often from high-impact events like car crashes or military combat. In civilian medicine, it commonly results from motor vehicle accidents, requiring immediate trauma assessments (such as x-rays or CT scans) to identify life-threatening conditions. In military settings, blast injuries from explosives frequently cause polytrauma, including traumatic brain injuries, limb loss, and fractures. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs operates specialized polytrauma rehabilitation centers to treat affected veterans, focusing on long-term recovery. Due to its complexity, polytrauma demands intensive, multidisciplinary care, often spanning months or years.

Fort Cumberland (built 1754) was an 18th-century frontier fort at the current site of Cumberland, Maryland, USA. It was an important military and economic center during the French and Indian War (1754–63) and figured significantly in the early career of George Washington.

Cuck is a 2019 American thriller film directed by Rob Lambert from a screenplay by Lambert and Joe Varkle. It stars Zachary Ray Sherman, Timothy V. Murphy, Sally Kirkland and Monique Parent.

The film had its world premiere at the Cleveland International Film Festival on March 28, 2019 and it was released on October 4, 2019.

Swing Kids is a 1993 American drama film directed by Thomas Carter and starring Christian Bale, Robert Sean Leonard and Frank Whaley. In pre-World War II Germany, two high school students attempt to be swing kids by night and Hitler Youth by day, a decision that acutely impacts their friends and families.

Zohra Begum Kazi (15 October 1912 – 7 November 2007) was a Bangladeshi physician and social worker. A pioneer in women's healthcare, she was the first female Muslim gynecologist in Bangladesh and a professor at Dhaka Medical College. She received the Ekushey Padak posthumously for her contributions to medicine and humanitarian work.

Petro Pysarchuk (Lang-uk, born June 6, 1955, in Chemeryntsi, village, Peremyshliany Raion, Lviv Oblast, Ukrainian SSR) is a Ukrainian Politician and Entrepreneur. People's Deputy to the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine 4th, 5th and 6th convocations.

Yankee Dutch crossing is the name of a dance performed in a 4-by-4 square formation of 8 couples (16 dancers). The original version, called simply Dutch Crossing, was choreographed in the English country dance style by Ernst van Brakel of the Netherlands in 1990. In 2004, contra dance caller Joseph Pimentel modified the choreography in the American style, sometimes called Yankee Dutch crossing to distinguish it from the original. Any number of squares can perform the dance simultaneously. It is not normally found at monthly, bi-monthly or weekly dances. The most likely venues are multi-day dance weekends or weeks, where it is taught in sessions of one hour or longer.

The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships is a racewalking event organised by the International Association of Athletics Federations. It has been held since 1961, and generally on a biennial basis. Women first entered the 1979 edition. It was formerly known as the Lugano Cup after the city that hosted the first event, then became the IAAF World Race Walking Cup until 2016 and then IAAF World Race Walking Team Championships until 2018. In 2004, a junior division was added, consisting of men and women aged under 20. Since 2008 it has been a constituent meeting of the World Athletics Challenge – Race Walking.

From 1975 to 1997 was awarded Lugano Trophy for combined team (20K + 50K). Since 1993 the medals have been awarded for the single events of the 20K and 50K teams, therefore in the 1993, 1995 and 1997 editions three team medals were assigned, from 1999 the combined was abolished and the team medals remained two until the present day.

Ninnes is a locality at the northeastern corner of Yorke Peninsula and western side of the Mid North of South Australia. It lies where the Upper Yorke Road from Kulpara to Bute is crossed by the road from Paskeville to Lochiel. The dominant industry is broadacre grain and sheep farming.

Princess Jane Mpologoma Nabanakulya is a daughter of Uganda's first President Mutesa II of Buganda. She was born in Sunga Village, Buyaga County, Bunyoro-Kitara Kingdom, on April 12, 1964 by Omuzaana Naome Nanyonga, a midwife of Major General Sir Mutesa II of Buganda.

John A. Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was an American Republican politician who served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1957–1961) and the Pennsylvania House of Representatives (1950–1957). He later led the American Kennel Club (1971–1979).

Lilian Staple Mead (fl. 1882–1900) was an Australian writer, suffragist, and educator. A signatory to the 1894 Women's Suffrage Petition, she advocated for women's rights and authored children's books, including *A Brother's Need* and *Patsie's Bricks*.

Rhizomatic learning is a variety of pedagogical practices informed by the work of Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari. Explored initially as an application of post-structural thought to education, it has more recently been identified as methodology for net-enabled education. In contrast to goal-directed and hierarchical theories of learning, it posits that learning is most effective when it allows participants to react to evolving circumstances, preserving lines of flight that allow a fluid and continually evolving redefinition of the task at hand. In such a structure, "the community is the curriculum", subverting traditional notions of instructional design where objectives pre-exist student involvement.
116 is a natural number following 115 and preceding 117. It is an even composite number with divisors 1, 2, 4, 29, 58, and 116.

Railroad brotherhoods were labor unions representing railroad workers in the U.S. from the late 19th to early 20th centuries. Key organizations included the Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and the Brotherhood of Railroad Trainmen. They focused on insurance, seniority rules, and wages, securing gains like the Adamson Act (1916). Though influential, they faced setbacks, including the failed 1919 strike and the 1922 shopmen's strike.

Basugaon (IPA:ˈbɑːsʊˌgɑ̃ʊ) is a Town in Chirang District of Assam, India. The name of this town is derived from its high bamboo plantation and bamboo market basically "basu" or "bash" means bamboo and "gaon" means village that is Basugaon, 'The village of bamboo'. It is an industrial town and a Municipal Board area. It is a neighbourhood of Bongaigaon UA, and it is situated approximately 180 km from Guwahati City in the western part of the Lower Assam Region.

Yamamiya Sengen-jinja (山宮浅間神社) is a shrine in Fujinomiya, Shizuoka, Japan. The shrine is unique as it lacks a honden and instead only has an altar.

In 2013 the shrine was inscribed as part of the World Heritage Site "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration".

Klinga Church (Norwegian: *Klinga kirke*) is a historic Evangelical Lutheran parish church in Namsos, Trøndelag, Norway. Originally built as a stave church in the 14th century, it was relocated and reconstructed in 1866 as a wooden long church designed by Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church, with a capacity of 270, replaced an earlier structure at Sævik. During World War II, its tower was dismantled by German forces and later rebuilt in 1948. It remains an active church and a listed cultural heritage site.

GRRR! is a greatest hits album by the Rolling Stones, released on 12 November 2012 to commemorate the band's 50th anniversary. Spanning their career from 1963 to 2012, it includes tracks from their early years as well as two new songs, "Doom and Gloom" and "One More Shot." The album was released in multiple formats, including 2-CD, 3-CD, and 4-CD editions. The cover artwork, created by Walton Ford, features a gorilla with the band's iconic tongue logo, symbolizing their enduring legacy. Produced by various collaborators, *GRRR!* highlights the Rolling Stones' influence across five decades of rock music.
Carola Richards (1916–2004) was an English artist and member of the Eastbourne Group.

USCGC Robert Yered (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter based in Miami, Florida. She was launched on November 23, 2012, and was commissioned on February 15, 2012. Debbie Wasserman Schultz, the Congressional Representative for the district containing the vessel's base, met the ship when she arrived in Miami on January 27, 2013.

Paschimottanasana (Sanskrit: पश्चिमोत्तानासन) is a seated forward-bending yoga pose. Its name derives from Sanskrit words meaning "west," "intense stretch," and "posture." Described in the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika*, it involves stretching the back while reaching for the feet. Variations include Janusirsasana and Upavishthakonasana.

The 9th Marine Regiment was an infantry regiment of the United States Marine Corps. Formed during World War II, it served until 1994, when it was deactivated during the post Cold War drawdown. Battalions of the Ninth Marine Regiment, but not the regiment itself, were reactivated from 2007 to 2014 as part of the Marine Corps' growth during the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. The battalions have subsequently been deactivated again.

Lau is a populated area, a socken (not to be confused with parish), on the Swedish island of Gotland. It comprises the same area as the administrative Lau District, established on 1 January 2016. Originally an island, it is now part of the main Gotland island due to the isostasy. It is mostly known for the good water from the spring Lau Käldu.

Donia Jessop (born 1970) is an American politician who serves as the current mayor of Hildale, Utah. Upon taking office, Jessop became the city’s first female mayor. For decades before Jessop's administration, Hildale was controlled by the Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints (FLDS Church). Jessop is Hildale's first mayor not endorsed by the FLDS Church.

"Flash" (stylized as "FLASH") is a single by Perfume, released on March 16, 2016. It is the fifth single from their fifth studio album Cosmic Explorer. The single reached first place in the real-time ranking of iTunes with 200,000+ downloads. It is also the band's highest selling single since "Love the World".

The single was used as the theme song for the live action adapted film Chihayafuru.
Zohra Begum Kazi (15 October 1912 – 7 November 2007) was the first Bengali Muslim female physician. She was awarded Tamgha-e-Pakistan (1964), Begum Rokeya Padak (2002) and Ekushey Padak (2008).

Ko.Si. Mani (13 September 1929 – 2 December 2016) was an Indian politician who was the minister for co-operation, statistics and ex-servicemen in the Tamil Nadu state of India between 2006 and 2011 Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK) regime. He was instrumental in developing kumbakonam fisheries market and darasuram vegetable market.

Sam Walker (born 16 June 2002) is a professional rugby league footballer who plays as a halfback & five-eighth for the Sydney Roosters in the NRL.

He has played for the Prime Minister's XIII at representative level.

Barasat Junction railway station is the primary rail hub in the city of Barasat It is one of the major suburban city railway stations in the district of North 24 Parganas. Generally, the people commuting daily use this station for travelling to Kolkata's Central Business District or other towns, with the busiest times at the station being the morning and evening peak periods. This station provides the facility of separate rest rooms for both male & female passengers. Barasat Junction Railway station consists of 6 permanent platforms (Platform no: 1–6).

Recently, the government-owned Indian Railways corporation approved a proposal for an underground metro railway station connecting Barasat Junction to Noapara metro station. The construction of the project has started in October 2011.

An EMU carshed is situated in the vicinity of Barasat Junction railway station.

Putte is a village in the Dutch province of North Brabant, located along the Belgian border. Part of the municipality of Woensdrecht, it has a population of 3,785 (2021). Historically divided by the 1648 Peace of Münster, Putte is known for its Jewish cemeteries and as the burial place of painter Jacob Jordaens.

"7empest" is a song by American rock band Tool, featured on their 2019 album *Fear Inoculum*. At 15 minutes and 43 seconds, it is the album's longest track, blending shifting time signatures, aggressive vocals, and intricate guitar work. Critics praised it as a standout, with *Loudwire* calling it guitarist Adam Jones' "greatest achievement." The song won the Grammy Award for Best Metal Performance in 2020. Its lyrics remain ambiguous, though some speculate a connection to Shakespeare’s *The Tempest*.

Hadith: A Re-evaluation is a book by Kassim Ahmad (or Ahmed). In it he writes that the hadith books in Shia and Sunni Islam were introduced without any authoritative basis. It claims that the hadith books were not endorsed by Muhammad, nor by the first four caliphs, nor by the Quran. The book makes the case for Quranism, a minority sect in Islam.

TransPeshawar is a bus rapid transit (BRT) system in Peshawar, Pakistan, operational since August 2020. It consists of a 26.5 km east–west corridor with 32 stations, featuring hybrid buses that operate on dedicated lanes. The system, developed with support from the Asian Development Bank, includes elevated sections, underpasses, and at-grade segments. TransPeshawar serves over 250,000 daily passengers with frequent service intervals and integrates feeder routes for wider connectivity. Despite criticism over costs and construction delays, it remains a key urban transport initiative in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is located in the Grand Palace in Bangkok Thailand. The museum replaced the 1873 Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building (Thai: Hor Ratsadakorn Pipat) of the Royal Treasury Ministry by a request of Queen Sirikit in 2003. The building was the original organization of the current Crown Property Bureau.

Might and Magic: Heroes Kingdoms (originally Heroes of Might and Magic Kingdoms) was a real-time strategy massively multiplayer online game based on the Heroes of Might and Magic series. The game was announced in 2007 with closed betas running until 2009. It was released in France on 3 November 2009. It closed its servers on 1 September 2014.

Pridwen is the legendary shield of King Arthur, first appearing in Geoffrey of Monmouth's 12th-century *Historia Regum Britanniae*. Its name, possibly meaning "white face" or "blessed form," derives from Welsh tradition, where *Prydwen* was Arthur's ship. Geoffrey describes Pridwen as bearing an image of the Virgin Mary, a motif later expanded in medieval texts like Wace's *Roman de Brut* and Layamon's *Brut*, which depict the shield with intricate religious symbolism. The shield also appears in works such as *Sir Gawain and the Green Knight*, reinforcing its Arthurian legacy.

The Beach 90th Street station is an elevated New York City Subway station in Rockaway Beach, Queens, served by the S train. Originally opened in 1880 as a Long Island Rail Road station, it was converted to subway use in 1956. The station has two side platforms and serves as a transfer point for MTA buses.

The Lincoln Home National Historic Site in Springfield, Illinois, preserves the only home ever owned by Abraham Lincoln. Purchased in 1844, it was where he lived before becoming president. The site, established in 1971, includes the restored house and surrounding neighborhood, administered by the National Park Service.

Aussie Gold Hunters is an Australian factual television show which follows crews of gold prospectors in Australia. The series is produced by Electric Pictures and began on the Discovery Channel on 15 September 2016. The series has been a ratings success and gone on to air in 125 countries including the United Kingdom.

In August 2022 the series was renewed for a eighth and ninth season, with season eight to premiere in 2023. Season seven concluded on 13 October 2022.

John Ricus Couperus (24 February 1816 – 13 October 1902) was a Dutch colonial official, lawyer, and composer in the Dutch East Indies. Born into the Indo landed gentry, he served as a member of the Council of Justice in Padang and the High Military Court. He also wrote political pamphlets opposing agrarian reforms and composed music, including pieces like *Rêverie d’un Grandpère*. His son, Louis Couperus, became a renowned novelist.

Judith Arnold (born April 7, 1953) is an American romance novelist who has written over 85 books under various pen names, including Ariel Berk and Thea Frederick. A three-time *Romantic Times* award winner, she was also involved in a 2012 class-action lawsuit against Harlequin over e-book royalties, which settled in 2016. She lives near Boston.

Nutrisystem is a commercial weight-loss and nutrition company founded in 1972 by Harold Katz and headquartered in Fort Washington, Pennsylvania. It provides pre-packaged meals, weight-management programs, and counseling services via telephone, internet, and mobile platforms. Owned by Kainos Capital, Nutrisystem operates primarily in the U.S. and Canada. Studies suggest short-term weight-loss efficacy, though long-term effectiveness remains unproven. The program's cost—averaging $274.99 for four weeks—has been criticized as potentially prohibitive. Additionally, its structured meal plans may complicate social dining. The company trades on the Nasdaq under the symbol NTRI.

Rüdiger Sagel (born 9 August 1955 in Lünen) is a German politician currently with the Left Party and previously with the Alliance '90/The Greens. From 1998 until 2012 he was a member of the state parliament (Landtag) for North Rhine-Westphalia. On 30 June 2012 he and Gunhild Böth were elected in Münster as state spokespersons for the Left in North Rhine-Westphalia.
Terry George (born 14 March 1965) is a British entrepreneur and nightclub owner, known for his gay nightclubs in Leeds and appearances in television documentaries.

Bora–Witóto (also Bora–Huitoto, Bora–Uitoto, or, ambiguously, Witotoan) is a proposal to unite the Boran and Witotoan language families of southwestern Colombia (Amazonas Department) and neighboring regions of Peru and Brazil. Kaufman (1994) added the Andoque language.
Israel Freedman (April 11, 1878 – March 18, 1934) was a Jewish Romanian-American Yiddish journalist.
Ciudad del Plata is a city in San José Department of Uruguay.
Veniamin Lvovich Beylin (Lang-ru; 25 August 1904 – 16 March 1982) was a Red Army major general.
Carola Richards (1917–2004) was a British painter known for her vibrant, surreal, and abstract works.

Robert Žák (born 6 May 1966) is a Czech football manager and former player.

As a player, he played in the Czechoslovak First League until its conclusion in 1993, after which he continued in the Gambrinus liga for one season before finishing his professional playing career in 1994.

He has managed three clubs in the Gambrinus liga, firstly České Budějovice followed by Most and most recently Bohemians Prague.
FC Ingolstadt 04 II is the reserve team of German club FC Ingolstadt 04. Founded in 2004, it currently competes in the Bayernliga Süd (V) and plays at the Tuja-Stadion.

A fish flake is a platform built on poles and spread with boughs for drying cod on the foreshores of fishing villages and small coastal towns in Newfoundland and Nordic countries. Spelling variations for fish flake in Newfoundland include flek, fleyke, fleake, flaik and fleack. The term's first recorded use in connection with fishing appeared in Richard Whitbourne's book Newfoundland (1623, p. 57). In Norway, a flake is known as a hjell.

2011 MD is a bright micro-asteroid, classified as near-Earth object of the Apollo and Amor group, respectively. On 27 June 2011, at around 17:00 UTC (13:00 EDT), the object passed exceptionally close to Earth's surface at a distance of approximately 12,000 kilometers (7,500 mi), roughly the diameter of the Earth.

*The Forging of a Rebel* is a trilogy of autobiographical novels by Arturo Barea, chronicling his life in Spain from childhood through the Spanish Civil War. Comprising *La Forja*, *La Ruta*, and *La Llama*, it explores themes of social inequality, war, and exile. First published in English (1941–1946), it gained acclaim for its vivid realism and historical insight. A 1990 Spanish TV adaptation further popularized the work.

Charles Abbott (footballer)  

Charles Percy Abbott (born 10 June 1939) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for Hawthorn in the VFL from 1961 to 1963. A lawyer and polo administrator, he later served in corporate and philanthropic roles.

Bugando Medical Centre (BMC) is a tertiary care medical facility owned by the Episcopal Conference of the Catholic Bishops of Tanzania. The hospital is operated in collaboration with the Tanzania Ministry of Health and Social Welfare.
The Matt Lucas Awards is a British entertainment series hosted by comedian and television personality Matt Lucas.

"Her Strut" is a song written by Bob Seger that was first released on his 1980 album Against the Wind. It was also released as the B-side of his single "The Horizontal Bop." The song was inspired by feminist icon Jane Fonda.

A three-point turn is a driving maneuver used to reverse direction on a narrow road. It involves turning toward the opposite curb, reversing while turning back, and then driving forward to complete the turn. The name varies by region—called a "turnabout" in Ireland and "turning in the road" in the UK—and may require more than three movements in tight spaces.

In medicine, Murphy's sign (also known as Sweeney’s sign) is a maneuver during a physical examination as part of the abdominal examination. It is useful for differentiating pain in the right upper quadrant. Typically, it is positive in cholecystitis, but negative in choledocholithiasis, pyelonephritis, and ascending cholangitis.
The MAGI '900 Museum is a contemporary art museum in Italy, housed in a converted 1930s silo.
Two Paddocks is a wine producer based in Central Otago, New Zealand. It is owned and operated by actor Sam Neill.

An eruption column is a towering plume of volcanic ash, gases, and tephra ejected during an explosive volcanic eruption. Driven by convection, these columns can rise several kilometers into the atmosphere, with sustained or transient structures depending on eruption dynamics. Their height is influenced by factors such as vent size, magma gas content, and atmospheric conditions, reaching up to 45 km (28 mi) in extreme cases. Columns penetrating the stratosphere can disperse ash globally, affecting climate by cooling temperatures.  

Eruption columns pose hazards when they collapse, generating deadly pyroclastic flows. They also endanger aviation, as volcanic ash can damage aircraft engines and obscure visibility. Notable incidents, such as the 1982 Galunggung eruptions, led to engine failures in commercial flights. To mitigate risks, Volcanic Ash Advisory Centers monitor eruption columns using satellite and pilot reports.

Yossi Gross (Lang-he; born February 5, 1947) is an Israeli engineer, inventor and entrepreneur. He is a founding partner of Rainbow Medical, an operational investment company, established to launch companies based on the technological ideas and inventions of Gross. Yossi Gross first started his professional career in the 20th century as a project manager of the Lavi (IAI Lavi) program for the Israel Air Force. From the 1990s through to the beginning of the 21st century, Gross started 27 medical device companies based on his various inventions in electronics, signal processing, nanotechnology, drug delivery and neurostimulation. Gross's various companies have developed or are currently developing treatments for diabetes, gastroenterology, stroke, ophthalmology, asthma, congestive heart failure, and urology. Gross has 567 filed patents.

Gross is a conference speaker. He spoke at the American Israel Chamber meeting on the topic: Accelerating Medical Device Start-ups the Israeli Way and at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania
Declo is a city in Cassia County, Idaho, United States. It is part of the Burley, Idaho micropolitan area. The population was 343 at the time of the 2010 census, up from 338 in 2000.
Doug Chapman is an American stunt performer, stunt double, and actor, known for his work in films like *300* and *Watchmen*, where he performed the first bare-skin burn stunt in a feature film.

Cóndores UNAM was an American football team representing the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), active from 1970 to 1997. Competing in ONEFA, they won 10 national championships, dominating college football in Mexico under coach Diego García Miravete. The team was dissolved in 1998 when UNAM merged its football programs under the Pumas name.

Harmony, also known as Node 2, is the "utility hub" of the International Space Station. It connects the laboratory modules of the United States, Europe and Japan, as well as providing electrical power and electronic data. Sleeping cabins for four of the crew are housed here.

Harmony was successfully launched into space aboard Space Shuttle flight STS-120 on 23 October 2007. After temporarily being attached to the port side of the Unity module, it was moved to its permanent location on the forward end of the Destiny module on 14 November 2007. Harmony added 70 m3 (2,500 cu ft) to the station's living volume, an increase of almost 20%, from 420 m3 (15,000 cu ft) to 490 m3 (17,000 cu ft). Its successful installation meant that from NASA's perspective, the station was considered to be "U.S. Core Complete".

The *New York World* was a major American newspaper founded in 1860 and published until 1931.
