Under Joseph Pulitzer’s ownership (1883–1911), it became a leader in investigative journalism and sensational "yellow journalism," popularizing comic strips and pioneering color printing. Notable for its exposés, including Nellie Bly’s undercover reporting, the *World* also introduced the first crossword puzzle and published *The World Almanac*. After Pulitzer’s death, his sons ran the paper until its sale to Scripps-Howard, which shut it down in 1931. A digital revival, *The New York World*, launched in 2011 but ceased updates by 2016.

John Ricus Couperus (1816 – 1902) was a Dutch lawyer, member of the Council of Justice in Padang, member of the High Military Court of the Dutch East Indies and the landheer of Tjikopo. He was also the father of the Dutch writer Louis Couperus and knight in the Order of the Netherlands Lion.

Among the paintings attributed to Giovanni da Rimini (fl. 1292–1336) are two panels from a former diptych, dated to 1300–1305, of which the left wing is in the collection of the National Gallery, London, and the right that of the Galleria Nazionale d'Arte Antica, Palazzo Barberini, Rome.
Ä (lower case ä) is a character that represents either a letter from several extended Latin alphabets, or the letter A with an umlaut mark or diaeresis.

Hatton Castle is a 16th-century tower house near Newtyle, Angus, Scotland. Built in 1575 by Laurence, 4th Lord Oliphant, it features an unusual scale-and-platt staircase and a historic Great Hall with exceptional acoustics. Originally part of the Oliphant family estates, it was abandoned in the 18th century but later restored as a private residence. The castle now hosts musical performances and cultural events, including traditional Scottish fiddle gatherings. Its gardens include dry-stone dykes, an orchard of heritage apple varieties, and remnants of the Dundee and Newtyle Railway, Scotland’s first northern railway.

Marine Medium Tiltrotor Squadron 363 (VMM-363) is a United States Marine Corps tiltrotor squadron consisting of MV-22B Ospreys. The squadron, known as the "Lucky Red Lions", is based at MCB Hawaii and falls under the command of Marine Aircraft Group 24 (MAG-24) and the 1st Marine Aircraft Wing.

Gomer Hill is a 2,106-foot (642 m) summit in Lewis County, New York, located west-northwest of Turin. A 67.5-foot (20.6 m) fire lookout tower was built on the summit in 1940 by the Civilian Conservation Corps. It operated until 1988, when aerial fire detection replaced its use. The tower is now closed but remains as an antenna support structure, while its cabin was relocated to the Lowville Demonstration Forest for exhibition.

GABA transporter 2 (GAT2; SLC6A13) also known as sodium- and chloride-dependent GABA transporter 2 is one of four GABA transporters, GAT1 (SLC6A1), GAT2 (SLC6A13), GAT3 (SLC6A11) and BGT1 (SLC6A12). Note that GAT2 is different from BGT1 despite the fact that the latter transporter is sometimes referred at as (mouse) GAT-2.

All these transporters are highly hydrophobic proteins with 12 transmembrane segments, extracellular glycosylation sites, and intracellular consensus sites for phosphorylation, and there is over 50% amino acid homology between each of them. Each binds GABA with varying affinities with BGT1 having the lowest affinity and GAT3 the highest. GAT2 (SLC6A13) is predominantly expressed in hepatocytes in the liver, but is also found in proximal tubules in the kidney as well as in the leptomeninges and in some blood vessels in the brain.

Molecular electronic transitions take place when electrons in a molecule are excited from one energy level to a higher energy level. The energy change associated with this transition provides information on the structure of a molecule and determines many molecular properties such as colour. The relationship between the energy involved in the electronic transition and the frequency of radiation is given by Planck's relation.
Michael Klein (10 October 1959 – 2 February 1993) was a Romanian footballer who played as a left back.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (née Nerima Wako), is a Kenyan political analyst, who serves as the executive director of Siasa Place, an organization, led by young people that engages young Kenyans, educating them about the constitution, governance and electoral processes, through community engagements and social media.

Charles Stephen "Steve" Jameson (born May 6, 1973) is a United States painter and children's book illustrator. Jameson paints under two brush names: "Cedar" and "Wodin".

Books illustrated by Jameson include Just Imagine, God Brother, Dreaming of the Beloved, and Cooking with Katie.

In 2009, Jameson was elected into the National Watercolor Society.

Georgy Vasilyevich Pigulevsky (10 December 1888 – 19 September 1964) was a Soviet chemist and biochemist known for his research on terpenes, essential oils, and plant resins. A professor at Leningrad State University, he contributed to industrial chemistry and wartime medicine production during the Siege of Leningrad.

Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Pakistani-Norwegian jurist, journalist and politician from the Labour Party. She served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. She previously served as Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She was 29 years of age at the time and became the youngest minister to serve in the Norwegian government. She is the first Cabinet member that is a Muslim. Tajik has served as a Member of Parliament representing Rogaland since 2017, and Oslo from 2009 to 2017. She was also the party's deputy leader from 2015 until 2022.

Ham Green Pottery was a medieval English pottery industry active until the late 13th century. Known for its distinctive glazed jugs, it was widely traded across Britain and Ireland, with fragments serving as key archaeological dating tools.

Lake Temescal is a man-made reservoir in Oakland, California, created in 1868 by damming Temescal Creek. It became one of the first parks in the East Bay Regional Park District in 1936. Today, it offers recreational activities, including fishing and swimming, and attracts around 200,000 visitors annually.

Richard Mellon Scaife (/skeɪf/; July 3, 1932 – July 4, 2014) was an American billionaire, a principal heir to the Mellon banking, oil, and aluminum fortune, and the owner and publisher of the Pittsburgh Tribune-Review. In 2005, Scaife was number 238 on the Forbes 400, with a personal fortune of $1.2 billion. By 2013, Scaife had dropped to number 371 on the listing, with a personal fortune of $1.4 billion.

During his life, Scaife was known for his financial support of conservative public policy organizations over the past four decades. He provided support for conservative and libertarian causes in the United States, mostly through the private, nonprofit foundations he controlled: the Sarah Scaife Foundation, Carthage Foundation, and Allegheny Foundation, and until 2001, the Scaife Family Foundation, now controlled by son David.

The Employer is a psychological thriller written and directed by Frank Merle. The film stars Malcolm McDowell as the title character who interviews five candidates for a job at a mysterious, powerful corporation. After a film festival circuit in early 2013, the film was released by Vision Films in the U.S. on June 7, 2013 as an On Demand premiere title, followed by a DVD release on July 2, 2013.
Terry George (born 14 March 1965) is a British businessman known primarily for his appearances in television programmes. He was born and raised in Leeds, West Yorkshire.

Sir John Wedderburn, 5th Baronet of Blackness, was a Perthshire gentleman who joined the 1745 rebellion of Charles Edward Stuart. He was captured at the Battle of Culloden, taken to London, and convicted of treason. He was hanged, his estates were forfeit to the Crown, and his family was attainted. The Blackness in his title is Blackness House in Dundee rather than Blackness in Lothian just west of Edinburgh. His son John Wedderburn of Ballendean fled after his father's death to Jamaica, where he re-established the family's fortunes via slave sugar, and eventually regained his father's title.

Coryphodon (from Greek κορῦφὴ, "point", and ὀδοὺς, "tooth", meaning peaked tooth, referring to "the development of the angles of the ridges into points [on the molars].") is an extinct genus of pantodonts of the family Coryphodontidae.

Coryphodon was a pantodont, a member of the world's first group of large browsing mammals. It migrated across what is now northern North America, replacing Barylambda, an earlier pantodont. It is regarded as the ancestor of the genus Hypercoryphodon of Late Eocene Mongolia.

Coryphodon is known from many specimens in North America and considerably fewer in Europe, Mongolia, and China. It is a small to medium-sized coryphodontid who differs from other members of the family in dental characteristics.

Bugando Medical Centre is a 950-bed tertiary referral, teaching, and research hospital in Mwanza, Tanzania. Affiliated with the Catholic University of Health and Allied Sciences, it serves as a key cancer treatment center and was established in 1971.

Jeff Danielson (born August 6, 1970) is an American politician and former Democratic member of the Iowa Senate, representing the 10th district (2005–2013) and later the 30th district (2013–2019). A firefighter by profession, he served as President Pro Tempore of the Iowa Senate from 2006 to 2012 and advocated for veterans' issues and online poker legalization. Danielson resigned in 2019 to work as a lobbyist for the American Wind Energy Association. He holds degrees from Hawkeye Community College and the University of Northern Iowa and resides in Waterloo, Iowa.

Molecular electronic transition refers to the movement of electrons between energy levels in molecules, often observed using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. Common transitions include σ→σ*, π→π*, and n→π*, which occur in chromophores. These transitions produce characteristic absorption bands, such as the R-, K-, B-, and E-bands. Solvents can influence transition energies, causing shifts in absorption spectra. Unlike atomic line spectra, molecular transitions typically appear as broad bands due to overlapping vibrational and rotational states.

The Department of Social Security (DSS) was an Australian government agency responsible for social welfare programs from 1972 to 1998. It administered income support, disability pensions, and services for Indigenous Australians and migrants. In 1997, its payment functions transferred to Centrelink, and it was dissolved in 1998, merging into the Department of Family and Community Services.

In ecology, rarefaction is a technique to assess species richness from the results of sampling. Rarefaction allows the calculation of species richness for a given number of individual samples, based on the construction of so-called rarefaction curves. This curve is a plot of the number of species as a function of the number of samples. Rarefaction curves generally grow rapidly at first, as the most common species are found, but the curves plateau as only the rarest species remain to be sampled.

The issue that occurs when sampling various species in a community is that the larger the number of individuals sampled, the more species that will be found. Rarefaction curves are created by randomly re-sampling the pool of N samples multiple times and then plotting the average number of species found in each sample (1,2,. N). "Thus rarefaction generates the expected number of species in a small collection of n individuals (or n samples) drawn at random from the large pool of N samples.".
The National Assembly is the unicameral legislature of Cape Verde, consisting of 72 members elected by proportional representation.
Eric Mattson is an American firefighter and politician serving as a Democratic member of the Illinois State Senate for the 43rd District since 2022.

Alligator Rivers is the name of an area in an Arnhem Land region of the Northern Territory of Australia, containing three rivers, the East, West, and South Alligator Rivers. It is regarded as one of the richest biological regions in Australia, with part of the region in the Kakadu National Park. It is an Important Bird Area (IBA), lying to the east of the Adelaide and Mary River Floodplains IBA. It also contains mineral deposits, especially uranium, and the Ranger Uranium Mine is located there. The area is also rich in Australian Aboriginal art, with 1500 sites. The Kakadu National Park is one of the few World Heritage sites on the list because of both its natural and human heritage values. They were explored by Lieutenant Phillip Parker King in 1820, who named them in the mistaken belief that the crocodiles in the estuaries were alligators.
Ilias Haddad (Lang-ar; born 1 March 1989, Dordrecht) is a Moroccan footballer who plays as a centre back for Union Touarga. Haddad holds both Dutch and Moroccan nationality.

Jim Radakovich is an American sculptor and painter living and working in New York City. He was a key figure in the East Village art scene in New York from 1982 to 1987 often showing together both Neo-Surrealist paintings and totem-like sculpture. He frequently exhibited with other artists who emerged at the time, including Kiki Smith, David Wojnarowicz, George Condo, Rick Prol, Peter Schuyff, Mark Kostabi and Marilyn Minter.

Milner is a city in Lamar County, Georgia, United States. Founded in 1912 and named after pioneer Willis R. Milner, it had a population of 772 in 2020. Located along U.S. Route 41, it sits on the Eastern Continental Divide.

Valsartan, sold under the brand name Diovan among others, is a medication used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and diabetic kidney disease. It belongs to a class of medications referred to as angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). It is a reasonable initial treatment for high blood pressure. It is taken by mouth. Versions are available as the combination valsartan/hydrochlorothiazide, valsartan/amlodipine, valsartan/amlodipine/hydrochlorothiazide, or valsartan/sacubitril.

Common side effects include feeling tired, dizziness, high blood potassium, diarrhea, and joint pain. Other serious side effects may include kidney problems, low blood pressure, and angioedema. Use in pregnancy may harm the baby and use when breastfeeding is not recommended. It is an angiotensin II receptor antagonist and works by blocking the effects of angiotensin II.

Valsartan was patented in 1990, and came into medical use in 1996. It is available as a generic medication. In 2020, it was the 123rd most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 5 million prescriptions.

The 2014 WNBA Finals was the championship series of the 2014 WNBA season, contested between the Phoenix Mercury and the Chicago Sky. The Mercury, who finished with a league-best 29–5 record, swept the Sky in three games to win their third championship. Diana Taurasi was named Finals MVP. The Sky, making their first Finals appearance, had advanced by defeating the Indiana Fever in the Eastern Conference Finals, while Phoenix overcame the Minnesota Lynx in the Western Conference Finals.

Franco-Albertans are a French-speaking minority in Alberta, Canada, forming the fourth-largest francophone community in the country. According to the 2016 Canadian Census, 86,705 Albertans reported French as their mother tongue, while 268,615 were bilingual in English and French. Most Franco-Albertans live in Edmonton and Calgary, though smaller bilingual communities like Beaumont, Falher, Legal, and Plamondon also exist.  

The community traces its origins to 18th-century French Canadian fur traders and missionaries. Despite early French-language education, English became dominant after Alberta's formation in 1905. Franco-Albertans later secured language rights through legal battles, including a 1990 Supreme Court ruling affirming their right to manage francophone schools. Today, Alberta has four francophone school boards and bilingual services in some municipalities.  

Franco-Albertan culture thrives through organizations like the *Association canadienne-française de l’Alberta*, French-language media (including Radio-Canada and *Le Franco*), and cultural events like the Franco-Alberta Festival. The University of Alberta’s Saint-Jean Campus offers French-language higher education. In 2017, Alberta recognized the Franco-Albertan flag and designated March as *Alberta Francophonie Month*.

Oxalis latifolia is a species of flowering plant in the woodsorrel family known by the common names garden pink-sorrel and broadleaf woodsorrel. It is native to Mexico and parts of Central and South America.

Antasena-class combat boat is a combat boat built by North Sea Boats and designed by LOMOcean Design for the Indonesian armed forces. The manufacturer designated it as Combat Boat X-18 / CB X-18. Initially, this class of ships was developed from a tank boat program from a consortium consisting of PT Lundin (North Sea Boats), PT Pindad, PT LEN, and PT Hariff Daya Tunggal Engineering, with John Cockerill as the turret supplier.

The Rekolokomotives of DR Class 52.80 first appeared in 1960 in service with the Deutsche Reichsbahn in East Germany as extensive rebuilds of the wartime locomotives or Kriegslokomotiven of the DRB Class 52 built by Nazi Germany. This modernisation, described as 'reconstruction' (Rekonstruktion, hence Rekolokomotive), extended to almost all of the components and systems on the engine.

Elizabeth Phillips Hughes (12 July 1851 – 19 December 1925) was a British educator and college administrator. She was the first principal of Cambridge Training College for Women (later Hughes Hall) and a pioneer in women's education. She also promoted education in Wales and served in World War I as a Red Cross hospital administrator.
Nina Coote (1883–1945) was a British croquet champion who won multiple titles, including the English ladies' open championship.

What Women Suffer is a 1911 Australian silent film directed by Alfred Rolfe. It is a Victorian melodrama, complete with a climax where a little child is placed on a moving saw bench and is considered a lost film.

Hochschild Kohn's was a Baltimore-based department store chain founded in 1897 by Max Hochschild, Benno Kohn, and Louis B. Kohn. It became Baltimore's largest department store by 1922 and later expanded into suburban malls, including Harundale Mall and The Mall in Columbia. Acquired by Supermarkets General in 1969, the chain struggled and was dissolved in 1984, with many locations sold to Hutzler's. Its Belvedere store was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2003.
Michael N. Delagi (April 22, 1879 – October 7, 1957) was an American lawyer, magistrate, and New York State Assemblyman from the Bronx.

Hassan Haskins (born November 26, 1999) is an American football running back for the Tennessee Titans of the NFL. He played college football at Michigan, where he set a single-season rushing touchdown record (20) and earned All-Big Ten honors in 2021. The Titans drafted him in the fourth round of the 2022 NFL Draft.

Patrick Desmond Callaghan (16 July 1926 – 6 April 1992) was a Pakistan Air Force officer who served in World War II and the Indo-Pakistani wars of 1965 and 1971. He later became a flight safety instructor for Pakistan International Airlines.

Zheng Zhi (born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional football manager and former player, currently in charge of Guangzhou F.C. A versatile midfielder, he spent most of his career with Guangzhou, captaining the club to multiple Chinese Super League titles and the 2013 AFC Champions League. He was named Asian Footballer of the Year in 2013, becoming only the second Chinese player to win the award.  

Zheng began his career at Liaoning Liaoqing before moving to Shenzhen Jianlibao, where he won his first league title. He later played abroad for Charlton Athletic in England and Celtic in Scotland. Internationally, he earned over 100 caps for China, serving as captain and representing the nation in the 2008 Olympics. After retiring as a player, he transitioned into management, taking over Guangzhou as caretaker before becoming head coach in 2022.

Charles J. Givens (February 5, 1941 – July 12, 1998) was a bestselling author of three books, Wealth Without Risk and Financial Self Defense and More Wealth Without Risk. Givens founded the Charles J. Givens Organization that grew to over 600,000 members. He frequently appeared on nationally syndicated daytime television shows to promote his "financial strategies" and hosted a weekly radio program with self named "Christian Financial Planner" James L. Paris. At his peak Givens extravagant lifestyle was profiled on the popular television program Lifestyles of the Rich and Famous. His organization collapsed after a number of lawsuits, and regulatory investigations of investments sold by Paris, central Florida radio personality Jack Dicks, and former vacuum cleaner salesman Charles C Smith Jr.

Wafa Mustafa (born in Masyaf, Syria) is a Syrian journalist and activist known for her advocacy for detainees and missing persons in Syria, including her father, Ali Mustafa, who was forcibly disappeared in 2013. After participating in early anti-government protests, she was arrested in 2011 and later fled to Turkey before settling in Germany. She graduated from Bard College Berlin in 2020. Mustafa has campaigned internationally, including at the UN Security Council and the Al Khatib trial in Germany, where she highlighted human rights abuses by the Syrian regime. She co-founded *Families for Freedom*, a movement demanding justice for Syria's disappeared.
Hans Christian Hagedorn (1888–1971) was a Danish physician who co-founded Nordisk Insulinlaboratorium and developed NPH insulin, a slow-acting insulin formulation using protamine.
General Applied Science Laboratory (GASL) is a U.S. aerospace research firm specializing in hypersonic propulsion and advanced energy technologies.

Progress MS-20 (Russian: Прогресс МC-20), also known as Progress 81P, was a Roscosmos resupply mission to the International Space Station (ISS). Launched on 3 June 2022 aboard a Soyuz-2.1a rocket, it delivered 2,500 kg of cargo and docked with the ISS's *Zvezda* module. The mission supported Expeditions 67 and 68 before its planned deorbiting in 2023.

Crymogæa (from Greek Κρυογαια, "ice-land") is a book in Latin written by the Icelandic scholar Arngrímur Jónsson and published in Hamburg in 1609 and again in 1610. It was the first historical description of Iceland since Íslendingabók and the first comprehensive book about the history of Iceland.

The New Philharmonic Society was a British music society, established in 1852, giving annual series of subscription concerts of orchestral music in London until 1879. The concerts in the first season were conducted by Hector Berlioz.

Kachche Heere (1982) is a Hindi action-drama film directed by Narender Bedi, starring Feroz Khan, Reena Roy, Danny Denzongpa, and Shakti Kapoor. The story follows a group of thieves who hide stolen loot in a village, only to reform after witnessing the villagers' suffering under a ruthless dacoit. Feroz Khan plays an orphan raised by a dacoit, who later fights for justice. The film blends themes of crime, redemption, and rural oppression, culminating in a climactic battle. The music was composed by Rahul Dev Burman.

Carfilzomib, sold under the brand name Kyprolis, is an anti-cancer medication used to treat relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. It is a proteasome inhibitor that irreversibly binds to and blocks the activity of the 20S proteasome, leading to the accumulation of damaged proteins and cancer cell death. Developed from the natural compound epoxomicin, carfilzomib was first studied by Craig Crews at Yale University and later advanced by Proteolix, Inc., which was acquired by Onyx Pharmaceuticals.  

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved carfilzomib in 2012 based on clinical trials showing efficacy in heavily pretreated patients. Common side effects include thrombocytopenia, anemia, and fatigue. It is administered intravenously and is often used in combination with lenalidomide and dexamethasone. The ASPIRE trial demonstrated improved progression-free survival with this combination compared to standard therapy.

Two Paddocks is a New Zealand winery in Central Otago, founded in 1993 by actor Sam Neill. It produces Pinot Noir, Riesling, and Sauvignon Blanc, including its flagship Proprietor's Reserve Pinot Noir.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place in Seneca County, New York, United States. The population was 6,681 at the 2010 census. The 2020 census population of Seneca Falls CDP was 6,809. The hamlet is in the Town of Seneca Falls, east of Geneva. It was an incorporated village from 1831 to 2011.

Finger Lakes Regional Airport (0G7) is south of the hamlet.

Seneca Falls was the site of the Seneca Falls Convention, a foundational event in the Women's Rights Movement. The town is believed by some to have been the inspiration for the fictional town of "Bedford Falls, N.Y." portrayed in filmmaker Frank Capra's classic 1940s film "It's a Wonderful Life".

Design studies can refer to any design-oriented studies but more formally is an academic discipline or field of study that pursues, through both theoretical and practical modes of inquiry, a critical understanding of design practice and its effects in society.

Powers Auditorium, in Youngstown, Ohio is one of the largest auditoriums in the Youngstown-Warren area. The facility is the main venue of downtown Youngstown's DeYor Performing Arts Center. The complex also includes the Adler Art Academy, Beecher Flad Pavilion, and Ford Family Recital Hall. Originally built in 1931 as the Warner Theatre, the former movie palace was renovated and reopened as Powers Auditorium in 1969.

The main tenant of Powers Auditorium is the Youngstown Symphony, which performs from October through May. The facility also hosts other musical acts, touring Broadway productions, and locally produced theater (primarily from Ballet Western Reserve and Easy Street Productions, two Youngstown-based theater companies).

*Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.* (Danish: *Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.*) is a series of three booklets by Hans Christian Andersen, published between 1835 and 1837. The first booklet included *The Tinderbox*, *Little Claus and Big Claus*, *The Princess and the Pea*, and *Little Ida's Flowers*. The second introduced *Thumbelina*, *The Naughty Boy*, and *The Travelling Companion*, while the third contained *The Little Mermaid* and *The Emperor's New Clothes*. Initially met with criticism for their informal style, these tales later became foundational works of literary fairy tales.

Sanjog (transl. Destiny) is a 1972 Indian Hindi-language drama film directed by S. S. Balan. The film stars Mala Sinha, Amitabh Bachchan and Aruna Irani in the lead roles. It is a remake of the Tamil film Iru Kodugal directed by K. Balachander.
On November 16, 2015 the beta version of the European Data Portal was launched. The European Data Portal is an initiative of the European Commission, and is part of the Digital Single Market.

1. FC Kaan-Marienborn is a German football club based in Kaan-Marienborn, currently competing in the Regionalliga West. Founded in 2007, the club has risen from the Landesliga to the Regionalliga under coach Thorsten Nehrbauer.

Lamakaan is a cultural center in Hyderabad, India, founded in 2010. It hosts events such as concerts, theatre performances, book releases, and Wikipedia meetups, and faced a brief closure threat in 2015.

Günther Krampf (8 February 1899 – 4 August 1950) was an Austrian-born cinematographer who worked in German and British cinema. Active from 1920 to 1950, he collaborated with directors such as F. W. Murnau and G. W. Pabst during the Weimar era. After moving to Britain in 1931, he worked for Gaumont British and later Welwyn Studios. He contributed to propaganda films with Alfred Hitchcock during World War II, and his final notable work was *Fame is the Spur* (1947).

Douglas Keszler is an American chemist and distinguished professor at Oregon State University, known for his research in inorganic and materials chemistry. His work includes developing hydrothermal dehydration techniques for solid synthesis and advancing amorphous oxide semiconductors for electronic displays. He earned his Ph.D. from Northwestern University in 1984 and has since contributed to innovations in thin-film transistors and aqueous-based nanomaterial synthesis.

Stewardson Township is a township in Potter County, Pennsylvania, United States. The population was 64 at the 2020 census. Ole Bull State Park, a Pennsylvania state park, is located in Stewardson Township.
Buck Brothers were a British three piece rock band. The band's sound is a mixture of pop and punk.
Fiction (stylized in all caps) is an extended play by the Canadian experimental band Suuns, released October 30, 2020 via Secret City Records and Joyful Noise Recordings.
Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was a professional cricketer who played first-class cricket for Somerset County Cricket Club from 1900 to 1911 as slow left-arm spin bowler.

*Crymogæa* (Latin: *Crymogæa, sive rerum Islandicarum libri tres*) is a 17th-century book by Icelandic scholar Arngrímur Jónsson. It presents Iceland's history, medieval heroes, and culture, shaping national identity and inspiring European interest in Norse mythology and Icelandic sagas. The work became a key text in Icelandic patriotism.

Fungistatics are substances that inhibit the growth of fungi without necessarily killing them. They include antifungal medications like fluconazole and itraconazole, used to treat infections, and food preservatives such as sodium benzoate and potassium sorbate, which prevent fungal spoilage.

RPT Realty, formerly known as RAMCO Properties Trust and Ramco-Gershenson Properties Trust, is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates open-air shopping centers. As of August 4, 2021, the company owned 50 shopping centers comprising 12.6 million square feet.

Notable properties owned by the company include The Shops on Lane Avenue in Upper Arlington, Ohio; Woodbury Lakes in Woodbury, Minnesota; Bedford Marketplace in Bedford, Massachusetts; Lakehills Plaza in Austin, Texas; River City Marketplace in Jacksonville, Florida; Mission Bay Plaza in Boca Raton, Florida; The Crossroads in Royal Palm Beach, Florida; Coral Creek Shops in Coconut Creek, Florida.

Susan Sonnenschein is an American developmental psychologist and professor at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC). She earned her Ph.D. from Stony Brook University and is a certified school psychologist. Since joining UMBC in 1979, she has taught courses in child development, cognitive development, and child assessment. Recognized for her mentorship, she has served on numerous dissertation and master's committees. In 2008, she received UMBC's Donald Creighton Award for Outstanding Faculty. Her research focuses on early childhood education and cognitive development.

Michael Keeping (22 August 1902 – 28 March 1984) was an English footballer and manager. A full-back, he spent most of his playing career at Southampton and Fulham. Later, he managed Real Madrid, winning the 1948 Copa Eva Duarte, before coaching Poole Town and Heracles Almelo.

An antifuse is an electrical device that performs the opposite function to a fuse. Whereas a fuse starts with a low resistance and is designed to permanently break an electrically conductive path (typically when the current through the path exceeds a specified limit), an antifuse starts with a high resistance, and programming it converts it into a permanent electrically conductive path (typically when the voltage across the antifuse exceeds a certain level). This technology has many applications.

Nassakh (1833–1889), born Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr, was a Bengali Muslim poet, scholar, and British Indian government official. Writing primarily in Urdu and Persian, he authored works such as *Sukhan-e-Shuara* and *Daftar-e-Bemisal*, which earned praise from poets like Ghalib. He also translated Persian literature and wrote biographical poetry on Islamic figures. His brother was the reformer Nawab Abdul Latif.

Blackwell Farm is the name of one of the estates situated on the Hog’s Back, on the West side of Guildford. Located in the parish of Compton, the historical farm is currently a controversial issue as part of the Guildford Borough Council’s Local Plan consultation.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during his summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore, it describes pixies in a woodland setting and reflects Coleridge's early poetic style. The poem was included in his 1796 collection *Poems on Various Subjects*.

Anima Yell! (アニマエール!, Anima Ēru!) is a Japanese four-panel manga series by Tsukasa Unohana. It was serialized in Houbunsha's seinen manga magazine Manga Time Kirara Carat from its April 2016 to October 2020 issues and has been collected in five tankōbon volumes. An anime television series adaptation by Doga Kobo aired from October 7 to December 23, 2018.

Swedish tourists Sven Urban Höglin, aged 23, and his fiancée Heidi Birgitta Paakkonen, aged 21, disappeared while tramping on the Coromandel Peninsula of New Zealand in 1989. Police, residents, and military personnel conducted the largest land-based search undertaken in New Zealand, attempting to find the couple. In December 1990, David Wayne Tamihere (born 1953) was convicted of murdering Höglin and Paakkonen, and sentenced to life imprisonment based largely on the testimony of three prison inmates.

Höglin's body was discovered in 1991, revealing evidence which contradicted the police case against Tamihere, who has always maintained his innocence and filed a series of unsuccessful appeals during the 1990s. Tamihere was released on parole in November 2010 after serving twenty years. In 2017, Secret Witness C, one of the former prisoners who had testified against Tamihere at his murder trial, was found guilty of perjury. On 26 April 2018, the identity of Witness C was revealed as Robert Conchie Harris, who had originally been convicted of the double murder of a couple in 1983.

Police Academy is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson and starring Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, and Bubba Smith. The plot follows a group of misfit recruits at a police academy after the mayor mandates open admissions. Carey Mahoney (Guttenberg), a troublemaker given a choice between jail or training, initially tries to get expelled but eventually bonds with his fellow cadets while clashing with the strict Lieutenant Harris. Despite mixed reviews, the film was a box office success, grossing $149.8 million worldwide and spawning a franchise. Critics noted its crude humor, with Roger Ebert calling it unfunny, while others praised its slapstick energy. The film was shot in Toronto, with key locations including the former Lakeshore Psychiatric Hospital.

Matthijs van Nieuwkerk (IPA-nl; born 8 September 1960) is a Dutch journalist and television presenter. He is best known for hosting the daily De Wereld Draait Door (DWDD) television show from 2005 to 2020.

The Leader of Alderney is the civil leader of Alderney. Alderney is a dependency of the Bailiwick of Guernsey. Its leader has traditionally been appointed by the British Crown and has been known by various titles including Lord of Alderney, Governor of Alderney, and the current President of the States of Alderney. The President of the States of Alderney is directly elected every four years and there is no constitutional limit to the number of terms served. The current president, William Tate has held the post since 2019.
Q. S. Serafijn (born in Roosendaal en Nispen) is a Dutch artist known for interactive sculptures like the *D-tower* and the equestrian statue *John Wayne*.
Léonie d'Aunet (1820–1879) was a French author and playwright, known for her Arctic travelogue and her affair with Victor Hugo.

Uncle Jam Wants You is the eleventh studio album by American funk rock band Funkadelic. It was originally released by Warner Bros. Records on September 21, 1979, and was later reissued on CD by Charly Groove Records and Priority Records. It was produced by George Clinton under the alias Dr. Funkenstein. It is the first Funkadelic album since America Eats Its Young in 1972 not to sport a cover illustrated by Funkadelic artist Pedro Bell, though Bell did provide artwork for the album’s back cover and interior. Uncle Jam Wants You was the second Funkadelic album to be certified gold. The album peaked at #18 on the US Billboard 200 and #2 on the US Billboard Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums charts.

Spindle is an autonomous cryobot under development by Stone Aerospace, designed to explore subglacial lakes such as Antarctica's Lake Vostok. Building on NASA-funded VALKYRIE technology, Spindle uses a high-energy laser to melt through ice rather than hot water jets. Equipped with radar and autonomous navigation, it is intended to penetrate ice sheets 1.5 to 4 km (0.93 to 2.49 mi) thick, deploy a submersible (HAUV) for underwater exploration, and resurface for sample retrieval. The project serves as a precursor for potential missions to icy moons like Europa or Enceladus, where it could search for biosignatures in subsurface oceans. Future versions may use a compact fission power plant for deep-space missions.

Duško Tarbuk (born in Zemun, Serbia) is a Serbian politician and member of the Progressive Party. He served as deputy mayor of Zemun (2008–2009) and has been a member of the National Assembly since 2016.
The Secret Committee was a group of Freud's closest followers, formed to uphold psychoanalytic orthodoxy.

Jody Hamilton (August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, trainer, and promoter, best known as one-half of the masked tag team The Assassins alongside Tom Renesto. Trained by Michael DiBiase, he debuted in 1955 and became a prominent figure in Georgia Championship Wrestling (GCW), where The Assassins held the NWA Georgia Tag Team Championship a record 12 times. After Renesto's retirement, Hamilton continued wrestling as a solo competitor under the Assassin and later The Flame personas, winning titles in Mid-South Wrestling and AWA Southern.  

Following his in-ring career, Hamilton founded Deep South Championship Wrestling and later became the director of WCW's Power Plant, training future stars like Bill Goldberg and Kevin Nash. After WCW's closure, he briefly revived Deep South Wrestling as a WWE developmental territory before joining Rampage Pro Wrestling as a producer. His son, Nick Patrick, was a longtime wrestling referee. Hamilton was inducted into the WCW Hall of Fame in 1994 and remained active in wrestling until his death in 2021.

Islesboro is a coastal town in Waldo County, Maine, located on a 14-mile-long island in Penobscot Bay. Incorporated in 1789, it was once home to Maine's largest commercial shipping fleet before becoming an affluent summer colony. The town includes several smaller islands and features a year-round population of 583 (2020 census). Islesboro Central School serves K–12 students, including some from the mainland. The island is known for its scenic harbors, historic estates, and seasonal tourism.

Gracefield is a city in La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality in the Outaouais region of Quebec, Canada. It was reorganized on 13 March 2002 when three former municipalities (Gracefield, Northfield, and Wright) were merged into one.

Its name comes from Patrick Grace who opened there the first business and was mayor of Wright from 1885 to 1890.

Gracefield is located along the Gatineau River and Picanoc River. Its area is dotted with many lakes, most of which are lined with numerous summer camps and cottages.

Vladimir Nemukhin (1925–2016) was a Russian avant-garde artist known for his abstract works featuring playing cards and card tables. A key figure in Soviet nonconformist art, he co-founded the Lianozovo Group and participated in landmark exhibitions like the 1974 "Bulldozer Exhibition." His works are held in major museums worldwide.

Andrea Nguyen (born 1969) is a Vietnamese-American food writer and cookbook author specializing in Vietnamese cuisine. A refugee who fled Vietnam in 1975, she is known for books like *Into the Vietnamese Kitchen* (2006), *Asian Dumplings* (2009), and *The Pho Cookbook* (2017), which won a James Beard Award. Her work explores the cultural and historical aspects of Asian food, blending detailed recipes with personal storytelling. She also writes for publications like *Cooking Light* and runs the blog *Viet World Kitchen*.

Dhrangadhra (pronunciation<\/small>"},"data":{"ipa":"","text":"","lang":"en","wikibase":"","file":"Dhrangdhra1.ogg"},"classes":["noexcerpt","ext-phonos-PhonosButton"]}">pronunciation) is a town, taluka headquarters and a municipality in Surendranagar district in the state of Gujarat, India. During the period of the British Raj, the city was the capital of Dhrangadhra State, one of the eight first-class princely states (13-gun salute) of the Kathiawar Agency in the Bombay Presidency.

Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (31 August 1885 – 13 May 1975) was an English female police officers who was among the first officers to take statements from female and juvenile assault victims, rather than relying on "assistants".

The University of Silesia in Katowice (Lang-pl) is an autonomous state-run university in Silesia Province, Katowice, Poland.

The university offers higher education and research facilities. It offers undergraduate, masters, and PhD degree programs, as well as postgraduate, postdoctoral research, habilitation, and continuous education and training programs.

Rangi Ruru Girls'School is a New Zealand private girls' day and boarding secondary school located in Merivale, an inner suburb of Christchurch. The school is affiliated to the Presbyterian Church, and serves approximately 698 girls from Years 7 to 13 (ages 10 to 18).

The Fairchild 9440 was a 16-bit microprocessor introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor in the late 1970s, implementing the instruction set architecture of Data General's Nova minicomputer. Designed without Data General's approval, it led to a series of lawsuits between the two companies. The 9440 was fabricated using Fairchild's Isoplanar Integrated Injection Logic (I³L) process, allowing it to run at speeds comparable to the Nova 1200 while requiring only a single +5V power supply. Unlike Data General's microNOVA mN601, the 9440 lacked an internal DRAM refresh system and hardware multiply/divide, relying instead on external support chips.  

An improved version, the 9445, was released in 1981, featuring a 2-micron process, hardware multiply/divide, and support for expanded memory addressing. Despite its performance advantages, legal disputes and market shifts toward 32-bit processors limited its success. Fairchild later adapted the design into the 9450, implementing the MIL-STD-1750A instruction set for military applications. Production ceased after National Semiconductor acquired Fairchild in 1987, though third-party manufacturers briefly continued the line.

The Great Sandy Desert is a large arid region in northwestern Australia, spanning parts of Western Australia and the Northern Territory. Covering 284,993 km² (110,036 sq mi), it features extensive sand dunes and the Wolfe Creek meteorite crater. The desert is sparsely populated, primarily by Indigenous Australian communities such as the Martu and Pintupi peoples. It has a hot, monsoon-influenced climate with seasonal rainfall. Vegetation is dominated by spinifex, and wildlife includes feral camels, dingoes, and red kangaroos. Economic activities include mining and Indigenous art.

The Twelve Apostles are a collection of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park in Victoria, Australia. Formed by erosion over millions of years, the stacks rise up to 50 metres (160 ft) high. Originally named the *Pinnacles* or *Sow and Piglets*, only eight stacks remained as of 2005. The site is part of the Port Campbell Limestone formation and is protected within the Twelve Apostles Marine National Park, following opposition to seismic exploration in the area.
Doon Valley is one of the nine electoral wards of East Ayrshire Council. Created in 2007, the ward elects three councillors using the single transferable vote electoral system.

Scott L. Thoele (born 1958) was a major general in the National Guard of the United States. His final assignment was as Deputy Commanding General for the Reserve Component of United States Army Forces Command. Thoele previously served as Deputy Commanding General, Army National Guard, United States Army Combined Arms Center, Fort Leavenworth. He retired on 30 September 2015 after 14 years of service in the U.S. Army and 21 years of service in the Illinois Army National Guard.

Nick Rotteveel (IPA-nl; born January 6, 1989), professionally known as Nicky Romero or Monocule, is a Dutch DJ, record producer and remixer from Amerongen, Utrecht Province. He has worked with, and received support from DJs, such as Tiësto, Fedde le Grand, Sander van Doorn, David Guetta, Calvin Harris, Armand van Helden, Avicii and Hardwell. He currently ranks at number 26 on DJ Mag's annual Top 100 DJs poll. He is known for his viral hit song "Toulouse".

Stephanie is a fictional character from the comic book series The Walking Dead and the television series of the same name, where she is portrayed by Margot Bingham as an alias for Maxxine Mercer while Chelle Ramos was cast as Shira who also uses the alias of Stephanie.

The Big Red Machine is a nickname for the Cincinnati Reds baseball team that dominated the National League from 1970 to 1979 and is widely recognized as being among the best in baseball history. The team won six National League West Division titles, four National League pennants, and two World Series titles. Between 1970 and 1979 the team averaged over 95 wins a season, with a total record of 953 wins and 657 losses.

The core of that Reds team had the best record in the Major Leagues in 1981, but did not make the postseason because of Bowie Kuhn's split-season playoff format due to the player's strike.

Sthanu Ravi Varma (reigned 844/45–870/71 AD), also known as Kulasekhara, was a ruler of the Chera Perumal Kingdom in medieval Kerala. His reign marked the consolidation of the Chera Perumal dynasty, though his direct authority was largely ceremonial, with real power held by local chieftains and Brahmin elites. Sthanu Ravi allied with the Chola king Rajakesari Varma in military campaigns, including an expedition into the Kongu region of Tamil Nadu.  

His court was notable for its patronage of astronomy, hosting the scholar Sankara Narayana, who wrote the Laghu Bhaskariya Vyakha, a commentary on the works of Bhaskara I. An astronomical observatory functioned in Kodungallur (Mahodayapuram), the Chera capital. Sthanu Ravi is also associated with the Quilon Syrian Christian copper plates (849 AD), which granted privileges to a Christian church under his vassal Ayyan Adikal.  

Later literary sources sometimes conflate him with Rama Rajasekhara, his successor, leading to historical ambiguities. His reign remains significant for its cultural and scientific contributions in early medieval South India.

A Zirkel is a monogram used by German *Studentenverbindungen* (student fraternities), typically featuring intertwined initials and an exclamation mark if active. Members display it on insignia and in signatures.
This article lists the squads of the women's hockey competition at the 2010 Commonwealth Games held in New Delhi, India from 4 to 13 October 2010.

The men's horizontal bar competition at the 2016 Summer Olympics was held at the HSBC Arena on 6 and 16 August. There were 71 competitors from 34 nations. The event was won by Fabian Hambüchen of Germany, the nation's first victory in the horizontal bar since 1996 and third overall. Danell Leyva won the United States'third silver medal in four Games in the event; Nile Wilson's bronze was Great Britain's first-ever medal in the horizontal bar.

The medals were presented by Claudia Bokel IOC member, Germany and Naomi Valenzo, FIG Executive Committee Member.

Dutchman is a play written by African-American playwright Amiri Baraka, then known as LeRoi Jones. Dutchman was first presented at the Cherry Lane Theatre in Greenwich Village, New York City, in March 1964. The play won an Obie Award; it shared this distinction with Adrienne Kennedy's Funnyhouse of a Negro. Baraka's stage play was made into a film in 1967, starring Shirley Knight and Al Freeman Jr. Dutchman was the last play produced by Baraka under his birth name, LeRoi Jones. At the time, he was in the process of divorcing his Jewish wife, Hettie Jones, and embracing Black Nationalism. Dutchman may be described as a political allegory depicting black and white relations during the time Baraka wrote it.

The play was revived in 2007 at the Cherry Lane Theatre starring Dulé Hill, and in 2013 was restaged by Rashid Johnson at the Russian and Turkish Baths in the East Village.

Ticagrelor (sold under the brand names Brilinta and Brilique) is an antiplatelet medication used to reduce the risk of stroke in patients with acute ischemic stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA), and to prevent atherothrombotic events in patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS) or a history of myocardial infarction. It works by reversibly inhibiting the P2Y12 adenosine diphosphate (ADP) receptor on platelets. Unlike clopidogrel, ticagrelor does not require metabolic activation and has a faster onset but shorter duration of action, requiring twice-daily dosing. Common side effects include bleeding and dyspnea (shortness of breath). It is metabolized by CYP3A4 and interacts with strong inhibitors or inducers of this enzyme. Ticagrelor is typically used with low-dose aspirin as part of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT).

Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was a British vehicle manufacturer that built buses, motorcoaches and trucks from 1912 until 1979. The name Associated Equipment Company was hardly ever used; instead it traded under the AEC and ACLO brands.

During World War One, AEC was the most prolific British lorry manufacturer; after building London's buses before the great war.

The National Audit Office of Tanzania (NAOT) is an independent parliamentary body responsible for auditing government finances. Established in 2001 under the Public Finance Act, it replaced the Exchequer and Audit Department. Headquartered in Dodoma, it is led by the Auditor General, currently Charles Kichere. The office ensures financial accountability within the Tanzanian government.

Matthew Asen Kantakouzenos or Cantacuzenus (Greek: Ματθαῖος Ἀσάνης Καντακουζηνός, Matthaios Asanēs Kantakouzēnos, c. 1325 – 1383 or 1391) was Byzantine Emperor from 1353 to 1357 and later Despot of the Morea from 1380 to 1383.

The Black sermonic tradition is a distinctive style of preaching in African American Christianity, characterized by emotional expressiveness, rhetorical embellishments, and musical vocalizations. It emphasizes authenticity, social critique, and spiritual transformation while maintaining historical ties to ancestral struggles. A notable feature is "whooping," a dynamic call-and-response style often accompanied by organ chords. The tradition has also influenced African American literature and poetry.

The Action Group on Erosion, Technology, and Concentration (ETC), is an international organization dedicated to "the conservation and sustainable advancement of cultural and ecological diversity and human rights." "ETC" is intended to be pronounced "et cetera." ETC often publishes opinions on scientific research by its staff and board members in topics including community and regional planning, ecology and evolutionary biology, and political science.

Andrea Batista Schlesinger (born October 27, 1976) is an American political writer and campaigner. She served as executive director of the Drum Major Institute and as a policy adviser to New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg.

Saint Vincent Martyr School is a private Roman Catholic elementary school in Madison, New Jersey, serving students from pre-kindergarten through eighth grade. Established in 1848, it is accredited by the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools and has been recognized as a National Blue Ribbon School. The school offers a variety of sports programs, including basketball, baseball, softball, track and field, cross country, and cheerleading, as well as scouting programs for Girl Scouts and Cub Scouts. With an enrollment of approximately 400 students, Saint Vincent Martyr School emphasizes both academic and extracurricular development within a faith-based environment.

"What Women Suffer" (1911) is a silent Australian melodrama directed by Alfred Rolfe, based on H. G. Brandon's play. The film follows Edith Norton, whose husband is wrongly imprisoned, while the villainous Herbert Standish schemes against her. It was a commercial success, praised for its cinematography.

The Whole Family is a collaborative novel published in 1908, written by twelve authors including William Dean Howells, Henry James, and Mary E. Wilkins Freeman. Edited by Elizabeth Jordan, the book follows the Talbert family as they react to daughter Peggy’s engagement. Each chapter was written by a different author, focusing on a different family member, leading to stylistic and thematic clashes—most notably Freeman’s reinterpretation of the "old maid" aunt as a bold, independent woman, which disrupted Howells’ original vision. Serialized anonymously in *Harper’s Bazar* before its book release, the novel became a subject of literary debate, with critics analyzing both its experimental format and the tensions among its contributors. Despite mixed reception, it remains a notable example of early 20th-century collaborative fiction.

Kisnána is a village in Heves County, Hungary, with a population of 1,049 (2008). It is known for its medieval Kisnána Castle, built by the Kompolti family, and a Gothic church with origins in the Árpád era.

Joanne M. Garvey is an American attorney known for her pioneering role as a female lawyer in tax law. Born in Oakland, California, she attended Holy Names High School and earned her undergraduate and law degrees from the University of California, Berkeley, where she was one of only five women in her law school graduating class.  

Garvey began her legal career in Santa Barbara in 1962 before joining the San Francisco firm Jordan, Keeler and Seligman, where she practiced for 25 years. She later worked at Heller Ehrman until its dissolution in 2008, after which she moved to Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton.  

Garvey has been recognized with multiple awards, including the State Bar of California Taxation Section's Joanne M. Garvey Award (named in her honor) and the American Bar Association's Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award (2003) for her contributions to advancing women in the legal profession.

Georgia Douglas Johnson (September 10, 1880 – May 15, 1966) was an African-American poet, playwright, and activist associated with the Harlem Renaissance. A prolific writer, she published four poetry collections, including *The Heart of a Woman* (1918), and wrote nearly 30 plays addressing racial injustice. She also hosted the influential S Street Salon, a gathering place for Black artists and intellectuals.

Fungistatics are anti-fungal agents that inhibit the growth of fungus (without killing the fungus). The term fungistatic may be used as both a noun and an adjective. Fungistatics have applications in agriculture, the food industry, the paint industry, and medicine.

Tracy Reiner (born July 7, 1964) is an American actress known for roles in *Die Hard* (1988), *When Harry Met Sally...* (1989), and *A League of Their Own* (1992). She is the daughter of Penny Marshall and was adopted by Rob Reiner.
St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum is a Grade I listed parish church in the Church of England Diocese of Exeter in Buckland Monachorum, Devon.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (1863–1910) was a British painter known for her social realist works. Born in Liverpool, she studied in Paris under Auguste Feyen-Perrin and Eduard Krug. Her painting *Fantine* (1886) earned an honorable mention and is held by the Walker Art Gallery. Hall traveled extensively before returning to England, where she died in 1910. Few of her works survive, including a portrait at Trinity College, Cambridge.

Joanna Mytkowska (1970) is a Polish curator and art critic. She has been the director of the Museum of Modern Art, Warsaw since 2007. She is the co-founder of the Foksal Gallery Foundation, which since 2001 has been operating independently from the Foksal Gallery.

Stockland Bristol is a small village in Somerset, England, with a population of 165 (2011). Historically part of Cannington Hundred, it lies near the River Parrett and was once owned by Bristol Corporation. The village features St. Mary Magdalene Church, rebuilt in 1865.

Ida S. Baker High School is a public high school located in Cape Coral, Florida. The school was founded in 2004, and is one of six high schools located in Cape Coral. For the 2011–12 school year it was rated as an "A+" school in the State of Florida.

John Armand Lafore Jr. (May 25, 1905 – January 24, 1993) was an American politician who was a Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania. He was also president of the American Kennel Club.

"D.A.N.C.E." is a 2007 nu-disco song by French electronic duo Justice, from their debut album *†*. Inspired by Michael Jackson, it features interpolations of his songs and vocals from a London choir. The track peaked at number 4 on *Rolling Stone*'s 100 Best Songs of 2007. Its animated music video, directed by Jonas & François, won an MTV Europe Music Award.

Phoebe Forrester is a fictional character from the CBS Daytime soap opera The Bold and the Beautiful. The first actress to play the character on a contract basis was Addison Hoover, who joined the show in April 2005 and left the following year. From July 11, 2006 to December 9 2008, Phoebe was played by MacKenzie Mauzy, until the character was killed off. Phoebe and her twin sister, Steffy (Jacqueline MacInnes Wood), were born in September 1999 to Ridge Forrester (Ronn Moss) and Taylor Hayes (Hunter Tylo). As a result of constant SORASing, Phoebe was seventeen by 2006. The character has been described as an "ill-fated beauty".
Wigstan (died 839) was a Mercian prince and saint who refused the throne of Mercia, preferring religious life. He was martyred and later venerated, with his relics enshrined at Evesham Abbey.
Andrew Keary (born 1987) is an Irish hurler who plays for Galway Championship club Killimor. He previously lined out with the Galway senior hurling team. Keary usually lines out as a forward.

Ma Zhou (601–648), courtesy name Binwang, formally the Duke of Gaotang (高唐公), was a Chinese politician who served as a chancellor during the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang dynasty. He was initially a commoner and a guest of the general Chang He (常何), and after Chang submitted suggestions that Ma drafted, Emperor Taizong was impressed and retained Ma as an imperial official, eventually rising to the post of chancellor.

Cecil Lewis Alexander (born August 2, 1935) is a former influential Democratic politician and lobbyist in Arkansas. He was a member of the Arkansas House of Representatives, serving from 1963 to 1979, including a term as Speaker of the House. Alexander later became a lobbyist followed by a tenure as chair of the Arkansas Racing Commission.

Vesna Györkös Žnidar (born 29 December 1977) is a Slovenian lawyer and politician who served as Minister of the Interior from 2014 to 2018 under Prime Minister Miro Cerar. A member of the Modern Centre Party, she previously worked in law and finance.

Stewardson Township is a sparsely populated township in Potter County, Pennsylvania, United States. Settled in 1852 and incorporated in 1856, it covers 74.06 square miles (191.83 km²) and had a population of 64 in 2020.

Lead Section for "Dejarte de Amar"  

*Dejarte de Amar* is the second studio album by Mexican Latin pop group Camila, released on February 9, 2010, by Sony Music Latin. Produced by Mario Domm, the album blends Latin pop, pop rock, and alternative rock, featuring hit singles such as "Mientes," "Aléjate de Mí," and "Bésame." The lead single, "Mientes," became a chart-topper on the *Billboard* Latin Pop Airplay chart and won two Latin Grammy Awards, including Song of the Year and Record of the Year.  

The album received critical acclaim for its polished arrangements and emotional depth, earning comparisons to Coldplay and Maroon 5. It won Best Pop Album by a Duo or Group at the 11th Latin Grammy Awards and was nominated for Album of the Year. Commercially, *Dejarte de Amar* debuted at number one on the *Billboard* Top Latin Albums and Latin Pop Albums charts, achieving Diamond certification in Mexico and Platinum in the U.S. for sales exceeding 450,000 and 100,000 copies, respectively. The album solidified Camila's success following their multi-platinum debut, *Todo Cambió* (2006).

Religious Zionism is a movement that combines Jewish religious observance with Zionism, advocating for the establishment and support of the State of Israel in accordance with Jewish religious values. Rooted in the teachings of Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook, it views Jewish nationalism as a divinely ordained step toward messianic redemption. The movement emphasizes the religious obligation to settle and develop the Land of Israel while maintaining Torah observance.  

Key organizations include Mizrachi, founded in 1902, and its youth movement Bnei Akiva, promoting Torah-based Zionism. Politically, Religious Zionists have been represented by parties such as the National Religious Party and, more recently, Jewish Home and Otzma Yehudit. The movement operates educational institutions like Mercaz HaRav Yeshiva and the Hesder program, which combines military service with Torah study.  

While most Religious Zionists align with right-wing politics, some support left-wing factions like Meimad. The movement is also associated with Israeli settlements and a strong presence in the Israel Defense Forces. Its adherents, often identified by knitted kippot, balance modern Israeli life with religious tradition.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic Carpenter Gothic plantation house and historic district, located one mile east of Uniontown, Alabama, US. The 2+1⁄2-story wood-framed main house was built in the Gothic Revival style in the late 1850s. The plantation historic district includes six other contributing buildings, in addition to the main house. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage on December 19, 1991, and subsequently to the National Register of Historic Places on May 29, 1992, due to its architectural and historical significance.

The "San Lorenzo march" is a military march composed in 1901 by Cayetano Alberto Silva, with lyrics later added by Carlos Javier Benielli in 1908. Dedicated to José de San Martín, it commemorates the Battle of San Lorenzo, his only battle in modern-day Argentina. First performed in 1902, it became internationally renowned and is considered one of the finest military marches. It has been played at British coronations, Buckingham Palace ceremonies, and was exchanged with the German army for *Alte Kameraden*. The march remains a key part of military band repertoires worldwide.

Caserma Ederle (Camp Ederle) is a military complex in Vicenza, Italy, where the United States Army has troops stationed. It is under Italian military control and can be managed anytime by the Italian authorities. The Vicenza Military Community is composed of soldiers, family members, civilians and retirees with a small number of airmen and sailors also stationed there. The post serves as the headquarters of United States Army Africa and the 173rd Airborne Brigade. Caserma Ederle serves as the headquarters of U.S. Army Garrison Italy of the United States Army Installation Management Command, an umbrella for all U.S. military properties in Vicenza. The post is named after Major Carlo Ederle, an Italian hero of World War I and recipient of the French Croix de Guerre (French War Cross), among other military honors.

The Republic of Lakotah was a proposed independent nation within the borders of the United States, declared by Lakota activists in 2007. Led by Russell Means, the group cited violations of the 1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie and sought to reclaim land in parts of North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Wyoming, and Montana. They argued that U.S. control had led to poverty and cultural decline, proposing a libertarian-style government with no federal taxes. The movement lacked support from recognized Lakota tribal governments and was dismissed by U.S. authorities. Despite efforts to gain international recognition, the proposal remained symbolic.

SBI Card is an Indian non-banking financial company and a subsidiary of the State Bank of India (SBI), specializing in credit card services. Founded in 1998 and headquartered in Gurugram, it became a publicly listed company in 2020. Initially a joint venture between SBI and GE Capital, SBI later increased its stake to 74% after GE's exit, with The Carlyle Group acquiring the remaining 26%. In 2019, SBI Card rebranded with a new logo and identity targeting millennials. It is one of India's leading credit card issuers, with significant revenue and market presence.

Adoniram Judson Wells, Jr. (April 1, 1917 – February 19, 2008) was an American chemist who worked for DuPont Company for much of his career. After he retired in 1989, he began working in the field of epidemiology, and went on to publish 12 papers on the health effects of tobacco smoke, particularly passive smoking.

The Lagoa do Parado Municipal Nature Park is a protected area in Guaratuba, Paraná, Brazil, known for its biodiverse lagoon, abundant wildlife, and dense caxeta forests. It borders the Saint-Hilaire/Lange National Park.

In particle physics, annihilation is the process that occurs when a subatomic particle collides with its respective antiparticle to produce other particles, such as an electron colliding with a positron to produce two photons. The total energy and momentum of the initial pair are conserved in the process and distributed among a set of other particles in the final state. Antiparticles have exactly opposite additive quantum numbers from particles, so the sums of all quantum numbers of such an original pair are zero. Hence, any set of particles may be produced whose total quantum numbers are also zero as long as conservation of energy and conservation of momentum are obeyed.

During a low-energy annihilation, photon production is favored, since these particles have no mass. High-energy particle colliders produce annihilations where a wide variety of exotic heavy particles are created.

The word "annihilation" takes use informally for the interaction of two particles that are not mutual antiparticles – not charge conjugate. Some quantum numbers may then not sum to zero in the initial state, but conserve with the same totals in the final state. An example is the "annihilation" of a high-energy electron antineutrino with an electron to produce a W boson-.

If the annihilating particles are composite, such as mesons or baryons, then several different particles are typically produced in the final state.

Margarita Iosifovna Aliger (Russian: Маргари́та Ио́сифовна Алиге́р, IPA: [mərɡɐˈrjitə ɪˈosjɪfəvnə ɐljɪˈɡjɛr]; October 7 [O.S. September 24] 1915 – August 1, 1992) was a Soviet and Russian poet, translator, and journalist.

Francisco de Holanda (originally Francisco d'Olanda; 6 September 1517 – 19 June 1585) was a Portuguese court painter and sculptor for King John III of Portugal, and later for Sebastian of Portugal. He wrote what is regarded as the first treatise on portrait painting in Europe, Do tirar polo natural (1549). He is considered to be one of the most important figures of the Portuguese Renaissance, also being an essayist, architect and historian. He represented the intelligible reality of the Holy Trinity through a "hypothetical" syntax of geometrical figures. He insisted on the contrast between the ideal plane, the incorporeal form and the "imperfect copy in the terrestrial zone". His visual language demonstrated a mixture of Neoplatonism, Christian Kabbalah and finally Lullism. In education, Francisco de Holanda emphasized mathematics and geometry, subsequently anticipating Clavius's reforms of the late 16th century. Sylvie Deswarte said that "Francisco de Holanda gives a privileged place to cosmography and astrology in the education of the painter. On par with geometry, mathematics and perspective, he recommended them. in order to reach the heavens in the hope of one day arriving to the Empyrean and realising celestial works."

George Washington's reception at Trenton was a celebration hosted by the Ladies of Trenton social club on April 21, 1789, in Trenton, New Jersey, as George Washington, then president-elect, journeyed from his home at Mount Vernon to his first inauguration in the then capital of the United States, New York City. A ceremonial triumphal arch was erected on the bridge over the Assunpink Creek to commemorate his two victories here, the Battle of Trenton on December 26, 1776 and the Battle of the Assunpink Creek on January 2, 1777.

Thomas Eugene DeWeese (January 31, 1934 – March 19, 2012) was an American writer of science fiction, best known for his Star Trek novels. He also wrote Gothic, mystery, and young adult fiction, totalling more than 40 books in his career. He published as Gene DeWeese and Jean DeWeese; his pseudonyms as a collaborator included Thomas Stratton and Victoria Thomas.
Gamawan Fauzi (born 9 November 1957) is an Indonesian politician who served as Interior Minister (2009–2014) and Governor of West Sumatra (2005–2009).
DeForest is a village in Dane County, Wisconsin, United States, along the Yahara River. The population was 10,811 at the 2020 census. It is part of the Madison Metropolitan Statistical Area.

Vesna Györkös Žnidar (born 29 December 1977) is a Slovenian politician who was Minister of Interior in the Cabinet of Miro Cerar between 18 September 2014 and 13 September 2018. She is a member of the Modern Centre Party.

Living Colour is an American rock band from New York City, formed in 1984. The band currently consists of guitarist Vernon Reid, lead vocalist Corey Glover, drummer Will Calhoun and bassist Doug Wimbish (who replaced Muzz Skillings in 1992). Stylistically, their music is a creative fusion influenced by heavy metal, funk, jazz, hip hop, punk, and alternative rock. The band's lyrics range from the personal to the political, including social commentary on racism in the United States.

Living Colour has released six studio albums so far. The band rose to fame with their debut album Vivid in 1988. Although they scored a number of hits, Living Colour is best known for their signature anthem "Cult of Personality", which won a Grammy Award for Best Hard Rock Performance in 1990. They were also named Best New Artist at the 1989 MTV Video Music Awards and won their second Grammy Award for their follow-up album Time's Up (1990). Their third album, Stain (1993), was also well received by music critics. After disbanding in 1995, Living Colour reunited in late 2000, and has released three more studio albums since then: Collideøscope (2003), The Chair in the Doorway (2009) and Shade (2017). The band has been in the process of working on new material for the follow-up to Shade.
Tracy Reiner (née Henry; born July 7, 1964) is an American actress. She is known for her roles in When Harry Met Sally., Masque of the Red Death, A League of Their Own, and Apollo 13.
al-Majd (Lang-ar) is a Palestinian village located eighteen kilometers south-west of Hebron. The village is in the Hebron Governorate Southern West Bank.

Arnot Art Museum, opened 1913, is a municipal art museum located at 235 Lake Street in Elmira, New York. Its permanent collection includes 17th-, 18th-, and 19th-century European paintings; and 19th- and 20th-century American art. Its 21st-century collection focuses on contemporary representational art. The building is a contributing property in the Elmira Civic Historic District.

Milner is a city in Lamar County, Georgia, United States. The population was 610 at the 2010 census, up from 522 at the 2000 census.

Milner is part of the official Atlanta 29 County Metropolitan Statistical Area - MSA.

L.A. Law: The Movie is a 2002 American made-for-television drama film based on the 1986–1994 television series L.A. Law which reunited most of the original cast, although not all prominent cast members returned, including Blair Underwood (Jonathan Rollins), Jimmy Smits (Victor Sifuentes), Amanda Donohoe (C.J. Lamb) and John Spencer (Tommy Mullaney). The film aired on NBC on May 12, 2002.

The film's initial working title was L.A. Law: Return To Justice.

Dhrangadhra is a town in Gujarat, India, located in the Surendranagar district. Historically the capital of the princely state of Jhalawad, it is known for its stone bedrock, which gives the town its name. Dhrangadhra houses the Dhrangadhra Chemical Works (DCW), India's first soda ash factory, and serves as a key railway junction. The town features historic temples, palaces, and step-wells, along with a diverse cultural heritage.
The Ideal 18 is a Canadian trailerable sailboat that was designed by Bruce Kirby as a one design racer and first built in 1989.

Alexander Edwin Michael Keeping (22 August 1902 – 28 March 1984) was an English footballer and manager. He coached Real Madrid CF from January 1948 to October 1950. His father was the Olympic medal winning cyclist Frederick Keeping.

Going Blank Again is the second studio album by English rock band Ride, released on 9 March 1992 on Creation Records. It was produced by Alan Moulder, and peaked at No. 5 in the UK Albums Chart. In October 2009 the album was certified gold by the British Phonographic Industry for sales of over 100,000 units.

Ellen Battelle Dietrick (1847–1895) was an American suffragist and author who was active in the movement's organizations in Kentucky and Massachusetts. She was a core member of the group that published The Woman's Bible in the 1890s.

Annette Krauss is a German artist and researcher known for her collaborative projects exploring institutional structures, unlearning practices, and hidden social norms. Her works include *Read the Masks. Tradition is Not Given* (critiquing Dutch blackface traditions), *Hidden Curriculum* (examining unofficial school learning), and *Sites for Unlearning* (experimental gatherings challenging ingrained habits). She co-founded the feminist collective *Read-in* and co-edited *Spaces of Commoning* on artistic research and everyday utopias.

"The Things That I Used to Do" is a blues standard written by Guitar Slim. He recorded it in New Orleans, where the young Ray Charles arranged and produced the session. Specialty Records released the song as a single in 1953 and it became a bestseller the following year. Specialty founder Art Rupe believed that the appeal would be limited to the Southern U.S. rural audience. However, urban rhythm and blues radio stations in the North began airing the song and built it into a national hit. As a result, Guitar Slim became in great demand as a performer and played at venues such as the Apollo Theater in New York City.

The single was one of the biggest hits in Specialty's history and stayed on the Billboard's Rhythm and Blues Records charts for 42 weeks. The song remained at number one for six weeks and was the best-selling R&B record of the year, selling more than a million copies.

George Stiebel (1821-1896) was a Jamaican trader and entrepreneur who became a millionaire with sea transport between North and South America and as the owner of a gold mine. Stiebel is notoriously named Jamaica's first black millionaire.
Egypt competed at the 2014 Summer Youth Olympics in Nanjing, China, with 83 athletes in 20 sports, winning eight medals (2 gold, 1 silver, 5 bronze).

The Wrocław Global Forum is an annual international conference held in Wrocław, Poland, since 2010. Organized in partnership with the Atlantic Council and other institutions, it focuses on transatlantic relations, security, and democracy. The event attracts policymakers, business leaders, and experts, featuring discussions on global challenges. It also hosts the Atlantic Council's Freedom Awards, honoring contributions to global freedom.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (26 May 1924 – 27 January 2017) was a French musicologist and biographer of Gustav Mahler.

Gerard Alsteens (born 3 August 1940, in Auderghem) is a Belgian political cartoonist, graphic artist and painter who works under the pseudonym "GAL". which, artistically speaking, share a closer resemblance to paintings than regularly drawn cartoons. Throughout the years his work has received several prizes and awards.
Salvatore Grippi (1921-2017) was an American artist and founder of the art department at Ithaca College.

Acetoin (3-hydroxybutanone) is a four-carbon organic compound with a yogurt-like odor. It is used as a food flavoring and fragrance, notably in butter-flavored products, and is also found in e-cigarette liquids. Some bacteria produce acetoin as a metabolic byproduct.
The Belgium–Korea Treaty of 1901 was negotiated between representatives of the Kingdom of Belgium and the Korean Empire.

Henrique Honoré Dumont (July 20, 1832 in Diamantina – August 30, 1892 in Rio de Janeiro) was a Brazilian engineer, coffee farmer and father of Alberto Santos-Dumont. A son of French immigrants, he is considered one of the three kings of the coffee of his time, introducing modern methods to coffee farming.

Alfred Edward Bailey (14 March 1871 – 1 August 1950) was an English cricketer who played first-class cricket for Somerset from 1900 to 1911 as a left-arm spin bowler. His best performance was 8/46 against Yorkshire in 1907.

1,3,2,4-Dithiadiphosphetane 2,4-disulfides are a class of organophosphorus compounds commonly used as thionation reagents in organic synthesis. The most well-known example is Lawesson's reagent, formed by reacting phosphorus pentasulfide (P₄S₁₀) with anisole. These compounds are widely employed to convert carbonyl groups (C=O) into thiocarbonyls (C=S) and can also facilitate dehydrations and heterocycle formations. Variations with increased solubility, such as those derived from butoxybenzene, have been developed. A notable subclass includes naphthalen-1,8-diyl derivatives, which exhibit unique reactivity due to their rigid structure. Additionally, selenium analogs like Woollins' reagent (Ph₂P₂Se₄) expand their utility in chalcogen chemistry. These compounds also participate in ring-opening reactions and have applications in synthesizing insecticides and metal-binding agents.

Duško Knežević (born 30 May 1959) is a Montenegrin economist, businessman, and political activist. He is the founder and president of Atlas Group, a conglomerate with interests in banking, real estate, tourism, and education.
