Knežević also established Mediterranean University, Montenegro's first private university, and the Belgrade Banking Academy.  

Educated at the University of Belgrade, he holds degrees in finance, banking, and law. His business ventures include partnerships with international firms such as Abu Dhabi Capital Group and Piraeus Bank. In recent years, Knežević has been involved in major real estate and tourism projects in Montenegro.  

In 2019, he became a central figure in political controversies after releasing videos allegedly showing illicit financial dealings involving Montenegro's ruling party. Accused of money laundering, he launched the DO SLOBODE movement, advocating for anti-corruption reforms. Knežević holds Montenegrin, British, and Cypriot citizenship and currently resides in London.

Ethel Esther Page (née Blunt; 20 September 1875 – 26 May 1958) was the first wife of Sir Earle Page, the 11th Prime Minister of Australia. She worked as a nurse before her marriage. Page supported her husband during his long political career, though never living in Canberra, and was politically active herself, serving on the state executive of the Country Party and as president of its women's section. She was also involved in various community organisations.
Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose. It was released in hardback on April 29, 1994 through Wesleyan University Press.

The Kinburn Spit (Ukrainian: *Кінбурнська коса*) is a 10-kilometer-long sandbar in Mykolaiv Oblast, Ukraine, known for its unique ecosystem and as part of the Kinburn Foreland Regional Landscape Park. Historically, it housed an Ottoman fort before being dismantled in the 19th century. During the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, it was occupied by Russian forces in June and used for electronic warfare and artillery strikes. Ukrainian forces launched multiple unsuccessful counterattacks before Russia's withdrawal from Kherson in November 2022. The war caused significant environmental damage to its wildlife, including pink pelicans and dolphins.

The National Audit Office (NAO) of Tanzania is an independent Parliamentary body in Tanzania which is responsible for auditing central government departments, government agencies and non-departmental public bodies. The NAO also carries out value for money (VFM) audits into the administration of public policy. The office is headed by the Chief Auditor General appointed by the President of Tanzania.

Fear (commonly stylized as fear) is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket. The album is their second album for Columbia Records, and was released on August 27, 1991. It became the first commercially successful album for the band, selling over a million copies and was certified platinum three years after release, on September 1, 1994. The album reached #49 on Billboard's Top 200 Albums in September 1992. Two of the album's singles charted in the US top 40, "All I Want" and "Walk on the Ocean" which peaked at #15 and #18 on the Billboard Hot 100, respectively.

Hasan Arat (born 27 September 1959) is a Turkish Sports Administrator, businessman and former professional basketball player. He was the leader of Istanbul's Bid to host the 2020 Olympic and Paralympic Games.

XBase++ is a programming language and development environment designed for database applications, maintaining backward compatibility with Clipper syntax. It supports traditional commands like `@SAY/GET` and includes a visual form designer, debugger, and resource compiler. XBase++ supports multiple database formats (DBF, FOX, NTX, etc.) via Replaceable Database Drivers (RDD) and can generate EXE or DLL files. It emerged after Computer Associates discontinued Clipper in favor of Visual Objects.

Murayama Tōan Antonio (村山等安) was a 17th-century Japanese magistrate of the city of Nagasaki (Nagasaki daikan, 長崎代官). He was born in Nagoya from a humble background, and he was a Christian. He played an important role in the handling of "Nanban trade" in Nagasaki with Christian powers, and led an invasion to Taiwan, before being executed for his Christian faith.
Odysseus is a role-playing game published by Fantasy Games Unlimited in 1980.

The Togliattiazot Affair is a series of events connected to criminal proceedings against the owners and executives of the Russian firm Togliattiazot (Toaz) and their foreign partners. The case spanned 14 years, from 2005 to 2019, and resulted in the owners and executives receiving prison sentences of between to 8.5 and 9 years. The activity of the involved parties was described as "a threat to [Russia’s] economic activity" by the Chairman of the Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation, Alexander Bastrykin.

Togliattiazot is the largest ammonia producer in Russia, with an estimated global market share of 7.6%.

Under the Whyte notation for the classification of steam locomotives, 0-8-0 represents the wheel arrangement of no leading wheels, eight powered and coupled driving wheels on four axles and no trailing wheels. Locomotives of this type are also referred to as eight coupled.

Milltown (Irish: *Baile an Mhuilinn*) is a village in County Kildare, Ireland, with a population of 344 (2016 census). It is part of the Allen Parish and features St. Brigid's Church, built in 1817. The village is home to Milltown GAA, a Gaelic Athletic Association club founded in 1888.

Road Rules: Down Under is the sixth season of the MTV reality television series Road Rules. It took place exclusively in Hawaii for part of the first episode, with the rest of the season exclusively in Oceania. This season is often noted as Road Rules: Australia.
Ham Green Pottery was produced between 1100 AD to 1250 AD at a hamlet above the village of Pill called Ham Green in the English county of Somerset.

Wang Dan (王旦; 957/958 – October 6, 1017) was a Chinese statesman who served as Grand Councilor of the Song dynasty under Emperor Zhenzong from 1006 to 1017. Born during the Later Zhou, he passed the imperial examinations in 980 and rose through bureaucratic ranks, earning recognition for his administrative skill. As Grand Councilor, he played a key role in institutionalizing civil service examination reforms and mentored future officials, including five who later became Grand Councilors. Known for his integrity and competence, he was posthumously honored with the name Wenzheng (文正). His career exemplified the scholar-official ideal of the Song dynasty.

Helene Hewitt OBE is a British climate scientist who is a research fellow at the Met Office. Her research considers climate and ocean models. Hewitt serves on the CLIVAR Ocean Model Development Panel. She was awarded an Order of the British Empire in the 2022 Birthday Honours.

"Imagine" is a song performed by Norwegian singer Tone Damli as a duet with Eric Saade. The song is from her fifth studio album Looking Back (2012). It was written per request by and features vocals from Swedish singer Eric Saade. It was released in Norway as a digital download on 27 April 2012.

The Mourne Mountains (/mɔːrn/ MORN; Lang-ga), also called the Mournes or Mountains of Mourne, are a granite mountain range in County Down in the south-east of Northern Ireland. They include the highest mountains in Northern Ireland, the highest of which is Slieve Donard at 850 m (2,790 ft). The Mournes are designated an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty and it has been proposed to make the area Northern Ireland's first national park. The area is partly owned by the National Trust and sees many visitors every year. The Mourne Wall crosses fifteen of the summits and was built to enclose the catchment basin of the Silent Valley and Ben Crom reservoirs.
GE Power (formerly known as GE Energy) is an American energy technology company, owned by General Electric.

Breac Maodhóg is an 11th-century Irish house-shaped reliquary, likely used as a battle standard, associated with St. Maedoc of Ferns. Made of bronze on a wooden base, it features finely detailed figures of saints, clerics, and apostles, with expressive faces and elaborate robes. One notable figure depicts the oldest known visual representation of a harp. Originally linked to Drumlane monastery in County Cavan, it was later associated with Rossinver, County Leitrim, before being stolen in the 19th century. The shrine, now in poor condition, is housed in the National Museum of Ireland. Its figures, resembling those in the *Soiscél Molaisse*, are praised for their lifelike humanity and intricate craftsmanship.

Candyman 3: Day of the Dead (sometimes titled just Candyman: Day of the Dead) is a 1999 American supernatural slasher film directed by Turi Meyer and starring Tony Todd and Donna D'Errico. It is the third installment in the Candyman series, and a direct sequel to the 1995 film Candyman: Farewell to the Flesh. Its plot follows Caroline, the daughter of Annie Tarrant and a descendant of the Candyman, the powerful spirit of the murdered son of a slave who kills those who invoked him, who finds herself targeted by him on the eve of the Day of the Dead.

Unlike the first two installments, the film did not get a theatrical release and was instead released direct-to-video on July 9, 1999. It was followed by a fourth film in 2021, titled Candyman, a direct sequel to the original 1992 film of the same name which returns the storyline to Chicago, Illinois.

Bajrang Punia (born 26 February 1994) is an Indian freestyle wrestler and Olympic bronze medalist. A dominant force in the 65 kg category, he has won multiple medals at the World Championships, Asian Games, and Commonwealth Games. Punia, a recipient of the Arjuna Award (2015), is one of India's most decorated wrestlers, known for his technical prowess and aggressive style.

Hamish Angus McLachlan (born 28 August 1975) is an Australian sports broadcaster and host with Seven Sport.

Since he joined the Seven Network in January 2008, he has been a host/presenter/reporter at multiple events, including being a part of every Australian Open tennis tournament until 2018 and hosting AFL Game Day since its beginning through to this day. McLachlan was the host for the network's Olympics and Commonwealth Games, a position previously held by the likes of Sandy Roberts and Matt White.

Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 - January 31, 1980) is the first Filipino recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award (Asian equivalent of Nobel Prize) and also the first awardee of the said award for Government Service (Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service). He is also a recipient in the government service category of the said award which he shared with Chintaman Deshmukh in 1959. He is also recognized as the Father of the Community School Movement in the Philippines, pioneering reforms to ensure that community life is integrated in the education system. His community school model banks on the partnership between teachers, parents, and community with the goal of ensuring a practical education using the vernacular as a medium of instruction.

Henry Gunther (June 6, 1895 – November 11, 1918) was an American soldier and the last U.S. serviceman killed in World War I. A German-American from Baltimore, he was drafted in 1917 and served in the 313th Infantry Regiment. Demoted for discouraging enlistment in a letter, he was killed charging a German machine-gun nest at 10:59 a.m. on Armistice Day. Posthumously restored to sergeant, he received the Distinguished Service Cross and was honored with memorials in France and Maryland.

Rugby League Week was an Australian magazine covering rugby league, published from 1970 to 2017. Focused primarily on the NRL, it featured match previews, player ratings, and regular columns by league personalities. It introduced *The Immortals*, honoring the sport's greatest players, and awarded an annual *Player of the Year*. Owned by Bauer Media Group, it ceased publication in 2017.

Fadi Sawan (born 1960) is a Lebanese judge known for his work on terrorism cases and his role as the lead investigator in the 2020 Beirut port explosion. A Maronite Christian, he graduated from Saint Joseph University and served for over three decades in the judiciary, including as an investigative judge at Lebanon’s Military Court. He gained prominence for prosecuting Islamist militants, including Ahmed Al-Assir and members of ISIS and Al-Nusra.  

In 2020, he was appointed to investigate the Beirut blast, charging several officials with negligence. However, his removal from the case in February 2021—following legal challenges by politicians he had accused—sparked controversy and accusations of political interference. His dismissal was condemned by Human Rights Watch. Sawan faced opposition from Hezbollah and former Prime Minister Saad Hariri during his tenure.

Paul De Keersmaeker (born [year]) is a Belgian businessman, politician, and former chairman of Interbrew.  

*(Note: The original text does not provide a birth year, so it is omitted. If needed, adjust based on additional sources.)*

Economic policy refers to the actions governments take to influence economic activity, including taxation, spending, monetary policy, and regulation. It aims to achieve goals such as stable prices, low unemployment, and sustainable growth. Policymakers use tools like interest rates, government budgets, and trade regulations, balancing short-term interventions with long-term rules. Historically, economic policy evolved from early taxation and trade controls to modern fiscal and monetary strategies, including Keynesian demand management and supply-side reforms. Recent approaches emphasize evidence-based decision-making, using experimental data to guide interventions. Debates continue over the effectiveness of discretionary policies versus rule-based frameworks, as well as the role of government in stabilizing economies and promoting development.

Tō Tsuneyori (東 常縁, 1401 - 1484) was a samurai lord and poet of the mid-Muromachi period and early Sengoku period. He was the lord of Shinowaki Castle in Mino Province and served as Governor of Shimotsuke Province. He is known as the founder of Kōkin Denju poetic school. He held the court rank of Junior Fifth Rank. He was also known as Tōyashū.

The Northampton Red Sox were a minor league baseball team based in Cape Charles, Virginia, from 1927 to 1928. They played in the Class D Eastern Shore League, winning the league title in 1928 before its midseason collapse. The team hosted games at Patton Field.

San José Castle -also known as Castillo de San José in Spanish- was opened to the public on 25 May 1846 on the «Buena Vista Hill» on the southeast of Guatemala City by general Rafael Carrera government; it was also known as «Carrera Castle». San José Fort was located where later Guatemalan administrations built a new city Hall, a National Theater and part of the Bolivar Avenue. The fort had a shooting range for the soldiers, ammunition warehouses, horses, diners and a small pond that was called "Soldier's Pond".

Hoang Anh Gia Lai Group (HAGL) is a Vietnamese conglomerate founded in 1990 by Đoàn Nguyên Đức. Headquartered in Pleiku, it operates in furniture, real estate, rubber, sugar, football, mining, and energy. Initially a small wood-processing firm, it expanded into real estate, rubber plantations in Vietnam, Cambodia, and Laos, and hydroelectric power projects. HAGL has faced controversy over land disputes in Cambodia, including accusations of destroying indigenous forests and sacred sites. The company has raised capital through global depository receipts and bonds, with investments from firms like Temasek Holdings. It also owns hotels, resorts, and a football club.

"The Things That I Used to Do" is a blues song recorded by Guitar Slim in 1953 for Specialty Records. Produced by Ray Charles, the track was recorded at J&M Studio in New Orleans and features a gospel-influenced arrangement with Charles on piano. The song became a major hit, blending electric blues with a rising and falling melody, and its distorted guitar tones influenced later rock musicians, including Jimi Hendrix.  

Recognized as a blues standard, it was included in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's *"500 Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll"*. The song has been covered by artists such as James Brown, Tina Turner, and Lonnie Brooks, and it played a key role in shaping soul music and the "electric sound" of rock.

"Charles" is a 1948 short story by Shirley Jackson about a boy who blames his misbehavior on a classmate named Charles. The twist ending reveals that Charles does not exist. It remains one of Jackson's most famous works.

The Bab Ballads are a collection of humorous poems and illustrations by W. S. Gilbert, first published in the magazine *Fun* between 1865 and 1871. The name "Bab" derived from Gilbert's childhood nickname and possibly as a nod to Charles Dickens's pen name "Boz." Initially appearing sporadically, the poems gained popularity, leading to multiple collected editions, including *The "Bab" Ballads* (1869), *More "Bab" Ballads* (1872), and *Fifty "Bab" Ballads* (1876). Gilbert later expanded the collection with lyrics from his Savoy Operas in *Songs of a Savoyard* (1890) and a final compilation in 1898.  

Known for their absurdity, satire, and whimsical illustrations, the ballads influenced Gilbert's later comic operas with Gilbert and Sullivan. While Gilbert himself curated the core collection, posthumous editions have debated the inclusion of additional unattributed works. Some ballads have been adapted into musical recordings and theatrical performances.

The 1975 Council of Ministers (Lang-pt, Lang-tet) was the Council of Ministers formed by the Fretilin political party in 1975 as the inaugural administration or cabinet of the Democratic Republic of East Timor proclaimed in November 1975.

Doğuş Balbay (IPA-tr; born 21 January 1989) is a Turkish professional basketball player and the team captain for Anadolu Efes Istanbul of the Turkish Basketbol Süper Ligi (BSL) and the EuroLeague. He is 6 ft 1 in (1.85 m) tall and he plays at the point guard position.

Conscious automatism (C.A.) is a position on the philosophic question that asks whether determinism, as distinguished from “free will”, can be considered the sole operant principle in human decision making.

Infanta Beatriz of Spain, Princess of Civitella-Cesi (Beatriz Isabel Federica Alfonsa Eugénie Cristina Maria Teresia Bienvenida Ladislàa de Borbón y Battenberg; 22 June 1909 – 22 November 2002) was a daughter of King Alfonso XIII of Spain and Victoria Eugenie of Battenberg, wife of Alessandro Torlonia, 5th Prince di Civitella-Cesi. She was a paternal aunt of King Juan Carlos I.

Chandler School is an independent, co-ed day school for students in kindergarten through grade eight located in Pasadena, California United States, founded in 1950. School enrollment is 450 students. The 4.5-acre (1.8 ha) campus overlooks the Pasadena Rose Bowl and the Arroyo Seco.

Freienfeld (Italian: *Campo di Trens*) is a comune in South Tyrol, Italy. Located in the Eisack Valley, it has a population of 2,668 (2010) and includes villages such as Trens, Mauls, and Stilfes. The area has historical significance, with Roman artifacts and medieval castles.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English Cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church and Archbishop of Canterbury between 1207 and his death in 1228. The dispute between King John of England and Pope Innocent III over his election was a major factor in the crisis which produced Magna Carta in 1215. Cardinal Langton is also credited with having divided the Bible into the standard modern arrangement of chapters used today.

Hostile vehicle mitigation (HVM) refers to physical security measures designed to prevent vehicle-based attacks, including ramming and vehicle-borne improvised explosive devices (VBIEDs). Common HVM solutions include crash-tested barriers, bollards, reinforced street furniture, and architectural adaptations. These systems are tested to specific standards to resist impacts from vehicles at varying speeds. HVM is used to protect buildings, crowded public spaces, and high-value targets while maintaining pedestrian access. Proper installation includes maintaining standoff distances to mitigate blast effects. Recent applications focus on preventing vehicle-ramming attacks on pedestrians and securing locations like financial institutions and museums.

John's River (Irish: *Abhainn Naomh Eoin*) is a 6-kilometre (3.7 mi) river in Waterford, Ireland, flowing from Tramore Marshes to the River Suir at Waterford Harbour. It has faced significant pollution issues.

David John Lodge CBE (born 28 January 1935) is an English author and critic. A literature professor at the University of Birmingham until 1987, some of his novels satirise academic life, notably the "Campus Trilogy" – Changing Places: A Tale of Two Campuses (1975), Small World: An Academic Romance (1984) and Nice Work (1988). The second two were shortlisted for the Booker Prize. Another theme is Roman Catholicism, beginning from his first published novel The Picturegoers (1960). Lodge has also written television screenplays and three stage plays. After retiring, he continued to publish literary criticism. His edition of Twentieth Century Literary Criticism (1972) includes essays on 20th-century writers such as T. S. Eliot.

Odd Reidar Humlegård (born 9 May 1961, in Porsgrunn) is a Norwegian lawyer, civil servant and the current National Police Commissioner. Humlegård previously served as head of the National Criminal Investigation Service, and was appointed as director to the National Police Directorate in August 2012 after the controversial resignation of Øystein Mæland.

Republic Township is a civil township of Marquette County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,060 at the 2010 census. The unincorporated community of Republic is located within the township.

Ninnes is a rural locality in South Australia, located 30 km (19 mi) east of Kadina. Established in the 1870s, it was once the seat of the former District Council of Ninnes (later renamed Bute). The area, known for its early settlement and agricultural prosperity, had a population of 65 in 2021.

Ernie Camacho (born February 1, 1956) is an American former professional baseball pitcher who played in Major League Baseball (MLB) from 1980 to 1990. Best known as a reliever, he recorded 45 career saves, including a 23-save season with the Cleveland Indians in 1984. After retiring, he worked in education and supported Alzheimer's awareness.

A marine evacuation system (MES) is a life-saving appliance designed for the rapid evacuation of passengers and crew from ships during emergencies. Capable of evacuating over 700 people in 30 minutes, it is widely used in the maritime industry due to its efficiency and reliability. The system can deploy in harsh conditions, including Beaufort Sea State 6 winds and 3-meter wave heights, and complies with international safety standards such as the EU Marine Equipment Directive and IMO regulations.  

An MES typically requires only one or two operators to activate. Once deployed, an inflatable chute extends into the water, where an attached life raft inflates within approximately 90 seconds. Evacuees then slide down the cushioned chute into the raft. The system is valued for its speed, high capacity, and ability to enhance ship safety in critical situations.

Colegio San Benito is a private Catholic PK-12 school in Mayagüez, Puerto Rico, founded in 1965. Operated by the Order of St. Benedict, it offers bilingual education and is known for its academic excellence and sports programs.

SMS Eber was the last of the six gunboats of the Iltis class of the German Imperial Navy prior to and during World War I. Other ships of the class are SMS Iltis, SMS Luchs, SMS Tiger, SMS Jaguar and SMS Panther. They were built between 1898 and 1903. All of them served primarily overseas, in the German colonies. Eber had a crew of 9 officers and 121 men.

Lau is a rural district and village on the east coast of Gotland, Sweden, centered around the medieval Lau Church. The area includes Lau Cove, the Lau Islets (a bird reserve), and the renowned Lau Spring. Historically part of the Burs thing, Lau was once an island before land elevation connected it to Gotland. The region features archaeological sites, including Bronze Age stone ships and a 12th-century defense tower. As of 2014, it had a population of 227.

Religious Zionism (Lang-he, translit. Tziyonut Datit) is an ideology that combines Zionism and Orthodox Judaism. Its adherents are also referred to as Dati Leumi (דָּתִי לְאֻומִּי‎ "National Religious"), and in Israel, they are most commonly known by the plural form of the first part of that term Datiim (דתיים "Religious"). The community is sometimes called כִּפָּה סְרוּגָה‎ Kippah seruga, literally, "Knitted kippah", the typical head covering which is worn by Jewish men.

Before the establishment of the State of Israel, most Religious Zionists were observant Jews who supported Zionist efforts to build a Jewish state in the Land of Israel. Religious Zionism revolves around three pillars: the Land of Israel, the People of Israel, and the Torah of Israel. The Hardal (חרדי לאומי‎ Ḥaredi Le'umi; lit., "Nationalist Haredi") are a sub-community, stricter in its observance, and more statist in its politics. Those Religious Zionists, who are less strict in their observance but not necessarily more liberal, are informally referred to as "dati lite".

GRRR! is a greatest hits album by the Rolling Stones. Released on 12 November 2012, it commemorates the band's 50th anniversary. The album features two new songs titled "Doom and Gloom" and "One More Shot", which were recorded in August 2012.

"Doom and Gloom" peaked at No. 61 in the UK Charts, No. 26 on the Billboard Japan Hot 100 and No. 30 on the Billboard Rock Songs chart in October 2012. Rolling Stone named "Doom and Gloom" the 18th best song of 2012.

The album reached #3 on the UK Albums Chart and #19 on the Billboard 200.

The 0-8-0 is a steam locomotive wheel arrangement with no leading wheels, eight powered and coupled driving wheels, and no trailing wheels. Primarily used for freight hauling and switching, it was widely adopted in the United States, Germany, Russia, and other countries. The design maximized tractive effort by distributing all weight on driving wheels, making it effective for heavy-duty operations. Notable examples include the USRA 0-8-0 in America and the Prussian G 8.1 in Germany.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, until 1919, when he fled after being assaulted following Ku Klux Klan death threats.
Diplomatic relations between Cuba and Suriname were established on 23 March 1979. Suriname has an embassy in Havana since 2003. Cuba has an embassy in Paramaribo since 1981.

The Whole Family: a Novel by Twelve Authors (1908) is a collaborative novel told in twelve chapters, each by a different author. This unusual project was conceived by novelist William Dean Howells and carried out under the direction of Harper's Bazaar editor Elizabeth Jordan, who (like Howells) would write one of the chapters herself. Howells's idea for the novel was to show how an engagement or marriage would affect and be affected by an entire family. The project became somewhat curious for the way the authors'contentious interrelationships mirrored the sometimes dysfunctional family they described in their chapters. Howells had hoped Mark Twain would be one of the authors, but Twain did not participate. Other than Howells himself, Henry James was probably the best-known author to contribute. The novel was serialized in Harper's Bazaar in 1907–08 and published as a book by Harper's in late 1908.

The economy of governments covers the systems for setting levels of taxation, government budgets, the money supply and interest rates as well as the labour market, national ownership, and many other areas of government interventions into the economy.

Most factors of economic policy can be divided into either fiscal policy, which deals with government actions regarding taxation and spending, or monetary policy, which deals with central banking actions regarding the money supply and interest rates.

Such policies are often influenced by international institutions like the International Monetary Fund or World Bank as well as political beliefs and the consequent policies of parties.
Lilian Staple Mead (30 June 1865 – unknown) was an Australian suffragette and children's book author. She was the only female student ever educated at Adelaide's Prince Alfred College.

Dejarte de Amar (English: Stop Loving You) is the second studio album recorded by Mexican pop rock band Camila, It was released by Sony Music Latin on February 9, 2010 (see 2010 in music) Produced by band leader Mario Domm and mostly written by Domm and Mónica Velez, the album became a success in the United States and Mexico. The album was certified Disco Platino in the United States by the RIAA and 4× Platinum in Mexico by AMPROFON. It was recorded in Mexico at Mamita Studio with theme about love, loneliness and lies. The album spawned three singles: "Mientes", "Aléjate de Mi", and "Bésame", all of which reached number-one on the Billboard Latin Pop Airplay chart. The album received generally positive reviews for its arrangements and compositions.

The album reached number-one on the Billboard Top Latin Albums and Latin Pop Albums in the United States and number-one on the Mexican Albums Chart. It received a Latin Grammy award for Best Pop Vocal Album by a Duo or Group and a nomination for Album of the Year. The lead single, "Mientes", received two Latin Grammy awards for Song of the Year and Record of the Year. The album is currently nominated a Lo Nuestro award for "Latin Pop Album of the Year". Mientes was featured in the telenovela Corazón Salvaje (2009-2010) starring Eduardo Yáñez and Aracely Arámbula. Bésame was also featured in the telenovela Triunfo del Amor (2010-2011).

Kafiristan (Persian: کافرستان, "Land of the Kafirs") was a historical region in present-day Afghanistan and Pakistan, inhabited by Indo-European pagan tribes before their forced conversion to Islam in 1895–1896. The name derives from the Arabic *kāfir* ("non-believer"), though it may also relate to local ethnic groups like the Kati people.  

Historically part of the ancient Kapiśa kingdom, Kafiristan was mentioned by Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang in the 7th century as a prosperous Buddhist region. By the medieval period, it became isolated as surrounding areas Islamized. The last surviving pagan culture, the Kalasha people, persists in Pakistan's Chitral District.  

European explorers, including Bento de Góis (1602) and George Scott Robertson (1890–91), documented Kafiristan's polytheistic traditions before Emir Abdur Rahman Khan conquered and forcibly converted the region, renaming it Nuristan ("Land of the Enlightened"). The area is known for its wooden carvings, some preserved in museums. Today, remnants of Kafiristan's culture survive among the Kalasha, while Nuristan became an Afghan province in 1991.

The Ladies of the House, also known under the working title of Stripped, is a 2014 grindhouse film and the directorial debut of actor John Stuart Wildman. The film had its world premiere on April 4, 2014 at the Dallas International Film Festival, and marks the first non-pornographic film by the former adult actress Belladonna.

Funding for The Ladies of the House was partially raised through a successful Kickstarter campaign.

Donald Clarke Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former ice hockey defenceman who played over 1,100 games in the National Hockey League (NHL), mostly with the Boston Bruins. He ranks among the top ten in many Bruins team statistics, including fourth overall in total games played. After retiring from hockey following the 2003–04 season, he worked briefly as a broadcaster before rejoining the Bruins as a team executive in 2006.

He is the current general manager of the Bruins, a position he has held since May 2015 after replacing Peter Chiarelli.

Ottery St Mary is a historic market town and civil parish in East Devon, England, situated on the River Otter. With a population of 4,898 (2011), it is known for its medieval architecture, including the 13th-century St Mary's Church, often called a "miniature Exeter Cathedral." The town was recorded in the Domesday Book (1086) and later became a collegiate foundation under Bishop John Grandisson in 1337.  

Ottery is the birthplace of poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge, and notable landmarks include The Chanter's House, where he lived, and The King's School, founded in 1545. The town is famous for its Tar Barrels festival, a centuries-old tradition where flaming barrels are carried through the streets on Guy Fawkes Night. Another local custom is Pixie Day, celebrating a legend of pixies banished from the town.  

The town has faced historical events such as a great fire in 1866 and a near-disastrous plane crash in 1980. Today, it retains its rural charm while offering modern amenities, including schools, sports facilities, and cultural events.

Kihikihi is a small town in the Waipa District of New Zealand's Waikato region, with an estimated population of 3,620 as of June 2024. Located along State Highway 3, it is connected to nearby Te Awamutu and Hamilton by bus services and a cycleway. The town has historical significance as the home of Ngāti Maniapoto leader Rewi Maniapoto and surveyor John Rochford, both of whom are commemorated locally. Kihikihi has 20 identified archaeological sites, nine of which are heritage-listed. The town's sole school, Kihikihi School, is a dual-medium primary institution offering education in both English and Te Reo Māori. Demographically, the population is predominantly European/Pākehā (78.8%), with a significant Māori community (29.8%). The area was historically important during the New Zealand Wars as a center of Māori resistance.

Haruka Nishio (西尾 晴香, Nishio Haruka, born March 31, 1994), better known by her stage name Yūka Nishio (西尾 夕香, Nishio Yūka) is a Japanese musician, singer, voice actress and DJ from Kanagawa Prefecture who is affiliated with Hibiki. She was formerly active as a solo musician under the name Haruca, releasing the single "Eien no Kotae" in 2017. She is known for her roles as Nanami Hiromachi in BanG Dream!, where she also serves as the bassist of the band Morfonica, and as Rinku Aimoto, the protagonist of the multimedia franchise D4DJ.

CCM (bicycle company) is a Canadian bicycle manufacturer founded in 1899 through the merger of four major Canadian bicycle producers. Originally known as C.C.M., it became Canada's leading bicycle brand, producing models like the Rambler and Flyte. After financial struggles, it was acquired by Procycle Group in 1983. Today, CCM-branded bikes are sold in Canadian retail stores.

Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American engineer and religious leader who was an apostle in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) from 1918 to 1943.

Lyman is often noted as the most recent LDS Church apostle to have been excommunicated. In 1943, J. Reuben Clark, the first counselor in the LDS church's First Presidency and the church's de facto leader due to church president Heber J. Grant's poor health, became aware that Lyman had for a number of years been in an intimate relationship with a woman he had earlier been assigned to counsel. The Quorum of the Twelve Apostles convened a disciplinary council to hear the case and Lyman's explanation, after which he was immediately excommunicated. Lyman was rebaptized in 1954 at age 83, and his full priesthood blessings were restored posthumously in 1970.

WNED-FM (94.5 MHz) is a non-commercial radio station licensed to Buffalo, New York. WNED-FM offers a classical music radio format. It is owned by the Western New York Public Broadcasting Association (formerly the Western New York Educational TV Association), doing business as Buffalo Toronto Public Media. The organization also operates PBS network affiliate Channel 17 WNED-TV and FM 88.7 WBFO (which offers a news/talk format and programming from NPR). While WNED-FM airs no commercials, it does conduct periodic pledge drives on the air to seek donations for the station. WNED-FM has local hosts in most day-parts, including weekends.

Programming on WNED-FM is simulcast on WNJA 89.7 FM in Jamestown, New York for listeners in Southwestern New York and some parts of Pennsylvania. WNED-FM's studios and offices are at Horizons Plaza on Lower Terrace in Buffalo, and the transmitter is off Zimmerman Road in Hamburg, New York. WNED-FM and TV also maintain an office in Toronto for listeners and contributors in Canada.

STS-50 (U.S. Microgravity Laboratory-1) was a NASA Space Shuttle mission, the 12th mission of the Columbia orbiter. Columbia landed at Kennedy Space Center for the first time ever due to bad weather at Edwards Air Force Base caused by the remnants of Hurricane Darby.

The French Covered Court Championships was a pre-open era indoor tennis tournament held from 1895 to 1971, primarily in Paris. Played on wood courts, it was an early precursor to the modern Paris Masters.
Feliksas Vaitkus (1907–1956), also known as Felix Waitkus, was an American-born Lithuanian pilot and the sixth pilot to fly solo across the Atlantic.

St Andrew's Church is a Grade I listed medieval Church of England parish church in Buckland Monachorum, Devon, rebuilt in the 15th century and restored in 1868–69. It contains notable monuments and an organ installed in 1849.

The Lady in the Lake (1943) is a detective novel by Raymond Chandler, featuring private investigator Philip Marlowe. Hired to find a missing woman, Marlowe uncovers a complex case involving mistaken identities, multiple murders, and police corruption. The story weaves together deception and revenge, culminating in a dramatic confrontation at a remote lake cabin. The novel is considered one of Chandler's finest works, praised for its intricate plot and sharp prose. It was adapted into a 1947 film and multiple radio dramas, including a notable BBC production.

Loop (ループ, Rūpu) is a horror novel by Japanese writer Koji Suzuki, and the third in his series of Ring.

The story revolves around a simulated reality, exactly the same as our own, known as the Loop: created to simulate the emergence and evolution of life. It is in this alternate universe that the events of the previous novels, Ring and Spiral took place.

Loop is the only book in the series to have not been the basis of any Ring films.

The Rodeo–Chediski Fire was a massive wildfire in Arizona in June–July 2002, merging from two separate blazes—one started by arson, the other accidentally. It burned 468,638 acres, destroyed 426 structures, and forced 30,000 evacuations. The fire led to major forest management debates and the Healthy Forests Restoration Act (2003). The arsonist received a 10-year prison sentence, while the accidental starter faced civil penalties. Restoration efforts included aerial seeding and erosion control.

Michael Van Wijk (born 30 September 1952) is an English fitness trainer and former television personality, best known as "Wolf" from the 1990s TV show *Gladiators*. He later moved to New Zealand, where he opened gyms and competed in martial arts. He also briefly played football for Gillingham F.C.'s reserves.

The Arnot Art Museum is a historic art museum in Elmira, New York, housed in a Greek Revival mansion built in 1833. Founded by banker Matthias H. Arnot, it features 19th-century European and American art, including works by Bouguereau, Bierstadt, Cole, and Millet. The museum expanded in 1982 and remains a contributing property to the Elmira Civic Historic District.
Eddy L. Harris (born January 26, 1956) is an American writer and filmmaker known for his travel memoirs exploring African-American identity.
The Antifascist Committee of Ukraine (АФКУ) is a left-wing political organization founded in 2006, led by Communist Party member Heorhiy Buiko.

Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is a co-operative of writers and photographers based in Weinstadt, near Stuttgart, Germany. The agency works for all the major German magazines such as Stern, Focus, GEO, and Die Zeit, as well as for international newspapers and magazines.

The agency’s signature style is reportage.

There is that split second in which a gesture, a disarming look, the play of colours in an iris coalesce into a moment that creates a full-length story.

In 2008, Zeitenspiegel members received awards, including the Ernst Schneider Award of the German Chamber of Industry and Commerce and a first place in the European Parliament Prize for Journalism. In addition to classic photo reportages, the Zeitenspiegel group also takes on short assignments. Zeitenspiegel Reportagen is active in the field of advertising (Daimler, Pfizer) and corporate publishing through their subsidiary agentur.zs.
The Villarreal Offensive (Lang-es) was an offensive of the Spanish Civil War which lasted from 30 November to 24 December 1936. Eusko Gudarostea's 4,300 men fought 600 men of the insurgent forces.

"Love Song" is the fourteenth single by Japanese rock band Luna Sea, released on November 8, 2000. It turned out to be the band's last single, as they held a press conference on the day of its release and announced they would be disbanding. They later reunited in 2010, and released "The One -Crash to Create-" in 2012. "Love Song" reached number 4 on the Oricon Singles Chart, and charted for five weeks.

Saint Vincent Martyr School (SVMS), the oldest Catholic elementary school (established in 1848) in New Jersey, is the coeducational parish school for Saint Vincent Martyr parish in Madison borough, Morris County, New Jersey, United States. SVMS educates approximately 400 students in grades PK-3 through Eighth Grade. The school is named for Vincent of Saragossa. The school nickname is the Eagles.

The school and parish are in the Southeastern Morris Deanery of the Diocese of Paterson of the Province of Newark of the Latin Rite of the Catholic Church.

SVMS was one of three elementary schools in New Jersey, and the only non-public school, recognized by the national Blue Ribbon Schools Program, awarded by the United States Department of Education, for the 2005-06 school year. It is fully accredited by the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools.

Marilyn Suiter is an American geoscientist known for her work in geoscience education and diversity initiatives. She has held leadership roles at the American Geosciences Institute and the National Science Foundation, where she directs programs promoting underrepresented groups in STEM. A fellow of the AAAS, she received the Geological Society of America's Bromery Award.

The Vladimir Lenin monument in Azov is a 10.8-meter granite statue erected in 1978. Designed by sculptor Yuri Orekhov and architects Vladimir Peterburzhtsev and Alexander Stepanov, it features a unique half-figure depiction of Lenin. The pedestal is made of polished granite.

Susan Lynn Gordon (July 27, 1949 – December 11, 2011) was an American child actress who appeared in films and numerous episodes of television programs such as The Twilight Zone, My Three Sons, and The Donna Reed Show.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (26 May 1924 – 27 January 2017) was a French musicologist and biographer, best known for his definitive four-volume biography of Gustav Mahler.

Meri (מר"י) was a left-wing political party in Israel, founded in the 1960s by journalist Uri Avnery. It won seats in the Knesset in 1965 and 1969 but dissolved in the 1970s after internal disputes. It later merged into the Left Camp of Israel ahead of the 1977 elections.

The Oneida Community Mansion House is a historic house and museum that was once the home of the Oneida Community, a religiously-based socialist Utopian group led by John Humphrey Noyes. Noyes and his followers moved to the site in Oneida from Putney, Vermont in 1848. The Community lived in the Mansion House communally until 1880, when they dissolved into a joint-stock company.

The Mansion House has been continually occupied as a residence since its construction in 1862. In the 20th century the Mansion House became a National Historic Landmark. It is currently overseen by a non-profit organization chartered in 1987 by New York State Board of Regents. It also includes residential apartments as well as guest rooms for overnight lodging.
This is a summary of 1984 in music in the United Kingdom, including the official charts from that year.
Hamiota Municipality is an incorporated municipality in the Canadian province of Manitoba.

Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is an Ethiopian author, poet, and crime journalist. Born in Addis Ababa, she began writing poetry in her youth, winning a national competition in 1979. She later published her first short story collection, *Ye Bakene Gize* (*Wasted Time*), in 1990. Bekele worked for 26 years at *Polis Ena Ermijaw*, the Federal Police newspaper, becoming its first female editor. A prominent literary figure, she supports emerging poets and advocates for women's rights, serving as president of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association and chair of the Network for Ethiopian Women's Associations.

Norma Aleandro (born 2 May 1936) is an Argentine actress known for her work in film, television, and theater. Born in Buenos Aires to actor parents, she began her career in the 1950s and gained international recognition for her role in *The Official Story* (1985), winning the Cannes Best Actress Award. Exiled during Argentina's military dictatorship, she returned after democracy was restored. Aleandro also appeared in Hollywood films, earning an Academy Award nomination for *Gaby: A True Story* (1987). A frequent collaborator with actor Héctor Alterio, she starred in acclaimed Argentine films such as *Son of the Bride* (2001) and *The Truce* (1974). Honored as an "Illustrious Citizen of Buenos Aires," she remains a prominent figure in Argentine cinema and theater.

Elections to the Legislative Assembly of the Indian state of Mysore were held on 26 March 1952. 394 candidates contested for 99 seats in 80 constituencies in the Assembly. There were 19 two-member constituencies and 61 single-member constituencies, accounting for 99 seats.

Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus (died 66 AD) was a Roman senator and Stoic philosopher known for his principled opposition to Emperor Nero. As suffect consul in 56, he later gained prominence by challenging imperial authority, notably opposing Nero’s excesses and advocating senatorial independence. His refusal to participate in the Senate’s flattery of Nero led to his eventual trial and forced suicide. Thrasea was also a biographer of Cato the Younger, reinforcing his Stoic ideals. His death, modeled after Socrates and Cato, became a symbol of resistance to tyranny.
Lucie Papineau (born in Longueuil, Quebec) is a Canadian writer and editor, known for her award-winning children's books.

Atlantic Coast Line 1504 is a 4-6-2 USRA Light Pacific steam locomotive built in March 1919 by the American Locomotive Company (ALCO) of Richmond, Virginia, for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad (ACL) as a member of the P-5-A class. It was assigned to pull ACL's mainline passenger trains until it was retired in 1952 and subsequently donated to the city of Jacksonville, Florida, where it sat on static display as the only original USRA Light Pacific steam locomotive to be preserved. In 1990, No. 1504 was designated as a National Historic Mechanical Engineering Landmark by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME).

As of 2022, the locomotive is being restored to operating condition for use in excursion service on the South Central Florida Express shortline railroad in Clewiston, Florida as part of U.S. Sugar's (USSC) heritage tourist passenger train named the Sugar Express, where it will eventually ran alongside ex-Florida East Coast Railway (FEC) No. 148.

Adriaan van Dis (born 16 December 1946) is a Dutch writer and television presenter. Born in Bergen aan Zee to a family with roots in the Dutch East Indies (now Indonesia), his upbringing in a multicultural household deeply influenced his literary work. He studied Dutch and Afrikaans, earning a doctorate in 1979. Van Dis gained fame with his debut novella *Nathan Sid* (1983), which won the Gouden Ezelsoor prize. He also became a well-known television personality, hosting literary programs and travel documentaries, including *Van Dis in Afrika* (2008) and a series on Dutch-Indonesian heritage (2012). His works often explore themes of identity, colonialism, and family trauma.

Connect Four (also known as Connect 4, Four Up, Plot Four, Find Four, Captain's Mistress, Four in a Row, Drop Four, and Gravitrips in the Soviet Union) is a two-player connection board game, in which the players choose a color and then take turns dropping colored tokens into a seven-column, six-row vertically suspended grid. The pieces fall straight down, occupying the lowest available space within the column. The objective of the game is to be the first to form a horizontal, vertical, or diagonal line of four of one's own tokens. Connect Four is a solved game. The first player can always win by playing the right moves.

The game was first sold under the Connect Four trademark by Milton Bradley in February 1974.

Local councils (Hebrew: plural: מוֹעָצוֹת מְקוֹמִיּוֹת‎ Mo'atzot Mekomiot / singular: מוֹעָצָה מְקוֹמִית‎ Mo'atza Mekomit, Arabic: plural: مجالس محليّة Majalis Mahaleea / singular: مجلس محلّي Majlis Mahalee) are one of the three types of local government found in Israel, the other two being cities and regional councils. There are 124 local councils in Israel.

Local councils should not be confused with local committees, which are lower-level administrative entities.

The rusty-margined guan (Penelope superciliaris) is a species of bird in the family Cracidae, which includes the chachalacas, guans, and curassows. It is found in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, and Paraguay.
Joseph Frederick Cotton (January 21, 1922 – May 5, 2016) was an American military test pilot.

Paul Pressler is the chair of the board of directors of eBay. He was previously CEO of Gap, Inc., chairman of Walt Disney Parks and Resorts and a partner at the private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice.

Brontotheriidae is a family of extinct mammals belonging to the order Perissodactyla, the order that includes horses, rhinoceroses, and tapirs. Superficially, they looked rather like rhinos, although they were actually more closely related to horses; Equidae and Brontotheriidae make up the suborder Hippomorpha. They lived around 56–34 million years ago, until the very close of the Eocene.

Villa Park is a neighborhood in West Denver, Colorado, bounded by Lakewood Gulch, Federal Boulevard, the 6th Avenue Freeway, and Sheridan Boulevard. It features hilly terrain, creeks, and parks, with a predominantly Latino population and a mix of residential and commercial areas. The neighborhood is served by the RTD W Line light rail.
Rémire Island is a small, 0.3 km² coral island in the Seychelles' Outer Islands, known for its guano mining history and as a former presidential retreat. It has a population of six.

Klinga Church (Lang-no) is a parish church of the Church of Norway in Namsos municipality in Trøndelag county, Norway. It is located in the village of Klinga. It is the church for the Klinga parish which is part of the Namdal prosti (deanery) in the Diocese of Nidaros. The white, wooden church was built in a long church style in 1866 using plans drawn up by the architects Ernst Kulaas and Jacob Wilhelm Nordan. The church seats about 270 people.

The AN/FPS-24 Radar was a long range early warning radar used by the United States Air Force Air Defense Command. It used a two-frequency signal in order to avoid fluctuation loss, which causes signals on single-frequency radars to fade in and out as the target moves. Reducing this effect results in a much steadier signal.

General Electric was the primary contractor for the design, which operated in the very high frequency (VHF) at 214 to 236 MHz.

Shikantaza (只管打坐) is Dogen's Japanese translation of the Chinese phrase zhǐguǎn dǎzuò (只管打坐 / 祇管 打坐), "just sitting." The phrase was used by his teacher Rujing, a monk of the Caodong school of Zen Buddhism, to refer to the meditation-practice called "Silent Illumination" (Chinese: 默照禅), or "Serene Reflection," taught by the Caodong master Hongzhi Zhengjue (1091-1157). In Japan, it is associated with the Soto school. In shikantaza one does not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath); instead, practitioners "just sit" in a state of conscious awareness.
William Tempest (1682–1761) was an English barrister, prothonotary, and Fellow of the Royal Society.

A lord proprietor was an individual granted royal authority to govern a British colonial territory, holding extensive powers over land, governance, and administration. Notable examples include the proprietors of Maryland, Carolina, and New Jersey, who managed colonies under charters from the English Crown. Many proprietary colonies later transitioned to royal control.

The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships (formerly the IAAF World Race Walking Cup) is a biennial international racewalking competition organized by World Athletics since 1961. Held in various host cities, the event features individual and team races, including the 20 km and 50 km distances, with team rankings determined by the Lugano Trophy. The competition has faced challenges, including relocations due to sanctions (such as Russia's suspension in 2016) and postponements (like the 2020 edition in Minsk due to the COVID-19 pandemic). Doping violations have affected several results, with multiple medalists—including Russians Sergey Kirdyapkin and Olga Kaniskina—later disqualified. The event remains a key fixture in racewalking, promoting clean competition through strict anti-doping measures.

Harry Kroger is an American physicist and electrical engineer. He used to be a Bartle professor of electrical engineering at Binghamton University, a part of the State University of New York (SUNY) system. He has been a member of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) since 1964 and became a Life Fellow of the IEEE in 2001. He initially retired to Florida, then moved back to Austin Texas.

Teresa Dent (born 1959) is a British conservationist and CEO of the Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust since 2001. A former farming consultant, she was appointed CBE in 2015 for services to wildlife conservation.

Winter Haven Hospital is a hospital and health service system based in Winter Haven, Florida. The hospital is managed and supported by the Winter Haven Hospital Foundation—a 501(c)3 non-profit organization—and is owned by BayCare Health System, a group of 15 not-for-profit hospitals in the Tampa Bay Area. The Winter Haven Hospital system includes the hospital proper, the Community Blood Center, the Regency Medical Center for Women and Infants, the Winter Haven Ambulatory Surgery and Diagnostic Center, the Center for Behavioral Health, and seven Family Health Centers in Winter Haven and surrounding communities.

Maharishi Institute of Management (MIM) is a group of private institutes founded by Maharishi Mahesh Yogi with locations in Maharishi Nagar(NOIDA), Bhopal, Indore, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Chennai, India. The Institute is accredited by the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) and offers Graduate and Post Graduate courses in Management and Computer Science. Maharishi Institute of Management is a part of Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's global education movement. MIM is established under the auspices of Maharishi Shiksha Sansthan (MSS).

Marahoué National Park is a national park in Ivory Coast. It was established in 1968, and covers 1,010 km2 (390 sq mi). However, it has lost nearly all its forest cover in the first two decades of the 21st century. Over the years, the park has been damaged and occupied by people and no longer provides suitable habitat for chimpanzees or many other large animals.

Matching logic is a formal logic where statements evaluate to sets of matching values rather than true or false. It operates on patterns, which may belong to different sorts (types). Used in the K Framework, it supports reachability logic and program semantics, and can be translated into first-order logic for automated theorem proving.

Emilie Tolnay (born Emilie Müller: 6 October 1901 - 5 July 1944) was an Austrian hairdresser who by the time of the German annexation in 1938 had become a resistance activist. She died on the guillotine in the Vienna district court complex.
116 (one hundred [and] sixteen) is the natural number following 115 and preceding 117.

The Beach 90th Street station (signed as Beach 90th Street–Holland station) is a station on the IND Rockaway Line of the New York City Subway. It is served by the Rockaway Park Shuttle at all times and ten daily rush-hour only A trains.

Marguerite Louppe (September 5, 1902 – 1989) was a French painter known for her still lifes and landscapes. A student of Parisian academies, she exhibited alongside her husband, Maurice Brianchon, and explored Purism and Cubism before developing a geometric, abstract style in later works.

The Yale Glee Club is a mixed chorus of men and women, consisting of students of Yale University in New Haven, Connecticut. Founded in 1861, it is the third oldest collegiate chorus in the United States after the Harvard Glee Club, founded in 1858, and the University of Michigan Men's Glee Club, founded in 1859. The Glee Club performs several concerts each year in New Haven and goes on tour each January. According to music critic Zachary Woolfe of the New York Times, it is "one of the best collegiate singing ensembles, and one of the most adventurous." Its members are "world famous for their harmonic precision" per New York Times music critic Robert Sherman.

Eskişehirspor Kulübü is a Turkish professional football club located in Eskişehir. Eskisehirspor has a total of six trophies, making them the sixth most trophies holders in Turkey. Founded in 1965, Eskişehirspor competed in the top division of Turkish football for 16 years until they were relegated. After being out of the Süper Lig for 12 years the club won the TFF First League playoffs and were promoted in May 2008. Since being relegated from the Süper Lig in 2016 they have competed in lower leagues.
Stefan Jackiw (/stɛˈfɑːn dʒæˈkiːv/; born 1985) is an American classical violinist.

John Bullock (died 1439–1440) was a Roman Catholic prelate who served as Bishop of Ross from 1418 until his death. A canon lawyer, he was appointed by Avignon Pope Benedict XIII during the Western Schism, though his consecration was later confirmed by Pope Martin V. Little is known of his episcopate, but he witnessed a key political agreement in 1439 before his death the following year.
Odostomia farallonensis is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Pyramidellidae, the pyrams and their allies.
Charlotte Mary Deane MBE (born 1975) is an English Professor of Structural Bioinformatics and the former Head of the Department of Statistics at the University of Oxford.

Jane Mpologoma Nabanakulya (born April 12, 1964) is a Ugandan princess, nurse, and pastor. The daughter of Mutesa II of Buganda, she trained as a nurse in Uganda and Greece before settling in London, where she co-pastors a church with her husband, David Segawa Mukasa.

Thomas Coates (1803 – October 11, 1895) was an American band conductor, composer, and cornetist, often called the "Father of Band Music in America." He led Pomp's Cornet Band and served as conductor of the 47th Pennsylvania Infantry's regimental band during the Civil War. His *Twelfth Funeral March* was performed at Ulysses S. Grant's funeral. A prominent figure in 19th-century band music, he worked with ensembles like P. T. Barnum's circus band and Harvey B. Dodworth's orchestra. He was later honored with a monument in Easton, Pennsylvania.

Dutchman is a 1964 one-act play by Amiri Baraka (then known as LeRoi Jones), first performed at New York City's Cherry Lane Theatre. The play centers on an intense and racially charged encounter between Lula, a provocative white woman, and Clay, a young Black man, aboard a New York City subway train. Through their escalating dialogue—which shifts from flirtation to hostility—Baraka explores themes of racial identity, assimilation, and systemic oppression. The play's title alludes to the Dutch slave ships and the myth of the *Flying Dutchman*, symbolizing the cyclical nature of racial conflict in America. *Dutchman* culminates in a violent climax, underscoring Baraka's critique of racial power dynamics. The play is widely regarded as a landmark work of African-American theater and the Black Arts Movement.

*Black Noise* (1994) is a book by Tricia Rose that analyzes rap music's cultural and political significance in the U.S. through lyrics, videos, and interviews. It won a 1995 American Book Award and is considered a foundational hip-hop study.

Daniël Termont (born 19 May 1953) is a Belgian politician, member of the Socialist Party and former mayor of Ghent, Belgium. He was the son of shopkeepers who lived in Mariakerke. His mother's family was strongly socialistically engaged. In 1971, Daniel obtained a degree in accounting at the Stedelijk Instituut voor Handel en Secretariaat. He did his military service as from April 1972 until April 1973 and worked later on for ten years as a federal charge of LTA (Mutuality of Young Workers), the youth organization of the Socialist Unions at that time. Until 1980 he was also an information officer at the East Flemish Division (Bond Moyson) in the area Ghent-Eeklo. He has been elected to the city council since 1976. Later on, from 1983 until 1988, he was also director of De Ceder holiday centre in Deinze. He was the first director of the centre after a renovation which followed after many years of decline of the institution. Subsequently, from 1988 to 1995, he was secretary-treasurer of Bond Moyson, the year he took his first full-time political office as a councilor in Ghent. From 1995 until 2006, he was alderman of the harbour (Port of Ghent). Between 2001 and 2006 he was also alderman of festivities.

After the municipal elections of 2006 he became mayor of Ghent. He was succeeded on January 1 2019 by Mathias De Clercq of Open VLD.
Fitchville Township is one of the nineteen townships of Huron County, Ohio, United States. As of the 2010 census the population of the township was 1,056.

Marahoué National Park is a national park in central Côte d'Ivoire, established in 1968. Covering 1,000 km² (390 sq mi), it was originally a mix of dense forest, savanna, and gallery forest. However, deforestation and agricultural encroachment have severely degraded the park, with primary forest disappearing by 2014. Once home to diverse wildlife, including chimpanzees and African elephants, many species have declined due to habitat loss and poaching. The park remains an important, though threatened, ecological area.

Top Chef Masters season 2 was the second season of the *Top Chef Masters* reality cooking competition, airing on Bravo from April 7 to June 9, 2010. Twenty-two chefs competed in preliminary rounds, with the top two from each episode advancing to the Champions' Round. The season featured Quickfire and Elimination challenges, with chefs earning stars and prize money for their charities. Marcus Samuelsson won the season, earning $100,000 for his charity.

Amanda DaCosta (born October 7, 1989) is an American-born Portuguese former professional soccer midfielder. She played for clubs in the U.S. and England, including Liverpool Ladies and the Washington Spirit, and represented Portugal internationally. A standout at Florida State University, she was a four-time All-ACC selection and helped lead the Seminoles to multiple NCAA Tournament appearances. After retiring in 2017, she transitioned into coaching and broadcasting.

Samira Ćosović (Serbian Cyrillic: Самира Ћосовић; born 19 November 1973) is a politician in Serbia. She was a member of the National Assembly of Serbia from 2020 to 2022 and is currently the deputy mayor of Prijepolje. Ćosović is a member of the Social Democratic Party of Serbia (SDPS) and also of its regional affiliate, the Sandžak Democratic Party.

HNoMS *Rauma* was an *Otra*-class minesweeper built for the Royal Norwegian Navy in 1939. During the 1940 German invasion of Norway, it engaged enemy forces at the Battle of Horten Harbour before being captured and renamed *Kamerun* by the Kriegsmarine. After World War II, it was returned to Norway, rebuilt as a minelayer training ship, and decommissioned in 1959.

Conscious automatism is a philosophical position asserting that human decision-making, though experienced as free, is entirely determined by prior conditioning and physiological factors. It rejects traditional notions of free will and moral responsibility, aligning with strict determinism. The concept, discussed by thinkers like Thomas Huxley, was recently revisited in Norman Haughness's *Grandest Illusion*, which critiques voluntarist views.

"Wild Wild Life" is a song by American rock band Talking Heads, released as the lead single from their seventh studio album True Stories. It was the band's third and last top 40 hit on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100.

Vellatanjur or Vellatanjoor is a small village in the Thrissur, District of Kerala, India on the banks of Kechery river. This village is located 25 km NorthEast of the Thrissur city, 20 km SouthEast of the Kunnamkulam town and 18 km NorthEast of the Guruvayur township where the famous Guruvayur temple is situated.

The main occupation of the people is farming which you can see from the vast paddy fields there. The main religions there are Hinduism & Christianity. Muslims are not found in Vellatanjur due to a curse that the people there believe has been put by Kootumuchikal Amma the Thatakthamma (Local Goddess) when Tippu Sultan ravished the land during his reign.

The village is very quiet and serene due to its location on the bank of the Kechery river.

Ä (or ä) is a letter used in several alphabets, including Finnish, Swedish, German, and Estonian, representing various vowel sounds such as /æ/ or /ɛ/. It originated from an "A" with a superimposed "E," later stylized as two dots. The letter is also found in some Cyrillic-based and Romani alphabets.

"Jam" is a song by American singer Michael Jackson, released on July 13, 1992, as the third single from his eighth studio album, *Dangerous* (1991). A fusion of new jack swing, industrial funk, and hip-hop, the track was co-written and produced by Jackson, Teddy Riley, Bruce Swedien, and René Moore. It features a rap cameo by Heavy D and received praise from critics for its energetic production and socially conscious lyrics. The music video, directed by David Kellogg, depicts Jackson and basketball legend Michael Jordan teaching each other dance moves and basketball tricks, with appearances by Kris Kross and Naughty by Nature. "Jam" was performed as the opening number on Jackson's *Dangerous World Tour* and at the 1993 Super Bowl halftime show. The song peaked within the top 10 in several countries, including the UK and France.
The Lagoa do Parado Municipal Nature Park (Lang-pt) is a municipal nature park in the state of Paraná, Brazil. It protects an area of marshland rich in biodiversity.

The Villarreal Offensive (November 30 – December 24, 1936) was a failed Republican and Basque attack during the Spanish Civil War to capture Vitoria. Despite initial advances, Nationalist forces repelled the assault, inflicting heavy casualties.

Courtney Andrew Walsh OJ (born 30 October 1962) is a former Jamaican cricketer who represented the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, captaining the West Indies in 22 Test matches. He is a fast bowler and considered one of the all time greats, best known for a remarkable opening bowling partnership along with fellow West Indian Curtly Ambrose for several years. Walsh played 132 Tests and 205 ODIs for the West Indies and took 519 and 227 wickets respectively. He shared 421 Test wickets with Ambrose in 49 matches. He held the record of most Test wickets from 2000, after he broke the record of Kapil Dev. This record was later broken in 2004 by Shane Warne. He was the first bowler to reach 500 wickets in Test cricket. His autobiography is entitled "Heart of the Lion". Walsh was named one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year in 1987. In October 2010, he was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame. He was appointed as the Specialist Bowling Coach of Bangladesh Cricket Team in August 2016.

Mélite, or The False Letters, is a comedy in five acts by Pierre Corneille. Written in 1625, it is Corneille's first play and debuted on stage in December 1629 in Berthaud’s Jeu de paume court, and was performed by the acting troupe of Montdory. Mélite represents Corneille’s creation of a new genre, the comedy of manners, which was a departure from the coarse or buffoonish farce in vogue at the time.

The 2013 Kashiwa Reysol season saw the club compete in the J1 League, Emperor's Cup, J.League Cup, and AFC Champions League. Managed by Nelsinho Baptista, they finished 10th in the league but won the J.League Cup and reached the Champions League semifinals. Masato Kudo was the top scorer with 27 goals across all competitions.

The Charleston Dirty Birds are an American professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia. They are a member of the South Division of the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball, a partner league of Major League Baseball. The Dirty Birds have played their home games at GoMart Ballpark since 2005.

Hardoi is a village in Raebareli district, Uttar Pradesh, India. Located in Maharajganj block, it covers 5.264 km² and had a population of 2,308 in 2011. Historically founded by the Bhar community, it was later ruled by the Jaunpur Sultanate and Mughals. The village features ruins of a mud fort and a tomb linked to local legends.

Attabad Lake is a natural lake in Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan, formed in 2010 after a landslide blocked the Hunza River. The disaster submerged villages, displaced thousands, and destroyed part of the Karakoram Highway. The lake spans 21 km (13 mi) and reaches depths of 109 m (358 ft). A realigned highway with tunnels was completed in 2015 to restore connectivity.

Wilpena Pound (Adnyamathanha: *Ikara*, meaning "meeting place") is a natural amphitheater of mountains in the Flinders Ranges, South Australia. Its highest peak, St Mary Peak, rises to 1,171 meters (3,842 ft). Formed by a synclinal basin, the Pound features steep ridges enclosing a gently sloping interior, with Wilpena Gap as the main entrance. Traditionally significant to the Adnyamathanha people, it was later explored by Europeans in the mid-19th century and used for pastoralism. Today, it is a major tourist destination within Flinders Ranges National Park, offering hiking, scenic flights, and cultural heritage sites.

The Breac Maodhóg (English: Speckled Shrine of Saint Maedog) is a relatively large Irish house-shaped reliquary, today in the National Museum of Ireland. It is thought to date from the second half of the 11th century, and while periods as early as the 9th century have been proposed, the later dating is believed more likely based on the style of its decoration.

The shrine is made from large plates of bronze on a wooden base, on which are placed series of relief figures on bronze plaques. It is best regarded for the individuality of these figure, as well other details in other panels give insight into contemporary ecclesiastical and social practices. At a height of 190mm and width of 92mm, it is relatively large compared to similar objects of its type. It was for centuries kept in a small decorated leather satchel added in the late medieval period, which is slightly too small for the shrine and is thought to have been originally designed to hold a manuscript.

The San Lorenzo march is an Argentine military march, whose lyrics celebrate the role played by the Regiment of Mounted Grenadiers commanded by José de San Martín at the Battle of San Lorenzo during the Argentine War of Independence. Special mention receives the heroic sergeant Juan Bautista Cabral. The music was composed in 1901 by Uruguayan musician Cayetano Alberto Silva, and the lyrics in 1908 by Carlos Javier Benielli. It was later incorporated into the musical repertoires of other military bands around the world.

Mathew Street is a historic street in Liverpool, England, known for its association with music and nightlife. It is home to the famous Cavern Club, where The Beatles performed, and was the site of the influential Eric's Club during the punk era. Originally named after merchant Mathew Pluckington, the street was once the center of Liverpool's wholesale fruit market. A statue of psychoanalyst Carl Jung, inspired by his dream of Liverpool, stands on the street. Today, Mathew Street remains a key part of the city's cultural and entertainment scene.

Brian C. J. Moore (born February 10, 1946) is a British psychologist and psychoacoustician known for his research on hearing, pitch perception, and hearing aid design. A professor emeritus at the University of Cambridge, he has contributed to models of loudness perception, auditory scene analysis, and diagnostic tests for hearing loss. His work on temporal fine structure in sound perception has influenced hearing aid and cochlear implant technology. Moore is the author of *An Introduction to the Psychology of Hearing*, a widely cited textbook. He is a Fellow of the Royal Society (FRS) and the Academy of Medical Sciences (FMedSci).

In medicine, an avulsion is an injury in which a body structure is torn off by either trauma or surgery (from the Latin avellere, meaning "to tear off"). The term most commonly refers to a surface trauma where all layers of the skin have been torn away, exposing the underlying structures (i.e., subcutaneous tissue, muscle, tendons, or bone). This is similar to an abrasion but more severe, as body parts such as an eyelid or an ear can be partially or fully detached from the body.

The 1913 Reading by-election was held after Liberal MP Rufus Isaacs was elevated to the House of Lords. Conservative Leslie Orme Wilson won the seat, narrowly defeating Liberal George Peabody Gooch and socialist J. G. Butler.
Mykhailo Polyanychko (1921–19??) was a Soviet Ukrainian diplomat who served as Ukraine's Permanent Representative to the United Nations from 1968 to 1973.

The Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles is a museum in Bangkok, Thailand, located within the Grand Palace. Housed in the historic Ratsadakorn-bhibhathana Building (built in 1873), it was repurposed in 2003 under Queen Sirikit's initiative to showcase Thai textiles and cultural heritage. The museum preserves and exhibits traditional fabrics and royal attire.
Manilal Gandhi (28 October 1892 – 5 April 1956) was the second son of Mahatma Gandhi, an editor of *Indian Opinion*, and a key figure in South Africa's Indian civil rights movement.

William J. Maddox Jr. (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a United States Army major general and highly decorated Army aviator, earning four Silver Stars, eight Distinguished Flying Crosses, and 127 Air Medals. He served in World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War, commanding key aviation units including the 164th Aviation Group and the U.S. Army Aviation Center. After retiring in 1976, he advised King Hussein of Jordan and led aviation programs in Saudi Arabia and Asia. Maddox was buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

Karlo Matković (born 30 March 2001) is a Bosnian-Croatian professional basketball player for Cedevita Olimpija. Drafted 52nd overall by the New Orleans Pelicans in 2022, he plays as a power forward/center.

The 2011–12 Kategoria Superiore was the 73rd season of Albania's top football league, running from September 2011 to May 2012. Skënderbeu won their third title, qualifying for the Champions League, while Teuta, Tirana, and Flamurtari earned Europa League spots. Kamza, Pogradeci, and Dinamo Tirana were relegated. The league expanded to 14 teams, with Pogradeci, Tomori, Kamza, and Apolonia promoted. Roland Dervishi was the top scorer with 20 goals.

St. Mary's High School is a co-educational school located in the Cantonment area in Belagavi, near the Head Post Office of Belagavi, India and is regulated and supervised by Karnataka Secondary Education Examination Board. It was founded in 1851 and is celebrating its 165th jubilee in 2016. Near by educational institutions are Islamiya College and B K Model High School.

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music artist James Otto. It was released in July 2007 as the first single from his album Sunset Man. On the Hot Country Songs chart dated for May 17, 2008, the song has also become Otto's first (and to date, his only) number-one hit. The song was also the number one song on Billboard's year-end 2008 Hot Country Songs chart.
Egypt competed at the 2014 Summer Youth Olympics, in Nanjing, China from 16 August to 28 August 2014.
Tasneem Roc (born 11 December 1978) is an Australian actress known for her roles in *East West 101*, *Reef Doctors*, and *Kath & Kimderella*.

Jefferson Joseph DeBlanc (February 15, 1921 – November 22, 2007) was an American World War II Marine Corps fighter pilot and flying ace, credited with shooting down nine Japanese aircraft during two tours of duty in the Pacific at Guadalcanal and Okinawa. On January 31, 1943, despite mechanical problems with his aircraft, he pressed forward with a mission and shot down five Japanese aircraft in minutes before being shot down himself. DeBlanc parachuted to safety and was rescued by native islanders, traded for a sack of rice, and picked up by a Navy seaplane. He received the Medal of Honor for his actions that day. He served in the Marine Corps Reserve until 1972. In that time, he also taught school and completed graduate and doctoral degrees in math, physics and education.

Mafdet was an ancient Egyptian goddess associated with justice, execution, and protection. Often depicted as a feline (such as a cheetah or serval), a mongoose, or a woman with such an animal's head, she was believed to defend Ra from threats during his nightly journey and aid in the resurrection of Osiris. Mafdet was also linked to Anubis in royal tombs, suggesting a role in hunting or executing wrongdoers. She was said to rip out the hearts of the guilty, presenting them to the pharaoh. In the New Kingdom, she was thought to rule over a judgment hall in the afterlife, where enemies of the pharaoh were decapitated with her claw. Her imagery frequently appeared on executioners' staffs, emphasizing her fierce and protective nature.

Gold Hill, Alabama is an unincorporated community in Lee County, Alabama. Settled in the 1830s, it was named after an early settler rather than gold deposits. Though briefly renamed "Gold Ridge" by the postal service, locals retained the original name. The community once had a historic general store barn, demolished in 2010, and was home to epilepsy scientist William P. Spratling. A post office operated from 1837 to 1967.
