"I'd Go to Jail" is a song written and recorded by Canadian country artist Dean Brody. The song was released to radio in October 2021, as the fifth single from Brody's seventh studio album Boys.
The Belgium–Korea Treaty of 1901 established diplomatic and trade relations between Belgium and Korea, following earlier Western treaties.

Marilyn J. Suiter is a geologist whose professional career has spanned teaching, working the oil and gas industry and public services. In her leadership roles at both the American Geosciences Institute and the National Science Foundation, Suiter has worked over decades to increase the ethnic diversity of the geosciences.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by English musician Elton John and songwriter Bernie Taupin, and performed by John. It was originally released on John's eponymous second album in 1970.

It was recorded at Trident Studios in London in January 1970 and released in the United States in October 1970 as the A-side of a single, with "Your Song" as the B-side. Both songs received airplay, but "Your Song" was preferred by disc jockeys, becoming the singer's first hit (on both sides of the Atlantic) and rendering "Take Me To The Pilot" as the B-side.

The country duo Brothers Osborne recorded a remake of the song for the 2018 tribute album Restoration: Reimagining the Songs of Elton John and Bernie Taupin.

CBS Playhouse is an American anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. Airing twelve plays over the course of its run, the series won ten Primetime Emmy Awards and featured many noteworthy actors and playwrights.

Johann Friedrich Christian Hessel (27 April 1796 – 3 June 1872) was a German physician (MD, University of Würzburg, 1817) and professor of mineralogy (PhD, University of Heidelberg, 1821) at the University of Marburg.

Oscar Braynon (born February 1, 1977) is an American Democratic politician who served in the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing parts of Miami-Dade County. He was Senate Minority Leader from 2016 to 2018. Previously, he served in the Florida House of Representatives (2008–2011) and on the Miami Gardens City Council (2003–2008).

The Irish Centre for High-End Computing (ICHEC) is a national high-performance computing (HPC) facility based in Dublin and Galway, Ireland. Established in 2005 and operated by NUI Galway, it provides supercomputing resources, research support, and industry collaboration in computational science and engineering.

Declo is a small city in Cassia County, Idaho, United States, with a population of 343 as of the 2010 census. Originally named Marshfield, it was renamed in 1916. The J.R. Simplot Company was founded nearby in 1923.
*Live in Boston* is a 1985 live album by Fleetwood Mac, recorded in February 1970 at the Boston Tea Party. It was later expanded into a multi-volume reissue under different titles.
Joseph F. Cotton (1922–2016) was a U.S. Air Force colonel, test pilot, and airline pilot who led the B-58 and XB-70 test programs.

Elections were held in the Australian state of Queensland on 19 May 1956 to elect the 75 members of the state's Legislative Assembly. The Labor government was seeking its ninth continuous term in office since the 1932 election; it would be Vince Gair's second election as Premier.

Robert Žák (born 6 May 1966) is a Czech former footballer and manager. As a midfielder, he played in the Czechoslovak First League for Slavia Prague, Hradec Králové, and České Budějovice. Later, he managed several clubs, including České Budějovice, Most, Bohemians Prague, and Litvínov, with limited success.

Michael Van Wijk (born 30 September 1952) is an Indian-born English bodybuilder, professional sportsperson, actor and TV presenter. He is most well known for his role as Wolf on British TV endurance sports game show Gladiators, a role he held for all its eight series (1992–1999).
In Search of Sunrise (abbreviated ISOS) is a trance music mix-compilation music CD series released on SongBird, a subdivision of Black Hole Recordings originally founded by Tiësto.

The University of St. Thomas School of Law is one of the professional graduate schools of University of St. Thomas in Minnesota, United States. It is one of three law schools in Minneapolis–Saint Paul. It currently enrolls 434 students. St. Thomas Law is accredited by the American Bar Association. It is also a member of The Association of American Law Schools. The University of St. Thomas School of Law is the second highest ranked law school in Minnesota amongst the state's three law schools. Founded in 1999, the School of Law graduated its first class in 2004.

Kris Fisher is a fictional character from the British Channel 4 soap opera Hollyoaks, played by Gerard McCarthy. The character first appeared on-screen in September 2006 and left in August 2010 after McCarthy quit the show. He is noted for his storylines involving the PEP drug, a bisexual love triangle, his outlandish personality and being the only permanent cross dressing character in a British soap opera. Following his departure, it was announced that McCarthy would return in November 2010 for a short stint.

Major prion protein (PrP) is a protein encoded by the *PRNP* gene in humans and plays a crucial role in prion diseases, a group of fatal neurodegenerative disorders. The normal cellular form, PrP<sup>C</sup>, is a glycoprotein anchored to cell membranes, primarily expressed in the nervous system and involved in copper binding, cell signaling, and synaptic function. Its misfolded isoform, PrP<sup>Sc</sup>, is protease-resistant and aggregates in the brain, leading to transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSEs) such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease and scrapie.  

PrP<sup>C</sup> has a predominantly α-helical structure, while PrP<sup>Sc</sup> is rich in β-sheets, facilitating its pathogenic aggregation. Polymorphisms in *PRNP* influence disease susceptibility, with some variants conferring resistance. Beyond prion diseases, PrP interacts with amyloid-beta in Alzheimer’s disease and modulates synaptic plasticity, memory, and immune responses. Research on PrP knockout mice reveals roles in circadian rhythm, neuronal survival, and metal ion homeostasis. The protein’s exact physiological function remains under investigation, but its misfolding is central to multiple neurodegenerative conditions.
Hanna Yussuf Atik (born 7 February 1959) is a Lebanese politician, and veteran of the Lebanese Civil War.

Qualified teacher status (QTS) is a professional accreditation in England and Wales required to teach in state-maintained schools. It is typically obtained through a teacher training program, such as a Postgraduate Certificate in Education (PGCE), School Direct, or an undergraduate degree with QTS. Candidates must meet academic requirements, pass skills tests, and complete an induction period. QTS is recognized across the UK and internationally, though requirements vary. Some schools, such as academies and independent schools, do not require QTS.

Henry Nicholas John Gunther (June 6, 1895 – November 11, 1918) was an American soldier and possibly the last soldier of any of the belligerents to be killed during World War I. He was killed at 10:59 a.m., about one minute before the Armistice was to take effect at 11:00 a.m.

Gunther had recently been demoted, and was seeking to regain his rank just before the war ended.

The Right (Italian: *La Destra*) was a far-right political party in Italy, founded in 2007 by Francesco Storace after splitting from National Alliance. It merged into the National Movement for Sovereignty in 2017.

The history of Koreans in Houston traces the growth of the Korean American community in the city since the 1970s. Initially small, the population grew significantly by the 1980s, with businesses, churches, and cultural organizations forming in areas like Spring Branch. By the 2010s, the community had established schools, festivals, and advocacy groups. Korean Air briefly operated a direct flight to Seoul, reflecting the community's economic and cultural ties to South Korea.
Thomas Zander Bleck Jr. (born 1985), known professionally as Zander Bleck, is an American recording artist, vocalist, and songwriter.

Malese Jow (born Elizabeth Melise Jow) is an American actress known for her roles in television and film. She gained recognition as Geena Fabiano in *Unfabulous* (2004–2007) and later played Anna in *The Vampire Diaries* (2010–2011). Jow starred in *Star-Crossed* (2014) and appeared as Linda Park/Dr. Light in *The Flash* (2014–2015). Her other notable roles include *Big Time Rush*, *The Shannara Chronicles*, and *NCIS: Los Angeles*. Born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, she began acting as a child and has worked in both live-action and animated projects.
Léonie Thévenot d'Aunet (2 July 1820 – 3 March 1879) was a French author, novelist, playwright and Arctic explorer.

Rhydymwyn (the name in Welsh means 'Ford of the Ore' and takes its placename from the ford across the River Alyn now replaced by a small iron bridge) is a village in Flintshire, Wales, located in the upper Alyn valley. Once a district of Mold, it was recognised as a separate parish from 1865. It is now part of the community of Cilcain.

E. S. Rose Park is a baseball, softball, soccer, and track & field venue in Nashville, Tennessee, United States. It is home to Nashville Union FC and Belmont Bruins baseball, softball, men's and women's soccer, and men's and women's track & field teams of the NCAA Division I Ohio Valley Conference. It is located approximately five minutes from Belmont University's main campus. The city-owned park was renovated extensively in 2010–2011.

Endohedral fullerenes, also called endofullerenes, are fullerenes that have additional atoms, ions, or clusters enclosed within their inner spheres. The first lanthanum C60 complex called La@C60 was synthesized in 1985. The @ (at sign) in the name reflects the notion of a small molecule trapped inside a shell. Two types of endohedral complexes exist: endohedral metallofullerenes and non-metal doped fullerenes.

ETC Group (formerly RAFI) is an international eco-justice organization advocating for farmers' rights, biodiversity, and responsible governance of emerging technologies. Founded in the 1970s, it campaigns against genetic engineering, biopiracy, and corporate monopolies on seeds. The group also opposes geoengineering and synthetic biology, pushing for stricter regulations. Known for coining terms like "biopiracy," ETC Group has influenced global policies, including the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources.

Spirito DiVino is the seventh studio album by Italian singer-songwriter Zucchero, released on 27 May 1995 by Polydor Records. Produced by Corrado Rustici, it blends soft rock, pop rock, and blues rock, featuring collaborations with Jeff Beck, Francesco De Gregori, and Sheila E. The album topped Italian charts, selling over 1.1 million copies domestically and 2.5 million worldwide, earning 11× Platinum certification in Italy. It was supported by the Spirito DiVino World Tour.

Muttrah, (Lang-ar) administratively a wilayah (province), is located in the Muscat Governorate of Oman. Before the discovery of oil in Oman, Muttrah was the center of commerce in Oman (Muscat). It is still a center of commerce as one of the largest sea ports of the region is located there. Other landmarks include Souq Muttrah, a traditional bazaar and Sour Al-Lawatiah, a small community of houses surrounded by an old wall. To the south lies Muscat District.
The Pontifical Congregation of the Benedictine Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face is a Roman Catholic order whose focus is providing Acts of Reparation to Jesus Christ.

The Apostolic Faith Church is a Holiness Pentecostal denomination founded in 1907 by Florence L. Crawford, a participant in the Azusa Street Revival. Headquartered in Portland, Oregon, it operates in 37 countries with over 715 congregations and 5,000 members. The church traces its origins to the Pentecostal movement and maintains Wesleyan-Holiness theology, emphasizing sanctification, Spirit baptism, and speaking in tongues as evidence.  

Crawford separated from William J. Seymour over doctrinal differences, taking control of *The Apostolic Faith* newspaper, which remains a key publication. The church teaches conditional security, modesty in dress, and abstinence from alcohol and tobacco. Unlike many Pentecostal groups, it does not pass an offering plate but allows voluntary donations. Services include testimonies, hymns, and altar calls, with ministers chosen by laying on of hands rather than formal training.  

During the COVID-19 pandemic, the church received federal loans to retain jobs. It continues outreach through literature distribution, bus ministries, and prison evangelism. The current Superintendent General is Darrel D. Lee.

Vagif Mustafazadeh (Lang-az; March 16, 1940 – December 16, 1979), also known as Vaqif Mustafa-Zadeh, was a Soviet-Azerbaijani jazz pianist and composer, acclaimed for fusing jazz and the traditional Azerbaijani folk music known as mugham. According to many world famous jazz musicians, Mustafazadeh is one of the pioneers and "the architect of jazz in Azerbaijan".

Ro-45 was an Imperial Japanese Navy Kaichū type submarine of the K6 sub-class. Completed and commissioned in January 1944, she served in the central Pacific Ocean during World War II and was sunk in April 1944 during her first combat sortie.
Nina Edith Coote (23 September 1883 – 6 January 1945) was an Irish croquet player.

Somalia first participated at the Olympic Games at the 1972 Summer Games in Munich, West Germany; the Somali Olympic Committee being recognised by the International Olympic Committee shortly prior. The nation has sent athletes to compete in most Summer Olympic Games since then, boycotting in 1976 due to the inclusion of New Zealand, and in 1980 when it joined with the American-led boycott. It also did not compete in 1992 due to the ongoing effects of a famine. Somalia entered their largest contingent of athletes at the 1984 Summer Olympics, a total of seven.

Fuxi (伏羲) is a mythological figure in Chinese tradition, regarded as one of the Three Sovereigns and a cultural hero. Alongside his sister Nüwa, he is credited with creating humanity and establishing social institutions like marriage. Fuxi is said to have invented fishing, hunting tools, and the I Ching, inspired by the He Map. He is often depicted with a serpent’s body and a human head, symbolizing his divine origins. His legacy is honored in temples, including one in Huaiyang, Henan.

Steve Jameson (born May 6, 1973) is an American artist and follower of Avatar Meher Baba, best known for his religious paintings of Meher Baba under the name "Wodin" and his landscape works as "Cedar." He has illustrated books by Meher Baba's disciples and designed the *Daddy God* art series. Jameson lives in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina, where he runs a sign shop and art studio.

Sendle is an Australian carbon-neutral parcel delivery service founded in 2014 by James Bradfield Moody, Sean Geoghegan, and Craig Davis. Headquartered in Sydney, it offers domestic shipping and integrates with e-commerce platforms like eBay and Shopify. The company won a 2016 trademark dispute against Australia Post and expanded internationally through a partnership with DHL. Sendle has raised over $60 million in funding.
Michael Klein (10 October 1959 – 2 February 1993) was a Romanian footballer who played as a left back for Corvinul Hunedoara, Dinamo București, and Bayer Uerdingen, earning 89 caps for Romania.

Brian O'Connor (born February 14, 1953) is an American actor, comedian, and guidance counselor, best known for his role as Horace "Schemer" Schemer on the children's TV series *Shining Time Station* (1989–1993). He began his career in comedy with the troupe Slap Happy before appearing in films like *Beverly Hills Cop II* (1987) and TV shows such as *Miami Vice*. Since 2005, he has worked as a children's counselor while continuing occasional acting and musical performances.

Vyacheslav Yaikov (born March 1, 1974) is a Russian serial killer known as "The Kopeysk Strangler." Between 2002 and 2003, he murdered six women in Chelyabinsk, strangling and raping his victims. Diagnosed with mental illness, he was declared legally insane and sentenced to compulsory psychiatric treatment in 2008.

Khan Bahadur Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr (1833-1889), better known by his pen name Nassakh (Lang-ur), was a British Indian officer, writer, literary critic and collector. He is best known for his magnum opus Sukhan-e-Shuara (Lang-ur Speech of Poets) which was a biography of prominent Urdu and Persian poets. He organised mushaira in places where he worked; inspiring young Urdu poets in Bengal.
The story of Little Muck is a fairy tale written by Wilhelm Hauff. It was published in 1826 in a collection of fairy tales and tells the story of an outsider called Little Muck.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, until he faced death threats and was assaulted forcing him to flee.

Janice Charette is a Canadian public servant serving as the clerk of the Privy Council and secretary to the Cabinet. She previously held the role from 2014 to 2016, before being appointed as the Canadian high commissioner to the United Kingdom in 2016.

She has been recognized as one of Canada’s Top 100 Most Powerful Women in 2013, 2014, and 2015.
Thomas Zander Bleck (born 1985) is an American singer-songwriter known for his rock and electro-pop music, including the singles *Temptation* and *Bring It On*.

Bunyan and Babe is a 2017 American computer-animated comedy film directed by Louis Ross, featuring the voices of John Goodman as Paul Bunyan and Jeff Foxworthy as Babe the Blue Ox. The film first premiered online on Google Play.

It has been made available to watch for free from January 12, 2017 to February 16, 2017. Then, it was theatrically released in 12 American markets on February 3, 2017.
Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev (Lang-bg) also known as Konstantin (Lang-bg) is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. He was the winner of the Bulgarian reality show VIP Brother 1 in 2006.

Odd Reidar Humlegård is a Norwegian police officer who served as Police Commissioner from 2012. Previously, he led the National Mobile Police Service (2003–2009) and the National Criminal Investigation Service (2009–2012), overseeing high-profile cases like *The Pocket Man*. He graduated from the Norwegian Police University College and later earned a law degree.

Pudliszki Sp. z o.o. is a brand and producer in the food industry of fruit and vegetables. Presently, the brand is under the American company Heinz, which holds considerable shares in the company. Pudliszki focuses on the production of tomato goods (ketchup and concentrate) and vegetables (peas, beans and corn).

Common ownership refers to the collective ownership of property or assets by a group, rather than by individuals. It has historical roots in early Christian communities and is practiced today by groups like the Hutterites and Bruderhof. In socialist and Marxist theory, common ownership of the means of production is seen as a key feature of a classless, post-scarcity society. In capitalist economies, it appears in cooperatives, nonprofits, and public institutions. Antitrust economists study common ownership in competing firms, where overlapping investors may reduce market competition. Contract theory examines joint ownership's efficiency, suggesting it can be optimal under certain conditions, despite traditional economic views favoring single ownership.

Bustard Island is Alberta's largest sand island, It is located in Lake Athabasca in the part of the Fidler-Greywillow Wildland Park. The Lucas Islands lie northeast from the island, the other major island in the park; Burntwood Island lays 11.8 kilometres (7.3 mi) to the north.

Rami Mehmed Pasha (1645–1706) was an Ottoman statesman and poet who served as Grand Vizier (1703) and governor of Cyprus and of Egypt (1704–06). He was known as a poet of divan literature (the epithet Rami, meaning "Obedient", is his pen name in his poems).

Culbert Levy Olson (November 7, 1876 – April 13, 1962) was an American lawyer and politician. A Democratic Party member, Olson was involved in Utah and California politics and was elected as the 29th governor of California from 1939 to 1943.

The Mercedes-Benz S-Series was a line of high-performance sports cars produced from 1926 to 1933, including the Model S, SS, SSK, and SSKL, known for their powerful supercharged engines and racing success.

Tbilisoba (Georgian: თბილისობა) is an annual October festival which celebrates the diversity and history of Tbilisi, the capital of Georgia. It was first held on October 28, 1979, and has since become an established tradition. The festival features open-air concerts of traditional music and dancing and various cultural events, centered on Old Tbilisi, the historical part of the city. Beyond celebrating the city's past and present, people from all over Georgia represent their region at the fair of the harvest and Rtveli. Awarding honorary citizenship of Tbilisi by the city government also occurs in the framework of Tbilisoba.
George Hall (September 21, 1795 - April 16, 1868) was an American businessman and politician who served as the first Mayor of Brooklyn.

The Holy Science is a book written by Swami Sri Yukteswar Giri in 1894 under the title Kaivalya Darsanam. Sri Yukteswar states that he wrote The Holy Science at the request of Mahavatar Babaji. The book compares parallel passages from the Bible and Upanishads in order to show the unity of all religions.

The Katkari also called Kathodi, are an Indian tribe from Maharashtra. They have been categorised as a Scheduled tribe. They are bilingual, speaking the Katkari language, a dialect of the Marathi-Konkani languages, with each other; they speak Marathi with the Marathi speakers, who are a majority in the populace where they live. In Maharashtra the Katkari have been designated a Particularly vulnerable tribal group (PVTG), along with two other groups included in this sub-category: the Madia Gond and the Kolam. In the case of the Katkari this vulnerability derives from their history as a nomadic, forest-dwelling people listed by the British Raj under the Criminal Tribes Act of 1871, a stigma that continues to this day.
"Kaintuck'" is a symphonic poem by William Grant Still, inspired by a train trip to Kentucky. It premiered in 1935 and evokes the sound of a steam locomotive.

Jhajhar is a village, situated in the erstwhile province of Shekhawati of Rajasthan, India. It is located in the district of Jhunjhunu, approximately 7 km from Nawalgarh. Formally it was the part of Pentalisa of Bhojyana.

The Black Country derby is a football rivalry between clubs in England's Black Country, primarily contested between West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers. One of the oldest league derbies, their first official meeting was in the 1885–86 FA Cup. The rivalry peaked in the 1950s when both clubs competed for top honors, including a 1954 Charity Shield match. Fixtures have often been heated, with notable clashes in the 2006–07 season and Premier League meetings in the 2010s. Walsall also occasionally features in derbies.

TV Sound and Image, British Television, Film and Library Composers 1956-80 is a 2012 compilation album released by Soul Jazz Records. The album consists of a style of music known as Library music, music developed by production houses with the intent that the recordings would be licensed to various film, television, and radio programs.

HMS *Fairy* was a C-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy in 1897. She served in Home Waters and was part of the Channel Fleet before World War I. During the war, she operated with the Grand Fleet and later the 7th Destroyer Flotilla, conducting convoy duties. On 31 May 1918, she sank the German submarine UC-75 in a ramming attack but was fatally damaged and foundered. She was awarded the battle honour "Belgian Coast 1914–17".

DXWT (92.3 FM), branded as Wild FM, is a radio station in Davao City, Philippines, owned by the UM Broadcasting Network. Launched in 1949 as AM station DXMC, it transitioned to FM in 1988 and adopted its current branding. Known for its dance and contemporary music format, Wild FM pioneered extended dance mixes and street discos, becoming a leading station in the region. It was also the first in Mindanao to use HD Radio technology.

The 2018 Atlanta United FC season was the second season of Atlanta United FC's existence, and the tenth year that a professional soccer club from Atlanta, Georgia competed in the top division of American soccer. Atlanta played their home matches at Mercedes-Benz Stadium and were coached by Gerardo "Tata" Martino. Outside of MLS, the team participated in the 2018 U.S. Open Cup and the 2018 MLS Cup Playoffs, as well as various preseason competitions.

On November 17, 2017, Atlanta United announced the launch of a reserve team, Atlanta United 2, to play in what is currently named the USL Championship at the start of the 2018 season.

With a win over Chicago Fire on October 21, Atlanta qualified for the 2019 CONCACAF Champions League by having the best aggregate record across the 2017 and 2018 MLS seasons. The club later qualified for the competition by winning MLS Cup 2018, so their previously-held spot was allocated to the team with the second best aggregate record, New York Red Bulls.

Chandler School is a private coeducational K-8 school in Pasadena, California, founded in 1950 by Thomas and Catherine Chandler. With an enrollment of 450 students, it offers a STEAM curriculum and emphasizes athletics, featuring 11 team sports. The school moved to its current campus in 1958 and expanded over the decades.

The Stone Flower (Russian: *Каменный цветок*) is a 1946 Soviet fantasy film directed by Aleksandr Ptushko, based on Pavel Bazhov's folktale of the same name. The story follows Danilo, a gifted gemcutter who becomes obsessed with crafting a perfect stone flower, leading him to the mystical Mistress of the Copper Mountain. The film blends fairy-tale elements with socialist realism, emphasizing craftsmanship and perseverance. It became a cultural touchstone in the USSR, popularizing a famous Russian catchphrase. While praised for its performances, Bazhov noted its lack of authentic Ural atmosphere. Produced by Mosfilm, the movie remains a notable early example of Soviet fantasy cinema.

The Kinburn Spit (Lang-uk) is a spit in Mykolaiv Raion, Mykolaiv Oblast, Ukraine. It occupies the westernmost part of the Kinburn Peninsula, being its natural extension stretching west into the Black Sea between the Dnieper-Bug estuary and the Bay of Yahorlyk. It is approximately 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) long, with a width of about 1 kilometre (0.62 mi) at its base, narrowing to about 100 metres (330 ft) in its western half.
Giorgio Zur (15 February 1930 – 8 January 2019) was a Catholic archbishop and diplomat of the Holy See.

Tamika Tremaglio is an American business executive and lawyer. She served as Managing Principal for Deloitte's Greater Washington Area before becoming executive director of the National Basketball Players Association (NBPA). Recognized for her leadership, she was named *Managing Partner of the Year* by the *Washington Business Journal* and one of Washington's *150 Most Powerful Women* by *Washingtonian Magazine*. She holds a JD and an MBA.

Sajek Valley (Bengali: সাজেক উপত্যকা) is a scenic hill station in Bangladesh's Rangamati District, near the Indian border. Known as the "Roof of Rangamati," it features mountains, forests, and rivers like the Sajek and Machalong. The region is home to indigenous communities, including Chakma, Marma, and Tripuri people, and is popular for its natural beauty and cultural diversity.

A gharana is a system of social organization in Indian classical music and dance, linking musicians or dancers through lineage or apprenticeship to a particular style. In khyal singing, gharanas dictate nuances like phrasing, improvisation, and rhythmic emphasis. Major dhrupad gharanas include four traditional styles, while thumri gharanas vary in poetic embellishment and regional influences. Tabla and Kathak also follow distinct gharanas, each with unique techniques and repertoires. The system emerged as musicians migrated from Mughal courts to regional princely states, preserving and evolving their artistic traditions.

Günther Krampf (8 February 1899 – 4 August 1950) was an Austrian cinematographer who later settled and worked in the UK. Krampf has been described as a "phantom of film history" because of his largely forgotten role working on a number of important films during the silent and early sound era. Only two of Krampf's films The Student of Prague (1926) and The Ghoul (1933) were expressionist, as he generally used a naturalistic style.

Our Lady and St Peter's Church is a Roman Catholic parish church in Bridlington, England. Built in 1893–1894 in the Gothic Revival style by Smith, Brodrick & Lowther, it replaced an earlier tin tabernacle. The Grade II-listed church serves the Our Lady Star of the Sea parish, alongside St George's in Eastfield and St Mary's in Filey. Masses are held weekly.

Jamiul Uloom Jawadia, also known as Jawadia Arabic College, is a Deeni Madarasa (religious educational institution) of Shia Muslims in Banaras (Varanasi) India. Major course of studies include Jurisprudence, Theology and Islamic Literature.

Bill Sizemore (born June 2, 1951) is an American conservative activist and politician known for his ballot initiatives in Oregon. A Republican, he founded *Oregon Taxpayers United* and successfully campaigned for tax-limiting measures, including Measure 47 (1996) and Measure 7 (2000). He ran for Governor of Oregon in 1998 and 2010, losing both times. His organizations were found liable in a racketeering lawsuit filed by teachers' unions, though he was later cleared of personal liability. In 2008, he was briefly jailed for contempt of court, and in 2011, he pleaded guilty to misdemeanor tax evasion. Before politics, Sizemore worked in business, radio, and education.

Day of Defeat is a team-based multiplayer first-person shooter video game set in the European theatre of World War II on the Western front. Originally a modification of the 1998 game Half-Life, the rights of the modification were purchased by Valve and released as a full retail title in 2003.

Set in the midst of World War II, Day of Defeat includes no single-player campaign, with focus left only on the game's multiplayer aspects. The game favors teamwork and features objective-based gameplay in combination with its system of classes. Maps are primarily made up of narrow paths, all of which typically lead to a few key locations. An official remake of the game, Day of Defeat: Source, was released by Valve in 2005.

Oksana Alexandrovna Kostina (Lang-ru; 15 April 1972 – 11 February 1993) was a Soviet and Russian individual rhythmic gymnast. She was the 1992 World All-around champion and 1992 European All-around bronze medalist.

The Republic of Benin was a short-lived puppet state of Biafra during the Nigerian Civil War. Proclaimed on September 19, 1967, it was established after Biafran forces occupied Nigeria's Mid-Western Region and appointed Albert Okonkwo as governor. The republic sought to gain local support by declaring independence from both Nigeria and Biafra, but it collapsed the next day when Nigerian federal troops recaptured Benin City. The failed state deepened ethnic tensions and hardened non-Igbo opposition to Biafra, contributing to the escalation of the war. The Republic of Benin received no international recognition.

Nomads R.F.C. is a Hong Kong rugby club founded in 1989, competing in the G4S League. Initially focused on developing local Chinese players, it has grown into an integrated club with multiple teams. Based at Tai Hang Tung, Nomads has achieved success in lower divisions and maintains ties with youth rugby through the Flying Kukris RFC.

"Charles" is a short story by Shirley Jackson, first published in Mademoiselle in July 1948. It was later included in her 1949 collection, The Lottery and Other Stories, and her 1953 novel, Life Among the Savages.

Urbanus is a Belgian comic strip created by Willy Linthout in 1982, based on the Flemish comedian Urbanus. The absurd and often vulgar humor follows a mischievous, bearded 12-year-old version of Urbanus in his hometown of Tollembeek. Originally published by Uitgeverij Loempia and later Standaard Uitgeverij, the series ran until 2022, spanning 201 albums. It features talking animals, Flemish dialect, and pop culture references. The comic inspired an animated film (*Urbanus de Vuilnisheld*, 2019) and crossovers with *De Kiekeboes*. Statues of the characters were erected in Tollembeek and Middelkerke.

The Mississippi Wing of Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the highest echelon of Civil Air Patrol in the state of Mississippi. Mississippi Wing headquarters are located in Jackson, Mississippi. The Mississippi Wing consists of over 500 cadet and adult members at over 16 locations across the state of Mississippi.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (born 1989) is a Kenyan political analyst and corporate executive. She is the executive director of Siasa Place, a youth-led political organization she co-founded in 2015. Educated at Jacksonville State University, she holds degrees in journalism, sociology, and public administration.
Paul Alden Brinegar Jr. (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American character actor best known for his roles in three Western series: The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp, Rawhide, and Lancer.
Serbs in Germany (Lang-sr; Lang-de) refers to persons living in Germany who have total or partial Serbian ancestry. They form the seventh largest group of foreigners in Germany.

The Virginia Constitutional Convention of 1864 was a gathering of Union-loyal delegates from occupied Virginia counties during the Civil War. Meeting in Alexandria from February to April 1864, it abolished slavery without compensation, ended viva voce voting, and established a new constitution. This document remained in effect until replaced by the 1870 Constitution.

Sokratis Kokkalis (born 27 May 1939) is a Greek billionaire businessman, founder of Intracom Holdings and Intralot, and former owner of Olympiacos FC and Olympiacos BC. His companies specialize in telecommunications, defense electronics, and gaming technology.

Richard Mellon Scaife (July 3, 1932 – July 4, 2014) was an American billionaire, newspaper publisher, and conservative philanthropist. An heir to the Mellon banking fortune, he owned the *Pittsburgh Tribune-Review* and funded numerous conservative causes, including The Heritage Foundation and the Hoover Institution. Scaife gained notoriety for financing opposition research against President Bill Clinton, including conspiracy theories surrounding Vince Foster's death. He also supported libertarian think tanks, media outlets like *Newsmax*, and cultural institutions such as the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra. Despite his political activism, he later reconciled with Clinton, who spoke at his memorial service. Scaife remained a polarizing figure in American politics until his death at 82.

TV Sound and Image is a 2012 compilation album of library music, released by Soul Jazz Records on CD and vinyl. Curated by Stuart Baker, Pete Reilly, and Steve Platt, it features classic TV themes and incidental music. Critics praised its nostalgic appeal and funky grooves, with *AllMusic* and *The Irish Times* highlighting its enduring quality and influence.

The Roland Space Echo is a series of tape delay effects units introduced in the 1970s, known for their warm, gritty sound. The most famous model, the RE-201, combined echo and spring reverb, using a continuous tape loop for reliable performance. Later models added features like chorus and balanced outputs.

The Ricoh 2A03 or RP2A03 (NTSC version) / Ricoh 2A07 or RP2A07 (PAL version) is an 8-bit microprocessor manufactured by Ricoh for the Nintendo Entertainment System video game console. It was also used as a sound chip and secondary CPU by Nintendo's arcade games Punch-Out!! and Donkey Kong 3.

Braeden Lemasters (born January 27, 1996) is an American actor and musician. He began his acting career as a child, appearing in *Six Feet Under* (2005) and later earning acclaim for his role in *Men of a Certain Age* (2009–2011). He has also starred in films such as *Easy A* (2010) and *A Christmas Story* sequel (2011). As a musician, he is the lead guitarist and vocalist of the indie rock band Wallows, which gained recognition with their debut album *Nothing Happens* (2019) and hits like *Are You Bored Yet?* The band has released multiple EPs and albums under Atlantic Records.

Ajantrik (lit. 'The Unmechanical') is a 1958 Indian Bengali-language drama film directed by Ritwik Ghatak. Based on a short story by Subodh Ghosh, it follows Bimal, a lonely taxi driver who forms an emotional bond with his dilapidated 1920 Chevrolet, named Jagaddal. Starring Kali Banerjee, the film explores themes of industrialization and human-machine relationships. With a score by Ali Akbar Khan and cinematography by Dinen Gupta, *Ajantrik* is regarded as an early example of modernist Indian cinema. It premiered at the Venice Film Festival, where it garnered critical acclaim despite its lack of subtitles.

The New Zealand Superstock Championship is an annually contested national championship for superstocks on a rotating track schedule. The current champion is Asher Rees, registered to Kihikihi Speedway, who won his first title at Paradise Valley Raceway, near Rotorua, in January 2021.

Graham Stretch was the inaugural champion, winning the title at Palmerston North International Speedway(Central Energy Trust Arena) in the 1965/66 season. Craig Boote and Kevin Free have been the most successful winners, each winning 3 times.

Attabad Lake (Lang-ur) is a lake located in the Gojal region of Hunza Valley in Gilgit−Baltistan, Pakistan. It was created in January 2010 as the result of a major landslide in Attabad. The lake has become one of the biggest tourist attractions in Gilgit−Baltistan, offering activities like boating, jet-skiing, fishing and other recreational activities.

Parmalat S.p.A. is a dairy and food corporation which is a subsidiary of French multinational company Lactalis. It was founded by Calisto Tanzi in 1961.

Having become the leading global company in the production of long-life milk using ultra-high-temperature processing, the company collapsed in 2003 with a €14bn ($20bn; £13bn) hole in its accounts in what remains Europe's biggest bankruptcy. Since 2011, it has been a subsidiary of French group Lactalis (with fuller control since 2019). Today, Parmalat is a company with a global presence, having operations in Europe, North America, South America, Australia, China, and South Africa.

Still specializing in UHT milk and milk derivatives (varieties of yogurt, cheese, butter, ice cream, etc.), the group also sells fruit juices distributed under the brand names Lactis, Santal, Malù, and Kyr. Its worldwide operations include almost 140 production centres and some 16,000 employees, with 5,000 Italian dairy farms supplying it. Its shares were listed on the Borsa Italiana.

Don Sweeney (born August 17, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey defenceman and current general manager of the Boston Bruins. He played 15 seasons with the Bruins, becoming one of only four players in franchise history to appear in over 1,000 games. After retiring in 2004, he transitioned to management, becoming the Bruins' general manager in 2015. Under his leadership, the team reached the 2019 Stanley Cup Finals, earning Sweeney the NHL General Manager of the Year Award. He previously played college hockey at Harvard University.

Matt Daniels (born September 27, 1989) is an American football coach and former safety, currently serving as the special teams coordinator for the Minnesota Vikings. He played in the NFL for the St. Louis Rams, Jacksonville Jaguars, and San Diego Chargers before transitioning to coaching with the Rams, Dallas Cowboys, and Vikings.

Mount Kineo is a 1,789-foot (545 m) mountain in Piscataquis County, Maine, known for its large rhyolite formation, once used by Indigenous peoples for toolmaking. Located on a peninsula in Moosehead Lake, it features a historic golf course and state park accessible only by boat. The site was a popular 19th-century resort destination, though its grand hotel burned down in the 20th century. The mountain offers hiking trails and scenic views.

The Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963 is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. At the time of its passage, the Act was intended to extend the protection of workplace health, safety and welfare under the Factories Act 1961 to other employees in Great Britain. Though as of 2008 some of it remains in force, it has largely been superseded by the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and regulations made under it.

Breach of the residual provisions is still a crime punishable on summary conviction in the magistrates' court by a fine of up to £400 or, on indictment in the Crown Court, imprisonment for up to two years and an unlimited fine.

In the event of damage arising from a breach of the Act, there may be civil liability for breach of statutory duty. Though no such liability is stipulated by the Act itself, none is excluded and the facts could be such as to give rise to a cause of action in that tort. A breach not actionable in itself may be evidential towards a claim for common law negligence. In particular, a criminal conviction may be given in evidence.

Baguma Crescent, popularly known as Beenie Gunter, is a Ugandan reggae dancehall artist, commonly known for his hit songs Pon Mi and Olina Work, the latter, a collaboration with Nigerian artist Skales. He released his 25 track debut studio album No Fear in 2019 that featured artists like Skales and Lydia Jazmine.

Peaked Mountain (pronounced pea-kid) is a nature reserve located in Monson, Massachusetts. The property is approximately 2,000 acres (8.1 km2) and is owned by The Trustees of Reservations, a non-profit conservation organization. The principal landowners of its western slope, Leonard and Roslyn Harrington and Richard Elliott, have long permitted the public to hike through their properties to the summit. Concerned about the mountain's future, they sought the assistance of The Trustees of Reservations in developing a long-term strategy for protecting the mountain and securing public access after their lifetimes.
The Antifascist Committee of Ukraine is an anti-fascist and pro-Russian organization, closely linked to the Communist Party of Ukraine.

Jean Messiha (born Hossam Boutros Messiha, September 10, 1970) is a French economist, civil servant, and far-right political commentator. Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a Coptic Christian family, he moved to France as a child and later became a naturalized citizen. Educated at Sciences Po and the École nationale d'administration, he worked as a high-ranking civil servant before joining Marine Le Pen's National Rally as an advisor. A vocal supporter of the Great Replacement conspiracy theory, he has criticized Islam and the European Union. After leaving the National Rally in 2020, he joined Éric Zemmour's Reconquête party in 2022. Messiha has been suspended from social media for violating hate speech policies.

*Emotional* is the debut studio album by American R&B singer Carl Thomas, released on April 18, 2000, by Bad Boy and Arista Records. The album features production from Sean "Diddy" Combs, Heavy D, and Chucky Thompson, among others. It includes the singles "Summer Rain," "I Wish," and the title track "Emotional." Critics praised its romantic themes and Thomas's vocal performance, with *Rolling Stone* naming it one of the Top 50 Albums of 2000.
Hans Christian Hagedorn (6 March 1888 – 6 October 1971) was the creator of NPH insulin and the founder of Nordisk Insulinlaboratorium, which is known today as Novo Nordisk.
The Rechtboomssloot, or Recht Boomssloot, is a canal in the Lastage (Nieuwmarktbuurt) neighborhood in the center of Amsterdam.

Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist best known for his 1798 work *Museum Boltenianum*, a catalog of mollusk specimens from the collection of Joachim Friedrich Bolten. Though initially overlooked, the catalog introduced many valid taxonomic names, later recognized by William Healey Dall. The International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN) ruled in 1956 that Röding, not Bolten, authored the taxonomic descriptions in the work.

David John Lee (born 26 November 1969) is an English former professional footballer who played as a central defender, most notably for Chelsea, where he spent ten years and made 196 appearances. After loan spells at Reading, Plymouth Argyle, Portsmouth, and Sheffield United, he later played for Bristol Rovers, Crystal Palace, and several non-league clubs. Lee also represented England at under-21 level. Following his playing career, he worked as a scout for Bristol City and briefly served as assistant manager and caretaker manager at Northampton Town in 2011.

Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus (died AD 66), Roman senator, who lived in the 1st century AD. Notable for his principled opposition to the emperor Nero and his interest in Stoicism, he was the husband of Arria, who was the daughter of A. Caecina Paetus and the elder Arria, father-in-law of Helvidius Priscus, and a friend and relative by marriage of the poet Persius. Thrasea was the most prominent member of the political faction known today as the Stoic Opposition.

Tamara Bowie (born June 3, 1981) is an American former professional basketball player. In 2007, she was named the Úrvalsdeild kvenna Foreign Player of the Year after leading the league in scoring and rebounds. She played professionally in Iceland, Latvia, Israel, Greece and Bulgaria, where she won the national championship in 2011.

Life is a 1999 American comedy-drama film directed by Ted Demme and starring Eddie Murphy and Martin Lawrence as two wrongfully convicted men serving life sentences in a Mississippi prison. The film follows their decades-long friendship, struggles with harsh prison life, and repeated escape attempts. Produced by Brian Grazer and Murphy, the screenplay was written by Robert Ramsey and Matthew Stone. Despite its $80 million budget, the film underperformed at the box office, grossing $73.3 million worldwide. Critics gave mixed reviews, praising the chemistry between Murphy and Lawrence but noting uneven humor. The soundtrack, featuring hip-hop and R&B, was a commercial success, reaching No. 10 on the *Billboard* 200. Filming took place in California, though the story is set in Mississippi.

The Forward Gal Stakes is a Grade III American Thoroughbred horse race for three-year-old fillies, over a distance of seven furlongs on the dirt held annually in late January or early February at Gulfstream Park in Hallandale Beach, Florida. The event currently carries a purse of $100,000.

Fluorenone is an aromatic organic compound with the formula C₁₃H₈O, consisting of a ketone group bonded to a fluorene core. It is a yellow solid, insoluble in water but soluble in organic solvents. Fluorenone serves as a precursor in organic synthesis and has applications in materials science, including organic light-emitting devices (OLEDs). Its derivatives, such as azafluorenones, exhibit biological activity, including antimicrobial and anti-malarial properties.

Rod Diridon Sr. (born February 8, 1939) is an American politician known for his leadership in mass transportation. He served as chair of the California High-Speed Rail Authority from 2001-2003. He currently chairs the advisory board of the United States High Speed Rail Association, and he chairs the National Heritage Area Project for Santa Clara County, California. The New York Times calls Diridon "a tireless advocate of public transport for the Bay Area."

Diridon has also been active in Silicon Valley in leadership on environmental issues, the arts and culture, higher education, community organizations, and philanthropy.

Eclipse Island is a barren island in Western Australia, 17 kilometres (10.6 mi) due south of Albany, and 6.1 km (3.8 mi) south of the nearest point of the mainland coast, which is Cave Point on the southern coast of Torndirrup Peninsula. Eclipse Island Lighthouse is located near the centre of the island. Nearby South West Island contains Western Australia's southernmost landmass.
Veniamin Beylin (1904–1982) was a Soviet major general who commanded the 91st Guards Rifle Division and served as chief of staff of the 3rd Shock Army during World War II.

Dick Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is an American former football player and coach. He played tight end and defensive end at Penn State (1960–1962) before becoming a longtime assistant under Joe Paterno (1973–1983, 1990–2011). He also served as head coach at Rutgers (1984–1989), compiling a 28–33–4 record. Anderson coached multiple All-American offensive linemen at Penn State, including Pro Football Hall of Famer Mike Munchak.
The Wodan Timbur Coaster is a wooden roller coaster at Europa-Park in Germany, themed after Norse mythology and featuring immersive special effects.

The April 19 University Movement (Spanish initials: MU19A) is a Nicaraguan student movement created on April 25th, 2018 in Managua, Nicaragua. The student group opposes the government of Daniel Ortega who from 1979-1990 served as the country's first President following the Nicaraguan Revolution, was re-elected in 2006, and is the nation's current President. The April 19 University Movement organized and participated in the 2018–2020 Nicaraguan protests that began April 18th, 2018 in response to Social Security (INSS) reforms. The group has also served as spokesperson in national dialogue.

This student movement is considered a terrorist group by the Nicaraguan government as mentioned by the press: “The leaders [of the April 19th University Movement] are accused of crimes including murder, robbery, terrorism, kidnapping, trafficking, possession and use of restricted weapons, fires, torture, injuries, exposing people to danger.”

The first season of Idol Puerto Rico premiered on August 7, 2011 and continued until November 21, 2011. It was won by Christian Pagán. The season was hosted by Jaime Augusto Mayol. The winner, Christian Pagán, signed with Universal Music Group, the label in partnership with Idol Puerto Rico, and has released two albums.

The 2nd Infantry Brigade was a formation of the New Zealand Military Forces, active from 1916 to 1919 and again from 1924 to 1964. During the First World War, it served on the Western Front as part of the New Zealand Division, fighting in battles such as Messines and Passchendaele. In the interwar and Second World War periods, it was a Territorial Force unit, initially as the Wellington Infantry Brigade before being redesignated. It was disbanded in 1964.
Feliksas Vaitkus (1907–1956) was a Lithuanian-American aviator who made a solo transatlantic flight in 1935, becoming the sixth pilot to achieve the feat. He later served in the U.S. Air Force.
Buck Brothers were a British pop-punk band (2005–2011) known for their energetic live performances and DIY approach, including setting a world record for most gigs in 12 hours.

Phoebe Forrester is a fictional character from the CBS soap opera *The Bold and the Beautiful*, portrayed primarily by MacKenzie Mauzy from 2006 to 2008. Introduced in 1999 as the twin daughter of Ridge Forrester (Ron Moss) and Taylor Hayes (Hunter Tylo), Phoebe was involved in major storylines, including a controversial romance with her legal uncle, Rick Forrester (Kyle Lowder). Her character was also central to a music-themed arc involving singer Constantine Parros (Constantine Maroulis). Phoebe was killed off in a 2008 car crash after a heated confrontation with Rick. Mauzy received praise for her performance, earning *Soap Opera Digest*'s "Hottest Newcomer" award in 2007.

Hainan HNA Infrastructure Investment Group Co., Ltd. formerly Hainan Island Construction Co., Ltd. is a Chinese listed company based in Haikou. in mid-2016 the company acquired HNA Infrastructure Group (Chinese: 海航基础产业集团) in a reverse IPO from intermediate parent company HNA Infrastructure Holding Group (Chinese: 海航基础控股集团), which HNA Infrastructure Group is the parent company of HNA Real Estate and HNA Airport Group (Chinese: 海航机场集团, in turn HNA Airport Group is the parent company of HNA Airport Holdings (Group) (Chinese: 海航机场控股(集团)) and HNA Airport Holdings is the parent company of Sanya Phoenix International Airport Co., Ltd.); HNA Airport Group is the largest shareholder of Haikou Meilan International Airport (19.58% as at October 2016), which in turn the largest shareholder of Hong Kong listed company HNA Infrastructure. In December 2016 a proposed capital increase of the HK-listed company was announced. HNA Infrastructure Investment Group would purchase a minority share directly. Haikou Meilan International Airport was the second-largest shareholder of Hainan Airlines; Hainan Airlines, however, also owned a minority stake in Haikou Meilan International Airport.

The registered office was located in Meilan District, but the general office was located in West Zone 21/F, HNA Building on 7 Guoxing Avenue.

Yusef Abdul Lateef (born William Emanuel Huddleston; October 9, 1920 – December 23, 2013) was an American jazz multi-instrumentalist, composer, and prominent figure among the Ahmadiyya Community in America.

Although Lateef's main instruments were the tenor saxophone and flute, he also played oboe and bassoon, both rare in jazz, and non-western instruments such as the bamboo flute, shanai, shofar, xun, arghul and koto. He is known for having been an innovator in the blending of jazz with "Eastern" music. Peter Keepnews, in his New York Times obituary of Lateef, wrote that the musician "played world music before world music had a name".

Lateef's books included two novellas entitled A Night in the Garden of Love and Another Avenue, the short story collections Spheres and Rain Shapes, also his autobiography, The Gentle Giant, written in collaboration with Herb Boyd. Along with his record label YAL Records, Lateef owned Fana Music, a music publishing company. Lateef published his own work through Fana, which includes Yusef Lateef's Flute Book of the Blues and many of his own orchestral compositions.

METAP2 (methionine aminopeptidase 2) is an enzyme that in humans is encoded by the *METAP2* gene. It catalyzes the removal of N-terminal methionine residues from nascent proteins, playing a role in protein processing. Structurally, it contains a dimetal active site, typically binding cobalt or zinc, though its physiological metal cofactor remains debated. METAP2 interacts with eukaryotic initiation factor 2α (eIF-2α), protecting it from inhibitory phosphorylation and regulating protein synthesis. Clinically, it is implicated in angiogenesis, and its inhibition by compounds like fumagillin and TNP-470 has been explored for anticancer and antimalarial applications. Beloranib, a METAP2 inhibitor, showed promise in obesity treatment but was discontinued due to safety concerns. METAP2 also interacts with protein kinase R (PKR).

The Oneida Community Mansion House is a historic building in Oneida, New York, that served as the communal residence of the Oneida Community, a 19th-century religious utopian group led by John Humphrey Noyes. Constructed in stages between 1848 and 1878, the mansion housed the community's unique social experiments, including complex marriage and stirpiculture (selective breeding). Designed in Italianate and Second Empire styles, it featured communal spaces like the Big Hall, where members gathered for discussions and mutual criticism. After the community disbanded in 1880, the mansion became a private residence and later a nonprofit museum. It was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1965 and remains a key site in the study of American utopian movements.

Ellen Battelle Dietrick (1847–1895) was an American suffragist, writer, and reformer. A key figure in the women's rights movement, she co-founded the Kentucky Equal Rights Association, advocated for labor reform, and contributed to *The Woman's Bible*. She also wrote on religion and gender equality.
Joseph Henry Rodriguez (born December 12, 1930) is a Senior United States district judge of the United States District Court for the District of New Jersey.

Buck Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is a former American football defensive tackle who played in the National Football League (NFL). An undrafted free agent in 2001, he spent time with the Miami Dolphins, Chicago Bears, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers, winning Super Bowl XXXVII with the latter. He played college football at Florida.

Freak Magnet is a studio album by Violent Femmes, released in 2000. It contains the single "Sleepwalkin'".

Originally scheduled for a 1998 release on Interscope Records, Freak Magnet was pulled when the band was dropped from the label. A reissue with live bonus tracks were released via Shout! Factory in 2005.

The Camp Beauregard Memorial is a historic Confederate monument near Water Valley, Kentucky, commemorating soldiers who died at a Civil War training camp. Erected in 1909 by the United Daughters of the Confederacy, it was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1997.

William J. Maddox Jr (May 22, 1921 – January 5, 2001) was a United States Army Aviator and a major general in the United States Army. During his career he amassed over 10,500 flying hours, almost 4,000 of those in combat over Korea, Vietnam and Cambodia.

He served three tours in Vietnam, the last as the commander of an armored division. In 1976, he commanded the United States Army Aviation Center. Maddox was instrumental in identifying the need for air mobility, developing a management structure that ensured the Army met its aviation objectives. Maddox is one of army's most highly decorated aviators.
Salvatore Grippi (1921–2017) was an American painter and printmaker whose work is held in major museum collections.

Fort Cumberland was a British colonial fort built in 1754 at present-day Cumberland, Maryland, during the French and Indian War. It served as a key military outpost and staging ground for General Braddock's expedition. The site is now occupied by Emmanuel Episcopal Church, though remnants of the fort remain underground.

Yemodish Bekele (born 1960) is a published author of more than seven books, including poetry, short stories, and a novella, and was the first Ethiopian woman to publish her own collection of short stories. A crime journalist, Yemedish was also the first woman to become the head editor for Polis Ena Ermijaw (‘Police and Future Journeys’) the newspaper of the Federal Police in Ethiopia.

An activist for women's rights, she works with and serves on board for organisations devoted to women's advancement. She is currently the president of the Ethiopian Women Writers’ Association, and serves as the chair of the board of the Network of Ethiopian Women's Associations.
Stefan Jackiw (born in Boston) is an American classical violinist.

Pope Benedict XVI, who led the Roman Catholic Church as Pope from 2005 to 2013, continued manouevring the Church through the dynamics of modernity, which the Church had begun engaging in with the Second Vatican Council. Because the question of religious pluralism is a key issue raised by modernity, ecumenism, the establishment of harmony and dialogue between the different Christian denominations, is a significant concern of a post Second Vatican Council Church. Pope Benedict XVI's approach has been characterised as leaning toward the conservative while still being expansive and engaged, involving the full breadth of Christendom, including the Orthodox Churches and Protestant churches, as well as freshly engaging with other Christian bodies considered by Roman Catholics to be more heterodox, such as The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

GABA transporter type 2 (GAT2) is a protein that transports the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and taurine in the body. Unlike GAT1 and GAT3, which are highly expressed in the brain and strongly influence GABAergic signaling, GAT2 is found primarily in peripheral tissues such as the liver and kidneys. In the liver, it is a major taurine transporter, while in the kidneys, it is located in the basolateral membranes of proximal tubules, though its exact role remains unclear. Although GAT2 is present at the blood-brain barrier, its deletion in mice causes only minor changes, such as slightly elevated brain taurine levels. While GABA transporters are implicated in epilepsy and neuropsychiatric disorders, GAT2 is less significant than GAT1 and GAT3 in brain function.

Colonel Sir John Wallace Pringle, CB, FRGS (23 May 1863 – 16 July 1938) was a British engineer who was Chief Inspecting Officer of the Railways Inspectorate of the Ministry of Transport from 1916 to 1929. As such he was in charge of investigations into a number of serious railway accidents in the UK.

Hannah Mary Bardell (born 1 June 1983) is a Scottish National Party (SNP) politician who has served as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Livingston since 2015. She has held various SNP spokesperson roles, including for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (2018–2019) and Work and Pensions (2015).

The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was the 22nd season of the Big 12 Conference in NCAA Division I FBS football. The season began on September 2, 2017, and concluded with the Big 12 Championship Game on December 2, where Oklahoma defeated TCU to win its third consecutive conference title. The Sooners, led by Heisman Trophy winner Baker Mayfield, finished the regular season 12–1 and earned a spot in the College Football Playoff.  

The preseason media poll predicted Oklahoma as the favorite, marking the fifth time since 2011 they were picked to win the conference. The season featured key non-conference matchups against Power Five opponents, including Ohio State and USC. Oklahoma's Mayfield was later selected first overall in the 2018 NFL Draft by the Cleveland Browns.  

The Big 12 Championship Game returned for the first time since 2010, with the conference abandoning its round-robin format. Seven Big 12 teams participated in bowl games, including Oklahoma in the Rose Bowl (CFP semifinal). The season also saw multiple players earn All-American honors.

Rugby League Week (frequently abbreviated to RLW) was the highest selling Australian rugby league magazine, ahead of major competitor Big League. It was published weekly (on Wednesdays) during the Australian rugby league season, which runs from March to late September (roughly corresponding to the southern hemisphere autumn and winter). The magazine was headquartered in Sydney.

The New York World was a newspaper published in New York City from 1860 until 1931. The paper played a major role in the history of American newspapers. It was a leading national voice of the Democratic Party. From 1883 to 1911 under publisher Joseph Pulitzer, it was a pioneer in yellow journalism, capturing readers' attention with sensation, sports, sex and scandal and pushing its daily circulation to the one-million mark. It was sold in 1930 and merged into the New York World-Telegram.

Alberta Ballet is a professional ballet company based in Calgary, Alberta, founded in 1966 by Ruth Carse and Muriel Taylor. Under artistic directors such as Mikko Nissinen and Jean Grand-Maître, it has gained recognition for collaborations with musicians like Joni Mitchell and Elton John. The company performs in both Calgary and Edmonton and operates the Alberta Ballet School, a training program for aspiring dancers.
Elizabeth Phillips Hughes MBE (12 July 1851 – 19 December 1925) was a Welsh scholar, teacher, and promoter of women's education, first principal of the Cambridge Training College for Women.

"Adrift" is the second episode of the second season of the American television series *Lost*. Directed by Stephen Williams and written by Steven Maeda and Leonard Dick, it originally aired on September 28, 2005. The episode follows Michael Dawson (Harold Perrineau) and James "Sawyer" Ford (Josh Holloway) as they struggle to survive at sea after their raft is destroyed by the Others. Meanwhile, John Locke (Terry O'Quinn) and Kate Austen (Evangeline Lilly) explore the mysterious hatch, encountering Desmond Hume (Henry Ian Cusick), who forces Locke to input a code into a computer. Flashbacks reveal Michael's past legal battle with his ex-girlfriend Susan (Tamara Taylor) over custody of their son, Walt.  

Critics gave mixed reviews, praising the hatch storyline but criticizing the slow pacing of the raft scenes. The episode was watched by 23.17 million viewers, making it the second most-watched episode of *Lost* at the time.

Emotional is the debut album by American R&B singer Carl Thomas. It was released by Bad Boy Records and Arista Records on April 18, 2000, in the United States. Emotional earned Thomas a nomination in the Best R&B/Soul Album at the 2001 Soul Train Music Awards, also garnering him a Male & Best R&B/Soul or Rap New Artist nod. "I Wish" was also nominated for Best R&B/Soul Single.
Cary Castle stood on Lodge Hill overlooking the town of Castle Cary, Somerset, England. It is a Scheduled Ancient Monument.

Hotel Kazakhstan is a 26-story hotel in Almaty, Kazakhstan, completed in 1977. Standing at 102 meters (335 ft), it is notable for its elliptical design and structural resilience in a high-seismic zone. Its facade features a distinctive golden crown-like attic and a vertical row of bay windows, while the lobby houses the 34-meter tapestry *The Rainbow of Kazakhstan*, which earned its creators the State Prize of the Kazakh SSR in 1980. Constructed with reinforced concrete and finished with marble, granite, and shell rock, the building was recognized as a historical and cultural monument by Almaty authorities in 1979.

Aristides Aquino Núñez (born April 22, 1994) is a Dominican professional baseball outfielder for the Chunichi Dragons of Nippon Professional Baseball (NPB). He has played for the Cincinnati Reds of Major League Baseball.

William Pole (1561–1635) was an English antiquary, politician, and landowner from Devon. He served as Sheriff of Devon and MP for Bossiney, and was knighted in 1606. His unpublished manuscripts on Devon's history, genealogy, and heraldry became key sources for later historians, including Tristram Risdon and John Prince.

The Chalfont Viaduct, also known as Misbourne Viaduct, is a brick railway bridge in Gerrards Cross, England, carrying the Chiltern Main Line over the M25 motorway. Built between 1902 and 1906 by the Great Western Railway, it features five semi-elliptical arches and was designed by engineer James Charles Inglis. During the M25's construction in the 1980s, the viaduct was preserved, with the motorway routed beneath its arches. The bridge gained local fame for its long-standing graffiti slogan, *"give peas a chance"*, visible to motorists. Over the years, the graffiti was altered multiple times, sparking public reactions and even a petition to restore the original message. In 2020, it was temporarily replaced with *"thank you NHS"* during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The railroad brotherhoods are labor unions of railroad workers in the United States. They first appeared in 1863 and they are still active. Until recent years they were largely independent of each other and of the American Federation of Labor.

Nicky Romero (born Nick Rotteveel; January 6, 1989) is a Dutch DJ, record producer, and label owner known for his contributions to electronic dance music (EDM). Rising to fame with hits like *"Toulouse"* (2012) and *"I Could Be the One"* (with Avicii, 2013), he has performed at major festivals worldwide. Romero founded Protocol Recordings in 2012 and hosts the weekly *Protocol Radio*. He has also produced tracks for artists like Rihanna, Britney Spears, and David Guetta.

HNA Infrastructure Investment Group (formerly Hainan Island Construction and Hainan Zhuxin Investment) is a Chinese publicly traded company (SSE: 600515) specializing in real estate, transport infrastructure, and the construction and operation of airports and seaports. Headquartered in Haikou, Hainan, it was founded in 1993 as The First Investment and Merchant and listed on the Shanghai Stock Exchange in 2002. The company underwent multiple name changes and ownership restructurings, eventually becoming a subsidiary of HNA Group through intermediate holdings.  

In 2016, it completed a reverse takeover of HNA Infrastructure Group, consolidating assets under its parent company, HNA Infrastructure Holding Group. The company has issued bonds to finance major projects, including the Nanhai Pearl Artificial Island development. As of its latest restructuring, HNA Industrial Group and HNA Infrastructure Holding Group hold significant ownership stakes.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics (FIS VŠE), also known as the School of Informatics and Statistics, is the fourth of six faculties at Prague University of Economics and Business. The faculty was established in 1991, following the dissolution of the Faculty of Direction. Its academic focus is informatics, statistics, econometrics and other mathematical methods applied to business practice. The faculty has eight departments and several research laboratories, and hosts around 2,500 students across its programs.

Mathew Street is a street in Liverpool, England, notable as the location of the new Cavern Club, the Beatles having played in the original club on numerous occasions in their early career.

Mathew Street is visited by thousands of tourists a year, who visit the Cavern Club and many surrounding attractions including a statue of John Lennon, a Beatles store, the Liverpool Beatles Museum and several pubs formerly frequented by the band. A wall in Mathew Street is adorned by a sculpture by Arthur Dooley entitled "Four Lads Who Shook the World".

Four Lane Ends is a Tyne and Wear Metro station, serving the suburbs of Benton and Longbenton, North Tyneside in Tyne and Wear, England. It joined the network on 11 August 1980, following the opening of the first phase of the network, between Haymarket and Tynemouth via Four Lane Ends.

Bilston is a small village in Midlothian, Scotland. It is located on the edge of Edinburgh, just south of Loanhead on the A701. The Bilston Burn Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) was occupied from 2002 until the mid-2010s by protestors who successfully opposed plans for a bypass.

The heute-show ("(the) today show"; stylized in its logo as heute SHOW) is a German late-night satirical television program airing every Friday evening on public broadcasting channel ZDF. A conceptual adaptation of The Daily Show with Jon Stewart, it is presented by German comedian and journalist Oliver Welke. Heute show presents the weekly news with funny or sarcastic and cynical comments, especially focusing on issues that are subject of the latest political discussions in Germany. Welke argues that he might help to make people interested in politics and might help to point out "what goes the wrong way" in parliamentary debates, in ministries, in institutions, and in parties. Sometimes, he simply mocks "people who deserve it" in his show.

Coryphodon is an extinct genus of large, semi-aquatic mammals that lived from the Late Paleocene to the Middle Eocene (57–46 million years ago). Among the largest mammals of its time, it reached about 1 metre (3.3 ft) at the shoulder and weighed up to 700 kg (1,500 lb). It had a small brain relative to its body size and short limbs adapted for supporting its weight.  

Coryphodon inhabited swampy environments, feeding on soft vegetation, fungi, and woody plants. Fossils from Ellesmere Island suggest it adapted to Arctic conditions by shifting its diet seasonally. The genus exhibited sexual dimorphism, with males likely possessing larger canines than females. Over 30 proposed species have been invalidated, leaving only a few recognized within the genus. Its size fluctuated over time, possibly in response to climate changes.

Kemal Deen is a Sri Lankan professor of surgery at the University of Kelaniya. Educated at Trinity College, Kandy, and the University of Peradeniya, he later earned a Doctor of Medicine from the University of Birmingham and trained at the Mayo Clinic. He played a key role in introducing liver transplantation to Sri Lanka. A fellow of multiple surgical colleges, Deen has held visiting professorships in India and Malaysia and has published extensively in medical research.

Oliver Samuels (born 4 November 1948) is a Jamaican actor and comedian, best known for his role in the television series *Oliver at Large*. He is widely regarded as one of the Caribbean's most celebrated comedic talents.

A local council in Israel is a form of municipal government for settlements with populations typically over 2,000, but not large enough to be declared a city. The Israeli Interior Minister grants this status, considering residents' preferences. Local councils handle town planning and other municipal functions. They are represented nationally by the Union of Local Authorities in Israel (ULAI), established in 1938 during the British Mandate.

CCM is a Canadian bicycle manufacturer based in Montreal, Quebec.

"CCM" was originally an initialism for Canada Cycle & Motor Co. Ltd. However, the company would eventually split into two separate entities both maintaining the CCM trademark, one maintaining bicycle manufacturing and the other, CCM (The Hockey Company), producing hockey equipment.

ESET NOD32 Antivirus, commonly known as NOD32, is an antivirus software package made by the Slovak company ESET. ESET NOD32 Antivirus is sold in two editions, Home Edition and Business Edition. The Business Edition packages add ESET Remote Administrator allowing for server deployment and management, mirroring of threat signature database updates and the ability to install on Microsoft Windows Server operating systems.

Clidastes is an extinct genus of mosasaurs, a group of marine reptiles that lived during the Late Cretaceous (Coniacian to Campanian, 85–80 million years ago). One of the smallest mosasaurs, it typically measured 2–4 meters (6.6–13.1 ft) in length, though some specimens reached up to 6.2 meters (20 ft). Its streamlined body and specialized vertebrae allowed for strong, agile swimming in shallow waters. The genus is distinguished by its interlocking vertebral processes, which provided stability while maintaining flexibility.  

First discovered by E. D. Cope in 1869, *Clidastes* fossils are primarily found in marine deposits in the United States, though past fragmentary findings were reported from Europe and the Middle East. The only valid species, *C. propython*, was designated as the type species after the poorly known original type, *C. iguanavus*, was deemed insufficiently diagnostic. *Clidastes* is considered one of the most basal hydropedal mosasaurines, closely related to *Mosasaurus* but more primitive than later genera like *Prognathodon*.

The Martin PBM Mariner was a twin-engine flying boat designed by the Glenn L. Martin Company for maritime patrol and anti-submarine warfare. First flown in 1939, it served with the U.S. Navy and Coast Guard during World War II, sinking ten U-boats and operating in both the Atlantic and Pacific. Later used in the Korean War, it was eventually replaced by the P5M Marlin. Variants were also operated by the RAF, RAAF, and Dutch Navy.

The men's horizontal bar competition at the 2016 Summer Olympics in Rio de Janeiro was held on 16 August at the HSBC Arena, following qualifying on 6 August. Seventy-one gymnasts from 34 nations competed, with a maximum of two per country advancing to the final. The event featured returning medalists Epke Zonderland (2012 gold) and Fabian Hambüchen (2012 silver), as well as reigning world champion Kōhei Uchimura. The winning score was 15.766. This marked the 24th appearance of the event in Olympic history.

Abubakar Umar Tutare (born 13 February 1965) is a Nigerian politician who served as Senator for Taraba Central from 2011 to 2015. A former commissioner and Secretary to the State Government in Taraba, he was elected after a contentious legal battle over his candidacy.

Hallie Flanagan (August 27, 1889 – June 23, 1969) was an American theater director, playwright, and educator, best known for leading the Federal Theatre Project (1935–1939) under the Works Progress Administration. A pioneer in experimental theater, she also directed drama programs at Vassar and Smith College.

José Vásquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 – January 31, 1980) was a Filipino educator and community development advocate. He pioneered the *Community School* model, integrating education with local agriculture and culture to improve literacy and productivity. Aguilar also led research on vernacular-based early education, demonstrating its effectiveness in Hiligaynon-speaking regions. After serving as a schools superintendent in multiple provinces, he became dean of the University of the Philippines College of Education. In 1959, he received the Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service for his contributions to Philippine education. Aguilar later co-directed the Philippine Center for Language Studies, influencing national language policies.

Dick Guindon (December 2, 1935 – February 27, 2022) was an American cartoonist known for his satirical single-panel cartoons and comic strips, including *Guindon* and *The Carp Chronicles*. His work appeared in publications such as *The Realist*, *Playboy*, and the *Detroit Free Press*.

The Long-a-Coming Depot is a historic train station in Berlin, New Jersey, built in 1856 by the Camden and Atlantic Railroad. Originally named Long-a-Coming, it was renamed Berlin in 1867 and closed in the 1960s. Restored in 1994, it now serves as a community meeting space. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1997.

Silent Sejm (Lang-pl; Lang-lt), also known as the Mute Sejm, is the name given to the session of the Sejm parliament of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth of 1 February 1717 held in Warsaw. A civil war in the Commonwealth was used by the Russian Tsar Peter the Great as an opportunity to intervene as a mediator. It marked the end of Augustus II of Poland's attempts to create an absolute monarchy in Poland, and the beginning of the Russian Empire's increasing influence and control over the Commonwealth.

A phantom border (Lang-de) is an informal delineation following the approximate course of an abolished political border, associated with demographic differences on each side as a continuing legacy of historical division, despite official geopolitical union. Not all former political borders are today phantom borders. Factors that may increase the likelihood of a political border becoming a phantom border upon dissolution, include: short time elapsed since the border's dissolution, long existence of the former border, imporousity of the former border, and divergent characteristics of the political entity formerly governing one side of the border.
